The Woman in White 


 by 

 Wilkie Collins 
 the STORY BEGUN BY WALTER HARTRIGHT 

  of Clement 's Inn , teacher of draw 

 this be the story of what a woman 's patience can endure , and what a Man 's resolution can achieve . 

 if the machinery of the Law could be depend on to fathom every case of suspicion , and to conduct every process of inquiry , with moderate assistance only from the lubricate influence of oil of gold , the event which fill these page might have claim their share of the public attention in a Court of Justice . 

 but the Law be still , in certain inevitable case , the pre - engaged servant of the long purse ; and the story be leave to be tell , for the first time , in this place . as the Judge might once have hear it , so the Reader shall hear it now . no circumstance of importance , from the beginning to the end of the disclosure , shall be relate on hearsay evidence . when the writer of these introductory line   Walter Hartright by name   happen to be more closely connect than other with the incident to be record , he will describe they in his own person . when his experience fail , he will retire from the position of narrator ; and his task will be continue , from the point at which he have leave it off , by other person who can speak to the circumstance under notice from their own knowledge , just as clearly and positively as he have speak before they . 

 thus , the story here present will be tell by more than one pen , as the story of an offence against the law be tell in Court by more than one witness with the same object , in both case , to present the truth always in its most direct and most intelligible aspect ; and to trace the course of one complete series of event , by make the person who have be most closely connect with they , at each successive stage , relate their own experience , word for word . 

 let Walter Hartright , teacher of drawing , age twenty - eight year , be hear first . 



 II 

 it be the last day of July . the long hot summer be draw to a close ; and we , the weary pilgrim of the London pavement , be begin to think of the cloud - shadow on the corn - field , and the autumn breeze on the sea - shore . 

 for my own poor part , the fading summer leave I out of health , out of spirit , and , if the truth must be tell , out of money as well . during the past year I have not manage my professional resource as carefully as usual ; and my extravagance now limit I to the prospect of spend the autumn economically between my mother 's cottage at Hampstead and my own chamber in town . 

 the evening , I remember , be still and cloudy ; the London air be at its heavy ; the distant hum of the street - traffic be at its faint ; the small pulse of the life within I , and the great heart of the city around I , seem to be sink in unison , languidly and more languidly , with the sink sun . I rouse myself from the book which I be dream over rather than read , and leave my chamber to meet the cool night air in the suburb . it be one of the two evening in every week which I be accustomed to spend with my mother and my sister . so I turn my step northward in the direction of Hampstead . 

 event which I have yet to relate make it necessary to mention in this place that my father have be dead some year at the period of which I be now write ; and that my sister Sarah and I be the sole survivor of a family of five child . my father be a drawing - master before I . his exertion have make he highly successful in his profession ; and his affectionate anxiety to provide for the future of those who be dependent on his labour have impel he , from the time of his marriage , to devote to the insuring of his life a much large portion of his income than most man consider it necessary to set aside for that purpose . thank to his admirable prudence and self - denial my mother and sister be leave , after his death , as independent of the world as they have be during his lifetime . I succeed to his connection , and have every reason to feel grateful for the prospect that await I at my starting in life . 

 the quiet twilight be still tremble on the topmost ridge of the heath ; and the view of London below I have sink into a black gulf in the shadow of the cloudy night , when I stand before the gate of my mother 's cottage . I have hardly ring the bell before the house door be open violently ; my worthy italian friend , Professor Pesca , appear in the servant 's place ; and dart out joyously to receive I , with a shrill foreign parody on an english cheer . 

 on his own account , and , I must be allow to add , on mine also , the Professor merit the honour of a formal introduction . accident have make he the starting - point of the strange family story which it be the purpose of these page to unfold . 

 I have first become acquainted with my italian friend by meet he at certain great house where he teach his own language and I teach drawing . all I then know of the history of his life be , that he have once hold a situation in the University of Padua ; that he have leave Italy for political reason   the nature of which he uniformly decline to mention to any one ; and that he have be for many year respectably establish in London as a teacher of language . 

 without be actually a dwarf for he be perfectly well proportion from head to foot Pesca be , I think , the small human being I ever see out of a show - room . remarkable anywhere , by his personal appearance , he be still far distinguish among the rank and file of mankind by the harmless eccentricity of his character . the rule idea of his life appear to be , that he be bind to show his gratitude to the country which have afford he an asylum and a means of subsistence by do his utmost to turn himself into an Englishman . not content with pay the nation in general the compliment of invariably carry an umbrella , and invariably wear gaiter and a white hat , the Professor far aspire to become an Englishman in his habit and amusement , as well as in his personal appearance . find we distinguish , as a nation , by our love of athletic exercise , the little man , in the innocence of his heart , devote himself impromptu to all our english sport and pastime whenever he have the opportunity of join they ; firmly persuade that he could adopt our national amusement of the field by an effort of will precisely as he have adopt our national gaiter and our national white hat . 

 I have see he risk his limb blindly at a fox - hunt and in a cricket - field ; and soon afterwards I see he risk his life , just as blindly , in the sea at Brighton . 

 we have meet there accidentally , and be bath together . if we have be engage in any exercise peculiar to my own nation I should , of course , have look after Pesca carefully ; but as foreigner be generally quite as well able to take care of themselves in the water as Englishmen , it never occur to I that the art of swimming might merely add one more to the list of manly exercise which the Professor believe that he could learn impromptu . soon after we have both strike out from shore , I stop , find my friend do not gain on I , and turn round to look for he . to my horror and amazement , I see nothing between I and the beach but two little white arm which struggle for an instant above the surface of the water , and then disappear from view . when I dive for he , the poor little man be lie quietly coil up at the bottom , in a hollow of shingle , look by many degree small than I have ever see he look before . during the few minute that elapse while I be take he in , the air revive he , and he ascend the step of the machine with my assistance . with the partial recovery of his animation come the return of his wonderful delusion on the subject of swimming . as soon as his chatter tooth would let he speak , he smile vacantly , and say he think it must have be the Cramp . 

 when he have thoroughly recover himself , and have join I on the beach , his warm southern nature break through all artificial english restraint in a moment . he overwhelm I with the wild expression of affection exclaim passionately , in his exaggerated italian way , that he would hold his life henceforth at my disposal and declare that he should never be happy again until he have find an opportunity of prove his gratitude by render I some service which I might remember , on my side , to the end of my day . 

 I do my good to stop the torrent of his tear and protestation by persist in treat the whole adventure as a good subject for a joke ; and succeed at last , as I imagine , in lessen Pesca 's overwhelming sense of obligation to I . little do I think then little do I think afterwards when our pleasant holiday have draw to an end that the opportunity of serve I for which my grateful companion so ardently long be soon to come ; that he be eagerly to seize it on the instant ; and that by so do he be to turn the whole current of my existence into a new channel , and to alter I to myself almost past recognition . 

 yet so it be . if I have not dive for Professor Pesca when he lie under water on his shingle bed , I should in all human probability never have be connect with the story which these page will relate I should never , perhaps , have hear even the name of the woman who have live in all my thought , who have possess herself of all my energy , who have become the one guide influence that now direct the purpose of my life . 



 III 

 Pesca 's face and manner , on the evening when we confront each other at my mother 's gate , be more than sufficient to inform I that something extraordinary have happen . it be quite useless , however , to ask he for an immediate explanation . I could only conjecture , while he be drag I in by both hand , that   know my habit   he have come to the cottage to make sure of meet I that night , and that he have some news to tell of an unusually agreeable kind . 

 we both bounce into the parlour in a highly abrupt and undignified manner . my mother sit by the open window laughing and fan herself . Pesca be one of her especial favourite and his wild eccentricity be always pardonable in her eye . poor dear soul ! from the first moment when she find out that the little Professor be deeply and gratefully attach to her son , she open her heart to he unreservedly , and take all his puzzle foreign peculiarity for grant , without so much as attempt to understand any one of they . 

 my sister Sarah , with all the advantage of youth , be , strangely enough , less pliable . she do full justice to Pesca 's excellent quality of heart ; but she could not accept he implicitly , as my mother accept he , for my sake . her insular notion of propriety rise in perpetual revolt against Pesca 's constitutional contempt for appearance ; and she be always more or less undisguisedly astonish at her mother 's familiarity with the eccentric little foreigner . I have observe , not only in my sister 's case , but in the instance of other , that we of the young generation be nothing like so hearty and so impulsive as some of our elder . I constantly see old people flush and excite by the prospect of some anticipate pleasure which altogether fail to ruffle the tranquillity of their serene grandchild . be we , I wonder , quite such genuine boy and girl now as our senior be in their time ? have the great advance in education take rather too long a stride ; and be we in these modern day , just the least trifle in the world too well bring up ? 

 without attempt to answer those question decisively , I may at least record that I never see my mother and my sister together in Pesca 's society , without find my mother much the young woman of the two . on this occasion , for example , while the old lady be laugh heartily over the boyish manner in which we tumble into the parlour , Sarah be perturbedly pick up the broken piece of a teacup , which the Professor have knock off the table in his precipitate advance to meet I at the door . 

 " I do not   know what would have happen , Walter , " say my mother , " if you have delay much long . Pesca have be half mad with impatience , and I have be half mad with curiosity . the Professor have bring some wonderful news with he , in which he say you be concern ; and he have cruelly refuse to give we the small hint of it till his friend Walter appear . " 

 " very provoking : it spoil the Set , " murmur Sarah to herself , mournfully absorb over the ruin of the broken cup . 

 while these word be be speak , Pesca , happily and fussily unconscious of the irreparable wrong which the crockery have suffer at his hand , be drag a large arm - chair to the opposite end of the room , so as to command we all three , in the character of a public speaker address an audience . have turn the chair with its back towards we , he jump into it on his knee , and excitedly address his small congregation of three from an impromptu pulpit . 

 " now , my good dear , " begin Pesca   who always say " good dear " when he mean " worthy friend " , " listen to I . the time have come I recite my good news I speak at last . " 

 " hear , hear ! " say my mother , humour the joke . 

 " the next thing he will break , mamma , " whisper Sarah , " will be the back of the good arm - chair . " 

 " I go back into my life , and I address myself to the noblest of create being , " continue Pesca , vehemently apostrophise my unworthy self over the top rail of the chair . " who find I dead at the bottom of the sea   through Cramp ; and who pull I up to the top ; and what do I say when I get into my own life and my own clothe again ? " 

 " much more than be at all necessary , " I answer as doggedly as possible ; for the least encouragement in connection with this subject invariably let loose the Professor 's emotion in a flood of tear . 

 " I say , " persist Pesca , " that my life belong to my dear friend , Walter , for the rest of my day and so it do . I say that I should never be happy again till I have find the opportunity of do a good something for Walter and I have never be content with myself till this most blessed day . now , " cry the enthusiastic little man at the top of his voice , " the overflow happiness burst out of I at every pore of my skin , like a perspiration ; for on my faith , and soul , and honour , the something be do at last , and the only word to say now be right - all - right ! " 

 it may be necessary to explain here that Pesca pride himself on be a perfect Englishman in his language , as well as in his dress , manner , and amusement . have pick up a few of our most familiar colloquial expression , he scatter they about over his conversation whenever they happen to occur to he , turn they , in his high relish for their sound and his general ignorance of their sense , into compound word and repetition of his own , and always run they into each other , as if they consist of one long syllable . 

 " among the fine London Houses where I teach the language of my native country , " say the Professor , rush into his long - defer explanation without another word of preface , " there be one , mighty fine , in the big place call Portland . you all know where that be ? yes , yes course - of - course . the fine house , my good dear , have get inside it a fine family . a Mamma , fair and fat ; three young Misses , fair and fat ; two young Misters , fair and fat ; and a Papa , the fairest and the fat of all , who be a mighty merchant , up to his eye in gold a fine man once , but see that he have get a naked head and two chin , fine no long at the present time . now mind ! I teach the sublime Dante to the young Misses , and ah ! my - soul - bless - my - soul ! it be not in human language to say how the sublime Dante puzzle the pretty head of all three ! no matter all in good time and the more lesson the well for I . now mind ! imagine to yourselves that I be teach the young Misses to - day , as usual . we be all four of we down together in the Hell of Dante . at the Seventh Circle but no matter for that : all the Circles be alike to the three young Misses , fair and fat , at the Seventh Circle , nevertheless , my pupil be stick fast ; and I , to set they go again , recite , explain , and blow myself up red - hot with useless enthusiasm , when a creak of boot in the passage outside , and in come the golden Papa , the mighty merchant with the naked head and the two chin . ha ! my good dear , I be close than you think for to the business , now . have you be patient so far ? or have you say to yourselves , ' deuce - what - the - deuce ! Pesca be long - wind to - night ? ' " 

 we declare that we be deeply interested . the Professor go on : 

 " in his hand , the golden Papa have a letter ; and after he have make his excuse for disturb we in our Infernal Region with the common mortal business of the house , he address himself to the three young miss , and begin , as you English begin everything in this blessed world that you have to say , with a great O. ' O , my dear , ' say the mighty merchant , ' I have get here a letter from my friend , Mr. ' the name have slip out of my mind ; but no matter ; we shall come back to that ; yes , yes right - all - right . so the Papa say , ' I have get a letter from my friend , the Mister ; and he want a recommend from I , of a drawing - master , to go down to his house in the country . ' my - soul - bless - my - soul ! when I hear the golden Papa say those word , if I have be big enough to reach up to he , I should have put my arm round his neck , and press he to my bosom in a long and grateful hug ! as it be , I only bounce upon my chair . my seat be on thorn , and my soul be on fire to speak but I hold my tongue , and let Papa go on . ' perhaps you know , ' say this good man of money , twiddle his friend 's letter this way and that , in his golden finger and thumb , ' perhaps you know , my dear , of a drawing - master that I can recommend ? ' the three young Misses all look at each other , and then say   with the indispensable great o to begin   " o , dear no , Papa ! but here be Mr. Pesca ' at the mention of myself I can hold no long the thought of you , my good dear , mount like blood to my head I start from my seat , as if a spike have grow up from the ground through the bottom of my chair I address myself to the mighty merchant , and I say   english phrase   ' dear sir , I have the man ! the first and foremost drawing - master of the world ! recommend he by the post to - night , and send he off , bag and baggage   english phrase again ha ! , send he off , bag and baggage , by the train to - morrow ! ' ' stop , stop , ' say papa ; ' be he a foreigner , or an Englishman ? ' ' english to the bone of his back , ' I answer . ' respectable ? ' say Papa . ' Sir , ' I say   for this last question of his outrage I , and I have do be familiar with he    ' Sir ! the immortal fire of genius burn in this Englishman 's bosom , and , what be more , his father have it before he ! ' ' never mind , ' say the golden barbarian of a papa , ' never mind about his genius , Mr. Pesca . we do not   want genius in this country , unless it be accompany by respectability and then we be very glad to have it , very glad indeed . can your friend produce testimonial letter that speak to his character ? ' I wave my hand negligently . ' letter ? ' I say . ' ha ! my - soul - bless - my - soul ! I should think so , indeed ! volume of letter and portfolio of testimonial , if you like ! ' ' one or two will do , ' say this man of phlegm and money . ' let he send they to I , with his name and address . and stop , stop , Mr. Pesca before you go to your friend , you have well take a note . ' ' bank - note ! ' I say , indignantly . ' no bank - note , if you please , till my brave Englishman have earn it first . ' ' bank - note ! ' say Papa , in a great surprise , ' who talk of bank - note ? I mean a note of the term a memorandum of what he be expect to do . go on with your lesson , Mr. Pesca , and I will give you the necessary extract from my friend 's letter . ' down sit the man of merchandise and money to his pen , ink , and paper ; and down I go once again into the Hell of Dante , with my three young Misses after I . in ten minute ' time the note be write , and the boot of Papa be creak themselves away in the passage outside . from that moment , on my faith , and soul , and honour , I know nothing more ! the glorious thought that I have catch my opportunity at last , and that my grateful service for my dear friend in the world be as good as do already , fly up into my head and make I drunk . how I pull my young Misses and myself out of our Infernal Region again , how my other business be do afterwards , how my little bit of dinner slide itself down my throat , I know no more than a man in the moon . enough for I , that here I be , with the mighty merchant 's note in my hand , as large as life , as hot as fire , and as happy as a king ! ha ! ha ! ha ! right - right - right - all - right ! " here the Professor wave the memorandum of term over his head , and end his long and voluble narrative with his shrill italian parody on an english cheer . " 

 my mother rise the moment he have do , with flushed cheek and brightened eye . she catch the little man warmly by both hand . 

 " my dear , good Pesca , " she say , " I never doubt your true affection for Walter but I be more than ever persuade of it now ! " 

 " I be sure we be very much oblige to Professor Pesca , for Walter 's sake , " add Sarah . she half rise , while she speak , as if to approach the arm - chair , in her turn ; but , observe that Pesca be rapturously kiss my mother 's hand , look serious , and resume her seat . " if the familiar little man treat my mother in that way , how will he treat I ? " face sometimes tell truth ; and that be unquestionably the thought in Sarah 's mind , as she sit down again . 

 although I myself be gratefully sensible of the kindness of Pesca 's motif , my spirit be hardly so much elevated as they ought to have be by the prospect of future employment now place before I . when the Professor have quite do with my mother 's hand , and when I have warmly thank he for his interference on my behalf , I ask to be allow to look at the note of term which his respectable patron have draw up for my inspection . 

 Pesca hand I the paper , with a triumphant flourish of the hand . 

 " Read ! " say the little man majestically . " I promise you my friend , the writing of the golden Papa speak with a tongue of trumpet for itself . " 

 the note of term be plain , straightforward , and comprehensive , at any rate . it inform I , 

 first , that Frederick Fairlie , Esquire , of Limmeridge House . Cumberland , want to engage the service of a thoroughly competent drawing - master , for a period of four month certain . 

 secondly , that the duty which the master be expect to perform would be of a twofold kind . he be to superintend the instruction of two young lady in the art of painting in water - colour ; and he be to devote his leisure time , afterwards , to the business of repair and mount a valuable collection of drawing , which have be suffer to fall into a condition of total neglect . 

 thirdly , that the term offer to the person who should undertake and properly perform these duty be four guineas a week ; that he be to reside at Limmeridge House ; and that he be to be treat there on the footing of a gentleman . 

 fourthly , and lastly , that no person need think of apply for this situation unless he could furnish the most unexceptionable reference to character and ability . the reference be to be send to Mr. Fairlie 's friend in London , who be empower to conclude all necessary arrangement . these instruction be follow by the name and address of Pesca 's employer in Portland Place and there the note , or memorandum , end . 

 the prospect which this offer of an engagement hold out be certainly an attractive one . the employment be likely to be both easy and agreeable ; it be propose to I at the autumn time of the year when I be least occupy ; and the term , judge by my personal experience in my profession , be surprisingly liberal . I know this ; I know that I ought to consider myself very fortunate if I succeed in secure the offer employment and yet , no soon have I read the memorandum than I feel an inexplicable unwillingness within I to stir in the matter . I have never in the whole of my previous experience find my duty and my inclination so painfully and so unaccountably at variance as I find they now . 

 " oh , Walter , your father never have such a chance as this ! " say my mother , when she have read the note of term and have hand it back to I . 

 " such distinguished people to know , " remark Sarah , straighten herself in the chair ; " and on such gratifying term of equality too ! " 

 " yes , yes ; the term , in every sense , be tempt enough , " I reply impatiently . " but before I send in my testimonial , I should like a little time to consider " 

 " consider ! " exclaim my mother . " why , Walter , what be the matter with you ? " 

 " consider ! " echo my sister . " what a very extraordinary thing to say , under the circumstance ! " 

 " consider ! " chime in the Professor . " what be there to consider about ? answer I this ! have you not be complain of your health , and have you not be long for what you call a smack of the country breeze ? well ! there in your hand be the paper that offer you perpetual choke mouthful of country breeze for four month ' time . be it not so ? ha ! again you want money . well ! be four golden guineas a week nothing ? my - soul - bless - my - soul ! only give it to I and my boot shall creak like the golden Papa 's , with a sense of the overpower richness of the man who walk in they ! four guineas a week , and , more than that , the charming society of two young miss ! and , more than that , your bed , your breakfast , your dinner , your gorge english tea and lunch and drink of foam beer , all for nothing why , Walter , my dear good friend deuce - what - the - deuce ! for the first time in my life I have not eye enough in my head to look , and wonder at you ! " 

 neither my mother 's evident astonishment at my behaviour , nor Pesca 's fervid enumeration of the advantage offer to I by the new employment , have any effect in shake my unreasonable disinclination to go to Limmeridge House . after start all the petty objection that I could think of to go to Cumberland , and after hear they answer , one after another , to my own complete discomfiture , I try to set up a last obstacle by ask what be to become of my pupil in London while I be teach Mr. Fairlie 's young lady to sketch from nature . the obvious answer to this be , that the great part of they would be away on their autumn travel , and that the few who remain at home might be confide to the care of one of my brother drawing - master , whose pupil I have once take off his hand under similar circumstance . my sister remind I that this gentleman have expressly place his service at my disposal , during the present season , in case I wish to leave town ; my mother seriously appeal to I not to let an idle caprice stand in the way of my own interest and my own health ; and Pesca piteously entreat that I would not wound he to the heart by reject the first grateful offer of service that he have be able to make to the friend who have save his life . 

 the evident sincerity and affection which inspire these remonstrance would have influence any man with an atom of good feeling in his composition . though I could not conquer my own unaccountable perversity , I have at least virtue enough to be heartily ashamed of it , and to end the discussion pleasantly by give way , and promise to do all that be want of I . 

 the rest of the evening pass merrily enough in humorous anticipation of my come life with the two young lady in Cumberland . Pesca , inspire by our national grog , which appear to get into his head , in the most marvellous manner , five minute after it have go down his throat , assert his claim to be consider a complete Englishman by make a series of speech in rapid succession , propose my mother 's health , my sister 's health , my health , and the health , in mass , of Mr. Fairlie and the two young miss , pathetically return thank himself , immediately afterwards , for the whole party . " a secret , Walter , " say my little friend confidentially , as we walk home together . " I be flush by the recollection of my own eloquence . my soul burst itself with ambition . one of these day I go into your noble Parliament . it be the dream of my whole life to be Honourable Pesca , M.P. ! " 

 the next morning I send my testimonial to the Professor 's employer in Portland Place . three day pass , and I conclude , with secret satisfaction , that my paper have not be find sufficiently explicit . on the fourth day , however , an answer come . it announce that Mr. Fairlie accept my service , and request I to start for Cumberland immediately . all the necessary instruction for my journey be carefully and clearly add in a postscript . 

 I make my arrangement , unwillingly enough , for leave London early the next day . towards evening Pesca look in , on his way to a dinner - party , to bid I good - bye . 

 " I shall dry my tear in your absence , " say the Professor gaily , " with this glorious thought . it be my auspicious hand that have give the first push to your fortune in the world . go , my friend ! when your sun shin in Cumberland   English proverb , in the name of heaven make your hay . marry one of the two young Misses ; become Honourable Hartright , M.P. ; and when you be on the top of the ladder remember that Pesca , at the bottom , have do it all ! " 

 I try to laugh with my little friend over his parting jest , but my spirit be not to be command . something jarred in I almost painfully while he be speak his light farewell word . 

 when I be leave alone again nothing remain to be do but to walk to the Hampstead cottage and bid my mother and Sarah good - bye . 



 IV 

 the heat have be painfully oppressive all day , and it be now a close and sultry night . 

 my mother and sister have speak so many last word , and have beg I to wait another five minute so many time , that it be nearly midnight when the servant lock the garden - gate behind I . I walk forward a few pace on the short way back to London , then stop and hesitate . 

 the moon be full and broad in the dark blue starless sky , and the broken ground of the heath look wild enough in the mysterious light to be hundred of mile away from the great city that lie beneath it . the idea of descend any soon than I could help into the heat and gloom of London repel I . the prospect of go to bed in my airless chamber , and the prospect of gradual suffocation , seem , in my present restless frame of mind and body , to be one and the same thing . I determine to stroll home in the purer air by the most roundabout way I could take ; to follow the white winding path across the lonely heath ; and to approach London through its most open suburb by strike into the Finchley Road , and so get back , in the cool of the new morning , by the western side of the Regent 's Park . 

 I wound my way down slowly over the heath , enjoy the divine stillness of the scene , and admire the soft alternation of light and shade as they follow each other over the broken ground on every side of I . so long as I be proceed through this first and prettiest part of my night walk my mind remain passively open to the impression produce by the view ; and I think but little on any subject indeed , so far as my own sensation be concern , I can hardly say that I think at all . 

 but when I have leave the heath and have turn into the by - road , where there be less to see , the idea naturally engender by the approach change in my habit and occupation gradually draw more and more of my attention exclusively to themselves . by the time I have arrive at the end of the road I have become completely absorb in my own fanciful vision of Limmeridge House , of Mr. Fairlie , and of the two lady whose practice in the art of water - colour painting I be so soon to superintend . 

 I have now arrive at that particular point of my walk where four road meet the road to Hampstead , along which I have return , the road to Finchley , the road to West End , and the road back to London . I have mechanically turn in this latter direction , and be stroll along the lonely high - road idly wonder , I remember , what the Cumberland young lady would look like when , in one moment , every drop of blood in my body be bring to a stop by the touch of a hand lay lightly and suddenly on my shoulder from behind I . 

 I turn on the instant , with my finger tighten round the handle of my stick . 

 there , in the middle of the broad bright high - road there , as if it have that moment spring out of the earth or drop from the heaven stand the figure of a solitary woman , dress from head to foot in white garment , her face bent in grave inquiry on mine , her hand pointing to the dark cloud over London , as I face she . 

 I be far too seriously startle by the suddenness with which this extraordinary apparition stand before I , in the dead of night and in that lonely place , to ask what she want . the strange woman speak first . 

 " be that the road to London ? " she say . 

 I look attentively at she , as she put that singular question to I . it be then nearly one o'clock . all I could discern distinctly by the moonlight be a colourless , youthful face , meagre and sharp to look at about the cheek and chin ; large , grave , wistfully attentive eye ; nervous , uncertain lip ; and light hair of a pale , brownish - yellow hue . there be nothing wild , nothing immodest in her manner : it be quiet and self - control , a little melancholy and a little touch by suspicion ; not exactly the manner of a lady , and , at the same time , not the manner of a woman in the humblest rank of life . the voice , little as I have yet hear of it , have something curiously still and mechanical in its tone , and the utterance be remarkably rapid . she hold a small bag in her hand : and her dress bonnet , shawl , and gown all of white be , so far as I could guess , certainly not compose of very delicate or very expensive material . her figure be slight , and rather above the average height her gait and action free from the slight approach to extravagance . this be all that I could observe of she in the dim light and under the perplexingly strange circumstance of our meeting . what sort of a woman she be , and how she come to be out alone in the high - road , an hour after midnight , I altogether fail to guess . the one thing of which I feel certain be , that the gross of mankind could not have misconstrue her motive in speak , even at that suspiciously late hour and in that suspiciously lonely place . 

 " do you hear I ? " she say , still quietly and rapidly , and without the least fretfulness or impatience . " I ask if that be the way to London . " 

 " yes , " I reply , " that be the way : it lead to St. John 's Wood and the Regent 's Park . you must excuse my not answer you before . I be rather startled by your sudden appearance in the road ; and I be , even now , quite unable to account for it . "

 she speak with unnecessary earnestness and agitation , and shrink back from I several pace . I do my good to reassure she .

 she turn , and point back to a place at the junction of the road to London and the road to Hampstead , where there be a gap in the hedge .

 steal after I and touch I ? why not call to I ? strange , to say the least of it . 

 " may I trust you ? " she ask . " you do not   think the bad of I because I have meet with an accident ? " she stop in confusion ; shift her bag from one hand to the other ; and sigh bitterly . 

 the loneliness and helplessness of the woman touch I . the natural impulse to assist she and to spare she get the well of the judgment , the caution , the worldly tact , which an old , wise , and cold man might have summon to help he in this strange emergency .

" the first touch of womanly tenderness that I have hear from she trembled in her voice as she say the word ; but no tear glistened in those large , wistfully attentive eye of her , which be still fix on I . " I have only be in London once before , " she go on , more and more rapidly , " and I know nothing about that side of it , yonder . can I get a fly , or a carriage of any kind ? be it too late ? I do not   know . if you could show I where to get a fly and if you will only promise not to interfere with I , and to let I leave you , when and how I please I have a friend in London who will be glad to receive I I want nothing else will you promise ? "

 what could I do ? here be a stranger utterly and helplessly at my mercy and that stranger a forlorn woman . no house be near ; no one be pass whom I could consult ; and no earthly right exist on my part to give I a power of control over she , even if I have know how to exercise it . I trace these line , self - distrustfully , with the shadow of after - event darken the very paper I write on ; and still I say , what could I do ?

 " will you promise ? " 

 " yes . " 

 one word ! the little familiar word that be on everybody 's lip , every hour in the day . oh I ! and I tremble , now , when I write it . 

 we set our face towards London , and walk on together in the first still hour of the new day I , and this woman , whose name , whose character , whose story , whose object in life , whose very presence by my side , at that moment , be fathomless mystery to I . it be like a dream . be I Walter Hartright ? be this the well - know , uneventful road , where holiday people stroll on Sundays ? have I really leave , little more than an hour since , the quiet , decent , conventionally domestic atmosphere of my mother 's cottage ? I be too bewildered too conscious also of a vague sense of something like self - reproach to speak to my strange companion for some minute . it be her voice again that first break the silence between we . 

 " I want to ask you something , " she say suddenly . " do you know many people in London ? " 

 " yes , a great many . "

 too much astonish to reply , I question she in my turn . 

 " why do you ask ? " 

 " because I hope , for my own sake , there be one Baronet that you do not   know . "

 as the reply pass my lip a little bitterly , perhaps she take my arm with the abruptness which characterise all her action .

 I have hitherto contrive to master my curiosity out of consideration for my companion ; but it get the well of I now .

 we move forward again at a quick pace ; and for half an hour , at least , not a word pass on either side . from time to time , be forbid to make any more inquiry , I steal a look at her face . it be always the same ; the lip close shut , the brow frown , the eye look straight forward , eagerly and yet absently . we have reach the first house , and be close on the new Wesleyan college , before her set feature relax and she speak once more . 

 " do you live in London ? " she say . 

 " yes . " as I answer , it strike I that she might have form some intention of appeal to I for assistance or advice , and that I ought to spare she a possible disappointment by warn she of my approach absence from home . so I add , " but to - morrow I shall be away from London for some time . I be go into the country . "

 " Cumberland ! " she repeat the word tenderly . " ah ! wish I be go there too . I be once happy in Cumberland . " 

 I try again to lift the veil that hang between this woman and I .

 he be not look out ; no one else be near the place when we pass through the gate . the sight of the gas - lamp and house seem to agitate she , and to make her impatient .

I hail the cab , as the driver mount the box again . when we cross the road , my companion 's impatience increase to such an extent that she almost force I to run .

 I have assure myself that the man be sober as well as civil before I let she enter the vehicle . and now , when she be seat inside , I entreat she to let I see she set down safely at her destination .

 my hand be on the cab door . she catch it in her , kiss it , and push it away . the cab drive off at the same moment I start into the road , with some vague idea of stop it again , I hardly know why hesitate from dread of frightening and distress she call , at last , but not loudly enough to attract the driver 's attention . the sound of the wheel grow fainter in the distance the cab melt into the black shadow on the road the woman in white be go . 


 ten minute or more have pass . I be still on the same side of the way ; now mechanically walk forward a few pace ; now stop again absently . at one moment I find myself doubt the reality of my own adventure ; at another I be perplexed and distress by an uneasy sense of have do wrong , which yet leave I confusedly ignorant of how I could have do right . I hardly know where I be go , or what I mean to do next ; I be conscious of nothing but the confusion of my own thought , when I be abruptly recall to myself awaken , I might almost say by the sound of rapidly approach wheel close behind I .

 the carriage pass I an open chaise drive by two man . 

 " stop ! " cry one . " there be a policeman . let us ask he . "

 " a woman in a lavender - colour gown "

 " if you or any of your man meet with the woman , stop she , and send she in careful keeping to that address . I will pay all expense , and a fair reward into the bargain . " 

 the policeman look at the card that be hand down to he .

 " do ! she have escape from my Asylum . do not   forget ; a woman in white . drive on . " 



 v 

 " she have escape from my Asylum ! " 

 I can not say with truth that the terrible inference which those word suggest flash upon I like a new revelation . some of the strange question put to I by the woman in white , after my ill - consider promise to leave she free to act as she please , have suggest the conclusion either that she be naturally flighty and unsettled , or that some recent shock of terror have disturb the balance of her faculty . but the idea of absolute insanity which we all associate with the very name of an Asylum , have , I can honestly declare , never occur to I , in connection with she . I have see nothing , in her language or her action , to justify it at the time ; and even with the new light throw on she by the word which the stranger have address to the policeman , I could see nothing to justify it now . 

 what have I do ? assist the victim of the most horrible of all false imprisonment to escape ; or cast loose on the wide world of London an unfortunate creature , whose action it be my duty , and every man 's duty , mercifully to control ? I turn sick at heart when the question occur to I , and when I feel self - reproachfully that it be ask too late . 

 in the disturbed state of my mind , it be useless to think of go to bed , when I at last get back to my chamber in Clement 's Inn . before many hour elapse it would be necessary to start on my journey to Cumberland . I sit down and try , first to sketch , then to read but the woman in white get between I and my pencil , between I and my book . have the forlorn creature come to any harm ? that be my first thought , though I shrink selfishly from confront it . other thought follow , on which it be less harrowing to dwell . where have she stop the cab ? what have become of she now ? have she be trace and capture by the man in the chaise ? or be she still capable of control her own action ; and be we two follow our widely part road towards one point in the mysterious future , at which we be to meet once more ? 

 it be a relief when the hour come to lock my door , to bid farewell to London pursuit , London pupil , and London friend , and to be in movement again towards new interest and a new life . even the bustle and confusion at the railway terminus , so wearisome and bewildering at other time , rouse I and do I good . 



 my travel instruction direct I to go to Carlisle , and then to diverge by a branch railway which run in the direction of the coast . as a misfortune to begin with , our engine break down between Lancaster and Carlisle . the delay occasion by this accident cause I to be too late for the branch train , by which I be to have go on immediately . I have to wait some hour ; and when a late train finally deposit I at the near station to Limmeridge House , it be past ten , and the night be so dark that I could hardly see my way to the pony - chaise which Mr. Fairlie have order to be in wait for I . 

 the driver be evidently discompose by the lateness of my arrival . he be in that state of highly respectful sulkiness which be peculiar to english servant . we drive away slowly through the darkness in perfect silence . the road be bad , and the dense obscurity of the night increase the difficulty of get over the ground quickly . it be , by my watch , nearly an hour and a half from the time of our leave the station before I hear the sound of the sea in the distance , and the crunch of our wheel on a smooth gravel drive . we have pass one gate before enter the drive , and we pass another before we draw up at the house . I be receive by a solemn man - servant out of livery , be inform that the family have retire for the night , and be then lead into a large and lofty room where my supper be await I , in a forlorn manner , at one extremity of a lonesome mahogany wilderness of dining - table . 

 I be too tired and out of spirit to eat or drink much , especially with the solemn servant wait on I as elaborately as if a small dinner party have arrive at the house instead of a solitary man . in a quarter of an hour I be ready to be take up to my bedchamber . the solemn servant conduct I into a prettily furnished room say , " breakfast at nine o'clock , sir " look all round he to see that everything be in its proper place , and noiselessly withdraw . 

 " what shall I see in my dream to - night ? " I think to myself , as I put out the candle ; " the woman in white ? or the unknown inhabitant of this Cumberland mansion ? " it be a strange sensation to be sleep in the house , like a friend of the family , and yet not to know one of the inmate , even by sight ! 



 VI 

 when I rise the next morning and draw up my blind , the sea open before I joyously under the broad August sunlight , and the distant coast of Scotland fringe the horizon with its line of melt blue . 

 the view be such a surprise , and such a change to I , after my weary London experience of brick and mortar landscape , that I seem to burst into a new life and a new set of thought the moment I look at it . a confused sensation of have suddenly lose my familiarity with the past , without acquire any additional clearness of idea in reference to the present or the future , take possession of my mind . circumstance that be but a few day old fade back in my memory , as if they have happen month and month since . Pesca 's quaint announcement of the mean by which he have procure I my present employment ; the farewell evening I have pass with my mother and sister ; even my mysterious adventure on the way home from Hampstead have all become like event which might have occur at some former epoch of my existence . although the woman in white be still in my mind , the image of she seem to have grow dull and faint already . 

 a little before nine o'clock , I descend to the ground - floor of the house . the solemn man - servant of the night before meet I wander among the passage , and compassionately show I the way to the breakfast - room . 

 my first glance round I , as the man open the door , disclose a well - furnish breakfast - table , stand in the middle of a long room , with many window in it . I look from the table to the window farthest from I , and see a lady stand at it , with her back turn towards I . the instant my eye rest on she , I be strike by the rare beauty of her form , and by the unaffected grace of her attitude . her figure be tall , yet not too tall ; comely and well - develop , yet not fat ; her head set on her shoulder with an easy , pliant firmness ; her waist , perfection in the eye of a man , for it occupy its natural place , it fill out its natural circle , it be visibly and delightfully undeformed by stay . she have not hear my entrance into the room ; and I allow myself the luxury of admire she for a few moment , before I move one of the chair near I , as the least embarrassing mean of attract her attention . she turn towards I immediately . the easy elegance of every movement of her limb and body as soon as she begin to advance from the far end of the room , set I in a flutter of expectation to see her face clearly . she leave the window and I say to myself , the lady be dark . she move forward a few step and I say to myself , the lady be young . she approach near and I say to myself   with a sense of surprise which word fail I to express , the lady be ugly ! 

 never be the old conventional maxim , that Nature can not err , more flatly contradict never be the fair promise of a lovely figure more strangely and startlingly belie by the face and head that crown it . the lady 's complexion be almost swarthy , and the dark down on her upper lip be almost a moustache . she have a large , firm , masculine mouth and jaw ; prominent , pierce , resolute brown eye ; and thick , coal - black hair , grow unusually low down on her forehead . her expression bright , frank , and intelligent appear , while she be silent , to be altogether want in those feminine attraction of gentleness and pliability , without which the beauty of the handsome woman alive be beauty incomplete . to see such a face as this set on shoulder that a sculptor would have long to model to be charm by the modest grace of action through which the symmetrical limb betray their beauty when they move , and then to be almost repel by the masculine form and masculine look of the feature in which the perfectly shape figure end be to feel a sensation oddly akin to the helpless discomfort familiar to we all in sleep , when we recognise yet can not reconcile the anomaly and contradiction of a dream . 

 " Mr. Hartright ? " say the lady interrogatively , her dark face light up with a smile , and soften and grow womanly the moment she begin to speak . " we resign all hope of you last night , and go to bed as usual . accept my apology for our apparent want of attention ; and allow I to introduce myself as one of your pupil . shall we shake hand ? I suppose we must come to it soon or later and why not soon ? " 

 these odd word of welcome be speak in a clear , ringing , pleasant voice . the offer hand rather large , but beautifully form be give to I with the easy , unaffected self - reliance of a highly - breed woman . we sit down together at the breakfast - table in as cordial and customary a manner as if we have know each other for year , and have meet at Limmeridge House to talk over old time by previous appointment . 

 " I hope you come here good - humouredly determined to make the good of your position , " continue the lady . " you will have to begin this morning by put up with no other company at breakfast than mine . my sister be in her own room , nurse that essentially feminine malady , a slight headache ; and her old governess , Mrs. Vesey , be charitably attend on she with restorative tea . my uncle , Mr. Fairlie , never join we at any of our meal : he be an invalid , and keep bachelor state in his own apartment . there be nobody else in the house but I . two young lady have be stay here , but they go away yesterday , in despair ; and no wonder . all through their visit   in consequence of Mr. Fairlie 's invalid condition   we produce no such convenience in the house as a flirtable , danceable , small - talkable creature of the male sex ; and the consequence be , we do nothing but quarrel , especially at dinner - time . how can you expect four woman to dine together alone every day , and not quarrel ? we be such fool , we can not   entertain each other at table . you see I do not   think much of my own sex , Mr. Hartright which will you have , tea or coffee ? no woman do think much of her own sex , although few of they confess it as freely as I do . dear I , you look puzzle . why ? be you wonder what you will have for breakfast ? or be you surprised at my careless way of talk ? in the first case , I advise you , as a friend , to have nothing to do with that cold ham at your elbow , and to wait till the omelette come in . in the second case , I will give you some tea to compose your spirit , and do all a woman can   which be very little , by - the - bye   to hold my tongue . " 

 she hand I my cup of tea , laugh gaily . her light flow of talk , and her lively familiarity of manner with a total stranger , be accompany by an unaffected naturalness and an easy inborn confidence in herself and her position , which would have secure she the respect of the most audacious man breathing . while it be impossible to be formal and reserved in her company , it be more than impossible to take the faint vestige of a liberty with she , even in thought . I feel this instinctively , even while I catch the infection of her own bright gaiety of spirit even while I do my good to answer she in her own frank , lively way . 

 " yes , yes , " she say , when I have suggest the only explanation I could offer , to account for my perplexed look , " I understand . you be such a perfect stranger in the house , that you be puzzle by my familiar reference to the worthy inhabitant . natural enough : I ought to have think of it before . at any rate , I can set it right now . suppose I begin with myself , so as to get do with that part of the subject as soon as possible ? my name be Marian Halcombe ; and I be as inaccurate as woman usually be , in call Mr. Fairlie my uncle , and Miss Fairlie my sister . my mother be twice marry : the first time to Mr. Halcombe , my father ; the second time to Mr. Fairlie , my half - sister 's father . except that we be both orphan , we be in every respect as unlike each other as possible . my father be a poor man , and Miss Fairlie 's father be a rich man . I have get nothing , and she have a fortune . I be dark and ugly , and she be fair and pretty . everybody think I crab and odd   with perfect justice ; and everybody think her sweet - temper and charming   with more justice still . in short , she be an angel ; and I be try some of that marmalade , Mr. Hartright , and finish the sentence , in the name of female propriety , for yourself . what be I to tell you about Mr. Fairlie ? upon my honour , I hardly know . he be sure to send for you after breakfast , and you can study he for yourself . in the meantime , I may inform you , first , that he be the late Mr. Fairlie 's young brother ; secondly , that he be a single man ; and thirdly , that he be Miss Fairlie 's guardian . I will not   live without she , and she can not   live without I ; and that be how I come to be at Limmeridge House . my sister and I be honestly fond of each other ; which , you will say , be perfectly unaccountable , under the circumstance , and I quite agree with you but so it be . you must please both of we , Mr. Hartright , or please neither of we : and , what be still more try , you will be throw entirely upon our society . Mrs. Vesey be an excellent person , who possess all the cardinal virtue , and count for nothing ; and Mr. Fairlie be too great an invalid to be a companion for anybody . I do not   know what be the matter with he , and the doctor do not   know what be the matter with he , and he do not   know himself what be the matter with he . we all say it be on the nerve , and we none of we know what we mean when we say it . however , I advise you to humour his little peculiarity , when you see he to - day . admire his collection of coin , print , and water - colour drawing , and you will win his heart . upon my word , if you can be content with a quiet country life , I do not   see why you should not get on very well here . from breakfast to lunch , Mr. Fairlie 's drawing will occupy you . after lunch , Miss Fairlie and I shoulder our sketch - book , and go out to misrepresent Nature , under your direction . drawing be her favourite whim , mind , not mine . woman can not   draw their mind be too flighty , and their eye be too inattentive . no matter my sister like it ; so I waste paint and spoil paper , for her sake , as composedly as any woman in England . as for the evening , I think we can help you through they . Miss Fairlie play delightfully . for my own poor part , I do not   know one note of music from the other ; but I can match you at chess , backgammon , ecarte , and   with the inevitable female drawback   even at billiard as well . what do you think of the programme ? can you reconcile yourself to our quiet , regular life ? or do you mean to be restless , and secretly thirst for change and adventure , in the humdrum atmosphere of Limmeridge House ? " 

 she have run on thus far , in her gracefully banter way , with no other interruption on my part than the unimportant reply which politeness require of I . the turn of the expression , however , in her last question , or rather the one chance word , " adventure , " lightly as it fall from her lip , recall my thought to my meeting with the woman in white , and urge I to discover the connection which the stranger 's own reference to Mrs. Fairlie inform I must once have exist between the nameless fugitive from the Asylum , and the former mistress of Limmeridge House . 

 " even if I be the most restless of mankind , " I say , " I should be in no danger of thirsting after adventure for some time to come . the very night before I arrive at this house , I meet with an adventure ; and the wonder and excitement of it , I can assure you , Miss Halcombe , will last I for the whole term of my stay in Cumberland , if not for a much long period . " 

 " you do not   say so , Mr. Hartright ! may I hear it ? " 

 " you have a claim to hear it . the chief person in the adventure be a total stranger to I , and may perhaps be a total stranger to you ; but she certainly mention the name of the late Mrs. Fairlie in term of the sincere gratitude and regard . " 

 " mention my mother 's name ! you interest I indescribably . pray go on . " 

 I at once relate the circumstance under which I have meet the woman in white , exactly as they have occur ; and I repeat what she have say to I about Mrs. Fairlie and Limmeridge House , word for word . 

 Miss Halcombe 's bright resolute eye look eagerly into mine , from the beginning of the narrative to the end . her face express vivid interest and astonishment , but nothing more . she be evidently as far from know of any clue to the mystery as I be myself . 

 " be you quite sure of those word refer to my mother ? " she ask . 

 " quite sure , " I reply . " whoever she may be , the woman be once at school in the village of Limmeridge , be treat with especial kindness by Mrs. Fairlie , and , in grateful remembrance of that kindness , feel an affectionate interest in all survive member of the family . she know that Mrs. Fairlie and her husband be both dead ; and she speak of Miss Fairlie as if they have know each other when they be child . " 

 " you say , I think , that she deny belong to this place ? " 

 " yes , she tell I she come from Hampshire . " 

 " and you entirely fail to find out her name ? " 

 " entirely . " 

 " very strange .  VII 

 my conductor lead I upstairs into a passage which take we back to the bedchamber in which I have sleep during the past night ; and open the door next to it , beg I to look in .

 if a man 's personal appearance , when he be out of his dressing - room , and when he have pass forty , can be accept as a safe guide to his time of life which be more than doubtful Mr. Fairlie 's age , when I see he , might have be reasonably compute at over fifty and under sixty year . his beardless face be thin , worn , and transparently pale , but not wrinkle ; his nose be high and hooked ; his eye be of a dim greyish blue , large , prominent , and rather red round the rim of the eyelid ; his hair be scanty , soft to look at , and of that light sandy colour which be the last to disclose its own change towards grey . he be dress in a dark frock - coat , of some substance much thin than cloth , and in waistcoat and trouser of spotless white . his foot be effeminately small , and be clothe in buff - colour silk stocking , and little womanish bronze - leather slipper . two ring adorn his white delicate hand , the value of which even my inexperienced observation detect to be all but priceless . upon the whole , he have a frail , languidly - fretful , over - refined look something singularly and unpleasantly delicate in its association with a man , and , at the same time , something which could by no possibility have look natural and appropriate if it have be transfer to the personal appearance of a woman . my morning 's experience of Miss Halcombe have predispose I to be pleased with everybody in the house ; but my sympathy shut themselves up resolutely at the first sight of Mr. Fairlie .

"

 when the sea - green curtain be closed , and when the two baize door be shut behind I , I stop for a moment in the little circular hall beyond , and draw a long , luxurious breath of relief . it be like come to the surface of the water after deep diving , to find myself once more on the outside of Mr. Fairlie 's room .

 viii 

 when I enter the room , I find Miss Halcombe and an elderly lady seat at the luncheon - table . 

 the elderly lady , when I be present to she , prove to be Miss Fairlie 's former governess , Mrs. Vesey , who have be briefly describe to I by my lively companion at the breakfast - table , as possess of " all the cardinal virtue , and count for nothing . " I can do little more than offer my humble testimony to the truthfulness of Miss Halcombe 's sketch of the old lady 's character . Mrs. Vesey look the personification of human composure and female amiability . a calm enjoyment of a calm existence beam in drowsy smile on her plump , placid face . some of we rush through life , and some of we saunter through life . Mrs. Vesey SAT through life . Sat in the house , early and late ; sit in the garden ; sit in unexpected window - seat in passage ; sit   on a camp - stool   when her friend try to take she out walking ; sit before she look at anything , before she talk of anything , before she answer yes , or no , to the commonest question always with the same serene smile on her lip , the same vacantly - attentive turn of the head , the same snugly - comfortable position of her hand and arm , under every possible change of domestic circumstance . a mild , a compliant , an unutterably tranquil and harmless old lady , who never by any chance suggest the idea that she have be actually alive since the hour of her birth . nature have so much to do in this world , and be engage in generate such a vast variety of co - existent production , that she must surely be now and then too flurried and confuse to distinguish between the different process that she be carry on at the same time . start from this point of view , it will always remain my private persuasion that Nature be absorb in make cabbage when Mrs. Vesey be bear , and that the good lady suffer the consequence of a vegetable preoccupation in the mind of the Mother of we all . 

 " now , Mrs. Vesey , " say Miss Halcombe , look bright , sharp , and ready than ever , by contrast with the undemonstrative old lady at her side , " what will you have ? a cutlet ? " 

 Mrs. Vesey cross her dimpled hand on the edge of the table , smile placidly , and say , " yes , dear . " 

 " what be that opposite Mr. Hartright ? boil chicken , be it not ? I think you like boil chicken well than cutlet , Mrs. Vesey ? " 

 Mrs. Vesey take her dimpled hand off the edge of the table and cross they on her lap instead ; nod contemplatively at the boil chicken , and say , " yes , dear . " 

 " well , but which will you have , to - day ? shall Mr. Hartright give you some chicken ? or shall I give you some cutlet ? " 

 Mrs. Vesey put one of her dimpled hand back again on the edge of the table ; hesitate drowsily , and say , " which you please , dear . " 

 " mercy on I ! it be a question for your taste , my good lady , not for mine . suppose you have a little of both ? and suppose you begin with the chicken , because Mr. Hartright look devour by anxiety to carve for you . " 

 Mrs. Vesey put the other dimpled hand back on the edge of the table ; brighten dimly one moment ; go out again the next ; bow obediently , and say , " if you please , sir . " 

 surely a mild , a compliant , an unutterably tranquil and harmless old lady ! but enough , perhaps , for the present , of Mrs. Vesey . 


 all this time , there be no sign of Miss Fairlie . we finish our luncheon ; and still she never appear . Miss Halcombe , whose quick eye nothing escape , notice the look that I cast , from time to time , in the direction of the door .
