" 

 she take up a parasol lie on a chair near she , and lead the way out , by a long window at the bottom of the room , which open on to the lawn . it be almost unnecessary to say that we leave Mrs. Vesey still seat at the table , with her dimpled hand still cross on the edge of it ; apparently settle in that position for the rest of the afternoon . 

 as we cross the lawn , Miss Halcombe look at I significantly , and shake her head . 

 " that mysterious adventure of your , " she say , ' still remain involved in its own appropriate midnight darkness . I have be all the morning look over my mother 's letter , and I have make no discovery yet . however , do not   despair , Mr. Hartright . this be a matter of curiosity ; and you have get a woman for your ally . under such condition success be certain , soon or later . the letter be not exhausted . I have three packet still leave , and you may confidently rely on my spending the whole evening over they . " 

 here , then , be one of my anticipation of the morning still unfulfilled . I begin to wonder , next , whether my introduction to Miss Fairlie would disappoint the expectation that I have be form of she since breakfast - time . 

 " and how do you get on with Mr. Fairlie ? " inquire Miss Halcombe , as we leave the lawn and turn into a shrubbery . " be he particularly nervous this morning ? never mind consider about your answer , Mr. Hartright . the mere fact of your be oblige to consider be enough for I . I see in your face that he be particularly nervous ; and , as I be amiably unwilling to throw you into the same condition , I ask no more . " 

 we turn off into a wind path while she be speak , and approach a pretty summer - house , build of wood , in the form of a miniature swiss chalet . the one room of the summer - house , as we ascend the step of the door , be occupy by a young lady . she be stand near a rustic table , look out at the inland view of moor and hill present by a gap in the tree , and absently turn over the leaf of a little sketch - book that lie at her side . this be Miss Fairlie . 

 how can I describe she ? how can I separate she from my own sensation , and from all that have happen in the later time ? how can I see she again as she look when my eye first rest on she as she should look , now , to the eye that be about to see she in these page ? 

 the water - colour drawing that I make of Laura Fairlie , at an after period , in the place and attitude in which I first see she , lie on my desk while I write . I look at it , and there dawn upon I brightly , from the dark greenish - brown background of the summer - house , a light , youthful figure , clothe in a simple muslin dress , the pattern of it form by broad alternate stripe of delicate blue and white . a scarf of the same material sit crisply and closely round her shoulder , and a little straw hat of the natural colour , plainly and sparingly trim with ribbon to match the gown , cover her head , and throw its soft pearly shadow over the upper part of her face . her hair be of so faint and pale a brown not flaxen , and yet almost as light ; not golden , and yet almost as glossy that it nearly melt , here and there , into the shadow of the hat . it be plainly part and draw back over her ear , and the line of it ripple naturally as it cross her forehead . the eyebrow be rather dark than the hair ; and the eye be of that soft , limpid , turquoise blue , so often sing by the poet , so seldom see in real life . lovely eye in colour , lovely eye in form large and tender and quietly thoughtful but beautiful above all thing in the clear truthfulness of look that dwell in their inmost depth , and shin through all their change of expression with the light of a purer and a well world . the charm most gently and yet most distinctly express which they shed over the whole face , so cover and transform its little natural human blemish elsewhere , that it be difficult to estimate the relative merit and defect of the other feature . it be hard to see that the low part of the face be too delicately refine away towards the chin to be in full and fair proportion with the upper part ; that the nose , in escape the aquiline bend   always hard and cruel in a woman , no matter how abstractedly perfect it may be , have err a little in the other extreme , and have miss the ideal straightness of line ; and that the sweet , sensitive lip be subject to a slight nervous contraction , when she smile , which draw they upward a little at one corner , towards the cheek . it might be possible to note these blemish in another woman 's face but it be not easy to dwell on they in her , so subtly be they connect with all that be individual and characteristic in her expression , and so closely do the expression depend for its full play and life , in every other feature , on the move impulse of the eye . 

 do my poor portrait of she , my fond , patient labour of long and happy day , show I these thing ? ah , how few of they be in the dim mechanical drawing , and how many in the mind with which I regard it ! a fair , delicate girl , in a pretty light dress , trifle with the leaf of a sketch - book , while she look up from it with truthful , innocent blue eye that be all the drawing can say ; all , perhaps , that even the deep reach of thought and pen can say in their language , either . the woman who first give life , light , and form to our shadowy conception of beauty , fill a void in our spiritual nature that have remain unknown to we till she appear . sympathy that lie too deep for word , too deep almost for thought , be touch , at such time , by other charm than those which the sens feel and which the resource of expression can realise . the mystery which underlie the beauty of woman be never raise above the reach of all expression until it have claim kindre with the deep mystery in our own soul . then , and then only , have it pass beyond the narrow region on which light fall , in this world , from the pencil and the pen . 

 think of she as you think of the first woman who quicken the pulse within you that the rest of her sex have no art to stir . let the kind , candid blue eye meet your , as they meet mine , with the one matchless look which we both remember so well . let her voice speak the music that you once love well , attune as sweetly to your ear as to mine . let she footstep , as she come and go , in these page , be like that other footstep to whose airy fall your own heart once beat time . take she as the visionary nursling of your own fancy ; and she will grow upon you , all the more clearly , as the live woman who dwell in mine . 

 among the sensation that crowd on I , when my eye first look upon her familiar sensation which we all know , which spring to life in most of our heart , die again in so many , and renew their bright existence in so few there be one that troubled and perplex I : one that seem strangely inconsistent and unaccountably out of place in Miss Fairlie 's presence . 

 mingle with the vivid impression produce by the charm of her fair face and head , her sweet expression , and her win simplicity of manner , be another impression , which , in a shadowy way , suggest to I the idea of something want . at one time it seem like something want in she : at another , like something want in myself , which hinder I from understand she as I ought . the impression be always strong in the most contradictory manner , when she look at I ; or , in other word , when I be most conscious of the harmony and charm of her face , and yet , at the same time , most trouble by the sense of an incompleteness which it be impossible to discover . something want , something want and where it be , and what it be , I could not say . 

 the effect of this curious caprice of fancy   as I think it then   be not of a nature to set I at my ease , during a first interview with Miss Fairlie . the few kind word of welcome which she speak find I hardly self - possess enough to thank she in the customary phrase of reply . observe my hesitation , and no doubt attribute it , naturally enough , to some momentary shyness on my part , Miss Halcombe take the business of talk , as easily and readily as usual , into her own hand . 

 " look there , Mr. Hartright , " she say , point to the sketch - book on the table , and to the little delicate wandering hand that be still trifle with it . " surely you will acknowledge that your model pupil be find at last ? the moment she hear that you be in the house , she seize her inestimable sketch - book , look universal nature straight in the face , and long to begin ! " 

 Miss Fairlie laugh with a ready good - humour , which break out as brightly as if it have be part of the sunshine above we , over her lovely face . 

 " I must not take credit to myself where no credit be due , " she say , her clear , truthful blue eye look alternately at Miss Halcombe and at I . " Fond as I be of draw , I be so conscious of my own ignorance that I be more afraid than anxious to begin . now I know you be here , Mr. Hartright , I find myself look over my sketch , as I use to look over my lesson when I be a little girl , and when I be sadly afraid that I should turn out not fit to be hear . " 

 she make the confession very prettily and simply , and , with quaint , childish earnestness , draw the sketch - book away close to her own side of the table . Miss Halcombe cut the knot of the little embarrassment forthwith , in her resolute , downright way . 

 " good , bad , or indifferent , " she say , " the pupil 's sketch must pass through the fiery ordeal of the master 's judgment and there be an end of it . suppose we take they with we in the carriage , Laura , and let Mr. Hartright see they , for the first time , under circumstance of perpetual jolting and interruption ? if we can only confuse he all through the drive , between Nature as it be , when he look up at the view , and nature as it be not when he look down again at our sketch - book , we shall drive he into the last desperate refuge of pay we compliment , and shall slip through his professional finger with our pet feather of vanity all unruffled . " 

 " I hope Mr. Hartright will pay I no compliment , " say Miss Fairlie , as we all leave the summer - house . 

 " may I venture to inquire why you express that hope ? " I ask . 

 " because I shall believe all that you say to I , " she answer simply . 

 in those few word she unconsciously give I the key to her whole character : to that generous trust in other which , in her nature , grow innocently out of the sense of her own truth . I only know it intuitively then . I know it by experience now . 

 we merely wait to rouse good Mrs. Vesey from the place which she still occupy at the deserted luncheon - table , before we enter the open carriage for our promise drive . the old lady and Miss Halcombe occupy the back seat , and Miss Fairlie and I sit together in front , with the sketch - book open between we , fairly exhibit at last to my professional eye . all serious criticism on the drawing , even if I have be dispose to volunteer it , be render impossible by Miss Halcombe 's lively resolution to see nothing but the ridiculous side of the Fine Arts , as practise by herself , her sister , and lady in general . I can remember the conversation that pass far more easily than the sketch that I mechanically look over . that part of the talk , especially , in which Miss Fairlie take any share , be still as vividly impressed on my memory as if I have hear it only a few hour ago . 

 yes ! let I acknowledge that on this first day I let the charm of her presence lure I from the recollection of myself and my position . the most trifling of the question that she put to I , on the subject of use her pencil and mix her colour ; the slight alteration of expression in the lovely eye that look into mine with such an earnest desire to learn all that I could teach , and to discover all that I could show , attract more of my attention than the fine view we pass through , or the grand change of light and shade , as they flow into each other over the wave moorland and the level beach . at any time , and under any circumstance of human interest , be it not strange to see how little real hold the object of the natural world amid which we live can gain on our heart and mind ? we go to Nature for comfort in trouble , and sympathy in joy , only in book . admiration of those beauty of the inanimate world , which modern poetry so largely and so eloquently describe , be not , even in the good of we , one of the original instinct of our nature . as child , we none of we possess it . no uninstructed man or woman possess it . those whose life be most exclusively pass amid the ever - change wonder of sea and land be also those who be most universally insensible to every aspect of Nature not directly associate with the human interest of their calling . our capacity of appreciate the beauty of the earth we live on be , in truth , one of the civilised accomplishment which we all learn as an art ; and , more , that very capacity be rarely practise by any of we except when our mind be most indolent and most unoccupied . how much share have the attraction of Nature ever have in the pleasurable or painful interest and emotion of ourselves or our friend ? what space do they ever occupy in the thousand little narrative of personal experience which pass every day by word of mouth from one of we to the other ? all that our mind can compass , all that our heart can learn , can be accomplish with equal certainty , equal profit , and equal satisfaction to ourselves , in the poor as in the rich prospect that the face of the earth can show . there be surely a reason for this want of inborn sympathy between the creature and the creation around it , a reason which may perhaps be find in the widely - differ destiny of man and his earthly sphere . the grand mountain prospect that the eye can range over be appoint to annihilation . the small human interest that the pure heart can feel be appoint to immortality . 

 we have be out nearly three hour , when the carriage again pass through the gate of Limmeridge House . 

 on our way back I have let the lady settle for themselves the first point of view which they be to sketch , under my instruction , on the afternoon of the next day . when they withdraw to dress for dinner , and when I be alone again in my little sitting - room , my spirit seem to leave I on a sudden . I feel ill at ease and dissatisfied with myself , I hardly know why . perhaps I be now conscious for the first time of have enjoy our drive too much in the character of a guest , and too little in the character of a drawing - master . perhaps that strange sense of something want , either in Miss Fairlie or in myself , which have perplex I when I be first introduce to she , haunt I still . anyhow , it be a relief to my spirit when the dinner - hour call I out of my solitude , and take I back to the society of the lady of the house . 

 I be strike , on enter the drawing - room , by the curious contrast , rather in material than in colour , of the dress which they now wear . while Mrs. Vesey and Miss Halcombe be richly clothe   each in the manner most become to her age , the first in silver - grey , and the second in that delicate primrose - yellow colour which match so well with a dark complexion and black hair , Miss Fairlie be unpretendingly and almost poorly dress in plain white muslin . it be spotlessly pure : it be beautifully put on ; but still it be the sort of dress which the wife or daughter of a poor man might have wear , and it make she , so far as external go , look less affluent in circumstance than her own governess . at a later period , when I learn to know more of Miss Fairlie 's character , I discover that this curious contrast , on the wrong side , be due to her natural delicacy of feeling and natural intensity of aversion to the slight personal display of her own wealth . neither Mrs. Vesey nor Miss Halcombe could ever induce she to let the advantage in dress desert the two lady who be poor , to lean to the side of the one lady who be rich . 

 when the dinner be over we return together to the drawing - room . although Mr. Fairlie   emulate the magnificent condescension of the monarch who have pick up Titian 's brush for he   have instruct his butler to consult my wish in relation to the wine that I might prefer after dinner , I be resolute enough to resist the temptation of sit in solitary grandeur among bottle of my own choosing , and sensible enough to ask the lady ' permission to leave the table with they habitually , on the civilised foreign plan , during the period of my residence at Limmeridge House . 

 the drawing - room , to which we have now withdraw for the rest of the evening , be on the ground - floor , and be of the same shape and size as the breakfast - room . large glass door at the low end open on to a terrace , beautifully ornament along its whole length with a profusion of flower . the soft , hazy twilight be just shade leaf and blossom alike into harmony with its own sober hue as we enter the room , and the sweet evening scent of the flower meet we with its fragrant welcome through the open glass door . Good Mrs. Vesey   always the first of the party to sit down   take possession of an arm - chair in a corner , and doze off comfortably to sleep . at my request Miss Fairlie place herself at the piano . as I follow she to a seat near the instrument , I see Miss Halcombe retire into a recess of one of the side window , to proceed with the search through her mother 's letter by the last quiet ray of the evening light . 

 how vividly that peaceful home - picture of the drawing - room come back to I while I write ! from the place where I sit I could see Miss Halcombe 's graceful figure , half of it in soft light , half in mysterious shadow , bend intently over the letter in her lap ; while , near to I , the fair profile of the player at the piano be just delicately define against the faintly - deepen background of the inner wall of the room . outside , on the terrace , the cluster flower and long grass and creeper wave so gently in the light evening air , that the sound of their rustling never reach we . the sky be without a cloud , and the dawn mystery of moonlight begin to tremble already in the region of the eastern heaven . the sense of peace and seclusion soothe all thought and feel into a rapt , unearthly repose ; and the balmy quiet , that deepen ever with the deepening light , seem to hover over we with a gentler influence still , when there steal upon it from the piano the heavenly tenderness of the music of Mozart . it be an evening of sight and sound never to forget . 

 we all sit silent in the place we have choose Mrs. Vesey still sleep , Miss Fairlie still play , Miss Halcombe still read till the light fail we . by this time the moon have steal round to the terrace , and soft , mysterious ray of light be slant already across the low end of the room . the change from the twilight obscurity be so beautiful that we banish the lamp , by common consent , when the servant bring they in , and keep the large room unlighte , except by the glimmer of the two candle at the piano . 

 for half an hour more the music still go on . after that the beauty of the moonlight view on the terrace tempt Miss Fairlie out to look at it , and I follow she . when the candle at the piano have be light Miss Halcombe have change her place , so as to continue her examination of the letter by their assistance . we leave she , on a low chair , at one side of the instrument , so absorb over her reading that she do not seem to notice when we move . 

 we have be out on the terrace together , just in front of the glass door , hardly so long as five minute , I should think ; and Miss Fairlie be , by my advice , just tie her white handkerchief over her head as a precaution against the night air when I hear Miss Halcombe 's voice low , eager , and alter from its natural lively tone pronounce my name . 

 " Mr. Hartright , " she say , " will you come here for a minute ? I want to speak to you . " 

 I enter the room again immediately . the piano stand about half - way down along the inner wall . on the side of the instrument farth from the terrace Miss Halcombe be sit with the letter scatter on her lap , and with one in her hand select from they , and hold close to the candle . on the side near to the terrace there stand a low ottoman , on which I take my place . in this position I be not far from the glass door , and I could see Miss Fairlie plainly , as she pass and repasse the opening on to the terrace , walk slowly from end to end of it in the full radiance of the moon . 

 " I want you to listen while I read the concluding passage in this letter , " say Miss Halcombe . " tell I if you think they throw any light upon your strange adventure on the road to London . the letter be address by my mother to her second husband , Mr. Fairlie , and the date refer to a period of between eleven and twelve year since . at that time Mr. and Mrs. Fairlie , and my half - sister Laura , have be live for year in this house ; and I be away from they complete my education at a school in Paris . " 

 she look and speak earnestly , and , as I think , a little uneasily as well . at the moment when she raise the letter to the candle before begin to read it , Miss Fairlie pass we on the terrace , look in for a moment , and see that we be engage , slowly walk on . 

 Miss Halcombe begin to read as follow : 


 " ' you will be tired , my dear Philip , of hear perpetually about my school and my scholar . Lay the blame , pray , on the dull uniformity of life at Limmeridge , and not on I . besides , this time I have something really interesting to tell you about a new scholar . 

 " ' you know old Mrs. Kempe at the village shop . well , after year of ailing , the doctor have at last give she up , and she be die slowly day by day . her only live relation , a sister , arrive last week to take care of she . this sister come all the way from Hampshire her name be Mrs. Catherick . four day ago Mrs. Catherick come here to see I , and bring she only child with she , a sweet little girl about a year old than our darling Laura ' " 


 as the last sentence fall from the reader 's lip , Miss Fairlie pass we on the terrace once more . she be softly sing to herself one of the melody which she have be play early in the evening . Miss Halcombe wait till she have pass out of sight again , and then go on with the letter 


 " ' Mrs. Catherick be a decent , well - behave , respectable woman ; middle - aged , and with the remain of have be moderately , only moderately , nice - looking . there be something in her manner and in her appearance , however , which I can not   make out . she be reserve about herself to the point of downright secrecy , and there be a look in her face I can not   describe it which suggest to I that she have something on her mind . she be altogether what you would call a walk mystery . her errand at Limmeridge House , however , be simple enough . when she leave Hampshire to nurse her sister , Mrs. Kempe , through her last illness , she have be oblige to bring her daughter with she , through have no one at home to take care of the little girl . Mrs. Kempe may die in a week 's time , or may linger on for month ; and Mrs. Catherick 's object be to ask I to let her daughter , Anne , have the benefit of attend my school , subject to the condition of her be remove from it to go home again with her mother , after Mrs. Kempe 's death . I consent at once , and when Laura and I go out for our walk , we take the little girl   who be just eleven year old   to the school that very day . ' " 


 once more Miss Fairlie 's figure , bright and soft in its snowy muslin dress her face prettily frame by the white fold of the handkerchief which she have tie under her chin pass by we in the moonlight . once more Miss Halcombe wait till she be out of sight , and then go on 


 " ' I have take a violent fancy , Philip , to my new scholar , for a reason which I mean to keep till the last for the sake of surprise you . her mother have tell I as little about the child as she tell I of herself , I be leave to discover   which I do on the first day when we try she at lesson   that the poor little thing 's intellect be not develop as it ought to be at her age . see this I have she up to the house the next day , and privately arrange with the doctor to come and watch she and question she , and tell I what he think . his opinion be that she will grow out of it . but he say her careful bringing - up at school be a matter of great importance just now , because her unusual slowness in acquire idea imply an unusual tenacity in keep they , when they be once receive into her mind . now , my love , you must not imagine , in your off - hand way , that I have be attach myself to an idiot . this poor little Anne Catherick be a sweet , affectionate , grateful girl , and say the quaint , prettiest thing   as you shall judge by an instance , in the most oddly sudden , surprised , half - frightened way . although she be dress very neatly , her clothe show a sad want of taste in colour and pattern . so I arrange , yesterday , that some of our darling Laura 's old white frock and white hat should be alter for Anne Catherick , explain to she that little girl of her complexion look neater and well all in white than in anything else . she hesitate and seem puzzled for a minute , then flush up , and appear to understand . her little hand clasp mine suddenly . she kiss it , Philip , and say   oh , so earnestly ! , " I will always wear white as long as I live . it will help I to remember you , ma'am , and to think that I be please you still , when I go away and see you no more . " this be only one speciman of the quaint thing she say so prettily . poor little soul ! she shall have a stock of white frock , make with good deep tuck , to let out for she as she grow ' " 


 Miss Halcombe pause , and look at I across the piano . 

 " do the forlorn woman whom you meet in the high - road seem young ? " she ask . " young enough to be two- or three - and - twenty ? " 

 " yes , Miss Halcombe , as young as that . " 

 " and she be strangely dress , from head to foot , all in white ? " 

 " all in white . " 

 while the answer be pass my lip Miss Fairlie glide into view on the terrace for the third time . instead of proceed on her walk , she stop , with her back turn towards we , and , lean on the balustrade of the terrace , look down into the garden beyond . my eye fix upon the white gleam of her muslin gown and head - dress in the moonlight , and a sensation , for which I can find no name a sensation that quicken my pulse , and raise a fluttering at my heart begin to steal over I . 

 " all in white ? " Miss Halcombe repeat . " the most important sentence in the letter , Mr. Hartright , be those at the end , which I will read to you immediately . but I can not   help dwell a little upon the coincidence of the white costume of the woman you meet , and the white frock which produce that strange answer from my mother 's little scholar . the doctor may have be wrong when he discover the child 's defect of intellect , and predict that she would ' grow out of they . ' she may never have grow out of they , and the old grateful fancy about dress in white , which be a serious feeling to the girl , may be a serious feeling to the woman still . " 

 I say a few word in answer I hardly know what . all my attention be concentrate on the white gleam of Miss Fairlie 's muslin dress . 

 " listen to the last sentence of the letter , " say Miss Halcombe . " I think they will surprise you . " 

 as she raise the letter to the light of the candle , Miss Fairlie turn from the balustrade , look doubtfully up and down the terrace , advance a step towards the glass door , and then stop , face we . 

 meanwhile Miss Halcombe read I the last sentence to which she have refer 


 " ' and now , my love , see that I be at the end of my paper , now for the real reason , the surprising reason , for my fondness for little Anne Catherick . my dear Philip , although she be not half so pretty , she be , nevertheless , by one of those extraordinary caprice of accidental resemblance which one sometimes see , the live likeness , in her hair , her complexion , the colour of her eye , and the shape of her face ' " 


 I start up from the ottoman before Miss Halcombe could pronounce the next word . a thrill of the same feeling which run through I when the touch be lay upon my shoulder on the lonely high - road chill I again . 

 there stand Miss Fairlie , a white figure , alone in the moonlight ; in her attitude , in the turn of her head , in her complexion , in the shape of her face , the live image , at that distance and under those circumstance , of the woman in white ! the doubt which have trouble my mind for hour and hour past flash into conviction in an instant . that ' something want " be my own recognition of the ominous likeness between the fugitive from the asylum and my pupil at Limmeridge House . 

 " you see it ! " say Miss Halcombe . she drop the useless letter , and her eye flash as they meet mine . " you see it now , as my mother see it eleven year since ! " 

 " I see it more unwillingly than I can say . to associate that forlorn , friendless , lost woman , even by an accidental likeness only , with Miss Fairlie , seem like cast a shadow on the future of the bright creature who stand look at we now . let I lose the impression again as soon as possible . call she in , out of the dreary moonlight pray call she in ! " 

 " Mr. Hartright , you surprise I . whatever woman may be , I think that man , in the nineteenth century , be above superstition . " 

 " pray call she in ! " 

 " hush , hush ! she be come of her own accord . say nothing in her presence . let this discovery of the likeness be keep a secret between you and I . come in , Laura , come in , and wake Mrs. Vesey with the piano . Mr. Hartright be petition for some more music , and he want it , this time , of the light and lively kind . " 



 IX 

 so end my eventful first day at Limmeridge House . 

 Miss Halcombe and I keep our secret . after the discovery of the likeness no fresh light seem destine to break over the mystery of the woman in white . at the first safe opportunity Miss Halcombe cautiously lead her half - sister to speak of their mother , of old time , and of Anne Catherick . Miss Fairlie 's recollection of the little scholar at Limmeridge be , however , only of the most vague and general kind . she remember the likeness between herself and her mother 's favourite pupil , as something which have be suppose to exist in past time ; but she do not refer to the gift of the white dress , or to the singular form of word in which the child have artlessly express her gratitude for they . she remember that Anne have remain at Limmeridge for a few month only , and have then leave it to go back to her home in Hampshire ; but she could not say whether the mother and daughter have ever return , or have ever be hear of afterwards . no further search , on Miss Halcombe 's part , through the few letter of Mrs. Fairlie 's writing which she have leave unread , assist in clear up the uncertainty still leave to perplex we . we have identify the unhappy woman whom I have meet in the night - time with Anne Catherick we have make some advance , at least , towards connect the probably defective condition of the poor creature 's intellect with the peculiarity of she be dress all in white , and with the continuance , in her mature year , of her childish gratitude towards Mrs. Fairlie and there , so far as we know at that time , our discovery have end . 


 the day pass on , the week pass on , and the track of the golden autumn wind its bright way visibly through the green summer of the tree . peaceful , fast - flow , happy time ! my story glide by you now as swiftly as you once glide by I . of all the treasure of enjoyment that you pour so freely into my heart , how much be leave I that have purpose and value enough to be write on this page ? nothing but the sad of all confession that a man can make the confession of his own folly . 

 the secret which that confession disclose should be tell with little effort , for it have indirectly escape I already . the poor weak word , which have fail to describe Miss Fairlie , have succeed in betray the sensation she awaken in I . it be so with we all . our word be giant when they do we an injury , and dwarf when they do we a service . 

 I love she . 

 ah ! how well I know all the sadness and all the mockery that be contain in those three word . I can sigh over my mournful confession with the tender woman who read it and pity I . I can laugh at it as bitterly as the hard man who toss it from he in contempt . I love she ! feel for I , or despise I , I confess it with the same immovable resolution to own the truth . 

 be there no excuse for I ? there be some excuse to be find , surely , in the condition under which my term of hire service be pass at Limmeridge House . 

 my morning hour succeed each other calmly in the quiet and seclusion of my own room . I have just work enough to do , in mount my employer 's drawing , to keep my hand and eye pleasurably employ , while my mind be leave free to enjoy the dangerous luxury of its own unbridled thought . a perilous solitude , for it last long enough to enervate , not long enough to fortify I . a perilous solitude , for it be follow by afternoon and evening spend , day after day and week after week alone in the society of two woman , one of whom possess all the accomplishment of grace , wit , and high - breeding , the other all the charm of beauty , gentleness , and simple truth , that can purify and subdue the heart of man . not a day pass , in that dangerous intimacy of teacher and pupil , in which my hand be not close to Miss Fairlie 's ; my cheek , as we bent together over her sketch - book , almost touch her . the more attentively she watch every movement of my brush , the more closely I be breathe the perfume of her hair , and the warm fragrance of her breath . it be part of my service to live in the very light of her eye at one time to be bend over she , so close to her bosom as to tremble at the thought of touch it ; at another , to feel she bend over I , bend so close to see what I be about , that her voice sink low when she speak to I , and her ribbon brush my cheek in the wind before she could draw they back . 

 the evening which follow the sketching excursion of the afternoon varied , rather than check , these innocent , these inevitable familiarity . my natural fondness for the music which she play with such tender feeling , such delicate womanly taste , and her natural enjoyment of give I back , by the practice of her art , the pleasure which I have offer to she by the practice of mine , only weave another tie which draw we close and close to one another . the accident of conversation ; the simple habit which regulate even such a little thing as the position of our place at table ; the play of Miss Halcombe 's ever - ready raillery , always direct against my anxiety as teacher , while it sparkle over her enthusiasm as pupil ; the harmless expression of poor Mrs. Vesey 's drowsy approval , which connect Miss Fairlie and I as two model young people who never disturb she every one of these trifle , and many more , combine to fold we together in the same domestic atmosphere , and to lead we both insensibly to the same hopeless end . 

 I should have remember my position , and have put myself secretly on my guard . I do so , but not till it be too late . all the discretion , all the experience , which have avail I with other woman , and secure I against other temptation , fail I with she . it have be my profession , for year past , to be in this close contact with young girl of all age , and of all order of beauty . I have accept the position as part of my calling in life ; I have train myself to leave all the sympathy natural to my age in my employer 's outer hall , as coolly as I leave my umbrella there before I go upstairs . I have long since learn to understand , composedly and as a matter of course , that my situation in life be consider a guarantee against any of my female pupil feel more than the most ordinary interest in I , and that I be admit among beautiful and captivate woman much as a harmless domestic animal be admit among they . this guardian experience I have gain early ; this guardian experience have sternly and strictly guide I straight along my own poor narrow path , without once let I stray aside , to the right hand or to the left . and now I and my trusty talisman be part for the first time . yes , my hardly - earn self - control be as completely lose to I as if I have never possess it ; lose to I , as it be lose every day to other man , in other critical situation , where woman be concern . I know , now , that I should have question myself from the first . I should have ask why any room in the house be well than home to I when she enter it , and barren as a desert when she go out again why I always notice and remember the little change in her dress that I have notice and remember in no other woman 's before why I see she , hear she , and touch she   when we shake hand at night and morning   as I have never see , hear , and touch any other woman in my life ? I should have look into my own heart , and find this new growth spring up there , and pluck it out while it be young . why be this easy , simplest work of self - culture always too much for I ? the explanation have be write already in the three word that be many enough , and plain enough , for my confession . I love she . 

 the day pass , the week pass ; it be approach the third month of my stay in Cumberland . the delicious monotony of life in our calm seclusion flow on with I , like a smooth stream with a swimmer who glide down the current . all memory of the past , all thought of the future , all sense of the falseness and hopelessness of my own position , lie hush within I into deceitful rest . lull by the Syren - song that my own heart sing to I , with eye shut to all sight , and ear close to all sound of danger , I drift near and near to the fatal rock . the warning that arouse I at last , and startle I into sudden , self - accuse consciousness of my own weakness , be the plain , the true , the kind of all warning , for it come silently from she . 

 we have part one night as usual . no word have fall from my lip , at that time or at any time before it , that could betray I , or startle she into sudden knowledge of the truth . but when we meet again in the morning , a change have come over she a change that tell I all . 

 I shrink then I shrink still from invade the innermost sanctuary of her heart , and lay it open to other , as I have lay open my own . let it be enough to say that the time when she first surprise my secret be , I firmly believe , the time when she first surprise her own , and the time , also , when she change towards I in the interval of one night . her nature , too truthful to deceive other , be too noble to deceive itself . when the doubt that I have hush asleep first lay its weary weight on her heart , the true face own all , and say , in its own frank , simple language I be sorry for he ; I be sorry for myself . 

 it say this , and more , which I could not then interpret . I understand but too well the change in her manner , to great kindness and quick readiness in interpret all my wish , before other to constraint and sadness , and nervous anxiety to absorb herself in the first occupation she could seize on , whenever we happen to be leave together alone . I understand why the sweet sensitive lip smile so rarely and so restrainedly now , and why the clear blue eye look at I , sometimes with the pity of an angel , sometimes with the innocent perplexity of a child . but the change mean more than this . there be a coldness in her hand , there be an unnatural immobility in her face , there be in all her movement the mute expression of constant fear and cling self - reproach . the sensation that I could trace to herself and to I , the unacknowledged sensation that we be feel in common , be not these . there be certain element of the change in she that be still secretly draw we together , and other that be , as secretly , begin to drive we apart . 

 in my doubt and perplexity , in my vague suspicion of something hide which I be leave to find by my own unaided effort , I examine Miss Halcombe 's look and manner for enlightenment . live in such intimacy as ours , no serious alteration could take place in any one of we which do not sympathetically affect the other . the change in Miss Fairlie be reflect in her half - sister . although not a word escape Miss Halcombe which hint at an altered state of feeling towards myself , her penetrate eye have contract a new habit of always watch I . sometimes the look be like suppressed anger , sometimes like suppress dread , sometimes like neither like nothing , in short , which I could understand . a week elapse , leave we all three still in this position of secret constraint towards one another . my situation , aggravate by the sense of my own miserable weakness and forgetfulness of myself , now too late awaken in I , be become intolerable . I feel that I must cast off the oppression under which I be live , at once and for ever yet how to act for the good , or what to say first , be more than I could tell . 

 from this position of helplessness and humiliation I be rescue by Miss Halcombe . her lip tell I the bitter , the necessary , the unexpected truth ; her hearty kindness sustain I under the shock of hear it ; her sense and courage turn to its right use an event which threaten the bad that could happen , to I and to other , in Limmeridge House . 



 X 

 it be on a Thursday in the week , and nearly at the end of the third month of my sojourn in Cumberland . 

 in the morning , when I go down into the breakfast - room at the usual hour , Miss Halcombe , for the first time since I have know she , be absent from her customary place at the table . 

 Miss Fairlie be out on the lawn . she bow to I , but do not come in . not a word have drop from my lip , or from her , that could unsettle either of we and yet the same unacknowledged sense of embarrassment make we shrink alike from meet one another alone . she wait on the lawn , and I wait in the breakfast - room , till Mrs. Vesey or Miss Halcombe come in . how quickly I should have join she : how readily we should have shake hand , and glide into our customary talk , only a fortnight ago . 

 in a few minute Miss Halcombe enter . she have a preoccupied look , and she make her apology for be late rather absently . 

 " I have be detain , " she say , " by a consultation with Mr. Fairlie on a domestic matter which he wish to speak to I about . " 

 Miss Fairlie come in from the garden , and the usual morning greeting pass between we . her hand strike cold to mine than ever . she do not look at I , and she be very pale . even Mrs. Vesey notice it when she enter the room a moment after . 

 " I suppose it be the change in the wind , " say the old lady . " the winter be come ah , my love , the winter be come soon ! " 

 in her heart and in mine it have come already ! 

 our morning meal once so full of pleasant good - humour discussion of the plan for the day be short and silent . Miss Fairlie seem to feel the oppression of the long pause in the conversation , and look appealingly to her sister to fill they up . Miss Halcombe , after once or twice hesitate and check herself , in a most uncharacteristic manner , speak at last . 

 " I have see your uncle this morning , Laura , " she say . " he think the purple room be the one that ought to be get ready , and he confirm what I tell you . Monday be the day not Tuesday . " 

 while these word be be speak Miss Fairlie look down at the table beneath she . her finger move nervously among the crumb that be scatter on the cloth . the paleness on her cheek spread to her lip , and the lip themselves tremble visibly . I be not the only person present who notice this . Miss Halcombe see it , too , and at once set we the example of rise from table . 

 Mrs. Vesey and Miss Fairlie leave the room together . the kind sorrowful blue eye look at I , for a moment , with the prescient sadness of a coming and a long farewell . I feel the answer pang in my own heart the pang that tell I I must lose she soon , and love she the more unchangeably for the loss . 

 I turn towards the garden when the door have close on she . Miss Halcombe be stand with her hat in her hand , and her shawl over her arm , by the large window that lead out to the lawn , and be look at I attentively . 

 " have you any leisure time to spare , " she ask , " before you begin to work in your own room ? " 

 " certainly , Miss Halcombe . I have always time at your service . " 

 " I want to say a word to you in private , Mr. Hartright . get your hat and come out into the garden . we be not likely to be disturb there at this hour in the morning . " 

 as we step out on to the lawn , one of the under - gardener a mere lad pass we on his way to the house , with a letter in his hand . Miss Halcombe stop he . 

 " be that letter for I ? " she ask . 

 " Nay , miss ; it be just say to be for Miss Fairlie , " answer the lad , hold out the letter as he speak .

 XII 

 our inquiry at Limmeridge be patiently pursue in all direction , and among all sort and condition of people . but nothing come of they . three of the villager do certainly assure we that they have see the woman , but as they be quite unable to describe she , and quite incapable of agree about the exact direction in which she be proceed when they last see she , these three bright exception to the general rule of total ignorance afford no more real assistance to we than the mass of their unhelpful and unobservant neighbour .

 Jacob 's fortitude desert he at the double disappearance of his schoolfellow and his prospect of dinner . he take his hand out of his pocket , look hard at his knuckle , raise they with great deliberation to his eye , and when they get there , ground they round and round slowly , accompany the action by short spasm of sniffing , which follow each other at regular interval the nasal minute gun of juvenile distress .

 the exposed situation of the churchyard have oblige I to be cautious in choose the position that I be to occupy .

 her " misfortune . " in what sense be she use that word ? in a sense which might explain her motive in write the anonymous letter ? in a sense which might show it to be the too common and too customary motive that have lead many a woman to interpose anonymous hindrance to the marriage of the man who have ruin she ?  she look up at I with the artless bewilderment of a child . not the slight confusion or change of colour not the faint trace of any secret consciousness of shame struggle to the surface appear in her face that face which betray every other emotion with such transparent clearness . no word that ever be speak could have assure I , as her look and manner now assure I , that the motive which I have assign for her write the letter and send it to Miss Fairlie be plainly and distinctly the wrong one . that doubt , at any rate , be now set at rest ; but the very removal of it open a new prospect of uncertainty . the letter , as I know from positive testimony , point at Sir Percival Glyde , though it do not name he . she must have have some strong motive , originate in some deep sense of injury , for secretly denounce he to Miss Fairlie in such term as she have employ , and that motive be unquestionably not to be trace to the loss of her innocence and her character . whatever wrong he might have inflict on she be not of that nature . of what nature could it be ? 

 " I do not   understand you , " she say , after evidently try hard , and try in vain , to discover the meaning of the word I have last say to she .

 I remember the place perfectly we have often pass by it in our drive . it be one of the old farm in the neighbourhood , situate in a solitary , shelter spot , inland at the junction of two hill .

I would have go anywhere to be quiet , and safe , and out of the way . but when I hear that Todd 's Corner be near Limmeridge oh ! I be so happy I would have walk all the way barefoot to get there , and see the school and the village and Limmeridge House again . they be very good people at Todd 's Corner . I hope I shall stay there a long time . there be only one thing I do not   like about they , and do not   like about Mrs. Clements "

" 

 her voice sink when she put the question about Miss Fairlie , and she turn her head far and far away from I .  she murmur a few word , but they be speak so confusedly , and in such a low tone , that I could not even guess at what they mean .

 she have be down on her knee for some little time past , carefully remove the last weather - stain leave about the inscription while we be speak together . the first sentence of the word I have just address to she make her pause in her occupation , and turn slowly without rise from her knee , so as to face I . the second sentence literally petrify she . the cloth she have be hold drop from her hand her lip fall apart all the little colour that there be naturally in her face leave it in an instant .

" the blood rush back into her face rush overwhelmingly , as the sense rush upon her mind that her own word have betray she . she strike her hand together in despair . "

 " ah ! Miss Fairlie Fairlie Fairlie " 

 the mere utterance of the love familiar name seem to quiet she . her face soften and grow like itself again .

 she be at Anne Catherick 's side , and have put one arm around she , before I could answer . " what be it , my dear ? " she say . " what have he do to you ? "

I be sorry I speak so rough to you , sir ; but you must own that appearance look suspicious to a stranger . it be more my fault than your , for humour her whim , and let she be alone in such a place as this . come , my dear come home now . "

 XIV 

 half an hour later I be back at the house , and be inform Miss Halcombe of all that have happen .

"

"

 we part for the night . 


 after breakfast the next morning , an obstacle , which the event of the evening before have put out of my memory , interpose to prevent our proceeding immediately to the farm . this be my last day at Limmeridge House , and it be necessary , as soon as the post come in , to follow Miss Halcombe 's advice , and to ask Mr. Fairlie 's permission to shorten my engagement by a month , in consideration of an unforeseen necessity for my return to London . 

 fortunately for the probability of this excuse , so far as appearance be concern , the post bring I two letter from London friend that morning . I take they away at once to my own room , and send the servant with a message to Mr. Fairlie , request to know when I could see he on a matter of business .

"

"

"
