 XV 

 as we walk round to the front of the house a fly from the railway approach we along the drive . Miss Halcombe wait on the door - step until the fly draw up , and then advance to shake hand with an old gentleman , who get out briskly the moment the step be let down . Mr. Gilmore have arrive . 

 I look at he , when we be introduce to each other , with an interest and a curiosity which I could hardly conceal . this old man be to remain at Limmeridge House after I have leave it , he be to hear Sir Percival Glyde 's explanation , and be to give Miss Halcombe the assistance of his experience in form her judgment ; he be to wait until the question of the marriage be set at rest ; and his hand , if that question be decide in the affirmative , be to draw the settlement which bind Miss Fairlie irrevocably to her engagement . even then , when I know nothing by comparison with what I know now , I look at the family lawyer with an interest which I have never feel before in the presence of any man breathe who be a total stranger to I . 

 in external appearance Mr. Gilmore be the exact opposite of the conventional idea of an old lawyer . his complexion be florid his white hair be wear rather long and keep carefully brush his black coat , waistcoat , and trouser fit he with perfect neatness his white cravat be carefully tie , and his lavender - colour kid glove might have adorn the hand of a fashionable clergyman , without fear and without reproach . his manner be pleasantly mark by the formal grace and refinement of the old school of politeness , quicken by the invigorate sharpness and readiness of a man whose business in life oblige he always to keep his faculty in good working order . a sanguine constitution and fair prospect to begin with a long subsequent career of creditable and comfortable prosperity a cheerful , diligent , widely - respect old age such be the general impression I derive from my introduction to Mr. Gilmore , and it be but fair to he to add , that the knowledge I gain by later and well experience only tend to confirm they . 

 I leave the old gentleman and Miss Halcombe to enter the house together , and to talk of family matter undisturbed by the restraint of a stranger 's presence . they cross the hall on their way to the drawing - room , and I descend the step again to wander about the garden alone . 

 my hour be number at Limmeridge House my departure the next morning be irrevocably settle my share in the investigation which the anonymous letter have render necessary be at an end . no harm could be do to any one but myself if I let my heart loose again , for the little time that be leave I , from the cold cruelty of restraint which necessity have force I to inflict upon it , and take my farewell of the scene which be associate with the brief dream - time of my happiness and my love . 

 I turn instinctively to the walk beneath my study - window , where I have see she the evening before with her little dog , and follow the path which her dear foot have tread so often , till I come to the wicket gate that lead into her rose garden . the winter bareness spread drearily over it now . the flower that she have teach I to distinguish by their name , the flower that I have teach she to paint from , be go , and the tiny white path that lead between the bed be damp and green already . I go on to the avenue of tree , where we have breathe together the warm fragrance of August evening , where we have admire together the myriad combination of shade and sunlight that dapple the ground at our foot . the leaf fall about I from the groan branch , and the earthy decay in the atmosphere chill I to the bone . a little far on , and I be out of the ground , and follow the lane that wound gently upward to the near hill . the old felled tree by the wayside , on which we have sit to rest , be sodden with rain , and the tuft of fern and grass which I have draw for she , nestle under the rough stone wall in front of we , have turn to a pool of water , stagnate round an island of draggle weed . I gain the summit of the hill , and look at the view which we have so often admire in the happy time . it be cold and barren it be no long the view that I remember . the sunshine of her presence be far from I the charm of her voice no long murmur in my ear . she have talk to I , on the spot from which I now look down , of her father , who be her last survive parent have tell I how fond of each other they have be , and how sadly she miss he still when she enter certain room in the house , and when she take up forget occupation and amusement with which he have be associate . be the view that I have see , while listen to those word , the view that I see now , stand on the hill - top by myself ? I turn and leave it I wound my way back again , over the moor , and round the sandhill , down to the beach . there be the white rage of the surf , and the multitudinous glory of the leap wave but where be the place on which she have once draw idle figure with her parasol in the sand the place where we have sit together , while she talk to I about myself and my home , while she ask I a woman 's minutely observant question about my mother and my sister , and innocently wonder whether I should ever leave my lonely chamber and have a wife and a house of my own ? wind and wave have long since smooth out the trace of she which she have leave in those mark on the sand , I look over the wide monotony of the sea - side prospect , and the place in which we two have idle away the sunny hour be as lose to I as if I have never know it , as strange to I as if I stand already on a foreign shore . 

 the empty silence of the beach strike cold to my heart . I return to the house and the garden , where trace be leave to speak of she at every turn . 

 on the west terrace walk I meet Mr. Gilmore . he be evidently in search of I , for he quicken his pace when we catch sight of each other . the state of my spirit little fit I for the society of a stranger ; but the meeting be inevitable , and I resign myself to make the good of it . 

 " you be the very person I want to see , " say the old gentleman . " I have two word to say to you , my dear sir ; and if you have no objection I will avail myself of the present opportunity . to put it plainly , Miss Halcombe and I have be talk over family affair affair which be the cause of my be here and in the course of our conversation she be naturally lead to tell I of this unpleasant matter connect with the anonymous letter , and of the share which you have most creditably and properly take in the proceeding so far . that share , I quite understand , give you an interest which you might not otherwise have feel , in know that the future management of the investigation which you have begin will be place in safe hand . my dear sir , make yourself quite easy on that point it will be place in MY hand . " 

 " you be , in every way , Mr. Gilmore , much fitter to advise and to act in the matter than I be . be it an indiscretion on my part to ask if you have decide yet on a course of proceeding ? " 

 " so far as it be possible to decide , Mr. Hartright , I have decide . I mean to send a copy of the letter , accompany by a statement of the circumstance , to Sir Percival Glyde 's solicitor in London , with whom I have some acquaintance . the letter itself I shall keep here to show to Sir Percival as soon as he arrive . the tracing of the two woman I have already provide for , by send one of Mr. Fairlie 's servant a confidential person to the station to make inquiry . the man have his money and his direction , and he will follow the woman in the event of his find any clue . this be all that can be do until Sir Percival come on Monday . I have no doubt myself that every explanation which can be expect from a gentleman and a man of honour , he will readily give . Sir Percival stand very high , sir an eminent position , a reputation above suspicion I feel quite easy about result quite easy , I be rejoice to assure you . thing of this sort happen constantly in my experience . anonymous letter unfortunate woman sad state of society . I do not   deny that there be peculiar complication in this case ; but the case itself be , most unhappily , common common . " 

 " I be afraid , Mr. Gilmore , I have the misfortune to differ from you in the view I take of the case . " 

 " just so , my dear sir just so . I be an old man , and I take the practical view . you be a young man , and you take the romantic view . let we not dispute about our view . I live professionally in an atmosphere of disputation , Mr. Hartright , and I be only too glad to escape from it , as I be escape here . we will wait for event yes , yes , yes we will wait for event . charming place this . good shooting ? probably not , none of Mr. Fairlie 's land be preserve , I think . charming place , though , and delightful people . you draw and paint , I hear , Mr. Hartright ? enviable accomplishment . what style ? " 

 we drop into general conversation , or rather , Mr. Gilmore talk and I listen . my attention be far from he , and from the topic on which he discourse so fluently . the solitary walk of the last two hour have work its effect on I it have set the idea in my mind of hasten my departure from Limmeridge House . why should I prolong the hard trial of say farewell by one unnecessary minute ? what further service be require of I by any one ? there be no useful purpose to be serve by my stay in Cumberland there be no restriction of time in the permission to leave which my employer have grant to I . why not end it there and then ? 

 I determine to end it . there be some hour of daylight still leave there be no reason why my journey back to London should not begin on that afternoon . I make the first civil excuse that occur to I for leave Mr. Gilmore , and return at once to the house . 

 on my way up to my own room I meet Miss Halcombe on the stair . she see , by the hurry of my movement and the change in my manner , that I have some new purpose in view , and ask what have happen . 

 I tell she the reason which induce I to think of hasten my departure , exactly as I have tell they here . 

 " no , no , " she say , earnestly and kindly , " leave we like a friend break bread with we once more . stay here and dine , stay here and help we to spend our last evening with you as happily , as like our first evening , as we can . it be my invitation Mrs. Vesey 's invitation " she hesitate a little , and then add , " Laura 's invitation as well . " 

 I promise to remain . God know I have no wish to leave even the shadow of a sorrowful impression with any one of they . 

 my own room be the good place for I till the dinner bell ring . I wait there till it be time to go downstairs . 

 I have not speak to Miss Fairlie I have not even see she all that day . the first meeting with she , when I enter the drawing - room , be a hard trial to her self - control and to mine . she , too , have do she good to make our last evening renew the golden bygone time the time that could never come again . she have put on the dress which I use to admire more than any other that she possess a dark blue silk , trim quaintly and prettily with old - fashioned lace ; she come forward to meet I with her former readiness she give I her hand with the frank , innocent good - will of happy day . the cold finger that tremble round mine the pale cheek with a bright red spot burn in the midst of they the faint smile that struggle to live on her lip and die away from they while I look at it , tell I at what sacrifice of herself her outward composure be maintain . my heart could take she no close to I , or I should have love she then as I have never love she yet . 

 Mr. Gilmore be a great assistance to we . he be in high good - humour , and he lead the conversation with unflagge spirit . Miss Halcombe second he resolutely , and I do all I could to follow her example . the kind blue eye , whose slight change of expression I have learn to interpret so well , look at I appealingly when we first sit down to table . help my sister the sweet anxious face seem to say help my sister , and you will help I . 

 we get through the dinner , to all outward appearance at least , happily enough . when the lady have rise from table , and Mr. Gilmore and I be leave alone in the dining - room , a new interest present itself to occupy our attention , and to give I an opportunity of quiet myself by a few minute of needful and welcome silence . the servant who have be despatch to trace Anne Catherick and Mrs. Clements return with his report , and be show into the dining - room immediately . 

 " well , " say Mr. Gilmore , " what have you find out ? " 

 " I have find out , sir , " answer the man , " that both the woman take ticket at our station here for Carlisle . " 

 " you go to Carlisle , of course , when you hear that ? " 

 " I do , sir , but I be sorry to say I could find no further trace of they . " 

 " you inquire at the railway ? " 

 " yes , sir . " 

 " and at the different inn ? " 

 " yes , sir . " 

 " and you leave the statement I write for you at the police station ? " 

 " I do , sir . " 

 " well , my friend , you have do all you could , and I have do all I could , and there the matter must rest till further notice . we have play our trump card , Mr. Hartright , " continue the old gentleman when the servant have withdraw . " for the present , at least , the woman have outmanoeuvre we , and our only resource now be to wait till Sir Percival Glyde come here on Monday next . will not   you fill your glass again ? good bottle of port , that sound , substantial , old wine . I have get well in my own cellar , though . " 

 we return to the drawing - room the room in which the happy evening of my life have be pass the room which , after this last night , I be never to see again . its aspect be alter since the day have shorten and the weather have grow cold . the glass door on the terrace side be closed , and hide by thick curtain . instead of the soft twilight obscurity , in which we use to sit , the bright radiant glow of lamplight now dazzle my eye . all be change indoor and out all be change . 

 Miss Halcombe and Mr. Gilmore sit down together at the card - table Mrs. Vesey take her customary chair . there be no restraint on the disposal of their evening , and I feel the restraint on the disposal of mine all the more painfully from observe it . I see Miss Fairlie linger near the music - stand . the time have be when I might have join she there . I wait irresolutely I know neither where to go nor what to do next . she cast one quick glance at I , take a piece of music suddenly from the stand , and come towards I of her own accord . 

 " shall I play some of those little melody of Mozart 's which you use to like so much ? " she ask , open the music nervously , and look down at it while she speak . 

 before I could thank she she hasten to the piano . the chair near it , which I have always be accustom to occupy , stand empty . she strike a few chord then glance round at I then look back again at her music . 

 " will not   you take your old place ? " she say , speak very abruptly and in very low tone . 

 " I may take it on the last night , " I answer . 

 she do not reply she keep her attention rivet on the music music which she know by memory , which she have play over and over again , in former time , without the book . I only know that she have hear I , I only know that she be aware of my be close to she , by see the red spot on the cheek that be near to I fade out , and the face grow pale all over . 

 " I be very sorry you be go , " she say , her voice almost sink to a whisper , her eye look more and more intently at the music , her finger fly over the key of the piano with a strange feverish energy which I have never notice in she before . 

 " I shall remember those kind word , Miss Fairlie , long after to - morrow have come and go . " 

 the paleness grow whiter on her face , and she turn it far away from I . 

 " do not   speak of to - morrow , " she say . " let the music speak to we of to - night , in a happy language than ours . " 

 her lip tremble a faint sigh flutter from they , which she try vainly to suppress . her finger waver on the piano she strike a false note , confuse herself in try to set it right , and drop her hand angrily on her lap . Miss Halcombe and Mr. Gilmore look up in astonishment from the card - table at which they be play . even Mrs. Vesey , doze in her chair , wake at the sudden cessation of the music , and inquire what have happen . 

 " you play at whist , Mr. Hartright ? " ask Miss Halcombe , with her eye direct significantly at the place I occupy . 

 I know what she mean I know she be right , and I rise at once to go to the card - table . as I leave the piano Miss Fairlie turn a page of the music , and touch the key again with a surer hand . 

 " I will play it , " she say , strike the note almost passionately . " I will play it on the last night . " 

 " Come , Mrs. Vesey , " say Miss Halcombe , " Mr. Gilmore and I be tired of ecarte come and be Mr. Hartright 's partner at whist . " 

 the old lawyer smile satirically . his have be the win hand , and he have just turn up a king . he evidently attribute Miss Halcombe 's abrupt change in the card - table arrangement to a lady 's inability to play the lose game . 

 the rest of the evening pass without a word or a look from she . she keep her place at the piano , and I keep mine at the card - table . she play unintermittingly play as if the music be her only refuge from herself . sometimes her finger touch the note with a lingering fondness a soft , plaintive , die tenderness , unutterably beautiful and mournful to hear ; sometimes they falter and fail she , or hurry over the instrument mechanically , as if their task be a burden to they . but still , change and waver as they might in the expression they impart to the music , their resolution to play never falter . she only rise from the piano when we all rise to say good - night . 

 Mrs. Vesey be the near to the door , and the first to shake hand with I . 

 " I shall not see you again , Mr. Hartright , " say the old lady . " I be truly sorry you be go away . you have be very kind and attentive , and an old woman like I feel kindness and attention . I wish you happy , sir I wish you a kind good - bye . " 

 Mr. Gilmore come next . 

 " I hope we shall have a future opportunity of better our acquaintance , Mr. Hartright . you quite understand about that little matter of business be safe in my hand ? yes , yes , of course . bless I , how cold it be ! do not   let I keep you at the door . Bon voyage , my dear sir bon voyage , as the French say . " 

 Miss Halcombe follow . 

 " half - past seven to - morrow morning , " she say then add in a whisper , " I have hear and see more than you think . your conduct to - night have make I your friend for life . " 

 Miss Fairlie come last . I could not trust myself to look at she when I take her hand , and when I think of the next morning . 

 " my departure must be a very early one , " I say . " I shall be go , Miss Fairlie , before you " 

 " no , no , " she interpose hastily , " not before I be out of my room . I shall be down to breakfast with Marian . I be not so ungrateful , not so forgetful of the past three month " 

 her voice fail she , her hand close gently round mine then drop it suddenly . before I could say " good - night " she be go . 


 the end come fast to meet I come inevitably , as the light of the last morning come at Limmeridge House . 

 it be barely half - past seven when I go downstairs , but I find they both at the breakfast - table wait for I . in the chill air , in the dim light , in the gloomy morning silence of the house , we three sit down together , and try to eat , try to talk . the struggle to preserve appearance be hopeless and useless , and I rise to end it . 

 as I hold out my hand , as Miss Halcombe , who be near to I , take it , Miss Fairlie turn away suddenly and hurry from the room . 

 " well so , " say Miss Halcombe , when the door have close " well so , for you and for she . " 

 I wait a moment before I could speak it be hard to lose she , without a parting word or a parting look . I control myself I try to take leave of Miss Halcombe in fitting term ; but all the farewell word I would fain have speak dwindle to one sentence . 

 " have I deserve that you should write to I ? " be all I could say . 

 " you have nobly deserve everything that I can do for you , as long as we both live . whatever the end be you shall know it . " 

 " and if I can ever be of help again , at any future time , long after the memory of my presumption and my folly be forget . . . " 

 I could add no more . my voice falter , my eye moisten in spite of I . 

 she catch I by both hand she press they with the strong , steady grasp of a man her dark eye glitter her brown complexion flush deep the force and energy of her face glow and grow beautiful with the pure inner light of her generosity and her pity . 

 " I will trust you if ever the time come I will trust you as my friend and her friend , as my brother and her brother . " she stop , draw I near to she the fearless , noble creature touch my forehead , sister - like , with her lip , and call I by my christian name . " God bless you , Walter ! " she say . " wait here alone and compose yourself I have well not stay for both our sake I have well see you go from the balcony upstairs . " 

 she leave the room . I turn away towards the window , where nothing face I but the lonely autumn landscape I turn away to master myself , before I too leave the room in my turn , and leave it for ever . 

 a minute pass it could hardly have be more when I hear the door open again softly , and the rustling of a woman 's dress on the carpet move towards I . my heart beat violently as I turn round . Miss Fairlie be approach I from the farth end of the room . 

 she stop and hesitate when our eye meet , and when she see that we be alone . then , with that courage which woman lose so often in the small emergency , and so seldom in the great , she come on near to I , strangely pale and strangely quiet , draw one hand after she along the table by which she walk , and hold something at her side in the other , which be hide by the fold of her dress . 

 " I only go into the drawing - room , " she say , " to look for this . it may remind you of your visit here , and of the friend you leave behind you . you tell I I have improve very much when I do it , and I think you might like " 

 she turn her head away , and offer I a little sketch , draw throughout by her own pencil , of the summer - house in which we have first meet . the paper tremble in her hand as she hold it out to I tremble in mine as I take it from she . 

 I be afraid to say what I feel I only answer , " it shall never leave I all my life long it shall be the treasure that I prize most . I be very grateful for it very grateful to you , for not let I go away without bid you good - bye . " 

 " oh ! " she say innocently , " how could I let you go , after we have pass so many happy day together ! " 

 " those day may never return , Miss Fairlie my way of life and yours be very far apart . but if a time should come , when the devotion of my whole heart and soul and strength will give you a moment 's happiness , or spare you a moment 's sorrow , will you try to remember the poor drawing - master who have teach you ? Miss Halcombe have promise to trust I will you promise too ? " 

 the farewell sadness in the kind blue eye shine dimly through her gathering tear . 

 " I promise it , " she say in broken tone . " oh , do not   look at I like that ! I promise it with all my heart . " 

 I venture a little near to she , and hold out my hand . 

 " you have many friend who love you , Miss Fairlie . your happy future be the dear object of many hope . may I say , at part , that it be the dear object of MY hope too ? " 

 the tear flow fast down her cheek . she rest one tremble hand on the table to steady herself while she give I the other . I take it in mine I hold it fast . my head droop over it , my tear fall on it , my lip press it not in love ; oh , not in love , at that last moment , but in the agony and the self - abandonment of despair . 

 " for God 's sake , leave I ! " she say faintly . 

 the confession of her heart 's secret burst from she in those plead word . I have no right to hear they , no right to answer they they be the word that banish I , in the name of her sacred weakness , from the room . it be all over . I drop her hand , I say no more . the blind tear shut she out from my eye , and I dash they away to look at she for the last time . one look as she sink into a chair , as her arm fall on the table , as her fair head drop on they wearily . one farewell look , and the door have close upon she the great gulf of separation have open between we the image of Laura Fairlie be a memory of the past already . 


 the end of Hartright 's Narrative . 




 the story continued BY VINCENT GILMORE 

  of Chancery Lane , Solicitor 



 I 

 I write these line at the request of my friend , Mr. Walter Hartright . they be intend to convey a description of certain event which seriously affect Miss Fairlie 's interest , and which take place after the period of Mr. Hartright 's departure from Limmeridge House . 

 there be no need for I to say whether my own opinion do or do not sanction the disclosure of the remarkable family story , of which my narrative form an important component part . Mr. Hartright have take that responsibility on himself , and circumstance yet to be relate will show that he have amply earn the right to do so , if he choose to exercise it . the plan he have adopt for present the story to other , in the most truthful and most vivid manner , require that it should be tell , at each successive stage in the march of event , by the person who be directly concerned in those event at the time of their occurrence . my appearance here , as narrator , be the necessary consequence of this arrangement . I be present during the sojourn of Sir Percival Glyde in Cumberland , and be personally concerned in one important result of his short residence under Mr. Fairlie 's roof . it be my duty , therefore , to add these new link to the chain of event , and to take up the chain itself at the point where , for the present only Mr. Hartright have drop it . 


 I arrive at Limmeridge House on Friday the second of November . 

 my object be to remain at Mr. Fairlie 's until the arrival of Sir Percival Glyde . if that event lead to the appointment of any give day for Sir Percival 's union with Miss Fairlie , I be to take the necessary instruction back with I to London , and to occupy myself in draw the lady 's marriage - settlement . 

 on the Friday I be not favour by Mr. Fairlie with an interview . he have be , or have fancy himself to be , an invalid for year past , and he be not well enough to receive I . Miss Halcombe be the first member of the family whom I see . she meet I at the house door , and introduce I to Mr. Hartright , who have be stay at Limmeridge for some time past . 

 I do not see Miss Fairlie until later in the day , at dinner - time . she be not look well , and I be sorry to observe it . she be a sweet lovable girl , as amiable and attentive to every one about she as her excellent mother use to be though , personally speak , she take after her father . Mrs. Fairlie have dark eye and hair , and her eld daughter , Miss Halcombe , strongly remind I of she . Miss Fairlie play to we in the evening not so well as usual , I think . we have a rubber at whist , a mere profanation , so far as play be concern , of that noble game . I have be favourably impress by Mr. Hartright on our first introduction to one another , but I soon discover that he be not free from the social failing incidental to his age . there be three thing that none of the young man of the present generation can do . they can not   sit over their wine , they can not   play at whist , and they can not   pay a lady a compliment . Mr. Hartright be no exception to the general rule . otherwise , even in those early day and on that short acquaintance , he strike I as be a modest and gentlemanlike young man . 

 so the Friday pass . I say nothing about the more serious matter which engage my attention on that day the anonymous letter to Miss Fairlie , the measure I think it right to adopt when the matter be mention to I , and the conviction I entertain that every possible explanation of the circumstance would be readily afford by Sir Percival Glyde , have all be fully notice , as I understand , in the narrative which precede this . 

 on the Saturday Mr. Hartright have leave before I get down to breakfast . Miss Fairlie keep her room all day , and Miss Halcombe appear to I to be out of spirit . the house be not what it use to be in the time of Mr. and Mrs. Philip Fairlie . I take a walk by myself in the forenoon , and look about at some of the place which I first see when I be stay at Limmeridge to transact family business , more than thirty year since . they be not what they use to be either . 

 at two o'clock Mr. Fairlie send to say he be well enough to see I . he have not alter , at any rate , since I first know he . his talk be to the same purpose as usual all about himself and his ailment , his wonderful coin , and his matchless rembrandt etching . the moment I try to speak of the business that have bring I to his house , he shut his eye and say I " upset " he . I persist in upset he by return again and again to the subject . all I could ascertain be that he look on his niece 's marriage as a settled thing , that her father have sanction it , that he sanction it himself , that it be a desirable marriage , and that he should be personally rejoice when the worry of it be over . as to the settlement , if I would consult his niece , and afterwards dive as deeply as I please into my own knowledge of the family affair , and get everything ready , and limit his share in the business , as guardian , to say yes , at the right moment why , of course he would meet my view , and everybody else 's view , with infinite pleasure . in the meantime , there I see he , a helpless sufferer , confine to his room . do I think he look as if he want tease ? no . then why tease he ? 

 I might , perhaps , have be a little astonished at this extraordinary absence of all self - assertion on Mr. Fairlie 's part , in the character of guardian , if my knowledge of the family affair have not be sufficient to remind I that he be a single man , and that he have nothing more than a life - interest in the Limmeridge property . as matter stand , therefore , I be neither surprised nor disappoint at the result of the interview . Mr. Fairlie have simply justify my expectation and there be an end of it . 

 Sunday be a dull day , out of door and in . a letter arrive for I from Sir Percival Glyde 's solicitor , acknowledge the receipt of my copy of the anonymous letter and my accompanying statement of the case . Miss Fairlie join we in the afternoon , look pale and depressed , and altogether unlike herself . I have some talk with she , and venture on a delicate allusion to Sir Percival . she listen and say nothing . all other subject she pursue willingly , but this subject she allow to drop . I begin to doubt whether she might not be repent of her engagement just as young lady often do , when repentance come too late . 

 on Monday Sir Percival Glyde arrive . 

 I find he to be a most prepossess man , so far as manner and appearance be concern . he look rather old than I have expect , his head be bald over the forehead , and his face somewhat marked and worn , but his movement be as active and his spirit as high as a young man 's . his meeting with Miss Halcombe be delightfully hearty and unaffected , and his reception of I , upon my being present to he , be so easy and pleasant that we get on together like old friend . Miss Fairlie be not with we when he arrive , but she enter the room about ten minute afterwards . Sir Percival rise and pay his compliment with perfect grace . his evident concern on see the change for the bad in the young lady 's look be express with a mixture of tenderness and respect , with an unassuming delicacy of tone , voice , and manner , which do equal credit to his good breeding and his good sense . I be rather surprised , under these circumstance , to see that Miss Fairlie continue to be constrain and uneasy in his presence , and that she take the first opportunity of leave the room again . Sir Percival neither notice the restraint in her reception of he , nor her sudden withdrawal from our society . he have not obtrude his attention on she while she be present , and he do not embarrass Miss Halcombe by any allusion to her departure when she be go . his tact and taste be never at fault on this or on any other occasion while I be in his company at Limmeridge House . 

 as soon as Miss Fairlie have leave the room he spar we all embarrassment on the subject of the anonymous letter , by advert to it of his own accord . he have stop in London on his way from Hampshire , have see his solicitor , have read the document forward by I , and have travel on to Cumberland , anxious to satisfy our mind by the speedy and the full explanation that word could convey . on hear he express himself to this effect , I offer he the original letter , which I have keep for his inspection . he thank I , and decline to look at it , say that he have see the copy , and that he be quite willing to leave the original in our hand . 

 the statement itself , on which he immediately enter , be as simple and satisfactory as I have all along anticipate it would be . 

 Mrs. Catherick , he inform we , have in past year lay he under some obligation for faithful service render to his family connection and to himself . she have be doubly unfortunate in be married to a husband who have desert she , and in have an only child whose mental faculty have be in a disturbed condition from a very early age . although her marriage have remove she to a part of Hampshire far distant from the neighbourhood in which Sir Percival 's property be situate , he have take care not to lose sight of her his friendly feeling towards the poor woman , in consideration of her past service , have be greatly strengthen by his admiration of the patience and courage with which she support her calamity . in course of time the symptom of mental affliction in her unhappy daughter increase to such a serious extent , as to make it a matter of necessity to place she under proper medical care . Mrs. Catherick herself recognise this necessity , but she also feel the prejudice common to person occupy her respectable station , against allow her child to be admit , as a pauper , into a public asylum . Sir Percival have respect this prejudice , as he respect honest independence of feeling in any rank of life , and have resolve to mark his grateful sense of Mrs. Catherick 's early attachment to the interest of himself and his family , by defray the expense of her daughter 's maintenance in a trustworthy private Asylum . to her mother 's regret , and to his own regret , the unfortunate creature have discover the share which circumstance have induce he to take in place she under restraint , and have conceive the most intense hatred and distrust of he in consequence . to that hatred and distrust which have express itself in various way in the Asylum the anonymous letter , write after her escape , be plainly attributable . if Miss Halcombe 's or Mr. Gilmore 's recollection of the document do not confirm that view , or if they wish for any additional particular about the Asylum   the address of which he mention , as well as the name and address of the two doctor on whose certificate the patient be admit , he be ready to answer any question and to clear up any uncertainty . he have do his duty to the unhappy young woman , by instruct his solicitor to spare no expense in trace she , and in restore she once more to medical care , and he be now only anxious to do his duty towards Miss Fairlie and towards her family , in the same plain , straightforward way . 

 I be the first to speak in answer to this appeal . my own course be plain to I . it be the great beauty of the Law that it can dispute any human statement , make under any circumstance , and reduce to any form . if I have feel professionally call upon to set up a case against Sir Percival Glyde , on the strength of his own explanation , I could have do so beyond all doubt . but my duty do not lie in this direction my function be of the purely judicial kind . I be to weigh the explanation we have just hear , to allow all due force to the high reputation of the gentleman who offer it , and to decide honestly whether the probability , on Sir Percival 's own showing , be plainly with he , or plainly against he . my own conviction be that they be plainly with he , and I accordingly declare that his explanation be , to my mind , unquestionably a satisfactory one . 

 Miss Halcombe , after look at I very earnestly , say a few word , on her side , to the same effect with a certain hesitation of manner , however , which the circumstance do not seem to I to warrant . "

Mr. Gilmore 's mind be at ease on this unpleasant subject , and your mind be at ease pray set my mind at ease also by write the note . "

"

 " no . "

 he pause for a moment , as if he be think over the last answer , and then add

"

 II 

 we all meet again at dinner - time .

"

 her frank , fearless face answer for she before she speak .

"

 sad ! to remember she , as I do , the lively , happy child that ever laugh the day through , and to see she now , in the flower of her age and her beauty , so broken and so bring down as this !

 a really irresistible man courteous , considerate , delightfully free from pride a gentleman , every inch of he . as I drive away to the station I feel as if I could cheerfully do anything to promote the interest of Sir Percival Glyde anything in the world , except draw the marriage settlement of his wife . 



 III 

 a week pass , after my return to London , without the receipt of any communication from Miss Halcombe . 

 on the eighth day a letter in her handwriting be place among the other letter on my table . 

 it announce that Sir Percival Glyde have be definitely accept , and that the marriage be to take place , as he have originally desire , before the end of the year . in all probability the ceremony would be perform during the last fortnight in December . Miss Fairlie 's twenty - first birthday be late in March . she would , therefore , by this arrangement , become Sir Percival 's wife about three month before she be of age . 

 I ought not to have be surprised , I ought not to have be sorry , but I be surprised and sorry , nevertheless . some little disappointment , cause by the unsatisfactory shortness of Miss Halcombe 's letter , mingle itself with these feeling , and contribute its share towards upset my serenity for the day . in six line my correspondent announce the propose marriage in three more , she tell I that Sir Percival have leave Cumberland to return to his house in Hampshire , and in two conclude sentence she inform I , first , that Laura be sadly in want of change and cheerful society ; secondly , that she have resolve to try the effect of some such change forthwith , by take her sister away with she on a visit to certain old friend in Yorkshire . there the letter end , without a word to explain what the circumstance be which have decide Miss Fairlie to accept Sir Percival Glyde in one short week from the time when I have last see she . 

 at a late period the cause of this sudden determination be fully explain to I . it be not my business to relate it imperfectly , on hearsay evidence . the circumstance come within the personal experience of Miss Halcombe , and when her narrative succeed mine , she will describe they in every particular exactly as they happen . in the meantime , the plain duty for I to perform before I , in my turn , lie down my pen and withdraw from the story be to relate the one remain event connect with Miss Fairlie 's propose marriage in which I be concern , namely , the drawing of the settlement . 

 it be impossible to refer intelligibly to this document without first enter into certain particular in relation to the bride 's pecuniary affair . I will try to make my explanation briefly and plainly , and to keep it free from professional obscurity and technicality . the matter be of the utmost importance . I warn all reader of these line that Miss Fairlie 's inheritance be a very serious part of Miss Fairlie 's story , and that Mr. Gilmore 's experience , in this particular , must be their experience also , if they wish to understand the narrative which be yet to come . 

 Miss Fairlie 's expectation , then , be of a twofold kind , comprise her possible inheritance of real property , or land , when her uncle die , and her absolute inheritance of personal property , or money , when she come of age . 

 let we take the land first . 

 in the time of Miss Fairlie 's paternal grandfather   whom we will call Mr. Fairlie , the eld   the entail succession to the Limmeridge estate stand thus 

 Mr. Fairlie , the elder , die and leave three son , Philip , Frederick , and Arthur . as eld son , Philip succeed to the estate , if he die without leave a son , the property go to the second brother , Frederick ; and if Frederick die also without leave a son , the property go to the third brother , Arthur . 

 as event turn out , Mr. Philip Fairlie die leave an only daughter , the Laura of this story , and the estate , in consequence , go , in course of law , to the second brother , Frederick , a single man . the third brother , Arthur , have die many year before the decease of Philip , leave a son and a daughter . the son , at the age of eighteen , be drown at Oxford . his death leave Laura , the daughter of Mr. Philip Fairlie , presumptive heiress to the estate , with every chance of succeed to it , in the ordinary course of nature , on her uncle Frederick 's death , if the say Frederick die without leave male issue . 

 except in the event , then , of Mr. Frederick Fairlie 's marrying and leave an heir   the two very last thing in the world that he be likely to do , his niece , Laura , would have the property on his death , possess , it must be remember , nothing more than a life - interest in it . if she die single , or die childless , the estate would revert to her cousin , Magdalen , the daughter of Mr. Arthur Fairlie . if she marry , with a proper settlement or , in other word , with the settlement I mean to make for she the income from the estate   a good three thousand a year   would , during her lifetime , be at her own disposal . if she die before her husband , he would naturally expect to be leave in the enjoyment of the income , for his lifetime . if she have a son , that son would be the heir , to the exclusion of her cousin Magdalen . thus , Sir Percival 's prospect in marry Miss Fairlie   so far as his wife 's expectation from real property be concern   promise he these two advantage , on Mr. Frederick Fairlie 's death : first , the use of three thousand a year   by his wife 's permission , while she live , and in his own right , on her death , if he survive she ; and , secondly , the inheritance of Limmeridge for his son , if he have one . 

 so much for the land property , and for the disposal of the income from it , on the occasion of Miss Fairlie 's marriage . thus far , no difficulty or difference of opinion on the lady 's settlement be at all likely to arise between Sir Percival 's lawyer and myself . 

 the personal estate , or , in other word , the money to which Miss Fairlie would become entitled on reach the age of twenty - one year , be the next point to consider . 

 this part of her inheritance be , in itself , a comfortable little fortune . it be derive under her father 's will , and it amount to the sum of twenty thousand pound . besides this , she have a life - interest in ten thousand pound more , which latter amount be to go , on her decease , to her aunt Eleanor , her father 's only sister . it will greatly assist in set the family affair before the reader in the clear possible light , if I stop here for a moment , to explain why the aunt have be keep wait for her legacy until the death of the niece . 

 Mr. Philip Fairlie have live on excellent term with his sister Eleanor , as long as she remain a single woman . but when her marriage take place , somewhat late in life , and when that marriage unite she to an italian gentleman name Fosco , or , rather , to an italian nobleman see that he rejoice in the title of Count Mr. Fairlie disapprove of her conduct so strongly that he cease to hold any communication with she , and even go the length of strike her name out of his will . the other member of the family all think this serious manifestation of resentment at his sister 's marriage more or less unreasonable . Count Fosco , though not a rich man , be not a penniless adventurer either . he have a small but sufficient income of his own . he have live many year in England , and he hold an excellent position in society . these recommendation , however , avail nothing with Mr. Fairlie . in many of his opinion he be an Englishman of the old school , and he hat a foreigner simply and solely because he be a foreigner . the utmost that he could be prevail on to do , in after year mainly at Miss Fairlie 's intercession be to restore his sister 's name to its former place in his will , but to keep she wait for her legacy by give the income of the money to his daughter for life , and the money itself , if her aunt die before she , to her cousin Magdalen . consider the relative age of the two lady , the aunt 's chance , in the ordinary course of nature , of receive the ten thousand pound , be thus render doubtful in the extreme ; and Madame Fosco resent her brother 's treatment of she as unjustly as usual in such case , by refuse to see her niece , and decline to believe that Miss Fairlie 's intercession have ever be exert to restore her name to Mr. Fairlie 's will . 

 such be the history of the ten thousand pound . here again no difficulty could arise with Sir Percival 's legal adviser . the income would be at the wife 's disposal , and the principal would go to her aunt or her cousin on her death . 

 all preliminary explanation be now clear out of the way , I come at last to the real knot of the case to the twenty thousand pound . 

 this sum be absolutely Miss Fairlie 's own on she complete her twenty - first year , and the whole future disposition of it depend , in the first instance , on the condition I could obtain for she in her marriage - settlement . the other clause contain in that document be of a formal kind , and need not be recite here . but the clause relate to the money be too important to be pass over . a few line will be sufficient to give the necessary abstract of it . 

 my stipulation in regard to the twenty thousand pound be simply this : the whole amount be to be settle so as to give the income to the lady for her life afterwards to Sir Percival for his life and the principal to the child of the marriage . in default of issue , the principal be to be dispose of as the lady might by she will direct , for which purpose I reserve to she the right of make a will . the effect of these condition may be thus sum up . if Lady Glyde die without leave child , her half - sister Miss Halcombe , and any other relative or friend whom she might be anxious to benefit , would , on her husband 's death , divide among they such share of her money as she desire they to have . if , on the other hand , she die leave child , then their interest , naturally and necessarily , supersede all other interest whatsoever . this be the clause and no one who read it can fail , I think , to agree with I that it mete out equal justice to all party . 

 we shall see how my proposal be meet on the husband 's side . 

 at the time when Miss Halcombe 's letter reach I I be even more busily occupy than usual . but I contrive to make leisure for the settlement . I have draw it , and have send it for approval to Sir Percival 's solicitor , in less than a week from the time when Miss Halcombe have inform I of the propose marriage . 

 after a lapse of two day the document be return to I , with note and remark of the baronet 's lawyer . his objection , in general , prove to be of the most trifling and technical kind , until he come to the clause relate to the twenty thousand pound . against this there be double line draw in red ink , and the follow note be append to they 

 " not admissible . the principal to go to Sir Percival Glyde , in the event of his survive Lady Glyde , and there be no issue . " 

 that be to say , not one farthing of the twenty thousand pound be to go to Miss Halcombe , or to any other relative or friend of Lady Glyde 's . the whole sum , if she leave no child , be to slip into the pocket of her husband . 

 the answer I write to this audacious proposal be as short and sharp as I could make it . " my dear sir . Miss Fairlie 's settlement . I maintain the clause to which you object , exactly as it stand . your truly . " the rejoinder come back in a quarter of an hour . " my dear sir . Miss Fairlie 's settlement . I maintain the red ink to which you object , exactly as it stand . your truly . " in the detestable slang of the day , we be now both " at a deadlock , " and nothing be leave for it but to refer to our client on either side . 

 as matter stand , my client Miss Fairlie not have yet complete her twenty - first year Mr. Frederick Fairlie , be her guardian . I write by that day 's post , and put the case before he exactly as it stand , not only urge every argument I could think of to induce he to maintain the clause as I have draw it , but state to he plainly the mercenary motive which be at the bottom of the opposition to my settlement of the twenty thousand pound . the knowledge of Sir Percival 's affair which I have necessarily gain when the provision of the deed on his side be submit in due course to my examination , have but too plainly inform I that the debt on his estate be enormous , and that his income , though nominally a large one , be virtually , for a man in his position , next to nothing . the want of ready money be the practical necessity of Sir Percival 's existence , and his lawyer 's note on the clause in the settlement be nothing but the frankly selfish expression of it . 

 Mr. Fairlie 's answer reach I by return of post , and prove to be wander and irrelevant in the extreme . turn into plain English , it practically express itself to this effect : " would dear Gilmore be so very obliging as not to worry his friend and client about such a trifle as a remote contingency ? be it likely that a young woman of twenty - one would die before a man of forty five , and die without child ? on the other hand , in such a miserable world as this , be it possible to over - estimate the value of peace and quietness ? if those two heavenly blessing be offer in exchange for such an earthly trifle as a remote chance of twenty thousand pound , be it not a fair bargain ? surely , yes . then why not make it ? " 

 I throw the letter away in disgust . just as it have flutter to the ground , there be a knock at my door , and Sir Percival 's solicitor , Mr. Merriman , be show in . there be many variety of sharp practitioner in this world , but I think the hard of all to deal with be the man who overreach you under the disguise of inveterate good - humour . a fat , well feed , smile , friendly man of business be of all party to a bargain the most hopeless to deal with . Mr. Merriman be one of this class . 

 " and how be good Mr. Gilmore ? " he begin , all in a glow with the warmth of his own amiability . " glad to see you , sir , in such excellent health . I be pass your door , and I think I would look in in case you might have something to say to I . do now pray do let we settle this little difference of our by word of mouth , if we can ! have you hear from your client yet ? " 

 " yes . have you hear from your ? " 

 " my dear , good sir ! I wish I have hear from he to any purpose I wish , with all my heart , the responsibility be off my shoulder ; but he be obstinate or let I rather say , resolute and he will not   take it off . ' Merriman , I leave detail to you . do what you think right for my interest , and consider I as have personally withdraw from the business until it be all over . ' those be Sir Percival 's word a fortnight ago , and all I can get he to do now be to repeat they . I be not a hard man , Mr. Gilmore , as you know . personally and privately , I do assure you , I should like to sponge out that note of mine at this very moment . but if Sir Percival will not   go into the matter , if Sir Percival will blindly leave all his interest in my sole care , what course can I possibly take except the course of assert they ? my hand be bind do not   you see , my dear sir ? my hand be bind . " 

 " you maintain your note on the clause , then , to the letter ? " I say . 

 " yes deuce take it ! I have no other alternative . " he walk to the fireplace and warm himself , hum the fag end of a tune in a rich convivial bass voice . " what do your side say ? " he go on ; " now pray tell I what do your side say ? " 

 I be ashamed to tell he . I attempt to gain time nay , I do bad . my legal instinct get the well of I , and I even try to bargain . 

 " twenty thousand pound be rather a large sum to be give up by the lady 's friend at two day ' notice , " I say . 

 " very true , " reply Mr. Merriman , look down thoughtfully at his boot . " properly put , sir most properly put ! " 

 " a compromise , recognise the interest of the lady 's family as well as the interest of the husband , might not perhaps have frighten my client quite so much , " I go on . " come , come ! this contingency resolve itself into a matter of bargaining after all . what be the least you will take ? " 

 " the least we will take , " say Mr. Merriman , " be nineteen- thousand - nine - hundred - and - ninety - nine - pound - nineteen - shillings- and - elevenpence - three - farthing . ha ! ha ! ha ! excuse I , Mr. Gilmore . I must have my little joke . " 

 " little enough , " I remark . " the joke be just worth the odd farthe it be make for . " 

 Mr. Merriman be delighted . he laugh over my retort till the room ring again . I be not half so good - humour on my side ; I come back to business , and close the interview . 

 " this be Friday , " I say . " give we till Tuesday next for our final answer . " 

 " by all mean , " reply Mr. Merriman . " long , my dear sir , if you like . " he take up his hat to go , and then address I again . " by the way , " he say , " your client in Cumberland have not hear anything more of the woman who write the anonymous letter , have they ? " 

 " nothing more , " I answer . " have you find no trace of she ? " 

 " not yet , " say my legal friend . " but we do not   despair . Sir Percival have his suspicion that somebody be keep she in hiding , and we be have that somebody watch . " 

 " you mean the old woman who be with she in Cumberland , " I say . 

 " quite another party , sir , " answer Mr. Merriman . " we do not   happen to have lay hand on the old woman yet . our somebody be a man . we have get he close under our eye here in London , and we strongly suspect he have something to do with help she in the first instance to escape from the Asylum . Sir Percival want to question he at once , but I say , ' no . question he will only put he on his guard watch he , and wait . ' we shall see what happen . a dangerous woman to be at large , Mr. Gilmore ; nobody know what she may do next . I wish you good - morning , sir . on Tuesday next I shall hope for the pleasure of hear from you . " he smile amiably and go out . 

 my mind have be rather absent during the latter part of the conversation with my legal friend . I be so anxious about the matter of the settlement that I have little attention to give to any other subject , and the moment I be leave alone again I begin to think over what my next proceeding ought to be . 

 in the case of any other client I should have act on my instruction , however personally distasteful to I , and have give up the point about the twenty thousand pound on the spot . but I could not act with this business - like indifference towards Miss Fairlie . I have an honest feeling of affection and admiration for she I remember gratefully that her father have be the kind patron and friend to I that ever man have I have feel towards she while I be draw the settlement as I might have feel , if I have not be an old bachelor , towards a daughter of my own , and I be determined to spare no personal sacrifice in her service and where her interest be concern . write a second time to Mr. Fairlie be not to be think of it would only be give he a second opportunity of slip through my finger . see he and personally remonstrate with he might possibly be of more use . the next day be Saturday . I determine to take a return ticket and jolt my old bone down to Cumberland , on the chance of persuade he to adopt the just , the independent , and the honourable course . it be a poor chance enough , no doubt , but when I have try it my conscience would be at ease . I should then have do all that a man in my position could do to serve the interest of my old friend 's only child . 

 the weather on Saturday be beautiful , a west wind and a bright sun . have feel latterly a return of that fulness and oppression of the head , against which my doctor warn I so seriously more than two year since , I resolve to take the opportunity of get a little extra exercise by send my bag on before I and walk to the terminus in Euston Square . as I come out into Holborn a gentleman walk by rapidly stop and speak to I . it be Mr. Walter Hartright . 

 if he have not be the first to greet I I should certainly have pass he . he be so changed that I hardly know he again . his face look pale and haggard his manner be hurried and uncertain and his dress , which I remember as neat and gentlemanlike when I see he at Limmeridge , be so slovenly now that I should really have be ashamed of the appearance of it on one of my own clerk . 

 " have you be long back from Cumberland ? " he ask . " I hear from Miss Halcombe lately . I be aware that Sir Percival Glyde 's explanation have be consider satisfactory . will the marriage take place soon ? do you happen to know Mr. Gilmore ? " 

 he speak so fast , and crowd his question together so strangely and confusedly , that I could hardly follow he . however accidentally intimate he might have be with the family at Limmeridge , I could not see that he have any right to expect information on their private affair , and I determine to drop he , as easily as might be , on the subject of Miss Fairlie 's marriage . 

 " Time will show , Mr. Hartright , " I say " time will show . I dare say if we look out for the marriage in the paper we shall not be far wrong . excuse my notice it , but I be sorry to see you not look so well as you be when we last meet . " 

 a momentary nervous contraction quiver about his lip and eye , and make I half reproach myself for have answer he in such a significantly guard manner . 

 " I have no right to ask about her marriage , " he say bitterly . " I must wait to see it in the newspaper like other people . yes , " he go on before I could make any apology " I have not be well lately . I be go to another country to try a change of scene and occupation . Miss Halcombe have kindly assist I with her influence , and my testimonial have be find satisfactory . it be a long distance off , but I do not   care where I go , what the climate be , or how long I be away . " he look about he while he say this at the throng of stranger pass we by on either side , in a strange , suspicious manner , as if he think that some of they might be watch we . 

 " I wish you well through it , and safe back again , " I say , and then add , so as not to keep he altogether at arm 's length on the subject of the Fairlies , " I be go down to Limmeridge to - day on business . Miss Halcombe and Miss Fairlie be away just now on a visit to some friend in Yorkshire . " 

 his eye brighten , and he seem about to say something in answer , but the same momentary nervous spasm cross his face again . he take my hand , press it hard , and disappear among the crowd without say another word . though he be little more than a stranger to I , I wait for a moment , look after he almost with a feeling of regret . I have gain in my profession sufficient experience of young man to know what the outward sign and token be of their beginning to go wrong , and when I resume my walk to the railway I be sorry to say I feel more than doubtful about Mr. Hartright 's future . 



 IV 

 leave by an early train , I get to Limmeridge in time for dinner . the house be oppressively empty and dull . I have expect that good Mrs. Vesey would have be company for I in the absence of the young lady , but she be confine to her room by a cold . the servant be so surprised at see I that they hurry and bustle absurdly , and make all sort of annoying mistake . even the butler , who be old enough to have know well , bring I a bottle of port that be chill . the report of Mr. Fairlie 's health be just as usual , and when I send up a message to announce my arrival , I be tell that he would be delighted to see I the next morning but that the sudden news of my appearance have prostrate he with palpitation for the rest of the evening . the wind howl dismally all night , and strange cracking and groan noise sound here , there , and everywhere in the empty house . I sleep as wretchedly as possible , and get up in a mighty bad humour to breakfast by myself the next morning . 

 at ten o'clock I be conduct to Mr. Fairlie 's apartment . he be in his usual room , his usual chair , and his usual aggravating state of mind and body . when I go in , his valet be stand before he , hold up for inspection a heavy volume of etching , as long and as broad as my office writing - desk . the miserable foreigner grin in the most abject manner , and look ready to drop with fatigue , while his master composedly turn over the etching , and bring their hide beauty to light with the help of a magnifying glass . 

 " you very well of good old friend , " say Mr. Fairlie , lean back lazily before he could look at I , " be you quite well ? how nice of you to come here and see I in my solitude . Dear Gilmore ! " 

 I have expect that the valet would be dismiss when I appear , but nothing of the sort happen . there he stand , in front of his master 's chair , tremble under the weight of the etching , and there Mr. Fairlie sit , serenely twirl the magnifying glass between his white finger and thumb . 

 " I have come to speak to you on a very important matter , " I say , " and you will therefore excuse I , if I suggest that we have well be alone . " 

 the unfortunate valet look at I gratefully . Mr. Fairlie faintly repeat my last three word , " well be alone , " with every appearance of the utmost possible astonishment . 

 I be in no humour for trifling , and I resolve to make he understand what I mean . 

 " Oblige I by give that man permission to withdraw , " I say , point to the valet . 

 Mr. Fairlie arch his eyebrow and purse up his lip in sarcastic surprise . 

 " Man ? " he repeat . " you provoke old Gilmore , what can you possibly mean by call he a man ? he be nothing of the sort . he might have be a man half an hour ago , before I want my etching , and he may be a man half an hour hence , when I do not   want they any long . at present he be simply a portfolio stand . why object , Gilmore , to a portfolio stand ? " 

 " I do object . for the third time , Mr. Fairlie , I beg that we may be alone . " 

 my tone and manner leave he no alternative but to comply with my request . he look at the servant , and point peevishly to a chair at his side . 

 " put down the etching and go away , " he say . " do not   upset I by lose my place . have you , or have you not , lose my place ? be you sure you have not ? and have you put my hand - bell quite within my reach ? yes ? then why the devil do not   you go ? " 

 the valet go out . Mr. Fairlie twist himself round in his chair , polish the magnifying glass with his delicate cambric handkerchief , and indulge himself with a sidelong inspection of the open volume of etching . it be not easy to keep my temper under these circumstance , but I do keep it . 

 " I have come here at great personal inconvenience , " I say , " to serve the interest of your niece and your family , and I think I have establish some slight claim to be favour with your attention in return . " 

 " do not   bully I ! " exclaim Mr. Fairlie , fall back helplessly in the chair , and close his eye . " please do not   bully I . I be not strong enough . " 

 I be determined not to let he provoke I , for Laura Fairlie 's sake . 

 " my object , " I go on , " be to entreat you to reconsider your letter , and not to force I to abandon the just right of your niece , and of all who belong to she . let I state the case to you once more , and for the last time . " 

 Mr. Fairlie shake his head and sigh piteously . 

 " this be heartless of you , Gilmore very heartless , " he say . " never mind , go on . " 

 I put all the point to he carefully I set the matter before he in every conceivable light . he lie back in the chair the whole time I be speak with his eye close . when I have do he open they indolently , take his silver smell - bottle from the table , and sniff at it with an air of gentle relish . 

 " Good Gilmore ! " he say between the sniff , " how very nice this be of you ! how you reconcile one to human nature ! " 

 " give I a plain answer to a plain question , Mr. Fairlie . I tell you again , Sir Percival Glyde have no shadow of a claim to expect more than the income of the money . the money itself if your niece have no child , ought to be under her control , and to return to her family . if you stand firm , Sir Percival must give way he must give way , I tell you , or he expose himself to the base imputation of marry Miss Fairlie entirely from mercenary motif . " 

 Mr. Fairlie shake the silver smell - bottle at I playfully . 

 " you dear old Gilmore , how you do hate rank and family , do not   you ? how you detest Glyde because he happen to be a baronet . what a Radical you be oh , dear I , what a Radical you be ! " 

 a Radical ! ! ! I could put up with a good deal of provocation , but , after hold the sound conservative principle all my life , I could not put up with be call a Radical . my blood boil at it I start out of my chair I be speechless with Indignation . 

 " do not   shake the room ! " cry Mr. Fairlie " for Heaven 's sake do not   shake the room ! Worthiest of all possible gilmore , I mean no offence . my own view be so extremely liberal that I think I be a Radical myself . yes . we be a pair of Radicals . please do not   be angry . I can not   quarrel I have not   stamina enough . shall we drop the subject ? yes . come and look at these sweet etching . do let I teach you to understand the heavenly pearliness of these line . do now , there be a good Gilmore ! " 

 while he be maunder on in this way I be , fortunately for my own self - respect , return to my sens . when I speak again I be compose enough to treat his impertinence with the silent contempt that it deserve . 

 " you be entirely wrong , sir , " I say , " in suppose that I speak from any prejudice against Sir Percival Glyde . I may regret that he have so unreservedly resign himself in this matter to his lawyer 's direction as to make any appeal to himself impossible , but I be not prejudiced against he . what I have say would equally apply to any other man in his situation , high or low . the principle I maintain be a recognise principle . if you be to apply at the near town here , to the first respectable solicitor you could find , he would tell you as a stranger what I tell you as a friend . he would inform you that it be against all rule to abandon the lady 's money entirely to the man she marry . he would decline , on ground of common legal caution , to give the husband , under any circumstance whatever , an interest of twenty thousand pound in his wife 's death . " 

 " would he really , Gilmore ? " say Mr. Fairlie . " if he say anything half so horrid , I do assure you I should tinkle my bell for Louis , and have he send out of the house immediately . " 

 " you shall not irritate I , Mr. Fairlie for your niece 's sake and for her father 's sake , you shall not irritate I . you shall take the whole responsibility of this discreditable settlement on your own shoulder before I leave the room . " 

 " do not ! now please do not ! " say Mr. Fairlie . " think how precious your time be , Gilmore , and do not   throw it away . I would dispute with you if I could , but I can not   I have not   stamina enough . you want to upset I , to upset yourself , to upset Glyde , and to upset Laura ; and oh , dear I ! all for the sake of the very last thing in the world that be likely to happen . no , dear friend , in the interest of peace and quietness , positively no ! " 

 " I be to understand , then , that you hold by the determination express in your letter ? " 

 " yes , please . so glad we understand each other at last . sit down again do ! " 

 I walk at once to the door , and Mr. Fairlie resignedly " tinkle " his hand - bell . before I leave the room I turn round and address he for the last time . 

 " whatever happen in the future , sir , " I say , " remember that my plain duty of warning you have be perform . as the faithful friend and servant of your family , I tell you , at parting , that no daughter of mine should be married to any man alive under such a settlement as you be force I to make for Miss Fairlie . " 

 the door open behind I , and the valet stand wait on the threshold . 

 " Louis , " say Mr. Fairlie , ' show Mr. Gilmore out , and then come back and hold up my etching for I again . make they give you a good lunch downstairs . do , Gilmore , make my idle beast of servant give you a good lunch ! " 

 I be too much disgusted to reply I turn on my heel , and leave he in silence . there be an up train at two o'clock in the afternoon , and by that train I return to London . 

 on the Tuesday I send in the altered settlement , which practically disinherit the very person whom Miss Fairlie 's own lip have inform I she be most anxious to benefit . I have no choice . another lawyer would have draw up the deed if I have refuse to undertake it . 


 my task be do . my personal share in the event of the family story extend no far than the point which I have just reach . other pen than mine will describe the strange circumstance which be now shortly to follow . seriously and sorrowfully I close this brief record . seriously and sorrowfully I repeat here the parting word that I speak at Limmeridge House : no daughter of mine should have be marry to any man alive under such a settlement as I be compel to make for Laura Fairlie . 


 the end of Mr. Gilmore 's Narrative . 




 the story continue BY MARIAN halcombe 

  in extract from her Diary 



 LIMMERIDGE HOUSE , Nov. 8 . 1 

  1   the passage omit , here and elsewhere , in Miss Halcombe 's Diary be only those which bear no reference to Miss Fairlie or to any of the person with whom she be associate in these page . 


 this morning Mr. Gilmore leave we . 

 his interview with Laura have evidently grieve and surprise he more than he like to confess . I feel afraid , from his look and manner when we part , that she might have inadvertently betray to he the real secret of her depression and my anxiety . this doubt grow on I so , after he have go , that I decline rid out with Sir Percival , and go up to Laura 's room instead . 

 I have be sadly distrustful of myself , in this difficult and lamentable matter , ever since I find out my own ignorance of the strength of Laura 's unhappy attachment . I ought to have know that the delicacy and forbearance and sense of honour which draw I to poor Hartright , and make I so sincerely admire and respect he , be just the quality to appeal most irresistibly to Laura 's natural sensitiveness and natural generosity of nature . and yet , until she open her heart to I of her own accord , I have no suspicion that this new feeling have take root so deeply . I once think time and care might remove it . I now fear that it will remain with she and alter she for life . the discovery that I have commit such an error in judgment as this make I hesitate about everything else . I hesitate about Sir Percival , in the face of the plainest proof . I hesitate even in speak to Laura . on this very morning I doubt , with my hand on the door , whether I should ask she the question I have come to put , or not . 

 when I go into her room I find she walk up and down in great impatience . she look flushed and excited , and she come forward at once , and speak to I before I could open my lip . 

 " I want you , " she say . " come and sit down on the sofa with I . Marian ! I can bear this no long I must and will end it . " 

 there be too much colour in her cheek , too much energy in her manner , too much firmness in her voice . the little book of Hartright 's drawing the fatal book that she will dream over whenever she be alone be in one of her hand . I begin by gently and firmly take it from she , and put it out of sight on a side - table . 

 " tell I quietly , my darling , what you wish to do , " I say . " have Mr. Gilmore be advise you ? " 

 she shake her head . " no , not in what I be think of now . he be very kind and good to I , Marian , and I be ashamed to say I distress he by cry . I be miserably helpless I can not   control myself . for my own sake , and for all our sake , I must have courage enough to end it . " 

 " do you mean courage enough to claim your release ? " I ask . 

 " no , " she say simply . " Courage , dear , to tell the truth . " 

 she put her arm round my neck , and rest her head quietly on my bosom . on the opposite wall hang the miniature portrait of her father . I bent over she , and see that she be look at it while her head lie on my breast . 

 " I can never claim my release from my engagement , " she go on . " whatever way it end it must end wretchedly for I . all I can do , Marian , be not to add the remembrance that I have break my promise and forget my father 's die word , to make that wretchedness bad . " 

 " what be it you propose , then ? " I ask . 

 " to tell Sir Percival Glyde the truth with my own lip , " she answer , " and to let he release I , if he will , not because I ask he , but because he know all . " 

 " what do you mean , Laura , by ' all ' ? Sir Percival will know enough   he have tell I so himself   if he know that the engagement be oppose to your own wish . " 

 " can I tell he that , when the engagement be make for I by my father , with my own consent ? I should have keep my promise , not happily , I be afraid , but still contentedly " she stop , turn her face to I , and lay her cheek close against mine " I should have keep my engagement , Marian , if another love have not grow up in my heart , which be not there when I first promise to be Sir Percival 's wife . " 

 " Laura ! you will never lower yourself by make a confession to he ? " 

 " I shall lower myself , indeed , if I gain my release by hide from he what he have a right to know . " 

 " he have not the shadow of a right to know it ! " 

 " wrong , Marian , wrong ! I ought to deceive no one least of all the man to whom my father give I , and to whom I give myself . " she put her lip to mine , and kiss I . " my own love , " she say softly , " you be so much too fond of I , and so much too proud of I , that you forget , in my case , what you would remember in your own . well that Sir Percival should doubt my motif , and misjudge my conduct if he will , than that I should be first false to he in thought , and then mean enough to serve my own interest by hide the falsehood . "

I look into the pale , quiet , resign young face I see the pure , innocent heart , in the love eye that look back at I and the poor worldly caution and objection that rise to my lip dwindle and die away in their own emptiness . I hang my head in silence . in her place the despicably small pride which make so many woman deceitful would have be my pride , and would have make I deceitful too . 

 " do not   be angry with I , Marian , " she say , mistake my silence .
