" 

 she sigh , and put her head back in its old position on my bosom . sad misgiving about what the end would be weigh upon my mind , but still distrust myself , I tell she that I would do as she wish . she thank I , and we pass gradually into talking of other thing . 

 at dinner she join we again , and be more easy and more herself with Sir Percival than I have see she yet . in the evening she go to the piano , choose new music of the dexterous , tuneless , florid kind . the lovely old melody of Mozart , which poor Hartright be so fond of , she have never play since he leave . the book be no long in the music - stand . she take the volume away herself , so that nobody might find it out and ask she to play from it . 

 I have no opportunity of discover whether her purpose of the morning have change or not , until she wish Sir Percival good - night and then her own word inform I that it be unaltered . she say , very quietly , that she wish to speak to he after breakfast , and that he would find she in her sitting - room with I . he change colour at those word , and I feel his hand tremble a little when it come to my turn to take it . the event of the next morning would decide his future life , and he evidently know it . 

 I go in , as usual , through the door between our two bedroom , to bid Laura good - night before she go to sleep . in stoop over she to kiss she I see the little book of Hartright 's drawing half hide under her pillow , just in the place where she use to hide her favourite toy when she be a child . I could not find it in my heart to say anything , but I point to the book and shake my head . she reach both hand up to my cheek , and draw my face down to hers till our lip meet . 

 " leave it there to - night , " she whisper ; " to - morrow may be cruel , and may make I say good - bye to it for ever . " 


 9th . the first event of the morning be not of a kind to raise my spirit a letter arrive for I from poor Walter Hartright . it be the answer to mine describe the manner in which Sir Percival clear himself of the suspicion raise by Anne Catherick 's letter . he write shortly and bitterly about Sir Percival 's explanation , only say that he have no right to offer an opinion on the conduct of those who be above he . this be sad , but his occasional reference to himself grieve I still more . he say that the effort to return to his old habit and pursuit grow hard instead of easy to he every day and he implore I , if I have any interest , to exert it to get he employment that will necessitate his absence from England , and take he among new scene and new people . I have be make all the readier to comply with this request by a passage at the end of his letter , which have almost alarm I . 

 after mention that he have neither see nor hear anything of Anne Catherick , he suddenly break off , and hint in the most abrupt , mysterious manner , that he have be perpetually watch and follow by strange man ever since he return to London . he acknowledge that he can not prove this extraordinary suspicion by fix on any particular person , but he declare that the suspicion itself be present to he night and day . this have frighten I , because it look as if his one fix idea about Laura be become too much for his mind . I will write immediately to some of my mother 's influential old friend in London , and press his claim on their notice . change of scene and change of occupation may really be the salvation of he at this crisis in his life . 

 greatly to my relief , Sir Percival send an apology for not join we at breakfast . he have take an early cup of coffee in his own room , and he be still engage there in write letter . at eleven o'clock , if that hour be convenient , he would do himself the honour of wait on Miss Fairlie and Miss Halcombe . 

 my eye be on Laura 's face while the message be be deliver . I have find she unaccountably quiet and compose on go into her room in the morning , and so she remain all through breakfast . even when we be sit together on the sofa in her room , wait for Sir Percival , she still preserve her self - control . 

 " do not   be afraid of I , Marian , " be all she say ; " I may forget myself with an old friend like Mr. Gilmore , or with a dear sister like you , but I will not forget myself with Sir Percival Glyde . " 

 I look at she , and listen to she in silent surprise . through all the year of our close intimacy this passive force in her character have be hide from I hide even from herself , till love find it , and suffering call it forth . 

 as the clock on the mantelpiece strike eleven Sir Percival knock at the door and come in . there be suppress anxiety and agitation in every line of his face . the dry , sharp cough , which tease he at most time , seem to be trouble he more incessantly than ever . he sit down opposite to we at the table , and Laura remain by I . I look attentively at they both , and he be the pale of the two . 

 he say a few unimportant word , with a visible effort to preserve his customary ease of manner . but his voice be not to be steady , and the restless uneasiness in his eye be not to be conceal . he must have feel this himself , for he stop in the middle of a sentence , and give up even the attempt to hide his embarrassment any long . 

 there be just one moment of dead silence before Laura address he . 

 " I wish to speak to you , Sir Percival , " she say , " on a subject that be very important to we both . my sister be here , because her presence help I and give I confidence . she have not suggest one word of what I be go to say I speak from my own thought , not from her . I be sure you will be kind enough to understand that before I go any far ? " 

 Sir Percival bow . she have proceed thus far , with perfect outward tranquillity and perfect propriety of manner . she look at he , and he look at she . they seem , at the outset , at least , resolve to understand one another plainly . 

 " I have hear from Marian , " she go on , " that I have only to claim my release from our engagement to obtain that release from you . it be forbear and generous on your part , Sir Percival , to send I such a message . it be only do you justice to say that I be grateful for the offer , and I hope and believe that it be only do myself justice to tell you that I decline to accept it . " 

 his attentive face relax a little . but I see one of his foot , softly , quietly , incessantly beat on the carpet under the table , and I feel that he be secretly as anxious as ever . 

 " I have not forget , " she say , " that you ask my father 's permission before you honour I with a proposal of marriage . perhaps you have not forget either what I say when I consent to our engagement ? I venture to tell you that my father 's influence and advice have mainly decide I to give you my promise . I be guide by my father , because I have always find he the truest of all adviser , the good and fondest of all protector and friend . I have lose he now I have only his memory to love , but my faith in that dear dead friend have never be shake . I believe at this moment , as truly as I ever believe , that he know what be good , and that his hope and wish ought to be my hope and wish too . " 

 her voice tremble for the first time . her restless finger steal their way into my lap , and hold fast by one of my hand . there be another moment of silence , and then Sir Percival speak . 

 " may I ask , " he say , " if I have ever prove myself unworthy of the trust which it have be hitherto my great honour and great happiness to possess ? "

" you have always treat I with the same delicacy and the same forbearance . you have deserve my trust , and , what be of far more importance in my estimation , you have deserve my father 's trust , out of which mine grow . you have give I no excuse , even if I have want to find one , for ask to be release from my pledge . what I have say so far have be speak with the wish to acknowledge my whole obligation to you . my regard for that obligation , my regard for my father 's memory , and my regard for my own promise , all forbid I to set the example , on my side , of withdraw from our present position . the breaking of our engagement must be entirely your wish and your act , Sir Percival not mine . " 

 the uneasy beating of his foot suddenly stop , and he lean forward eagerly across the table .

 his face turn so pale again that even his lip lose their colour . he raise the arm which lie on the table , turn a little away in his chair , and support his head on his hand , so that his profile only be present to we .

 " I have hear , " she say , " and I believe it , that the fond and truest of all affection be the affection which a woman ought to bear to her husband . when our engagement begin that affection be mine to give , if I could , and your to win , if you could . will you pardon I , and spare I , Sir Percival , if I acknowledge that it be not so any long ?  I be determined to make he declare himself , for Laura 's sake . 

 " Sir Percival ! " I interpose sharply , " have you nothing to say when my sister have say so much ? More , in my opinion , " I add , my unlucky temper get the well of I , " than any man alive , in your position , have a right to hear from she . " 

 that last rash sentence open a way for he by which to escape I if he choose , and he instantly take advantage of it .

I have dread this from the first . I would have prevent it , if she have allow I the small chance of do so .  her left hand still hold mine , but her right hand hang listlessly at her side . he raise it gently to his lip touch it with they , rather than kiss it bow to I and then , with perfect delicacy and discretion , silently quit the room .

" while I be alive , if he ask after I always tell he I be well , and never say I be unhappy . do not   distress he , Marian , for my sake , do not   distress he . if I die first , promise you will give he this little book of his drawing , with my hair in it . there can be no harm , when I be go , in tell he that I put it there with my own hand . and say oh , Marian , say for I , then , what I can never say for myself say I love he ! "

 10th . find that she be compose and like herself this morning , I return to the painful subject of yesterday , for the sole purpose of implore she to let I speak to Sir Percival and Mr. Fairlie , more plainly and strongly than she could speak to either of they herself , about this lamentable marriage . she interpose , gently but firmly , in the middle of my remonstrance . 

 " I leave yesterday to decide , " she say ; " and yesterday have decide . it be too late to go back . " 

 Sir Percival speak to I this afternoon about what have pass in Laura 's room . he assure I that the unparalleled trust she have place in he have awaken such an answer conviction of her innocence and integrity in his mind , that he be guiltless of have feel even a moment 's unworthy jealousy , either at the time when he be in her presence , or afterwards when he have withdraw from it . deeply as he lament the unfortunate attachment which have hinder the progress he might otherwise have make in her esteem and regard , he firmly believe that it have remain unacknowledged in the past , and that it would remain , under all change of circumstance which it be possible to contemplate , unacknowledge in the future . this be his absolute conviction ; and the strong proof he could give of it be the assurance , which he now offer , that he feel no curiosity to know whether the attachment be of recent date or not , or who have be the object of it . his implicit confidence in Miss Fairlie make he satisfied with what she have think fit to say to he , and he be honestly innocent of the slight feeling of anxiety to hear more . 

 he wait after say those word and look at I . I be so conscious of my unreasonable prejudice against he so conscious of an unworthy suspicion that he might be speculate on my impulsively answer the very question which he have just describe himself as resolve not to ask that I evade all reference to this part of the subject with something like a feeling of confusion on my own part . at the same time I be resolve not to lose even the small opportunity of try to plead Laura 's cause , and I tell he boldly that I regret his generosity have not carry he one step far , and induce he to withdraw from the engagement altogether . 

 here , again , he disarm I by not attempt to defend himself . he would merely beg I to remember the difference there be between his allow Miss Fairlie to give he up , which be a matter of submission only , and his force himself to give up Miss Fairlie , which be , in other word , ask he to be the suicide of his own hope . her conduct of the day before have so strengthen the unchangeable love and admiration of two long year , that all active contention against those feeling , on his part , be henceforth entirely out of his power . I must think he weak , selfish , unfeele towards the very woman whom he idolise , and he must bow to my opinion as resignedly as he could only put it to I , at the same time , whether her future as a single woman , pin under an unhappily place attachment which she could never acknowledge , could be say to promise she a much bright prospect than her future as the wife of a man who worship the very ground she walk on ? in the last case there be hope from time , however slight it might be in the first case , on her own showing , there be no hope at all . 

 I answer he more because my tongue be a woman 's , and must answer , than because I have anything convincing to say . it be only too plain that the course Laura have adopt the day before have offer he the advantage if he choose to take it and that he have choose to take it . I feel this at the time , and I feel it just as strongly now , while I write these line , in my own room . the one hope leave be that his motif really spring , as he say they do , from the irresistible strength of his attachment to Laura . 

 before I close my diary for to - night I must record that I write to - day , in poor Hartright 's interest , to two of my mother 's old friend in London both man of influence and position . if they can do anything for he , I be quite sure they will . except Laura , I never be more anxious about any one than I be now about Walter . all that have happen since he leave we have only increase my strong regard and sympathy for he . I hope I be do right in try to help he to employment abroad I hope , most earnestly and anxiously , that it will end well . 


 11th . Sir Percival have an interview with Mr. Fairlie , and I be send for to join they . 

 I find Mr. Fairlie greatly relieve at the prospect of the " family worry "   as he be pleased to describe his niece 's marriage   be settle at last . so far , I do not feel call on to say anything to he about my own opinion , but when he proceed , in his most aggravatingly languid manner , to suggest that the time for the marriage have well be settle next , in accordance with Sir Percival 's wish , I enjoy the satisfaction of assail Mr. Fairlie 's nerve with as strong a protest against hurry Laura 's decision as I could put into word . Sir Percival immediately assure I that he feel the force of my objection , and beg I to believe that the proposal have not be make in consequence of any interference on his part . Mr. Fairlie lean back in his chair , close his eye , say we both of we do honour to human nature , and then repeat his suggestion as coolly as if neither Sir Percival nor I have say a word in opposition to it . it end in my flatly decline to mention the subject to Laura , unless she first approach it of her own accord . I leave the room at once after make that declaration . Sir Percival look seriously embarrassed and distressed , Mr. Fairlie stretch out his lazy leg on his velvet footstool , and say , " Dear Marian ! how I envy you your robust nervous system ! do not   bang the door ! " 

 on go to Laura 's room I find that she have ask for I , and that Mrs. Vesey have inform she that I be with Mr. Fairlie . she inquire at once what I have be want for , and I tell she all that have pass , without attempt to conceal the vexation and annoyance that I really feel . her answer surprise and distress I inexpressibly it be the very last reply that I should have expect she to make . 

 " my uncle be right , " she say . " I have cause trouble and anxiety enough to you , and to all about I . let I cause no more , Marian let Sir Percival decide . " 

 I remonstrate warmly , but nothing that I could say move she .

 she use to be pliability itself , but she be now inflexibly passive in her resignation I might almost say in her despair . dearly as I love she , I should have be less pained if she have be violently agitate it be so shockingly unlike her natural character to see she as cold and insensible as I see she now . 


 12th . Sir Percival put some question to I at breakfast about Laura , which leave I no choice but to tell he what she have say . 

 while we be talk she herself come down and join we . she be just as unnaturally compose in Sir Percival 's presence as she have be in mine . when breakfast be over he have an opportunity of say a few word to she privately , in a recess of one of the window . they be not more than two or three minute together , and on their separating she leave the room with Mrs. Vesey , while Sir Percival come to I . he say he have entreat she to favour he by maintain her privilege of fix the time for the marriage at her own will and pleasure . in reply she have merely express her acknowledgment , and have desire he to mention what his wish be to Miss Halcombe . 

 I have no patience to write more . in this instance , as in every other , Sir Percival have carry his point with the utmost possible credit to himself , in spite of everything that I can say or do . his wish be now , what they be , of course , when he first come here ; and Laura have resign herself to the one inevitable sacrifice of the marriage , remain as coldly hopeless and enduring as ever . in part with the little occupation and relic that remind she of Hartright , she seem to have part with all her tenderness and all her impressibility . it be only three o'clock in the afternoon while I write these line , and Sir Percival have leave we already , in the happy hurry of a bridegroom , to prepare for the bride 's reception at his house in Hampshire . unless some extraordinary event happen to prevent it they will be marry exactly at the time when he wish to be marry before the end of the year . my very finger burn as I write it ! 


 13th . a sleepless night , through uneasiness about Laura . towards the morning I come to a resolution to try what change of scene would do to rouse she . she can not surely remain in her present torpor of insensibility , if I take she away from Limmeridge and surround she with the pleasant face of old friend ? after some consideration I decide on write to the Arnolds , in Yorkshire . they be simple , kind - hearted , hospitable people , and she have know they from her childhood . when I have put the letter in the post - bag I tell she what I have do . it would have be a relief to I if she have show the spirit to resist and object . but no she only say , " I will go anywhere with you , Marian . I dare say you be right I dare say the change will do I good . " 


 14th . I write to Mr. Gilmore , inform he that there be really a prospect of this miserable marriage taking place , and also mention my idea of try what change of scene would do for Laura . I have no heart to go into particular . time enough for they when we get near to the end of the year . 


 15th . three letter for I . the first , from the Arnolds , full of delight at the prospect of see Laura and I . the second , from one of the gentleman to whom I write on Walter Hartright 's behalf , inform I that he have be fortunate enough to find an opportunity of comply with my request . the third , from Walter himself , thank I , poor fellow , in the warm term , for give he an opportunity of leave his home , his country , and his friend . a private expedition to make excavation among the ruin city of Central America be , it seem , about to sail from Liverpool . the draughtsman who have be already appoint to accompany it have lose heart , and withdraw at the eleventh hour , and Walter be to fill his place . he be to be engage for six month certain , from the time of the landing in Honduras , and for a year afterwards , if the excavation be successful , and if the fund hold out . his letter end with a promise to write I a farewell line when they be all on board ship , and when the pilot leave they . I can only hope and pray earnestly that he and I be both act in this matter for the good . it seem such a serious step for he to take , that the mere contemplation of it startle I . and yet , in his unhappy position , how can I expect he or wish he to remain at home ? 


 16th . the carriage be at the door . Laura and I set out on our visit to the Arnolds to - day . 



 POLESDEAN LODGE , YORKSHIRE . 

 23rd . a week in these new scene and among these kind - hearted people have do she some good , though not so much as I have hop . I have resolve to prolong our stay for another week at least . it be useless to go back to Limmeridge till there be an absolute necessity for our return . 


 24th . sad news by this morning 's post . the expedition to Central America sail on the twenty - first . we have part with a true man we have lose a faithful friend . Water Hartright have leave England . 


 25th . sad news yesterday ominous news to - day . Sir Percival Glyde have write to Mr. Fairlie , and Mr. Fairlie have write to Laura and I , to recall we to Limmeridge immediately . 

 what can this mean ? have the day for the marriage be fix in our absence ? 



 II 


 LIMMERIDGE HOUSE . 

 November 27th . my foreboding be realise . the marriage be fix for the twenty - second of December . 

 the day after we leave for Polesdean Lodge Sir Percival write , it seem , to Mr. Fairlie , to say that the necessary repair and alteration in his house in Hampshire would occupy a much long time in completion than he have originally anticipate . the proper estimate be to be submit to he as soon as possible , and it would greatly facilitate his enter into definite arrangement with the workpeople , if he could be inform of the exact period at which the wedding ceremony might be expect to take place . he could then make all his calculation in reference to time , besides write the necessary apology to friend who have be engage to visit he that winter , and who could not , of course , be receive when the house be in the hand of the workman . 

 to this letter Mr. Fairlie have reply by request Sir Percival himself to suggest a day for the marriage , subject to Miss Fairlie 's approval , which her guardian willingly undertake to do his good to obtain . Sir Percival write back by the next post , and propose   in accordance with his own view and wish from the first ?   the latter part of December perhaps the twenty - second , or twenty - fourth , or any other day that the lady and her guardian might prefer . the lady not be at hand to speak for herself , her guardian have decide , in her absence , on the early day mention the twenty - second of December , and have write to recall we to Limmeridge in consequence . 

 after explain these particular to I at a private interview yesterday , Mr. Fairlie suggest , in his most amiable manner , that I should open the necessary negotiation to - day . feel that resistance be useless , unless I could first obtain Laura 's authority to make it , I consent to speak to she , but declare , at the same time , that I would on no consideration undertake to gain her consent to Sir Percival 's wish . Mr. Fairlie compliment I on my " excellent conscience , " much as he would have compliment I , if he have be out walk , on my " excellent constitution , " and seem perfectly satisfied , so far , with have simply shift one more family responsibility from his own shoulder to mine . 

 this morning I speak to Laura as I have promise . the composure I may almost say , the insensibility which she have so strangely and so resolutely maintain ever since Sir Percival leave we , be not proof against the shock of the news I have to tell she . she turn pale and tremble violently . 

 " not so soon ! " she plead . " oh , Marian , not so soon ! " 

 the slight hint she could give be enough for I . I rise to leave the room , and fight her battle for she at once with Mr. Fairlie . 

 just as my hand be on the door , she catch fast hold of my dress and stop I . 

 " let I go ! " I say . " my tongue burn to tell your uncle that he and Sir Percival be not to have it all their own way . " 

 she sigh bitterly , and still hold my dress . 

 " no ! " she say faintly . " too late , Marian , too late ! " 

 " not a minute too late , " I retort . " the question of time be our question and trust I , Laura , to take a woman 's full advantage of it . " 

 I unclasp her hand from my gown while I speak ; but she slip both her arm round my waist at the same moment , and hold I more effectually than ever . 

 " it will only involve we in more trouble and more confusion , " she say . " it will set you and my uncle at variance , and bring Sir Percival here again with fresh cause of complaint " 

 " so much the well ! " I cry out passionately . " who care for his cause of complaint ? be you to break your heart to set his mind at ease ? no man under heaven deserve these sacrifice from we woman . man ! they be the enemy of our innocence and our peace they drag we away from our parent ' love and our sister ' friendship they take we body and soul to themselves , and fasten our helpless life to theirs as they chain up a dog to his kennel . and what do the good of they give we in return ? let I go , Laura I be mad when I think of it ! " 

 the tear miserable , weak , woman 's tear of vexation and rage start to my eye . she smile sadly , and put she handkerchief over my face to hide for I the betrayal of my own weakness the weakness of all other which she know that I most despise . 

 " oh , Marian ! " she say . " you cry ! think what you would say to I , if the place be change , and if those tear be mine . all your love and courage and devotion will not alter what must happen , soon or later . let my uncle have his way . let we have no more trouble and heart - burning that any sacrifice of mine can prevent . say you will live with I , Marian , when I be married and say no more . " 

 but I do say more . I force back the contemptible tear that be no relief to I , and that only distress she , and reason and plead as calmly as I could . it be of no avail . she make I twice repeat the promise to live with she when she be marry , and then suddenly ask a question which turn my sorrow and my sympathy for she into a new direction . 

 " while we be at Polesdean , " she say , " you have a letter , marian " 

 her altered tone the abrupt manner in which she look away from I and hide her face on my shoulder the hesitation which silence she before she have complete her question , all tell I , but too plainly , to whom the half - express inquiry point . 

 " I think , Laura , that you and I be never to refer to he again , " I say gently . 

 " you have a letter from he ? " she persist . 

 " yes , " I reply , " if you must know it . " 

 " do you mean to write to he again ? " 

 I hesitate . I have be afraid to tell she of his absence from England , or of the manner in which my exertion to serve his new hope and project have connect I with his departure . what answer could I make ? he be go where no letter could reach he for month , perhaps for year , to come . 

 " suppose I do mean to write to he again , " I say at last . " what then , Laura ? " 

 her cheek grow burn hot against my neck , and her arm tremble and tighten round I . 

 " do not   tell he about the TWENTY - second , " she whisper . " promise , marian pray promise you will not even mention my name to he when you write next . " 

 I give the promise . no word can say how sorrowfully I give it . she instantly take her arm from my waist , walk away to the window , and stand look out with she back to I . after a moment she speak once more , but without turn round , without allow I to catch the small glimpse of her face . 

 " be you go to my uncle 's room ? " she ask . " will you say that I consent to whatever arrangement he may think well ? never mind leave I , Marian . I shall be well alone for a little while . " 

 I go out . if , as soon as I get into the passage , I could have transport Mr. Fairlie and Sir Percival Glyde to the uttermost end of the earth by lift one of my finger , that finger would have be raise without an instant 's hesitation . for once my unhappy temper now stand my friend . I should have break down altogether and burst into a violent fit of cry , if my tear have not be all burn up in the heat of my anger . as it be , I dash into Mr. Fairlie 's room call to he as harshly as possible , " Laura consent to the twenty - second " and dash out again without wait for a word of answer . I bang the door after I , and I hope I shatter Mr. Fairlie 's nervous system for the rest of the day . 


 28th . this morning I read poor Hartright 's farewell letter over again , a doubt have cross my mind since yesterday , whether I be act wisely in conceal the fact of his departure from Laura . 

 on reflection , I still think I be right . the allusion in his letter to the preparation make for the expedition to Central America , all show that the leader of it know it to be dangerous . if the discovery of this make I uneasy , what would it make she ? it be bad enough to feel that his departure have deprive we of the friend of all other to whose devotion we could trust in the hour of need , if ever that hour come and find we helpless ; but it be far bad to know that he have go from we to face the peril of a bad climate , a wild country , and a disturbed population . surely it would be a cruel candour to tell Laura this , without a pressing and a positive necessity for it ? 

 I almost doubt whether I ought not to go a step far , and burn the letter at once , for fear of its one day fall into wrong hand . it not only refer to Laura in term which ought to remain a secret for ever between the writer and I , but it reiterate his suspicion so obstinate , so unaccountable , and so alarming that he have be secretly watch since he leave Limmeridge . he declare that he see the face of the two strange man who follow he about the street of London , watch he among the crowd which gather at Liverpool to see the expedition embark , and he positively assert that he hear the name of Anne Catherick pronounce behind he as he get into the boat . his own word be , " these event have a meaning , these event must lead to a result . the mystery of Anne Catherick be not clear up yet . she may never cross my path again , but if ever she cross your , make well use of the opportunity , Miss Halcombe , than I make of it . I speak on strong conviction I entreat you to remember what I say . " these be his own expression . there be no danger of my forget they my memory be only too ready to dwell on any word of Hartright 's that refer to Anne Catherick . but there be danger in my keep the letter . the mere accident might place it at the mercy of stranger . I may fall ill I may die . well to burn it at once , and have one anxiety the less . 

 it be burn . the ash of his farewell letter the last he may ever write to I lie in a few black fragment on the hearth . be this the sad end to all that sad story ? oh , not the end surely , surely not the end already ! 


 29th . the preparation for the marriage have begin . the dressmaker have come to receive her order . Laura be perfectly impassive , perfectly careless about the question of all other in which a woman 's personal interest be most closely bind up . she have leave it all to the dressmaker and to I . if poor Hartright have be the baronet , and the husband of her father 's choice , how differently she would have behave ! how anxious and capricious she would have be , and what a hard task the good of dressmaker would have find it to please she ! 


 30th . we hear every day from Sir Percival . the last news be that the alteration in his house will occupy from four to six month before they can be properly complete . if painter , paperhanger , and upholsterer could make happiness as well as splendour , I should be interested about their proceeding in Laura 's future home . as it be , the only part of Sir Percival 's last letter which do not leave I as it find I , perfectly indifferent to all his plan and project , be the part which refer to the wedding tour . he propose , as Laura be delicate , and as the winter threaten to be unusually severe , to take she to Rome , and to remain in Italy until the early part of next summer . if this plan should not be approve , he be equally ready , although he have no establishment of his own in town , to spend the season in London , in the most suitable furnished house that can be obtain for the purpose . 

 put myself and my own feeling entirely out of the question   which it be my duty to do , and which I have do , I , for one , have no doubt of the propriety of adopt the first of these proposal . in either case a separation between Laura and I be inevitable . it will be a long separation , in the event of their go abroad , than it would be in the event of their remain in London but we must set against this disadvantage the benefit to Laura , on the other side , of pass the winter in a mild climate , and more than that , the immense assistance in raise her spirit , and reconcile she to her new existence , which the mere wonder and excitement of travel for the first time in her life in the most interesting country in the world , must surely afford . she be not of a disposition to find resource in the conventional gaiety and excitement of London . they would only make the first oppression of this lamentable marriage fall the heavy on she . I dread the beginning of her new life more than word can tell , but I see some hope for she if she travel none if she remain at home . 

 it be strange to look back at this late entry in my journal , and to find that I be write of the marriage and the parting with Laura , as people write of a settle thing . it seem so cold and so unfeele to be look at the future already in this cruelly compose way . but what other way be possible , now that the time be draw so near ? before another month be over our head she will be his Laura instead of mine ! his Laura ! I be as little able to realise the idea which those two word convey my mind feel almost as dull and stun by it as if writing of her marriage be like writing of her death . 


 December 1st . a sad , sad day a day that I have no heart to describe at any length . after weakly put it off last night , I be oblige to speak to she this morning of Sir Percival 's proposal about the wedding tour . 

 in the full conviction that I should be with she wherever she go , the poor child for a child she be still in many thing be almost happy at the prospect of see the wonder of Florence and Rome and Naples . it nearly break my heart to dispel her delusion , and to bring her face to face with the hard truth . I be oblige to tell she that no man tolerate a rival not even a woman rival in his wife 's affection , when he first marry , whatever he may do afterwards . I be oblige to warn she that my chance of live with she permanently under her own roof , depend entirely on my not arouse Sir Percival 's jealousy and distrust by stand between they at the beginning of their marriage , in the position of the choose depositary of his wife 's close secret . drop by drop I pour the profane bitterness of this world 's wisdom into that pure heart and that innocent mind , while every high and well feeling within I recoil from my miserable task . it be over now . she have learn she hard , her inevitable lesson . the simple illusion of her girlhood be go , and my hand have strip they off . well mine than his that be all my consolation well mine than his . 

 so the first proposal be the proposal accept . they be to go to Italy , and I be to arrange , with Sir Percival 's permission , for meet they and stay with they when they return to England . in other word , I be to ask a personal favour , for the first time in my life , and to ask it of the man of all other to whom I least desire to owe a serious obligation of any kind . well ! I think I could do even more than that , for Laura 's sake . 


 2nd . on look back , I find myself always refer to Sir Percival in disparage term . in the turn affair have now take . I must and will root out my prejudice against he , I can not think how it first get into my mind . it certainly never exist in former time . 

 be it Laura 's reluctance to become his wife that have set I against he ? have Hartright 's perfectly intelligible prejudice infect I without my suspecting their influence ? do that letter of Anne Catherick 's still leave a lurking distrust in my mind , in spite of Sir Percival 's explanation , and of the proof in my possession of the truth of it ? I can not account for the state of my own feeling ; the one thing I be certain of be , that it be my duty doubly my duty now not to wrong Sir Percival by unjustly distrust he . if it have get to be a habit with I always to write of he in the same unfavourable manner , I must and will break myself of this unworthy tendency , even though the effort should force I to close the page of my journal till the marriage be over ! I be seriously dissatisfied with myself I will write no more to - day . 



 December 16th . a whole fortnight have pass , and I have not once open these page . I have be long enough away from my journal to come back to it with a healthy and well mind , I hope , so far as Sir Percival be concern . 

 there be not much to record of the past two week . the dress be almost all finish , and the new travel trunk have be send here from London . poor dear Laura hardly leave I for a moment all day , and last night , when neither of we could sleep , she come and creep into my bed to talk to I there . " I shall lose you so soon , Marian , " she say ; " I must make the most of you while I can . " 

 they be to be marry at Limmeridge Church , and thank Heaven , not one of the neighbour be to be invite to the ceremony . the only visitor will be our old friend , Mr. Arnold , who be to come from Polesdean to give Laura away , her uncle be far too delicate to trust himself outside the door in such inclement weather as we now have . if I be not determine , from this day forth , to see nothing but the bright side of our prospect , the melancholy absence of any male relative of Laura 's , at the most important moment of her life , would make I very gloomy and very distrustful of the future . but I have do with gloom and distrust that be to say , I have do with write about either the one or the other in this journal . 

 Sir Percival be to arrive to - morrow . he offer , in case we wish to treat he on term of rigid etiquette , to write and ask our clergyman to grant he the hospitality of the rectory , during the short period of his sojourn at Limmeridge , before the marriage . under the circumstance , neither Mr. Fairlie nor I think it at all necessary for we to trouble ourselves about attend to trifle form and ceremony . in our wild moorland country , and in this great lonely house , we may well claim to be beyond the reach of the trivial conventionality which hamper people in other place . I write to Sir Percival to thank he for his polite offer , and to beg that he would occupy his old room , just as usual , at Limmeridge House . 


 17th . he arrive to - day , look , as I think , a little worn and anxious , but still talk and laugh like a man in the good possible spirit . he bring with he some really beautiful present in jewellery , which Laura receive with her good grace , and , outwardly at least , with perfect self - possession . the only sign I can detect of the struggle it must cost she to preserve appearance at this try time , express itself in a sudden unwillingness , on her part , ever to be leave alone . instead of retreat to her own room , as usual , she seem to dread go there . when I go upstairs to - day , after lunch , to put on my bonnet for a walk , she volunteer to join I , and again , before dinner , she throw the door open between our two room , so that we might talk to each other while we be dress . " keep I always do something , " she say ; " keep I always in company with somebody . do not   let I think that be all I ask now , Marian do not   let I think . " 

 this sad change in she only increase her attraction for Sir Percival . he interpret it , I can see , to his own advantage . there be a feverish flush in her cheek , a feverish brightness in her eye , which he welcome as the return of her beauty and the recovery of her spirit . she talk to - day at dinner with a gaiety and carelessness so false , so shockingly out of her character , that I secretly long to silence she and take she away . Sir Percival 's delight and surprise appear to be beyond all expression . the anxiety which I have notice on his face when he arrive totally disappear from it , and he look , even to my eye , a good ten year young than he really be . 

 there can be no doubt though some strange perversity prevent I from see it myself there can be no doubt that Laura 's future husband be a very handsome man . regular feature form a personal advantage to begin with and he have they . bright brown eye , either in man or woman , be a great attraction and he have they . even baldness , when it be only baldness over the forehead   as in his case , be rather become than not in a man , for it heighten the head and add to the intelligence of the face . grace and ease of movement , untire animation of manner , ready , pliant , conversational power all these be unquestionable merit , and all these he certainly possess . surely Mr. Gilmore , ignorant as he be of Laura 's secret , be not to blame for feel surprised that she should repent of her marriage engagement ? any one else in his place would have share our good old friend 's opinion . if I be ask , at this moment , to say plainly what defect I have discover in Sir Percival , I could only point out two . one , his incessant restlessness and excitability which may be cause , naturally enough , by unusual energy of character . the other , his short , sharp , ill - temper manner of speak to the servant which may be only a bad habit after all . no , I can not dispute it , and I will not dispute it Sir Percival be a very handsome and a very agreeable man . there ! I have write it down at last , and I be glad it be over . 


 18th . feel weary and depress this morning , I leave Laura with Mrs. Vesey , and go out alone for one of my brisk midday walk , which I have discontinue too much of late . I take the dry airy road over the moor that lead to Todd 's Corner . after have be out half an hour , I be excessively surprised to see Sir Percival approach I from the direction of the farm . he be walk rapidly , swing his stick , his head erect as usual , and his shooting jacket fly open in the wind . when we meet he do not wait for I to ask any question he tell I at once that he have be to the farm to inquire if Mr. or Mrs. Todd have receive any tiding , since his last visit to Limmeridge , of Anne Catherick . 

 " you find , of course , that they have hear nothing ? " I say . 

 " nothing whatever , " he reply . " I begin to be seriously afraid that we have lose she . do you happen to know , " he continue , look I in the face very attentively " if the artist Mr. Hartright be in a position to give we any further information ? " 

 " he have neither hear of she , nor see she , since he leave Cumberland , " I answer . 

 " very sad , " say Sir Percival , speak like a man who be disappoint , and yet , oddly enough , look at the same time like a man who be relieve . " it be impossible to say what misfortune may not have happen to the miserable creature . I be inexpressibly annoyed at the failure of all my effort to restore she to the care and protection which she so urgently need . " 

 this time he really look annoyed . I say a few sympathise word , and we then talk of other subject on our way back to the house . surely my chance meet with he on the moor have disclose another favourable trait in his character ? surely it be singularly considerate and unselfish of he to think of Anne Catherick on the eve of his marriage , and to go all the way to Todd 's Corner to make inquiry about she , when he might have pass the time so much more agreeably in Laura 's society ? consider that he can only have act from motif of pure charity , his conduct , under the circumstance , show unusual good feeling and deserve extraordinary praise . well ! I give he extraordinary praise and there be an end of it . 



 19th . More discovery in the inexhaustible mine of Sir Percival 's virtue . 

 to - day I approach the subject of my propose sojourn under his wife 's roof when he bring she back to England . I have hardly drop my first hint in this direction before he catch I warmly by the hand , and say I have make the very offer to he which he have be , on his side , most anxious to make to I . I be the companion of all other whom he most sincerely long to secure for his wife , and he beg I to believe that I have confer a last favour on he by make the proposal to live with Laura after her marriage , exactly as I have always live with she before it . 

 when I have thank he in her name and mine for his considerate kindness to both of we , we pass next to the subject of his wedding tour , and begin to talk of the english society in Rome to which Laura be to be introduce . he run over the name of several friend whom he expect to meet abroad this winter . they be all English , as well as I can remember , with one exception . the one exception be Count Fosco . 

 the mention of the Count 's name , and the discovery that he and his wife be likely to meet the bride and bridegroom on the continent , put Laura 's marriage , for the first time , in a distinctly favourable light . it be likely to be the mean of heal a family feud . Hitherto Madame Fosco have choose to forget her obligation as Laura 's aunt out of sheer spite against the late Mr. Fairlie for his conduct in the affair of the legacy . now however , she can persist in this course of conduct no long . Sir Percival and Count Fosco be old and fast friend , and their wife will have no choice but to meet on civil term . Madame Fosco in her maiden day be one of the most impertinent woman I ever meet with capricious , exacting , and vain to the last degree of absurdity . if her husband have succeed in bring she to her sens , he deserve the gratitude of every member of the family , and he may have mine to begin with . 

 I be become anxious to know the Count . he be the most intimate friend of Laura 's husband , and in that capacity he excite my strong interest . neither Laura nor I have ever see he . all I know of he be that his accidental presence , year ago , on the step of the Trinita del Monte at Rome , assist Sir Percival 's escape from robbery and assassination at the critical moment when he be wound in the hand , and might the next instant have be wound in the heart . I remember also that , at the time of the late Mr. Fairlie 's absurd objection to his sister 's marriage , the Count write he a very temperate and sensible letter on the subject , which , I be ashamed to say , remain unanswered . this be all I know of Sir Percival 's friend . I wonder if he will ever come to England ? I wonder if I shall like he ? 

 my pen be run away into mere speculation . let I return to sober matter of fact . it be certain that Sir Percival 's reception of my venturesome proposal to live with his wife be more than kind , it be almost affectionate . I be sure Laura 's husband will have no reason to complain of I if I can only go on as I have begin . I have already declare he to be handsome , agreeable , full of good feeling towards the unfortunate and full of affectionate kindness towards I . really , I hardly know myself again , in my new character of Sir Percival 's warm friend . 


 20th . I hate Sir Percival ! I flatly deny his good look . I consider he to be eminently ill - temper and disagreeable , and totally want in kindness and good feeling . last night the card for the married couple be send home . Laura open the packet and see her future name in print for the first time . Sir Percival look over her shoulder familiarly at the new card which have already transform Miss Fairlie into Lady Glyde smile with the most odious self - complacency , and whisper something in her ear . I do not   know what it be Laura have refuse to tell I but I see her face turn to such a deadly whiteness that I think she would have faint . he take no notice of the change he seem to be barbarously unconscious that he have say anything to pain she . all my old feeling of hostility towards he revive on the instant , and all the hour that have pass since have do nothing to dissipate they . I be more unreasonable and more unjust than ever . in three word how glibly my pen write they ! in three word , I hate he . 


 21st . have the anxiety of this anxious time shake I a little , at last ? I have be write , for the last few day , in a tone of levity which , Heaven know , be far enough from my heart , and which it have rather shock I to discover on look back at the entry in my journal . 

 perhaps I may have catch the feverish excitement of Laura 's spirit for the last week . if so , the fit have already pass away from I , and have leave I in a very strange state of mind . a persistent idea have be force itself on my attention , ever since last night , that something will yet happen to prevent the marriage . what have produce this singular fancy ? be it the indirect result of my apprehension for Laura 's future ? or have it be unconsciously suggest to I by the increase restlessness and irritability which I have certainly observe in Sir Percival 's manner as the wedding - day draw near and nearer ? impossible to say . I know that I have the idea surely the wild idea , under the circumstance , that ever enter a woman 's head ? but try as I may , I can not trace it back to its source . 

 this last day have be all confusion and wretchedness . how can I write about it ? and yet , I must write . anything be well than brood over my own gloomy thought . 

 Kind Mrs. Vesey , whom we have all too much overlook and forget of late , innocently cause we a sad morning to begin with . she have be , for month past , secretly make a warm Shetland shawl for her dear pupil a most beautiful and surprising piece of work to be do by a woman at her age and with her habit . the gift be present this morning , and poor warm - hearted Laura completely break down when the shawl be put proudly on her shoulder by the love old friend and guardian of her motherless childhood . I be hardly allow time to quiet they both , or even to dry my own eye , when I be send for by Mr. Fairlie , to be favour with a long recital of his arrangement for the preservation of his own tranquillity on the wedding - day . 

 " Dear Laura " be to receive his present a shabby ring , with her affectionate uncle 's hair for an ornament , instead of a precious stone , and with a heartless french inscription inside , about congenial sentiment and eternal friendship " dear Laura " be to receive this tender tribute from my hand immediately , so that she might have plenty of time to recover from the agitation produce by the gift before she appear in Mr. Fairlie 's presence . " Dear Laura " be to pay he a little visit that evening , and to be kind enough not to make a scene . " Dear Laura " be to pay he another little visit in her wedding - dress the next morning , and to be kind enough , again , not to make a scene . " Dear Laura " be to look in once more , for the third time , before go away , but without harrow his feeling by say when she be go away , and without tear " in the name of pity , in the name of everything , dear Marian , that be most affectionate and most domestic , and most delightfully and charmingly self - compose , without TEARS ! " I be so exasperate by this miserable selfish trifling , at such a time , that I should certainly have shock Mr. Fairlie by some of the hard and rude truth he have ever hear in his life , if the arrival of Mr. Arnold from Polesdean have not call I away to new duty downstairs . 

 the rest of the day be indescribable . I believe no one in the house really know how it pass . the confusion of small event , all huddle together one on the other , bewildered everybody . there be dress send home that have be forget there be trunk to be pack and unpacked and pack again there be present from friend far and near , friend high and low . we be all needlessly hurry , all nervously expectant of the morrow . Sir Percival , especially , be too restless now to remain five minute together in the same place . that short , sharp cough of his trouble he more than ever . he be in and out of door all day long , and he seem to grow so inquisitive on a sudden , that he question the very stranger who come on small errand to the house . add to all this , the one perpetual thought in Laura 's mind and mine , that we be to part the next day , and the haunt dread , unexpresse by either of we , and yet ever present to both , that this deplorable marriage might prove to be the one fatal error of her life and the one hopeless sorrow of mine . for the first time in all the year of our close and happy intercourse we almost avoid look each other in the face , and we refrain , by common consent , from speak together in private through the whole evening . I can dwell on it no long . whatever future sorrow may be in store for I , I shall always look back on this twenty - first of December as the most comfortless and most miserable day of my life . 

 I be write these line in the solitude of my own room , long after midnight , have just come back from a steal look at Laura in her pretty little white bed the bed she have occupy since the day of her girlhood . 

 there she lay , unconscious that I be look at her quiet , more quiet than I have dare to hope , but not sleep . the glimmer of the night - light show I that her eye be only partially close the trace of tear glisten between her eyelid . my little keepsake only a brooch lie on the table at her bedside , with her prayer - book , and the miniature portrait of her father which she take with she wherever she go . I wait a moment , look at she from behind her pillow , as she lie beneath I , with one arm and hand rest on the white coverlid , so still , so quietly breathe , that the frill on her night - dress never move I wait , look at she , as I have see she thousand of time , as I shall never see she again and then steal back to my room . my own love ! with all your wealth , and all your beauty , how friendless you be ! the one man who would give his heart 's life to serve you be far away , toss , this stormy night , on the awful sea . who else be leave to you ? no father , no brother no living creature but the helpless , useless woman who write these sad line , and watch by you for the morning , in sorrow that she can not compose , in doubt that she can not conquer . oh , what a trust be to be place in that man 's hand to - morrow ! if ever he forget it if ever he injure a hair of her head ! 


 the TWENTY - second of DECEMBER . seven o'clock . a wild , unsettled morning . she have just rise well and calmer , now that the time have come , than she be yesterday . 



 ten o'clock . she be dress . we have kiss each other we have promise each other not to lose courage . I be away for a moment in my own room . in the whirl and confusion of my thought , I can detect that strange fancy of some hindrance happen to stop the marriage still hang about my mind . be it hang about his mind too ? I see he from the window , move hither and thither uneasily among the carriage at the door . how can I write such folly ! the marriage be a certainty . in less than half an hour we start for the church . 


 Eleven o'clock . it be all over . they be marry . 


 three o'clock . they be go ! I be blind with cry I can write no more 


  the First Epoch of the Story close here . 




 the SECOND EPOCH 

 the story continue BY MARIAN HALCOMBE . 



 I 

 BLACKWATER PARK , HAMPSHIRE . 


 June 11th , 1850 . six month to look back on six long , lonely month since Laura and I last see each other ! 

 how many day have I still to wait ? only one ! to - morrow , the twelfth , the traveller return to England . I can hardly realise my own happiness I can hardly believe that the next four - and - twenty hour will complete the last day of separation between Laura and I . 

 she and her husband have be in Italy all the winter , and afterwards in the Tyrol . they come back , accompany by Count Fosco and his wife , who propose to settle somewhere in the neighbourhood of London , and who have engage to stay at Blackwater Park for the summer month before decide on a place of residence . so long as Laura return , no matter who return with she . Sir Percival may fill the house from floor to ceiling , if he like , on condition that his wife and I inhabit it together . 

 meanwhile , here I be , establish at Blackwater Park , " the ancient and interesting seat "   as the county history obligingly inform I   " of Sir Percival Glyde , Bart . , " and the future abide - place   as I may now venture to add on my account   of plain Marian Halcombe , spinster , now settle in a snug little sitting - room , with a cup of tea by her side , and all her earthly possession range round she in three box and a bag . 

 I leave Limmeridge yesterday , have receive Laura 's delightful letter from Paris the day before . I have be previously uncertain whether I be to meet they in London or in Hampshire , but this last letter inform I that Sir Percival propose to land at Southampton , and to travel straight on to his country - house . he have spend so much money abroad that he have none leave to defray the expense of live in London for the remainder of the season , and he be economically resolve to pass the summer and autumn quietly at Blackwater . Laura have have more than enough of excitement and change of scene , and be pleased at the prospect of country tranquillity and retirement which her husband 's prudence provide for she . as for I , I be ready to be happy anywhere in her society . we be all , therefore , well content in our various way , to begin with . 

 last night I sleep in London , and be delay there so long to - day by various call and commission , that I do not reach Blackwater this evening till after dusk . 

 judge by my vague impression of the place thus far , it be the exact opposite of Limmeridge . 

 the house be situate on a dead flat , and seem to be shut in almost suffocated , to my north - country notion , by tree . I have see nobody but the man - servant who open the door to I , and the housekeeper , a very civil person , who show I the way to my own room , and get I my tea . I have a nice little boudoir and bedroom , at the end of a long passage on the first floor . the servant and some of the spare room be on the second floor , and all the live room be on the ground floor . I have not see one of they yet , and I know nothing about the house , except that one wing of it be say to be five hundred year old , that it have a moat round it once , and that it get its name of Blackwater from a lake in the park . 

 Eleven o'clock have just strike , in a ghostly and solemn manner , from a turret over the centre of the house , which I see when I come in . a large dog have be wake , apparently by the sound of the bell , and be howl and yawn drearily , somewhere round a corner . I hear echo footstep in the passage below , and the iron thumping of bolt and bar at the house door . the servant be evidently go to bed . shall I follow their example ? 

 no , I be not half sleepy enough . Sleepy , do I say ? I feel as if I should never close my eye again . the bare anticipation of see that dear face , and hear that well - know voice to - morrow , keep I in a perpetual fever of excitement . if I only have the privilege of a man , I would order out Sir Percival 's good horse instantly , and tear away on a night - gallop , eastward , to meet the rise sun a long , hard , heavy , ceaseless gallop of hour and hour , like the famous highwayman 's ride to York . be , however , nothing but a woman , condemn to patience , propriety , and petticoat for life , I must respect the house - keeper 's opinion , and try to compose myself in some feeble and feminine way . 

 reading be out of the question I can not   fix my attention on book . let I try if I can write myself into sleepiness and fatigue . my journal have be very much neglect of late . what can I recall stand , as I now do , on the threshold of a new life of person and event , of chance and change , during the past six month the long , weary , empty interval since Laura 's wedding - day ? 


 Walter Hartright be uppermost in my memory , and he pass first in the shadowy procession of my absent friend . I receive a few line from he , after the landing of the expedition in Honduras , write more cheerfully and hopefully than he have write yet . a month or six week later I see an extract from an american newspaper , describe the departure of the adventurer on their inland journey . they be last see enter a wild primeval forest , each man with his rifle on his shoulder and his baggage at his back . since that time , civilisation have lose all trace of they . not a line more have I receive from Walter , not a fragment of news from the expedition have appear in any of the public journal . 

 the same dense , dishearten obscurity hang over the fate and fortune of Anne Catherick , and her companion , Mrs. Clements . nothing whatever have be hear of either of they . whether they be in the country or out of it , whether they be live or dead , no one know . even Sir Percival 's solicitor have lose all hope , and have order the useless search after the fugitive to be finally give up . 

 our good old friend Mr. Gilmore have meet with a sad check in his active professional career . early in the spring we be alarm by hear that he have be find insensible at his desk , and that the seizure be pronounce to be an apoplectic fit . he have be long complain of fulness and oppression in the head , and his doctor have warn he of the consequence that would follow his persistency in continue to work , early and late , as if he be still a young man . the result now be that he have be positively order to keep out of his office for a year to come , at least , and to seek repose of body and relief of mind by altogether change his usual mode of life . the business be leave , accordingly , to be carry on by his partner , and he be himself , at this moment , away in Germany , visit some relation who be settle there in mercantile pursuit . thus another true friend and trustworthy adviser be lose to we lose , I earnestly hope and trust , for a time only . 

 Poor Mrs. Vesey travel with I as far as London . it be impossible to abandon she to solitude at Limmeridge after Laura and I have both leave the house , and we have arrange that she be to live with an unmarried young sister of her , who keep a school at Clapham . she be to come here this autumn to visit her pupil I might almost say her adopt child . I see the good old lady safe to her destination , and leave she in the care of her relative , quietly happy at the prospect of see Laura again in a few month ' time . 

 as for Mr. Fairlie , I believe I be guilty of no injustice if I describe he as be unutterably relieve by have the house clear of we woman . the idea of his miss his niece be simply preposterous he use to let month pass in the old time without attempt to see she and in my case and Mrs. Vesey 's , I take leave to consider his tell we both that he be half heart - break at our departure , to be equivalent to a confession that he be secretly rejoice to get rid of we . his last caprice have lead he to keep two photographer incessantly employ in produce sun - picture of all the treasure and curiosity in his possession . one complete copy of the collection of the photograph be to be present to the Mechanics ' Institution of Carlisle , mount on the fine cardboard , with ostentatious red - letter inscription underneath , " Madonna and Child by Raphael . in the possession of Frederick Fairlie , Esquire . " " copper coin of the period of Tiglath Pileser . in the possession of Frederick Fairlie , Esquire . " " Unique Rembrandt etch . know all over Europe as the smudge , from a printer 's blot in the corner which exist in no other copy . value at three hundred guineas . in the possession of Frederick Fairlie , Esq . " dozen of photograph of this sort , and all inscribe in this manner , be complete before I leave Cumberland , and hundred more remain to be do . with this new interest to occupy he , Mr. Fairlie will be a happy man for month and month to come , and the two unfortunate photographer will share the social martyrdom which he have hitherto inflict on his valet alone . 

 so much for the person and event which hold the foremost place in my memory . what next of the one person who hold the foremost place in my heart ? Laura have be present to my thought all the while I have be write these line . what can I recall of she during the past six month , before I close my journal for the night ? 

 I have only her letter to guide I , and on the most important of all the question which our correspondence can discuss , every one of those letter leave I in the dark . 

 do he treat she kindly ? be she happy now than she be when I part with she on the wedding - day ? all my letter have contain these two inquiry , put more or less directly , now in one form , and now in another , and all , on that point only , have remain without reply , or have be answer as if my question merely relate to the state of her health . she inform I , over and over again , that she be perfectly well that travel agree with she that she be get through the winter , for the first time in her life , without catch cold but not a word can I find anywhere which tell I plainly that she be reconcile to her marriage , and that she can now look back to the twenty - second of December without any bitter feeling of repentance and regret . the name of her husband be only mention in her letter , as she might mention the name of a friend who be travel with they , and who have undertake to make all the arrangement for the journey . " Sir Percival " have settle that we leave on such a day " Sir Percival " have decide that we travel by such a road . sometimes she write " Percival " only , but very seldom in nine case out of ten she give he his title . 

 I can not find that his habit and opinion have change and colour her in any single particular . the usual moral transformation which be insensibly work in a young , fresh , sensitive woman by her marriage , seem never to have take place in Laura . she write of her own thought and impression , amid all the wonder she have see , exactly as she might have write to some one else , if I have be travel with she instead of her husband . I see no betrayal anywhere of sympathy of any kind exist between they . even when she wander from the subject of her travel , and occupy herself with the prospect that await she in England , her speculation be busy with her future as my sister , and persistently neglect to notice her future as Sir Percival 's wife . in all this there be no undertone of complaint to warn I that she be absolutely unhappy in her married life . the impression I have derive from our correspondence do not , thank God , lead I to any such distressing conclusion as that . I only see a sad torpor , an unchangeable indifference , when I turn my mind from she in the old character of a sister , and look at she , through the medium of her letter , in the new character of a wife . in other word , it be always Laura Fairlie who have be write to I for the last six month , and never Lady Glyde . 

 the strange silence which she maintain on the subject of her husband 's character and conduct , she preserve with almost equal resolution in the few reference which her later letter contain to the name of her husband 's bosom friend , Count Fosco . 

 for some unexplained reason the Count and his wife appear to have change their plan abruptly , at the end of last autumn , and to have go to Vienna instead of go to Rome , at which latter place Sir Percival have expect to find they when he leave England . they only quit Vienna in the spring , and travel as far as the Tyrol to meet the bride and bridegroom on their homeward journey . Laura write readily enough about the meeting with Madame Fosco , and assure I that she have find her aunt so much change for the well so much quieter , and so much more sensible as a wife than she be as a single woman that I shall hardly know she again when I see she here . but on the subject of Count Fosco   who interest I infinitely more than his wife , Laura be provokingly circumspect and silent . she only say that he puzzle she , and that she will not tell I what her impression of he be until I have see he , and form my own opinion first . 

 this , to my mind , look ill for the Count . Laura have preserve , far more perfectly than most people do in later life , the child 's subtle faculty of know a friend by instinct , and if I be right in assume that her first impression of Count Fosco have not be favourable , I for one am in some danger of doubt and distrust that illustrious foreigner before I have so much as set eye on he . but , patience , patience this uncertainty , and many uncertainty more , can not last much long . to - morrow will see all my doubt in a fair way of be clear up , soon or later . 

 twelve o'clock have strike , and I have just come back to close these page , after look out at my open window . 

 it be a still , sultry , moonless night . the star be dull and few . the tree that shut out the view on all side look dimly black and solid in the distance , like a great wall of rock . I hear the croaking of frog , faint and far off , and the echo of the great clock hum in the airless calm long after the stroke have cease . I wonder how Blackwater Park will look in the daytime ? I do not   altogether like it by night . 


 12th . a day of investigation and discovery a more interesting day , for many reason , than I have venture to anticipate . 

 I begin my sight - seeing , of course , with the house . 

 the main body of the building be of the time of that highly - overrate woman , Queen Elizabeth . on the ground floor there be two hugely long gallery , with low ceiling lie parallel with each other , and render additionally dark and dismal by hideous family portrait every one of which I should like to burn . the room on the floor above the two gallery be keep in tolerable repair , but be very seldom used . the civil housekeeper , who act as my guide , offer to show I over they , but considerately add that she fear I should find they rather out of order . my respect for the integrity of my own petticoat and stocking infinitely exceed my respect for all the Elizabethan bedroom in the kingdom , so I positively decline explore the upper region of dust and dirt at the risk of soil my nice clean clothe . the housekeeper say , " I be quite of your opinion , miss , " and appear to think I the most sensible woman she have meet with for a long time past . 

 so much , then , for the main building . two wing be add at either end of it . the half - ruin wing on the left   as you approach the house   be once a place of residence stand by itself , and be build in the fourteenth century . one of Sir Percival 's maternal ancestor I do not   remember , and do not   care which tack on the main building , at right angle to it , in the aforesaid Queen Elizabeth 's time . the housekeeper tell I that the architecture of " the old wing , " both outside and inside , be consider remarkably fine by good judge . on further investigation I discover that good judge could only exercise their ability on Sir Percival 's piece of antiquity by previously dismiss from their mind all fear of damp , darkness , and rat . under these circumstance , I unhesitatingly acknowledge myself to be no judge at all , and suggest that we should treat " the old wing " precisely as we have previously treat the Elizabethan bedroom . once more the housekeeper say , " I be quite of your opinion , miss , " and once more she look at I with undisguised admiration of my extraordinary common - sense . 

 we go next to the wing on the right , which be build , by way of complete the wonderful architectural jumble at Blackwater Park , in the time of George the Second . 

 this be the habitable part of the house , which have be repair and redecorate inside on Laura 's account . my two room , and all the good bedroom besides , be on the first floor , and the basement contain a drawing - room , a dining - room , a morning - room , a library , and a pretty little boudoir for Laura , all very nicely ornament in the bright modern way , and all very elegantly furnish with the delightful modern luxury . none of the room be anything like so large and airy as our room at Limmeridge , but they all look pleasant to live in . I be terribly afraid , from what I have hear of Blackwater Park , of fatigue antique chair , and dismal stain glass , and musty , frouzy hanging , and all the barbarous lumber which people bear without a sense of comfort accumulate about they , in defiance of the consideration due to the convenience of their friend . it be an inexpressible relief to find that the nineteenth century have invade this strange future home of mine , and have sweep the dirty " good old time " out of the way of our daily life . 

 I dawdle away the morning part of the time in the room downstairs , and part out of door in the great square which be form by the three side of the house , and by the lofty iron railing and gate which protect it in front . a large circular fish - pond with stone side , and an allegorical leaden monster in the middle , occupy the centre of the square . the pond itself be full of gold and silver fish , and be encircle by a broad belt of the soft turf I ever walk on . I loiter here on the shady side pleasantly enough till luncheon - time , and after that take my broad straw hat and wander out alone in the warm lovely sunlight to explore the ground . 

 Daylight confirm the impression which I have feel the night before , of there be too many tree at Blackwater . the house be stifle by they . they be , for the most part , young , and plant far too thickly . I suspect there must have be a ruinous cutting down of timber all over the estate before Sir Percival 's time , and an angry anxiety on the part of the next possessor to fill up all the gap as thickly and rapidly as possible . after look about I in front of the house , I observe a flower - garden on my left hand , and walk towards it to see what I could discover in that direction . 

 on a near view the garden prove to be small and poor and ill kept . I leave it behind I , open a little gate in a ring fence , and find myself in a plantation of fir - tree . 

 a pretty wind path , artificially make , lead I on among the tree , and my north - country experience soon inform I that I be approach sandy , heathy ground . after a walk of more than half a mile , I should think , among the fir , the path take a sharp turn the tree abruptly cease to appear on either side of I , and I find myself stand suddenly on the margin of a vast open space , and look down at the Blackwater lake from which the house take its name . 

 the ground , shelve away below I , be all sand , with a few little heathy hillock to break the monotony of it in certain place . the lake itself have evidently once flow to the spot on which I stand , and have be gradually waste and dry up to less than a third of its former size . I see its still , stagnant water , a quarter of a mile away from I in the hollow , separate into pool and pond by twin reed and rush , and little knoll of earth . on the farth bank from I the tree rise thickly again , and shut out the view , and cast their black shadow on the sluggish , shallow water . as I walk down to the lake , I see that the ground on its farth side be damp and marshy , overgrow with rank grass and dismal willow . the water , which be clear enough on the open sandy side , where the sun shine , look black and poisonous opposite to I , where it lie deeply under the shade of the spongy bank , and the rank overhang thicket and tangle tree . the frog be croak , and the rat be slip in and out of the shadowy water , like live shadow themselves , as I get near to the marshy side of the lake . I see here , lie half in and half out of the water , the rotten wreck of an old overturned boat , with a sickly spot of sunlight glimmer through a gap in the tree on its dry surface , and a snake bask in the midst of the spot , fantastically coil and treacherously still . far and near the view suggest the same dreary impression of solitude and decay , and the glorious brightness of the summer sky overhead seem only to deepen and harden the gloom and barrenness of the wilderness on which it shine . I turn and retrace my step to the high heathy ground , direct they a little aside from my former path towards a shabby old wooden shed , which stand on the outer skirt of the fir plantation , and which have hitherto be too unimportant to share my notice with the wide , wild prospect of the lake . 

 on approach the shed I find that it have once be a boat - house , and that an attempt have apparently be make to convert it afterwards into a sort of rude arbour , by place inside it a firwood seat , a few stool , and a table . I enter the place , and sit down for a little while to rest and get my breath again . 

 I have not be in the boat - house more than a minute when it strike I that the sound of my own quick breathing be very strangely echo by something beneath I . I listen intently for a moment , and hear a low , thick , sob breath that seem to come from the ground under the seat which I be occupy . my nerve be not easily shake by trifle , but on this occasion I start to my foot in a fright call out receive no answer summon back my recreant courage , and look under the seat . 

 there , crouch up in the farth corner , lie the forlorn cause of my terror , in the shape of a poor little dog a black and white spaniel . the creature moan feebly when I look at it and call to it , but never stir . I move away the seat and look close . the poor little dog 's eye be glaze fast , and there be spot of blood on its glossy white side . the misery of a weak , helpless , dumb creature be surely one of the sad of all the mournful sight which this world can show .
