I lift the poor dog in my arm as gently as I could , and contrive a sort of make - shift hammock for he to lie in , by gather up the front of my dress all round he . in this way I take the creature , as painlessly as possible , and as fast as possible , back to the house . 

 find no one in the hall I go up at once to my own sitting - room , make a bed for the dog with one of my old shawl , and ring the bell . the large and fat of all possible house - maid answer it , in a state of cheerful stupidity which would have provoke the patience of a saint . the girl 's fat , shapeless face actually stretch into a broad grin at the sight of the wound creature on the floor . 

 " what do you see there to laugh at ? " I ask , as angrily as if she have be a servant of my own . " do you know whose dog it be ? " 

 " no , miss , that I certainly do not . " she stoop , and look down at the spaniel 's injure side brighten suddenly with the irradiation of a new idea and point to the wound with a chuckle of satisfaction , say , " that be Baxter 's doing , that be . " 

 I be so exasperate that I could have box her ear . " Baxter ? " I say . " who be the brute you call Baxter ? " 

 the girl grin again more cheerfully than ever . " bless you , miss ! Baxter 's the keeper , and when he find strange dog hunt about , he take and shoot them . it be keeper 's dooty miss , I think that dog will die . here be where he be be shoot , be not   it ? that be Baxter 's doing , that be . Baxter 's doing , miss , and Baxter 's dooty . " 

 I be almost wicked enough to wish that Baxter have shoot the housemaid instead of the dog . see that it be quite useless to expect this densely impenetrable personage to give I any help in relieve the suffering creature at our foot , I tell she to request the housekeeper 's attendance with my compliment . she go out exactly as she have come in , grin from ear to ear . as the door close on she she say to herself softly , " it be Baxter 's doing and Baxter 's dooty that be what it be . " 

 the housekeeper , a person of some education and intelligence , thoughtfully bring upstairs with she some milk and some warm water . the instant she see the dog on the floor she start and change colour . 

 " why , Lord bless I , " cry the housekeeper , " that must be Mrs. Catherick 's dog ! " 

 " Whose ? " I ask , in the utmost astonishment . 

 " Mrs. Catherick 's . you seem to know Mrs. Catherick , Miss Halcombe ? " 

 " not personally , but I have hear of she . do she live here ? have she have any news of her daughter ? " 

 " no , Miss Halcombe , she come here to ask for news . " 

 " when ? " 

 " only yesterday . she say some one have report that a stranger answer to the description of her daughter have be see in our neighbourhood . no such report have reach we here , and no such report be know in the village , when I send to make inquiry there on Mrs. Catherick 's account . she certainly bring this poor little dog with she when she come , and I see it trot out after she when she go away . I suppose the creature stray into the plantation , and get shoot . where do you find it , Miss Halcombe ? " 

 " in the old shed that look out on the lake . " 

 " ah , yes , that be the plantation side , and the poor thing drag itself , I suppose , to the near shelter , as dog will , to die . if you can moisten its lip with the milk , Miss Halcombe , I will wash the clot hair from the wound . I be very much afraid it be too late to do any good . however , we can but try . " 

 Mrs. Catherick ! the name still ring in my ear , as if the housekeeper have only that moment surprise I by utter it . while we be attend to the dog , the word of Walter Hartright 's caution to I return to my memory : " if ever Anne Catherick cross your path , make well use of the opportunity , Miss Halcombe , than I make of it . " the finding of the wound spaniel have lead I already to the discovery of Mrs. Catherick 's visit to Blackwater Park , and that event might lead in its turn , to something more . I determine to make the most of the chance which be now offer to I , and to gain as much information as I could . 

 " do you say that Mrs. Catherick live anywhere in this neighbourhood ? " I ask . 

 " oh dear , no , " say the housekeeper . " she live at Welmingham , quite at the other end of the county five - and - twenty mile off , at least . " 

 " I suppose you have know Mrs. Catherick for some year ? " 

 " on the contrary , Miss Halcombe , I never see she before she come here yesterday . I have hear of she , of course , because I have hear of Sir Percival 's kindness in put her daughter under medical care . Mrs. Catherick be rather a strange person in her manner , but extremely respectable - looking . she seem sorely put out when she find that there be no foundation none , at least , that any of we could discover for the report of her daughter have be see in this neighbourhood . " 

 " I be rather interested about Mrs. Catherick , " I go on , continue the conversation as long as possible . " I wish I have arrive here soon enough to see she yesterday . do she stay for any length of time ? " 

 " yes , " say the housekeeper , ' she stay for some time ; and I think she would have remain long , if I have not be call away to speak to a strange gentleman a gentleman who come to ask when Sir Percival be expect back . Mrs. Catherick get up and leave at once , when she hear the maid tell I what the visitor 's errand be . she say to I , at part , that there be no need to tell Sir Percival of she come here . I think that rather an odd remark to make , especially to a person in my responsible situation . " 

 I think it an odd remark too . Sir Percival have certainly lead I to believe , at Limmeridge , that the most perfect confidence exist between himself and Mrs. Catherick . if that be the case , why should she be anxious to have her visit at Blackwater Park keep a secret from he ? 

 " probably , " I say , see that the housekeeper expect I to give my opinion on Mrs. Catherick 's parting word , " probably she think the announcement of her visit might vex Sir Percival to no purpose , by remind he that her lost daughter be not find yet . do she talk much on that subject ? " 

 " very little , " reply the housekeeper . " she talk principally of Sir Percival , and ask a great many question about where he have be travel , and what sort of lady his new wife be . she seem to be more soured and put out than distressed , by fail to find any trace of her daughter in these part . ' I give she up , ' be the last word she say that I can remember ; ' I give she up , ma'am , for lost . ' and from that she pass at once to her question about Lady Glyde , want to know if she be a handsome , amiable lady , comely and healthy and young ah , dear ! I think how it would end . look , Miss Halcombe , the poor thing be out of its misery at last ! " 

 the dog be dead . it have give a faint , sob cry , it have suffer an instant 's convulsion of the limb , just as those last word , " comely and healthy and young , " drop from the housekeeper 's lip . the change have happen with startling suddenness in one moment the creature lie lifeless under our hand . 

 eight o'clock . I have just return from din downstairs , in solitary state . the sunset be burn redly on the wilderness of tree that I see from my window , and I be pore over my journal again , to calm my impatience for the return of the traveller . they ought to have arrive , by my calculation , before this . how still and lonely the house be in the drowsy evening quiet ! oh I ! how many minute more before I hear the carriage wheel and run downstairs to find myself in Laura 's arm ? 

 the poor little dog ! I wish my first day at Blackwater Park have not be associate with death , though it be only the death of a stray animal . 

 Welmingham I see , on look back through these private page of mine , that Welmingham be the name of the place where Mrs. Catherick live . her note be still in my possession , the note in answer to that letter about her unhappy daughter which Sir Percival oblige I to write . one of these day , when I can find a safe opportunity , I will take the note with I by way of introduction , and try what I can make of Mrs. Catherick at a personal interview . I do not   understand she wish to conceal her visit to this place from Sir Percival 's knowledge , and I do not   feel half so sure , as the housekeeper seem to do , that her daughter Anne be not in the neighbourhood after all . what would Walter Hartright have say in this emergency ? poor , dear Hartright ! I be begin to feel the want of his honest advice and his willing help already . 

 surely I hear something . be it a bustle of footstep below stair ? yes ! I hear the horse ' foot I hear the roll wheel 



 II 

 June 15th . the confusion of their arrival have have time to subside . two day have elapse since the return of the traveller , and that interval have suffice to put the new machinery of our life at Blackwater Park in fair working order . I may now return to my journal , with some little chance of be able to continue the entry in it as collectedly as usual . 

 I think I must begin by put down an odd remark which have suggest itself to I since Laura come back . 

 when two member of a family or two intimate friend be separate , and one go abroad and one remain at home , the return of the relative or friend who have be travel always seem to place the relative or friend who have be stay at home at a painful disadvantage when the two first meet . the sudden encounter of the new thought and new habit eagerly gain in the one case , with the old thought and old habit passively preserve in the other , seem at first to part the sympathy of the most love relative and the fond friend , and to set a sudden strangeness , unexpected by both and uncontrollable by both , between they on either side . after the first happiness of my meeting with Laura be over , after we have sit down together hand in hand to recover breath enough and calmness enough to talk , I feel this strangeness instantly , and I could see that she feel it too . it have partially wear away , now that we have fall back into most of our old habit , and it will probably disappear before long . but it have certainly have an influence over the first impression that I have form of she , now that we be live together again for which reason only I have think fit to mention it here . 

 she have find I unaltered , but I have find she change . 

 change in person , and in one respect change in character . I can not absolutely say that she be less beautiful than she use to be I can only say that she be less beautiful to I . 

 other , who do not look at she with my eye and my recollection , would probably think she improve . there be more colour and more decision and roundness of outline in her face than there use to be , and her figure seem more firmly set and more sure and easy in all its movement than it be in her maiden day . but I miss something when I look at her something that once belong to the happy , innocent life of Laura Fairlie , and that I can not find in Lady Glyde . there be in the old time a freshness , a softness , an ever - vary and yet ever - remain tenderness of beauty in her face , the charm of which it be not possible to express in word , or , as poor Hartright use often to say , in paint either . this be go . I think I see the faint reflection of it for a moment when she turn pale under the agitation of our sudden meeting on the evening of her return , but it have never reappear since . none of her letter have prepare I for a personal change in she . on the contrary , they have lead I to expect that her marriage have leave she , in appearance at least , quite unaltered . perhaps I read her letter wrongly in the past , and be now read her face wrongly in the present ? no matter ! whether her beauty have gain or whether it have lose in the last six month , the separation either way have make her own dear self more precious to I than ever , and that be one good result of her marriage , at any rate ! 

 the second change , the change that I have observe in her character , have not surprise I , because I be prepared for it in this case by the tone of her letter . now that she be at home again , I find she just as unwilling to enter into any detail on the subject of her married life as I have previously find she all through the time of our separation , when we could only communicate with each other by write . at the first approach I make to the forbidden topic she put her hand on my lip with a look and gesture which touchingly , almost painfully , recall to my memory the day of her girlhood and the happy bygone time when there be no secret between we . 

 " whenever you and I be together , marian , " she say , " we shall both be happy and easy with one another , if we accept my married life for what it be , and say and think as little about it as possible . I would tell you everything , darling , about myself , " she go on , nervously buckling and unbuckle the ribbon round my waist , " if my confidence could only end there . but they could not they would lead I into confidence about my husband too ; and now I be married , I think I have well avoid they , for his sake , and for your sake , and for mine . I do not   say that they would distress you , or distress I I would not   have you think that for the world . but I want to be so happy , now I have get you back again , and I want you to be so happy too " she break off abruptly , and look round the room , my own sitting - room , in which we be talk . " ah ! " she cry , clap her hand with a bright smile of recognition , " another old friend find already ! your book - case , Marian your dear - little - shabby - old - satin - wood book - case how glad I be you bring it with you from Limmeridge ! and the horrid heavy man 's umbrella , that you always would walk out with when it rain ! and first and foremost of all , your own dear , dark , clever , gipsy - face , look at I just as usual ! it be so like home again to be here . how can we make it more like home still ? I will put my father 's portrait in your room instead of in mine and I will keep all my little treasure from Limmeridge here and we will pass hour and hour every day with these four friendly wall round we . oh , Marian ! " she say , suddenly seat herself on a footstool at my knee , and look up earnestly in my face , " promise you will never marry , and leave I . it be selfish to say so , but you be so much well off as a single woman unless unless you be very fond of your husband but you will not   be very fond of anybody but I , will you ? " she stop again , cross my hand on my lap , and lay her face on they . " have you be write many letter , and receive many letter lately ? " she ask , in low , suddenly - alter tone . I understand what the question mean , but I think it my duty not to encourage she by meet her half way . " have you hear from he ? " she go on , coax I to forgive the more direct appeal on which she now venture , by kiss my hand , upon which her face still rest . " be he well and happy , and get on in his profession ? have he recover himself and forget I ? " 

 she should not have ask those question . she should have remember her own resolution , on the morning when Sir Percival hold she to her marriage engagement , and when she resign the book of Hartright 's drawing into my hand for ever . but , ah I ! where be the faultless human creature who can persevere in a good resolution , without sometimes fail and fall back ? where be the woman who have ever really tear from her heart the image that have be once fix in it by a true love ? book tell we that such unearthly creature have exist but what do our own experience say in answer to book ? 

 I make no attempt to remonstrate with she : perhaps , because I sincerely appreciate the fearless candour which let I see , what other woman in her position might have have reason for conceal even from their dear friend perhaps , because I feel , in my own heart and conscience , that in her place I should have ask the same question and have the same thought . all I could honestly do be to reply that I have not write to he or hear from he lately , and then to turn the conversation to less dangerous topic . 

 there have be much to sadden I in our interview my first confidential interview with she since her return . the change which her marriage have produce in our relation towards each other , by place a forbidden subject between we , for the first time in our life ; the melancholy conviction of the dearth of all warmth of feeling , of all close sympathy , between her husband and herself , which her own unwilling word now force on my mind ; the distressing discovery that the influence of that ill - fated attachment still remain   no matter how innocently , how harmlessly   root as deeply as ever in her heart all these be disclosure to sadden any woman who love she as dearly , and feel for she as acutely , as I do . 

 there be only one consolation to set against they a consolation that ought to comfort I , and that do comfort I . all the grace and gentleness of her character all the frank affection of her nature all the sweet , simple , womanly charm which use to make she the darling and delight of every one who approach she , have come back to I with herself . of my other impression I be sometimes a little inclined to doubt . of this last , good , happy of all impression , I grow more and more certain every hour in the day . 

 let I turn , now , from she to her travel companion . her husband must engage my attention first . what have I observe in Sir Percival , since his return , to improve my opinion of he ? 

 I can hardly say . small vexation and annoyance seem to have beset he since he come back , and no man , under those circumstance , be ever present at his good . he look , as I think , thin than he be when he leave England . his wearisome cough and his comfortless restlessness have certainly increase . his manner at least his manner towards I be much more abrupt than it use to be . he greet I , on the evening of his return , with little or nothing of the ceremony and civility of former time no polite speech of welcome no appearance of extraordinary gratification at see I nothing but a short shake of the hand , and a sharp " how - d'ye - do , Miss Halcombe glad to see you again . " he seem to accept I as one of the necessary fixture of Blackwater Park , to be satisfied at find I establish in my proper place , and then to pass I over altogether . 

 Most man show something of their disposition in their own house , which they have conceal elsewhere , and Sir Percival have already display a mania for order and regularity , which be quite a new revelation of he , so far as my previous knowledge of his character be concern . if I take a book from the library and leave it on the table , he follow I and put it back again . if I rise from a chair , and let it remain where I have be sit , he carefully restore it to its proper place against the wall . he pick up stray flower - blossom from the carpet , and mutter to himself as discontentedly as if they be hot cinder burn hole in it , and he storm at the servant if there be a crease in the tablecloth , or a knife miss from its place at the dinner - table , as fiercely as if they have personally insult he . 

 I have already refer to the small annoyance which appear to have trouble he since his return . much of the alteration for the bad which I have notice in he may be due to these . I try to persuade myself that it be so , because I be anxious not to be dishearten already about the future . it be certainly try to any man 's temper to be meet by a vexation the moment he set foot in his own house again , after a long absence , and this annoying circumstance do really happen to Sir Percival in my presence . 

 on the evening of their arrival the housekeeper follow I into the hall to receive her master and mistress and their guest . the instant he see she , Sir Percival ask if any one have call lately . the housekeeper mention to he , in reply , what she have previously mention to I , the visit of the strange gentleman to make inquiry about the time of her master 's return . he ask immediately for the gentleman 's name . no name have be leave . the gentleman 's business ? no business have be mention . what be the gentleman like ? the housekeeper try to describe he , but fail to distinguish the nameless visitor by any personal peculiarity which her master could recognise . Sir Percival frown , stamp angrily on the floor , and walk on into the house , take no notice of anybody . why he should have be so discompose by a trifle I can not say but he be seriously discompose , beyond all doubt . 

 upon the whole , it will be good , perhaps , if I abstain from form a decisive opinion of his manner , language , and conduct in his own house , until time have enable he to shake off the anxiety , whatever they may be , which now evidently trouble his mind in secret . I will turn over to a new page , and my pen shall let Laura 's husband alone for the present . 

 the two guest the Count and Countess Fosco come next in my catalogue . I will dispose of the Countess first , so as to have do with the woman as soon as possible . 

 Laura be certainly not chargeable with any exaggeration , in write I word that I should hardly recognise her aunt again when we meet . never before have I behold such a change produce in a woman by her marriage as have be produce in Madame Fosco . 

 as Eleanor Fairlie   aged seven - and - thirty , she be always talk pretentious nonsense , and always worry the unfortunate man with every small exaction which a vain and foolish woman can impose on long - suffer male humanity . as Madame Fosco   age three - and - forty , she sit for hour together without say a word , freeze up in the strange manner in herself . the hideously ridiculous love - lock which use to hang on either side of her face be now replace by stiff little row of very short curl , of the sort one see in old - fashioned wig . a plain , matronly cap cover her head , and make she look , for the first time in her life since I remember she , like a decent woman . nobody   put her husband out of the question , of course   now see in she , what everybody once see I mean the structure of the female skeleton , in the upper region of the collar - bone and the shoulder - blade . clothe in quiet black or grey gown , make high round the throat dress that she would have laugh at , or scream at , as the whim of the moment incline she , in her maiden day she sit speechless in corner ; her dry white hand   so dry that the pore of her skin look chalky   incessantly engage , either in monotonous embroidery work or in roll up endless cigarette for the Count 's own particular smoking . on the few occasion when her cold blue eye be off her work , they be generally turn on her husband , with the look of mute submissive inquiry which we be all familiar with in the eye of a faithful dog . the only approach to an inward thaw which I have yet detect under her outer covering of icy constraint , have betray itself , once or twice , in the form of a suppress tigerish jealousy of any woman in the house   the maid include   to whom the Count speak , or on whom he look with anything approach to special interest or attention . except in this one particular , she be always , morning , noon , and night , indoor and out , fair weather or foul , as cold as a statue , and as impenetrable as the stone out of which it be cut . for the common purpose of society the extraordinary change thus produce in she be , beyond all doubt , a change for the well , see that it have transform she into a civil , silent , unobtrusive woman , who be never in the way . how far she be really reform or deteriorate in her secret self , be another question . I have once or twice see sudden change of expression on her pinched lip , and hear sudden inflexion of tone in her calm voice , which have lead I to suspect that her present state of suppression may have seal up something dangerous in her nature , which use to evaporate harmlessly in the freedom of her former life . it be quite possible that I may be altogether wrong in this idea . my own impression , however , be , that I be right . time will show . 

 and the magician who have work this wonderful transformation the foreign husband who have tame this once wayward english woman till her own relation hardly know she again the count himself ? what of the Count ? 

 this in two word : he look like a man who could tame anything . if he have marry a tigress , instead of a woman , he would have tame the tigress . if he have marry I , I should have make his cigarette , as his wife do I should have hold my tongue when he look at I , as she hold her . 

 I be almost afraid to confess it , even to these secret page . the man have interest I , have attract I , have force I to like he . in two short day he have make his way straight into my favourable estimation , and how he have work the miracle be more than I can tell . 

 it absolutely startle I , now he be in my mind , to find how plainly I see he ! how much more plainly than I see Sir Percival , or Mr. Fairlie , or Walter Hartright , or any other absent person of whom I think , with the one exception of Laura herself ! I can hear his voice , as if he be speak at this moment . I know what his conversation be yesterday , as well as if I be hear it now . how be I to describe he ? there be peculiarity in his personal appearance , his habit , and his amusement , which I should blame in the bold term , or ridicule in the most merciless manner , if I have see they in another man . what be it that make I unable to blame they , or to ridicule they in HIM ? 

 for example , he be immensely fat . before this time I have always especially dislike corpulent humanity . I have always maintain that the popular notion of connect excessive grossness of size and excessive good - humour as inseparable ally be equivalent to declare , either that no people but amiable people ever get fat , or that the accidental addition of so many pound of flesh have a directly favourable influence over the disposition of the person on whose body they accumulate . I have invariably combat both these absurd assertion by quote example of fat people who be as mean , vicious , and cruel as the leanest and the bad of their neighbour . I have ask whether Henry the Eighth be an amiable character ? whether Pope Alexander the Sixth be a good man ? whether Mr. Murderer and Mrs. Murderess Manning be not both unusually stout people ? whether hire nurse , proverbially as cruel a set of woman as be to be find in all England , be not , for the most part , also as fat a set of woman as be to be find in all England ? and so on , through dozen of other example , modern and ancient , native and foreign , high and low . hold these strong opinion on the subject with might and main as I do at this moment , here , nevertheless , be Count Fosco , as fat as Henry the Eighth himself , establish in my favour , at one day 's notice , without let or hindrance from his own odious corpulence . marvellous indeed ! 

 be it his face that have recommend he ? 

 it may be his face . he be a most remarkable likeness , on a large scale , of the great Napoleon . his feature have Napoleon 's magnificent regularity his expression recall the grandly calm , immovable power of the Great soldier 's face . this striking resemblance certainly impress I , to begin with ; but there be something in he besides the resemblance , which have impress I more . I think the influence I be now try to find be in his eye . they be the most unfathomable grey eye I ever see , and they have at time a cold , clear , beautiful , irresistible glitter in they which force I to look at he , and yet cause I sensation , when I do look , which I would rather not feel . other part of his face and head have their strange peculiarity . his complexion , for instance , have a singular sallow - fairness , so much at variance with the dark - brown colour of his hair , that I suspect the hair of be a wig , and his face , closely shaven all over , be smooth and free from all mark and wrinkle than mine , though   accord to Sir Percival 's account of he   he be close on sixty year of age . but these be not the prominent personal characteristic which distinguish he , to my mind , from all the other man I have ever see . the marked peculiarity which single he out from the rank and file of humanity lie entirely , so far as I can tell at present , in the extraordinary expression and extraordinary power of his eye . 

 his manner and his command of our language may also have assist he , in some degree , to establish himself in my good opinion . he have that quiet deference , that look of pleased , attentive interest in listen to a woman , and that secret gentleness in his voice in speak to a woman , which , say what we may , we can none of we resist . here , too , his unusual command of the english language necessarily help he . I have often hear of the extraordinary aptitude which many Italians show in master our strong , hard , northern speech ; but , until I see Count Fosco , I have never suppose it possible that any foreigner could have speak English as he speak it . there be time when it be almost impossible to detect , by his accent that he be not a countryman of our own , and as for fluency , there be very few bear englishman who can talk with as few stoppage and repetition as the Count . he may construct his sentence more or less in the foreign way , but I have never yet hear he use a wrong expression , or hesitate for a moment in his choice of a word . 

 all the small characteristic of this strange man have something strikingly original and perplexingly contradictory in they . fat as he be and old as he be , his movement be astonishingly light and easy . he be as noiseless in a room as any of we woman , and more than that , with all his look of unmistakable mental firmness and power , he be as nervously sensitive as the weak of we . he start at chance noise as inveterately as Laura herself . he wince and shudder yesterday , when Sir Percival beat one of the spaniel , so that I feel ashamed of my own want of tenderness and sensibility by comparison with the Count . 

 the relation of this last incident remind I of one of his most curious peculiarity , which I have not yet mention his extraordinary fondness for pet animal . 

 some of these he have leave on the continent , but he have bring with he to this house a cockatoo , two canary - bird , and a whole family of white mouse . he attend to all the necessity of these strange favourite himself , and he have teach the creature to be surprisingly fond of he and familiar with he . the cockatoo , a most vicious and treacherous bird towards every one else , absolutely seem to love he . when he let it out of its cage , it hop on to his knee , and claw its way up his great big body , and rub its top - knot against his sallow double chin in the most caressing manner imaginable . he have only to set the door of the canary ' cage open , and to call they , and the pretty little cleverly train creature perch fearlessly on his hand , mount his fat outstretche finger one by one , when he tell they to " go upstairs , " and sing together as if they would burst their throat with delight when they get to the top finger . his white mouse live in a little pagoda of gaily - paint wirework , design and make by himself . they be almost as tame as the canary , and they be perpetually let out like the canary . they crawl all over he , pop in and out of his waistcoat , and sit in couple , white as snow , on his capacious shoulder . he seem to be even fonder of his mouse than of his other pet , smile at they , and kiss they , and call they by all sort of endear name . if it be possible to suppose an Englishman with any taste for such childish interest and amusement as these , that Englishman would certainly feel rather ashamed of they , and would be anxious to apologise for they , in the company of grow - up people . but the Count , apparently , see nothing ridiculous in the amazing contrast between his colossal self and his frail little pet . he would blandly kiss his white mouse and twitter to his canary - bird amid an assembly of english fox - hunter , and would only pity they as barbarian when they be all laugh their loud at he . 

 it seem hardly credible while I be write it down , but it be certainly true , that this same man , who have all the fondness of an old maid for his cockatoo , and all the small dexterity of an organ - boy in manage his white mouse , can talk , when anything happen to rouse he , with a daring independence of thought , a knowledge of book in every language , and an experience of society in half the capital of Europe , which would make he the prominent personage of any assembly in the civilised world . this trainer of canary - bird , this architect of a pagoda for white mouse , be   as Sir Percival himself have tell I   one of the first experimental chemist live , and have discover , among other wonderful invention , a means of petrify the body after death , so as to preserve it , as hard as marble , to the end of time . this fat , indolent , elderly man , whose nerve be so finely string that he start at chance noise , and wince when he see a house - spaniel get a whipping , go into the stable - yard on the morning after his arrival , and put his hand on the head of a chain bloodhound a beast so savage that the very groom who feed he keep out of his reach . his wife and I be present , and I shall not forget the scene that follow , short as it be . 

 " mind that dog , sir , " say the groom ; " he fly at everybody ! " " he do that , my friend , " reply the Count quietly , " because everybody be afraid of he . let we see if he fly at I . " and he lay his plump , yellow - white finger , on which the canary - bird have be perch ten minute before , upon the formidable brute 's head , and look he straight in the eye . " you big dog be all coward , " he say , address the animal contemptuously , with his face and the dog 's within an inch of each other . " you would kill a poor cat , you infernal coward . you would fly at a starve beggar , you infernal coward . anything that you can surprise unawares anything that be afraid of your big body , and your wicked white tooth , and your slobbering , bloodthirsty mouth , be the thing you like to fly at . you could throttle I at this moment , you mean , miserable bully , and you dare not   so much as look I in the face , because I be not afraid of you . will you think well of it , and try your tooth in my fat neck ? bah ! not you ! " he turn away , laugh at the astonishment of the man in the yard , and the dog creep back meekly to his kennel . " ah ! my nice waistcoat ! " he say pathetically . " I be sorry I come here . some of that brute 's slobber have get on my pretty clean waistcoat . " those word express another of his incomprehensible oddity . he be as fond of fine clothe as the veri fool in existence , and have appear in four magnificent waistcoat already all of light garish colour , and all immensely large even for he in the two day of his residence at Blackwater Park . 

 his tact and cleverness in small thing be quite as noticeable as the singular inconsistency in his character , and the childish triviality of his ordinary taste and pursuit . 

 I can see already that he mean to live on excellent term with all of we during the period of his sojourn in this place . he have evidently discover that Laura secretly dislike he   she confess as much to I when I press she on the subject   but he have also find out that she be extravagantly fond of flower . whenever she want a nosegay he have get one to give she , gather and arrange by himself , and greatly to my amusement , he be always cunningly provide with a duplicate , compose of exactly the same flower , group in exactly the same way , to appease his icily jealous wife before she can so much as think herself aggrieve . his management of the Countess   in public   be a sight to see . he bow to she , he habitually address she as " my angel , " he carry his canary to pay she little visit on his finger and to sing to she , he kiss her hand when she give he his cigarette ; he present she with sugar - plum in return , which he put into her mouth playfully , from a box in his pocket . the rod of iron with which he rule she never appear in company it be a private rod , and be always keep upstairs . 

 his method of recommend himself to I be entirely different . he flatter my vanity by talk to I as seriously and sensibly as if I be a man . yes ! I can find he out when I be away from he I know he flatter my vanity , when I think of he up here in my own room and yet , when I go downstairs , and get into his company again , he will blind I again , and I shall be flatter again , just as if I have never find he out at all ! he can manage I as he manage his wife and Laura , as he manage the bloodhound in the stable - yard , as he manage Sir Percival himself , every hour in the day . " my good Percival ! how I like your rough english humour ! " " my good Percival ! how I enjoy your solid english sense ! " he put the rude remark Sir Percival can make on his effeminate taste and amusement quietly away from he in that manner always call the baronet by his christian name , smile at he with the calm superiority , pat he on the shoulder , and bear with he benignantly , as a good - humour father bear with a wayward son . 

 the interest which I really can not help feel in this strangely original man have lead I to question Sir Percival about his past life . 

 Sir Percival either know little , or will tell I little , about it . he and the Count first meet many year ago , at Rome , under the dangerous circumstance to which I have allude elsewhere . since that time they have be perpetually together in London , in Paris , and in Vienna but never in Italy again ; the Count have , oddly enough , not cross the frontier of his native country for year past . perhaps he have be make the victim of some political persecution ? at all event , he seem to be patriotically anxious not to lose sight of any of his own countryman who may happen to be in England . on the evening of his arrival he ask how far we be from the near town , and whether we know of any italian gentleman who might happen to be settle there . he be certainly in correspondence with people on the continent , for his letter have all sort of odd stamp on they , and I see one for he this morning , wait in his place at the breakfast - table , with a huge , official - look seal on it . perhaps he be in correspondence with his government ? and yet , that be hardly to be reconcile either with my other idea that he may be a political exile . 

 how much I seem to have write about Count Fosco ! and what do it all amount to ? as poor , dear Mr. Gilmore would ask , in his impenetrable business - like way I can only repeat that I do assuredly feel , even on this short acquaintance , a strange , half - willing , half - unwilling liking for the Count . he seem to have establish over I the same sort of ascendency which he have evidently gain over Sir Percival . free , and even rude , as he may occasionally be in his manner towards his fat friend , Sir Percival be nevertheless afraid , as I can plainly see , of give any serious offence to the Count . I wonder whether I be afraid too ? I certainly never see a man , in all my experience , whom I should be so sorry to have for an enemy . be this because I like he , or because I be afraid of he ? Chi sa ? as Count Fosco might say in his own language . who know ? 


 June 16th . something to chronicle to - day besides my own idea and impression . a visitor have arrive quite unknown to Laura and to I , and apparently quite unexpected by Sir Percival . 

 we be all at lunch , in the room with the new french window that open into the verandah , and the Count   who devour pastry as I have never yet see it devour by any human being but girl at boarding - school   have just amuse we by ask gravely for his fourth tart when the servant enter to announce the visitor . 

 " Mr. Merriman have just come , Sir Percival , and wish to see you immediately . " 

 Sir Percival start , and look at the man with an expression of angry alarm . 

 " Mr. Merriman ! " he repeat , as if he think his own ear must have deceive he . 

 " yes , Sir Percival Mr. Merriman , from London . " 

 " where be he ? " 

 " in the library , Sir Percival . " 

 he leave the table the instant the last answer be give , and hurry out of the room without say a word to any of we . 

 " who be Mr. Merriman ? " ask Laura , appeal to I . 

 " I have not the least idea , " be all I could say in reply . 

 the Count have finish his fourth tart , and have go to a side - table to look after his vicious cockatoo . he turn round to we with the bird perch on his shoulder . 

 " Mr. Merriman be Sir Percival 's solicitor , " he say quietly . 

 Sir Percival 's solicitor . it be a perfectly straightforward answer to Laura 's question , and yet , under the circumstance , it be not satisfactory . if Mr. Merriman have be specially send for by his client , there would have be nothing very wonderful in his leave town to obey the summon . but when a lawyer travel from London to Hampshire without be send for , and when his arrival at a gentleman 's house seriously startle the gentleman himself , it may be safely take for grant that the legal visitor be the bearer of some very important and very unexpected news news which may be either very good or very bad , but which can not , in either case , be of the common everyday kind . 

 Laura and I sit silent at the table for a quarter of an hour or more , wonder uneasily what have happen , and wait for the chance of Sir Percival 's speedy return . there be no sign of his return , and we rise to leave the room . 

 the Count , attentive as usual , advanced from the corner in which he have be feed his cockatoo , with the bird still perch on his shoulder , and open the door for we . Laura and Madame Fosco go out first . just as I be on the point of follow they he make a sign with his hand , and speak to I , before I pass he , in the oddest manner . 

 " yes , " he say , quietly answer the unexpressed idea at that moment in my mind , as if I have plainly confide it to he in so many word " yes , Miss Halcombe , something have happen . " 

 I be on the point of answering , " I never say so , " but the vicious cockatoo ruffle his clipped wing and give a screech that set all my nerve on edge in an instant , and make I only too glad to get out of the room . 

 I join Laura at the foot of the stair . the thought in her mind be the same as the thought in mine , which Count Fosco have surprise , and when she speak her word be almost the echo of his . she , too , say to I secretly that she be afraid something have happen . 



 III 

 June 16th . I have a few line more to add to this day 's entry before I go to bed to - night . 

 about two hour after Sir Percival rise from the luncheon - table to receive his solicitor , Mr. Merriman , in the library , I leave my room alone to take a walk in the plantation . just as I be at the end of the landing the library door open and the two gentleman come out . think it well not to disturb they by appear on the stair , I resolve to defer go down till they have cross the hall . although they speak to each other in guarded tone , their word be pronounce with sufficient distinctness of utterance to reach my ear . 

 " make your mind easy , Sir Percival , " I hear the lawyer say ; " it all rest with Lady Glyde . " 

 I have turn to go back to my own room for a minute or two , but the sound of Laura 's name on the lip of a stranger stop I instantly . I daresay it be very wrong and very discreditable to listen , but where be the woman , in the whole range of our sex , who can regulate her action by the abstract principle of honour , when those principle point one way , and when her affection , and the interest which grow out of they , point the other ? 

 I listen and under similar circumstance I would listen again yes ! with my ear at the keyhole , if I could not possibly manage it in any other way . 

 " you quite understand , Sir Percival , " the lawyer go on . " Lady Glyde be to sign her name in the presence of a witness or of two witness , if you wish to be particularly careful and be then to put her finger on the seal and say , ' I deliver this as my act and deed . ' if that be do in a week 's time the arrangement will be perfectly successful , and the anxiety will be all over . if not " 

 " what do you mean by ' if not ' ? " ask Sir Percival angrily . " if the thing must be do it shall be do . I promise you that , Merriman . " 

 " just so , Sir Percival just so ; but there be two alternative in all transaction , and we lawyer like to look both of they in the face boldly . if through any extraordinary circumstance the arrangement should not be make , I think I may be able to get the party to accept bill at three month . but how the money be to be raise when the bill fall due " 

 " Damn the bill ! the money be only to be get in one way , and in that way , I tell you again , it shall be get . take a glass of wine , Merriman , before you go . " 

 " much oblige , Sir Percival , I have not a moment to lose if I be to catch the up - train . you will let I know as soon as the arrangement be complete ? and you will not forget the caution I recommend " 

 " of course I will not . there be the dog - cart at the door for you . my groom will get you to the station in no time . Benjamin , drive like mad ! jump in . if Mr. Merriman miss the train you lose your place . hold fast , Merriman , and if you be upset trust to the devil to save his own . " with that parting benediction the baronet turn about and walk back to the library . 

 I have not hear much , but the little that have reach my ear be enough to make I feel uneasy . the ' something " that " have happen " be but too plainly a serious money embarrassment , and Sir Percival 's relief from it depend upon Laura . the prospect of see she involve in her husband 's secret difficulty fill I with dismay , exaggerated , no doubt , by my ignorance of business and my settled distrust of Sir Percival . instead of go out , as I propose , I go back immediately to Laura 's room to tell she what I have hear . 

 she receive my bad news so composedly as to surprise I . she evidently know more of her husband 's character and her husband 's embarrassment than I have suspect up to this time . 

 " I fear as much , " she say , " when I hear of that strange gentleman who call , and decline to leave his name . "

 " no , I know no particular . " 

 " you will sign nothing , Laura , without first look at it ? " 

 " certainly not , Marian . whatever I can harmlessly and honestly do to help he I will do for the sake of make your life and mine , love , as easy and as happy as possible . but I will do nothing ignorantly , which we might , one day , have reason to feel ashamed of . let we say no more about it now . you have get your hat on suppose we go and dream away the afternoon in the ground ? " 

 on leave the house we direct our step to the near shade . 

 as we pass an open space among the tree in front of the house , there be Count Fosco , slowly walk backwards and forwards on the grass , sun himself in the full blaze of the hot June afternoon . he have a broad straw hat on , with a violet - colour ribbon round it . a blue blouse , with profuse white fancy - work over the bosom , cover his prodigious body , and be gird about the place where his waist might once have be with a broad scarlet leather belt . nankeen trouser , display more white fancy - work over the ankle , and purple morocco slipper , adorn his low extremity . he be sing Figaro 's famous song in the Barber of Seville , with that crisply fluent vocalisation which be never hear from any other than an italian throat , accompany himself on the concertina , which he play with ecstatic throwing - up of his arm , and graceful twisting and turning of his head , like a fat St. Cecilia masquerade in male attire . " figaro qua ! figaro la ! Figaro su ! Figaro giu ! " sing the Count , jauntily toss up the concertina at arm 's length , and bow to we , on one side of the instrument , with the airy grace and elegance of Figaro himself at twenty year of age . 

 " take my word for it , Laura , that man know something of Sir Percival 's embarrassment , " I say , as we return the Count 's salutation from a safe distance .

" 

 the rest of the day and evening pass quietly enough . the Count and I play at chess . for the first two game he politely allow I to conquer he , and then , when he see that I have find he out , beg my pardon , and at the third game checkmate I in ten minute . I think I can guess I be afraid Laura can guess and I be sure Count Fosco know . I catch Sir Percival look at he for approval more than once in the course of the evening . 


 June 17th . a day of event . I most fervently hope I may not have to add , a day of disaster as well . 

 Sir Percival be as silent at breakfast as he have be the evening before , on the subject of the mysterious " arrangement "   as the lawyer call it   which be hang over our head . an hour afterwards , however , he suddenly enter the morning - room , where his wife and I be wait , with our hat on , for Madame Fosco to join we , and inquire for the Count .

 he chirrup paternally at his small white child through the bar of the pagoda , and we all leave the house for the lake . 

 in the plantation Sir Percival stray away from we . it seem to be part of his restless disposition always to separate himself from his companion on these occasion , and always to occupy himself when he be alone in cut new walking - stick for his own use . the mere act of cut and lop at hazard appear to please he . he have fill the house with walking - stick of his own making , not one of which he ever take up for a second time . when they have be once use his interest in they be all exhaust , and he think of nothing but go on and make more . 

 at the old boat - house he join we again .  the boat - house be large enough to hold we all , but Sir Percival remain outside trim the last new stick with his pocket - axe . we three woman find plenty of room on the large seat .  the morning be windy and cloudy , and the rapid alternation of shadow and sunlight over the waste of the lake make the view look doubly wild , weird , and gloomy . 

 " some people call that picturesque , " say Sir Percival , point over the wide prospect with his half - finish walking - stick . " I call it a blot on a gentleman 's property . in my great - grandfather 's time the lake flow to this place . look at it now ! it be not four foot deep anywhere , and it be all puddle and pool . I wish I could afford to drain it , and plant it all over . my bailiff   a superstitious idiot   say he be quite sure the lake have a curse on it , like the Dead Sea . what do you think , Fosco ? it look just the place for a murder , do not   it ? "

 " Humbug ! " say Sir Percival , cut away fiercely at his stick . " you know what I mean . the dreary scenery , the lonely situation . if you choose to understand I , you can if you do not   choose , I be not go to trouble myself to explain my meaning . "

" he lift one of the white mouse in the palm of his hand , and speak to it in his whimsical way . " the fool 's crime be the crime that be find out , and the wise man 's crime be the crime that be not find out . if I could give you an instance , it would not be the instance of a wise man . Dear Lady Glyde , your sound english common sense have be too much for I . it be checkmate for I this time , Miss Halcombe ha ? "

 Sir Percival burst out laugh , so violently , so outrageously , that he quite startle we all the count more than any of we .

 " it be truly wonderful , " he say , " how easily Society can console itself for the bad of its shortcoming with a little bit of clap - trap . the machinery it have set up for the detection of crime be miserably ineffective and yet only invent a moral epigram , say that it work well , and you blind everybody to its blunder from that moment . crime cause their own detection , do they ? and murder will out   another moral epigram , will it ? ask Coroners who sit at inquest in large town if that be true , Lady Glyde . ask secretary of life - assurance company if that be true , Miss Halcombe . read your own public journal . in the few case that get into the newspaper , be there not instance of slain body find , and no murderer ever discover ? Multiply the case that be report by the case that be not report , and the body that be find by the body that be not find , and what conclusion do you come to ? this . that there be foolish criminal who be discover , and wise criminal who escape . the hiding of a crime , or the detection of a crime , what be it ? a trial of skill between the police on one side , and the individual on the other . when the criminal be a brutal , ignorant fool , the police in nine case out of ten win . when the criminal be a resolute , educate , highly - intelligent man , the police in nine case out of ten lose . if the police win , you generally hear all about it . if the police lose , you generally hear nothing . and on this tottering foundation you build up your comfortable moral maxim that Crime cause its own detection ! yes all the crime you know of . and what of the rest ? " 

 " devilish true , and very well put , " cry a voice at the entrance of the boat - house . Sir Percival have recover his equanimity , and have come back while we be listen to the Count .

" accept your illustration , surely we have one unquestionable virtue in England which be want in China . the chinese authority kill thousand of innocent people on the most frivolous pretext . we in England be free from all guilt of that kind we commit no such dreadful crime we abhor reckless bloodshed with all our heart . John Bull do abhor the crime of John Chinaman . he be the quick old gentleman at find out fault that be his neighbour ' , and the slow old gentleman at find out the fault that be his own , who exist on the face of creation . be he so very much well in this way than the people whom he condemn in their way ? English Society , Miss Halcombe , be as often the accomplice as it be the enemy of crime . yes ! yes ! crime be in this country what crime be in other country a good friend to a man and to those about he as often as it be an enemy . a great rascal provide for his wife and family . the bad he be the more he make they the object for your sympathy . he often provide also for himself . a profligate spendthrift who be always borrow money will get more from his friend than the rigidly honest man who only borrow of they once , under pressure of the dire want . in the one case the friend will not be at all surprised , and they will give . in the other case they will be very much surprised , and they will hesitate . be the prison that Mr. Scoundrel live in at the end of his career a more uncomfortable place than the workhouse that Mr. Honesty live in at the end of his career ? when John - Howard - Philanthropist want to relieve misery he go to find it in prison , where crime be wretched not in hut and hovel , where virtue be wretche too . who be the english poet who have win the most universal sympathy who make the easy of all subject for pathetic writing and pathetic painting ? that nice young person who begin life with a forgery , and end it by a suicide your dear , romantic , interesting Chatterton . which get on well , do you think , of two poor starving dressmaker the woman who resist temptation and be honest , or the woman who fall under temptation and steal ? you all know that the stealing be the making of that second woman 's fortune it advertise she from length to breadth of good - humour , charitable England and she be relieve , as the breaker of a commandment , when she would have be leave to starve , as the keeper of it . come here , my jolly little Mouse ! hey ! presto ! pass ! I transform you , for the time be , into a respectable lady . stop there , in the palm of my great big hand , my dear , and listen . you marry the poor man whom you love , Mouse , and one half your friend pity , and the other half blame you . and now , on the contrary , you sell yourself for gold to a man you do not   care for , and all your friend rejoice over you , and a minister of public worship sanction the base horror of the vilest of all human bargain , and smile and smirk afterwards at your table , if you be polite enough to ask he to breakfast . hey ! presto ! pass ! be a mouse again , and squeak . if you continue to be a lady much long , I shall have you tell I that society abhor crime and then , Mouse , I shall doubt if your own eye and ear be really of any use to you . ah ! I be a bad man , Lady Glyde , be I not ? I say what other people only think , and when all the rest of the world be in a conspiracy to accept the mask for the true face , mine be the rash hand that tear off the plump pasteboard , and show the bare bone beneath . I will get up on my big elephant 's leg , before I do myself any more harm in your amiable estimation I will get up and take a little airy walk of my own . dear lady , as your excellent Sheridan say , I go and leave my character behind I . " 

 he get up , put the cage on the table , and pause for a moment to count the mouse in it . " one , two , three , four ha ! " he cry , with a look of horror , " where , in the name of Heaven , be the fifth the young , the whitest , the most amiable of all my Benjamin of mouse ! " 

 neither Laura nor I be in any favorable disposition to be amuse . the Count 's glib cynicism have reveal a new aspect of his nature from which we both recoil . but it be impossible to resist the comical distress of so very large a man at the loss of so very small a mouse . we laugh in spite of ourselves ; and when Madame Fosco rise to set the example of leave the boat - house empty , so that her husband might search it to its remotest corner , we rise also to follow she out .

 the housekeeper 's report of Mrs. Catherick 's desire to conceal her visit to Blackwater Park from Sir Percival 's knowledge recur to my memory the moment he put that last question , and I half doubt the discretion of answer it ; but in my anxiety to quiet the general alarm , I have thoughtlessly advance too far to draw back , except at the risk of exciting suspicion , which might only make matter bad . there be nothing for it but to answer at once , without reference to result .

 the manner in which he put the question be even more offensive than the language in which he express it . I mark my sense of his want of common politeness by silently turn away from he .

Laura , however , unconsciously help he to extract all my information , by make inquiry herself , which leave I no alternative but to reply to she , or to appear in the very unenviable and very false character of a depositary of Sir Percival 's secret . the end of it be , that , in about ten minute ' time , the Count know as much as I know of Mrs. Catherick , and of the event which have so strangely connect we with her daughter , Anne , from the time when Hartright meet with she to this day .

 " and your shin courageous Brown Molly for the long ? "

 when Sir Percival be in Cumberland   I think to myself , he walk away a long distance , on Anne 's account , to question the family at Todd 's Corner . now he be in Hampshire , be he go to drive away a long distance , on Anne 's account again , to question Mrs. Catherick at Welmingham ? 

 we all enter the house . as we cross the hall Sir Percival come out from the library to meet we . he look hurried and pale and anxious but for all that , he be in his most polite mood when he speak to we . 

 " I be sorry to say I be oblige to leave you , " he begin " a long drive a matter that I can not   very well put off . I shall be back in good time to - morrow but before I go I should like that little business - formality , which I speak of this morning , to be settle . Laura , will you come into the library ? it will not   take a minute a mere formality . Countess , may I trouble you also ? I want you and the Countess , Fosco , to be witness to a signature nothing more . come in at once and get it over . " 

 he hold the library door open until they have pass in , follow they , and shut it softly . 

 I remain , for a moment afterwards , stand alone in the hall , with my heart beat fast and my mind misgive I sadly . then I go on to the staircase , and ascend slowly to my own room . 



 IV 

 June 17th . just as my hand be on the door of my room , I hear Sir Percival 's voice call to I from below . 

 " I must beg you to come downstairs again , " he say . " it be Fosco 's fault , Miss Halcombe , not mine . he have start some nonsensical objection to his wife be one of the witness , and have oblige I to ask you to join we in the library . " 

 I enter the room immediately with Sir Percival . Laura be wait by the writing - table , twist and turn her garden hat uneasily in her hand . Madame Fosco sit near she , in an arm - chair , imperturbably admire her husband , who stand by himself at the other end of the library , pick off the dead leaf from the flower in the window . 

 the moment I appear the Count advanced to meet I , and to offer his explanation . 

 " a thousand pardon , Miss Halcombe , " he say . " you know the character which be give to my countryman by the English ? we Italians be all wily and suspicious by nature , in the estimation of the good John Bull . set I down , if you please , as be no well than the rest of my race . I be a wily italian and a suspicious italian . you have think so yourself , dear lady , have you not ? well ! it be part of my wiliness and part of my suspicion to object to Madame Fosco be a witness to Lady Glyde 's signature , when I be also a witness myself . " 

 " there be not the shadow of a reason for his objection , " interpose Sir Percival . " I have explain to he that the law of England allow Madame Fosco to witness a signature as well as her husband . " 

 " I admit it , " resume the Count . " the law of England say , yes , but the conscience of Fosco say , no . " he spread out his fat finger on the bosom of his blouse , and bow solemnly , as if he wish to introduce his conscience to we all , in the character of an illustrious addition to the society . " what this document which Lady Glyde be about to sign may be , " he continue , " I neither know nor desire to know . I only say this , circumstance may happen in the future which may oblige Percival , or his representative , to appeal to the two witness , in which case it be certainly desirable that those witness should represent two opinion which be perfectly independent the one of the other . this can not be if my wife sign as well as myself , because we have but one opinion between we , and that opinion be mine . I will not have it cast in my tooth , at some future day , that Madame Fosco act under my coercion , and be , in plain fact , no witness at all . I speak in Percival 's interest , when I propose that my name shall appear   as the near friend of the husband , and your name , Miss Halcombe   as the near friend of the wife . I be a Jesuit , if you please to think so a splitter of straw a man of trifle and crochet and scruple but you will humour I , I hope , in merciful consideration for my suspicious italian character , and my uneasy italian conscience . " he bow again , step back a few pace , and withdraw his conscience from our society as politely as he have introduce it . 

 the Count 's scruple might have be honourable and reasonable enough , but there be something in his manner of express they which increase my unwillingness to be concern in the business of the signature . no consideration of less importance than my consideration for Laura would have induce I to consent to be a witness at all . one look , however , at her anxious face decide I to risk anything rather than desert she . 

 " I will readily remain in the room , " I say . " and if I find no reason for start any small scruple on my side , you may rely on I as a witness . "

 Madame Fosco look for her order again , get they again , say she would prefer leave we to our business , and resolutely walk out . the Count light a cigarette , go back to the flower in the window , and puff little jet of smoke at the leaf , in a state of the deep anxiety about kill the insect . 

 meanwhile Sir Percival unlock a cupboard beneath one of the book - case , and produce from it a piece of parchment , fold longwise , many time over . he place it on the table , open the last fold only , and keep his hand on the rest . the last fold display a strip of blank parchment with little wafer stick on it at certain place . every line of the writing be hide in the part which he still hold fold up under his hand . Laura and I look at each other . her face be pale , but it show no indecision and no fear .

" you and Fosco be to sign afterwards , Miss Halcombe , opposite those two wafer . come here , Fosco ! witness a signature be not to be do by moon out of window and smoking into the flower . "

 " what be it I be to sign ? " she ask quietly . 

 " I have no time to explain , " he answer . " the dog - cart be at the door , and I must go directly . besides , if I have time , you would not   understand . it be a purely formal document , full of legal technicality , and all that sort of thing . come ! come ! sign your name , and let we have do as soon as possible . "

 " nonsense ! what have woman to do with business ? I tell you again , you can not   understand it . " 

 " at any rate , let I try to understand it . whenever Mr. Gilmore have any business for I to do , he always explain it first , and I always understand he . "

 she still have the pen in her hand , but she make no approach to sign her name with it . 

 " if my signature pledge I to anything , " she say , ' surely I have some claim to know what that pledge be ? " 

 he lift up the parchment , and strike it angrily on the table . 

 " speak out ! " he say . " you be always famous for tell the truth . never mind Miss Halcombe , never mind Fosco say , in plain term , you distrust I . " 

 the Count take one of his hand out of his belt and lay it on Sir Percival 's shoulder . Sir Percival shake it off irritably . the Count put it on again with unruffled composure . 

 " control your unfortunate temper , Percival , " he say " Lady Glyde be right . "

 I have not speak hitherto , and I would much rather not have speak now . but the expression of distress in Laura 's face when she turn it towards I , and the insolent injustice of her husband 's conduct , leave I no other alternative than to give my opinion , for her sake , as soon as I be ask for it .

Laura 's objection seem to I a perfectly fair one , and speak for myself only , I can not assume the responsibility of witness her signature , unless she first understand what the writing be which you wish she to sign . "

 the Count , who have watch Laura and I with a close and silent attention , interpose for the second time .

 Sir Percival turn on he speechless with passion . the Count 's firm hand slowly tighten its grasp on his shoulder , and the Count 's steady voice quietly repeat , " be good enough , if you please , to remember it too . " 

 they both look at each other . I have tell she this be merely a formal document and what more can she want ? you may say what you please , but it be no part of a woman 's duty to set her husband at defiance . once more , Lady Glyde , and for the last time , will you sign or will you not ? "

 " I will sign with pleasure , " she say , " if you will only treat I as a responsible being . I care little what sacrifice be require of I , if it will affect no one else , and lead to no ill result "

" 

 this unfortunate , yet most natural , reference to the Count 's extraordinary power over her husband , indirect as it be , set Sir Percival 's smoulder temper on fire again in an instant .

 the Count , who be no stranger , see it as distinctly as I do . when I leave my chair to join Laura , I hear he whisper under his breath to Sir Percival , " you idiot ! " 

 Laura walk before I to the door as I advance , and at the same time her husband speak to she once more . 

 " you positively refuse , then , to give I your signature ? " he say , in the altered tone of a man who be conscious that he have let his own licence of language seriously injure he . 

 " after what you have just say to I , " she reply firmly , " I refuse my signature until I have read every line in that parchment from the first word to the last . come away , Marian , we have remain here long enough . " 

 " one moment ! " interpose the count before Sir Percival could speak again " one moment , Lady Glyde , I implore you ! " 

 Laura would have leave the room without notice he , but I stop she . 

 " do not   make an enemy of the Count ! " I whisper . " whatever you do , do not   make an enemy of the Count ! " 

 she yield to I . I close the door again , and we stand near it wait . Sir Percival sit down at the table , with his elbow on the fold parchment , and his head rest on his clench fist . the Count stand between we master of the dreadful position in which we be place , as he be master of everything else .

" he turn sharply towards Sir Percival . " be it absolutely necessary , " he ask " that this thing here , under your elbow , should be sign to - day ? " 

 " it be necessary to my plan and wish , " return the other sulkily . " but that consideration , as you may have notice , have no influence with Lady Glyde . " 

 " answer my plain question plainly . can the business of the signature be put off till to - morrow yes or no ? " 

 " yes , if you will have it so . " 

 " then what be you waste your time for here ? let the signature wait till to - morrow let it wait till you come back . " 

 Sir Percival look up with a frown and an oath . 

 " you be take a tone with I that I do not   like , " he say . " a tone I will not   bear from any man . "

" give yourself time give Lady Glyde time . have you forget that your dog - cart be wait at the door ? my tone surprise you ha ? I dare say it do it be the tone of a man who can keep his temper . how many dos of good advice have I give you in my time ? More than you can count . have I ever be wrong ? I defy you to quote I an instance of it . go ! take your drive . the matter of the signature can wait till to - morrow . let it wait and renew it when you come back . " 

 Sir Percival hesitate and look at his watch . his anxiety about the secret journey which he be to take that day , revive by the Count 's word , be now evidently dispute possession of his mind with his anxiety to obtain Laura 's signature . he consider for a little while , and then get up from his chair . 

 " it be easy to argue I down , " he say , " when I have no time to answer you . I will take your advice , Fosco not because I want it , or believe in it , but because I can not   stop here any long . " he pause , and look round darkly at his wife . " if you do not   give I your signature when I come back to - morrow ! " the rest be lose in the noise of his opening the book - case cupboard again , and lock up the parchment once more . he take his hat and glove off the table , and make for the door .  we wait to give he time to cross the hall and drive away . the Count approach we while we be stand near the door .

I thank the count civilly , and let she out . yes ! I thank he : for I feel already , with a sense of inexpressible helplessness and humiliation , that it be either his interest or his caprice to make sure of my continue to reside at Blackwater Park , and I know after Sir Percival 's conduct to I , that without the support of the Count 's influence , I could not hope to remain there . his influence , the influence of all other that I dread most , be actually the one tie which now hold I to Laura in the hour of her utmost need ! 

 we hear the wheel of the dog - cart crash on the gravel of the drive as we come into the hall . Sir Percival have start on his journey .

 " certainly not , " I reply . " she must be quite as ignorant as we be . " 

 Laura shake her head doubtfully .
