" she rise in sudden agitation , and walk about the room . " I have keep many thing from your knowledge , Marian , for fear of distress you , and make you unhappy at the outset of our new life . you do not   know how he have use I . and yet you ought to know , for you see how he use I to - day . you hear he sneer at my presuming to be scrupulous you hear he say I have make a virtue of necessity in marry he . " she sit down again , her face flush deeply , and her hand twist and twin together in her lap . " I can not   tell you about it now , " she say ; " I shall burst out cry if I tell you now later , Marian , when I be more sure of myself . my poor head ache , darling ache , ache , ache . where be your smell - bottle ? let I talk to you about yourself . I wish I have give he my signature , for your sake . shall I give it to he to - morrow ? I would rather compromise myself than compromise you . after your take my part against he , he will lay all the blame on you if I refuse again . what shall we do ? oh , for a friend to help we and advise we ! a friend we could really trust ! " 

 she sigh bitterly . I see in her face that she be think of Hartright see it the more plainly because her last word set I think of he too . in six month only from her marriage we want the faithful service he have offer to we in his farewell word . how little I once think that we should ever want it at all ! 

 " we must do what we can to help ourselves , " I say . " let we try to talk it over calmly , Laura let we do all in our power to decide for the good . " 

 put what she know of her husband 's embarrassment and what I have hear of his conversation with the lawyer together , we arrive necessarily at the conclusion that the parchment in the library have be draw up for the purpose of borrow money , and that Laura 's signature be absolutely necessary to fit it for the attainment of Sir Percival 's object . 

 the second question , concern the nature of the legal contract by which the money be to be obtain , and the degree of personal responsibility to which Laura might subject herself if she sign it in the dark , involve consideration which lie far beyond any knowledge and experience that either of we possess . my own conviction lead I to believe that the hidden content of the parchment conceal a transaction of the mean and the most fraudulent kind . 

 I have not form this conclusion in consequence of Sir Percival 's refusal to show the writing or to explain it , for that refusal might well have proceed from his obstinate disposition and his domineering temper alone . my sole motive for distrust his honesty spring from the change which I have observe in his language and his manner at Blackwater Park , a change which convince I that he have be act a part throughout the whole period of his probation at Limmeridge House . his elaborate delicacy , his ceremonious politeness which harmonise so agreeably with Mr. Gilmore 's old - fashioned notion , his modesty with Laura , his candour with I , his moderation with Mr. Fairlie all these be the artifice of a mean , cunning , and brutal man , who have drop his disguise when his practised duplicity have gain its end , and have openly show himself in the library on that very day . I say nothing of the grief which this discovery cause I on Laura 's account , for it be not to be express by any word of mine . I only refer to it at all , because it decide I to oppose her sign the parchment , whatever the consequence might be , unless she be first make acquainted with the content . 

 under these circumstance , the one chance for we when to - morrow come be to be provide with an objection to give the signature , which might rest on sufficiently firm commercial or legal ground to shake Sir Percival 's resolution , and to make he suspect that we two woman understand the law and obligation of business as well as himself . 

 after some ponder , I determine to write to the only honest man within reach whom we could trust to help we discreetly in our forlorn situation . that man be Mr. Gilmore 's partner , Mr. Kyrle , who conduct the business now that our old friend have be oblige to withdraw from it , and to leave London on account of his health . I explain to Laura that I have Mr. Gilmore 's own authority for place implicit confidence in his partner 's integrity , discretion , and accurate knowledge of all her affair , and with her full approval I sit down at once to write the letter , I begin by state our position to Mr. Kyrle exactly as it be , and then ask for his advice in return , express in plain , downright term which he could comprehend without any danger of misinterpretation and mistake . my letter be as short as I could possibly make it , and be , I hope , unencumbered by needless apology and needless detail . 

 just as I be about to put the address on the envelope an obstacle be discover by Laura , which in the effort and preoccupation of writing have escape my mind altogether . 

 " how be we to get the answer in time ? " she ask . " your letter will not be deliver in London before to - morrow morning , and the post will not bring the reply here till the morning after . " 

 the only way of overcome this difficulty be to have the answer bring to we from the lawyer 's office by a special messenger . I write a postscript to that effect , beg that the messenger might be despatch with the reply by the eleven o'clock morning train , which would bring he to our station at twenty minute past one , and so enable he to reach Blackwater Park by two o'clock at the late . he be to be direct to ask for I , to answer no question address to he by any one else , and to deliver his letter into no hand but mine . 

 " in case Sir Percival should come back to - morrow before two o'clock , " I say to Laura , " the wise plan for you to adopt be to be out in the ground all the morning with your book or your work , and not to appear at the house till the messenger have have time to arrive with the letter . I will wait here for he all the morning , to guard against any misadventure or mistake . by follow this arrangement I hope and believe we shall avoid be take by surprise . let we go down to the drawing - room now . we may excite suspicion if we remain shut up together too long . " 

 " Suspicion ? " she repeat . " whose suspicion can we excite , now that Sir Percival have leave the house ? do you mean Count Fosco ? " 

 " perhaps I do , Laura . " 

 " you be begin to dislike he as much as I do , Marian . " 

 " no , not to dislike he . dislike be always more or less associate with contempt I can see nothing in the Count to despise . " 

 " you be not afraid of he , be you ? " 

 " perhaps I be a little . " 

 " Afraid of he , after his interference in our favour to - day ! " 

 " yes . I be more afraid of his interference than I be of Sir Percival 's violence . remember what I say to you in the library . whatever you do , Laura , do not   make an enemy of the count ! " 

 we go downstairs . Laura enter the drawing - room , while I proceed across the hall , with my letter in my hand , to put it into the post - bag , which hang against the wall opposite to I . 

 the house door be open , and as I cross past it , I see Count Fosco and his wife stand talk together on the step outside , with their face turn towards I . 

 the Countess come into the hall rather hastily , and ask if I have leisure enough for five minute ' private conversation . feel a little surprised by such an appeal from such a person , I put my letter into the bag , and reply that I be quite at her disposal . she take my arm with unaccustomed friendliness and familiarity , and instead of lead I into an empty room , draw I out with she to the belt of turf which surround the large fish - pond . 

 as we pass the Count on the step he bow and smile , and then go at once into the house , push the hall door to after he , but not actually close it . 

 the Countess walk I gently round the fish - pond . I expect to be make the depositary of some extraordinary confidence , and I be astonish to find that Madame Fosco 's communication for my private ear be nothing more than a polite assurance of her sympathy for I , after what have happen in the library . her husband have tell she of all that have pass , and of the insolent manner in which Sir Percival have speak to I . this information have so shocked and distress she , on my account and on Laura 's , that she have make up her mind , if anything of the sort happen again , to mark her sense of Sir Percival 's outrageous conduct by leave the house . the Count have approve of her idea , and she now hop that I approve of it too . 

 I think this a very strange proceeding on the part of such a remarkably reserve woman as Madame Fosco , especially after the interchange of sharp speech which have pass between we during the conversation in the boat - house on that very morning . however , it be my plain duty to meet a polite and friendly advance on the part of one of my elder with a polite and friendly reply . I answer the Countess accordingly in her own tone , and then , think we have say all that be necessary on either side , make an attempt to get back to the house . 

 but Madame Fosco seem resolve not to part with I , and to my unspeakable amazement , resolve also to talk . Hitherto the most silent of woman , she now persecute I with fluent conventionality on the subject of married life , on the subject of Sir Percival and Laura , on the subject of her own happiness , on the subject of the late Mr. Fairlie 's conduct to she in the matter of her legacy , and on half a dozen other subject besides , until she have detain I walk round and round the fish - pond for more than half an hour , and have quite weary I out . whether she discover this or not , I can not say , but she stop as abruptly as she have begin look towards the house door , resume her icy manner in a moment , and drop my arm of her own accord before I could think of an excuse for accomplish my own release from she . 

 as I push open the door and enter the hall , I find myself suddenly face to face with the Count again . he be just put a letter into the post - bag . 

 after he have drop it in and have close the bag , he ask I where I have leave Madame Fosco . I tell he , and he go out at the hall door immediately to join his wife . his manner when he speak to I be so unusually quiet and subdue that I turn and look after he , wonder if he be ill or out of spirit . 

 why my next proceeding be to go straight up to the post - bag and take out my own letter and look at it again , with a vague distrust on I , and why the look at it for the second time instantly suggest the idea to my mind of seal the envelope for its great security be mystery which be either too deep or too shallow for I to fathom . woman , as everybody know , constantly act on impulse which they can not explain even to themselves , and I can only suppose that one of those impulse be the hidden cause of my unaccountable conduct on this occasion . 

 whatever influence animate I , I find cause to congratulate myself on have obey it as soon as I prepare to seal the letter in my own room . I have originally close the envelope in the usual way by moisten the adhesive point and press it on the paper beneath , and when I now try it with my finger , after a lapse of full three - quarter of an hour , the envelope open on the instant , without stick or tearing . perhaps I have fasten it insufficiently ? perhaps there might have be some defect in the adhesive gum ? 

 or , perhaps no ! it be quite revolt enough to feel that third conjecture stir in my mind . I would rather not see it confront I in plain black and white . 

 I almost dread to - morrow so much depend on my discretion and self - control . there be two precaution , at all event , which I be sure not to forget . I must be careful to keep up friendly appearance with the Count , and I must be well on my guard when the messenger from the office come here with the answer to my letter . 



 V 

 June 17th . when the dinner hour bring we together again , Count Fosco be in his usual excellent spirit . he exert himself to interest and amuse we , as if he be determined to efface from our memory all recollection of what have pass in the library that afternoon . lively description of his adventure in travel , amusing anecdote of remarkable people whom he have meet with abroad , quaint comparison between the social custom of various nation , illustrate by example draw from man and woman indiscriminately all over Europe , humorous confession of the innocent folly of his own early life , when he rule the fashion of a second - rate italian town , and write preposterous romance on the french model for a second - rate italian newspaper all flow in succession so easily and so gaily from his lip , and all address our various curiosity and various interest so directly and so delicately , that Laura and I listen to he with as much attention and , inconsistent as it may seem , with as much admiration also , as Madame Fosco herself . woman can resist a man 's love , a man 's fame , a man 's personal appearance , and a man 's money , but they can not resist a man 's tongue when he know how to talk to they . 

 after dinner , while the favourable impression which he have produce on we be still vivid in our mind , the Count modestly withdraw to read in the library . 

 Laura propose a stroll in the ground to enjoy the close of the long evening . it be necessary in common politeness to ask Madame Fosco to join we , but this time she have apparently receive her order beforehand , and she beg we would kindly excuse she . " the Count will probably want a fresh supply of cigarette , " she remark by way of apology , " and nobody can make they to his satisfaction but myself . " her cold blue eye almost warm as she speak the word she look actually proud of be the officiating medium through which her lord and master compose himself with tobacco - smoke ! 

 Laura and I go out together alone . 

 it be a misty , heavy evening . there be a sense of blight in the air ; the flower be droop in the garden , and the ground be parch and dewless . the western heaven , as we see it over the quiet tree , be of a pale yellow hue , and the sun be set faintly in a haze . come rain seem near it would fall probably with the fall of night . 

 " which way shall we go ? " I ask 

 " towards the lake , Marian , if you like , " she answer . 

 " you seem unaccountably fond , Laura , of that dismal lake . " 

 " no , not of the lake but of the scenery about it . the sand and heath and the fir - tree be the only object I can discover , in all this large place , to remind I of Limmeridge . but we will walk in some other direction if you prefer it . " 

 " I have no favourite walk at Blackwater Park , my love . one be the same as another to I . let we go to the lake we may find it cool in the open space than we find it here . " 

 we walk through the shadowy plantation in silence . the heaviness in the evening air oppress we both , and when we reach the boat - house we be glad to sit down and rest inside . 

 a white fog hung low over the lake . the dense brown line of the tree on the opposite bank appear above it , like a dwarf forest float in the sky . the sandy ground , shelve downward from where we sit , be lose mysteriously in the outward layer of the fog . the silence be horrible . no rustling of the leaf no bird 's note in the wood no cry of water - fowl from the pool of the hidden lake . even the croaking of the frog have cease to - night . 

 " it be very desolate and gloomy , " say Laura . " but we can be more alone here than anywhere else . " 

 she speak quietly and look at the wilderness of sand and mist with steady , thoughtful eye . I could see that her mind be too much occupy to feel the dreary impression from without which have fasten themselves already on mine . 

 " I promise , Marian , to tell you the truth about my married life , instead of leave you any long to guess it for yourself , " she begin . " that secret be the first I have ever have from you , love , and I be determined it shall be the last . I be silent , as you know , for your sake and perhaps a little for my own sake as well . it be very hard for a woman to confess that the man to whom she have give she whole life be the man of all other who care least for the gift . if you be marry yourself , Marian and especially if you be happily married you would feel for I as no single woman can feel , however kind and true she may be . " 

 what answer could I make ? I could only take her hand and look at she with my whole heart as well as my eye would let I . 

 " how often , " she go on , " I have hear you laugh over what you use to call your ' poverty ! ' how often you have make I mock - speech of congratulation on my wealth ! oh , Marian , never laugh again . thank God for your poverty it have make you your own mistress , and have save you from the lot that have fall on I . " 

 a sad beginning on the lip of a young wife ! sad in its quiet plain - speak truth . the few day we have all pass together at Blackwater Park have be many enough to show I to show any one what her husband have marry she for . 

 " you shall not be distress , " she say , " by hear how soon my disappointment and my trial begin or even by know what they be . it be bad enough to have they on my memory . if I tell you how he receive the first and last attempt at remonstrance that I ever make , you will know how he have always treat I , as well as if I have describe it in so many word . it be one day at Rome when we have ride out together to the tomb of Cecilia Metella . the sky be calm and lovely , and the grand old ruin look beautiful , and the remembrance that a husband 's love have raise it in the old time to a wife 's memory , make I feel more tenderly and more anxiously towards my husband than I have ever feel yet . ' would you build such a tomb for ME , Percival ? ' I ask he . ' you say you love I dearly before we be marry , and yet , since that time ' I could get no far . marian ! he be not even look at I ! I pull down my veil , think it well not to let he see that the tear be in my eye . I fancy he have not pay any attention to I , but he have . he say , ' come away , ' and laugh to himself as he help I on to my horse . he mount his own horse and laugh again as we ride away . ' if I do build you a tomb , ' he say , ' it will be do with your own money . I wonder whether Cecilia Metella have a fortune and pay for her . ' I make no reply how could I , when I be cry behind my veil ? ' ah , you light - complexion woman be all sulky , ' he say . ' what do you want ? compliment and soft speech ? well ! I be in a good humour this morning . consider the compliment pay and the speech say . ' man little know when they say hard thing to we how well we remember they , and how much harm they do we . it would have be well for I if I have go on crying , but his contempt dry up my tear and harden my heart . from that time , Marian , I never check myself again in thinking of Walter Hartright . I let the memory of those happy day , when we be so fond of each other in secret , come back and comfort I . what else have I to look to for consolation ? if we have be together you would have help I to well thing . I know it be wrong , darling , but tell I if I be wrong without any excuse . " 

 I be oblige to turn my face from she . " do not   ask I ! " I say . " have I suffer as you have suffer ? what right have I to decide ? " 

 " I use to think of he , " she pursue , drop her voice and move close to I , " I use to think of he when Percival leave I alone at night to go among the Opera people . I use to fancy what I might have be if it have please God to bless I with poverty , and if I have be his wife . I use to see myself in my neat cheap gown , sit at home and wait for he while he be earn our bread sit at home and work for he and love he all the well because I have to work for he see he come in tired and take off his hat and coat for he , and , Marian , please he with little dish at dinner that I have learn to make for his sake . oh ! I hope he be never lonely enough and sad enough to think of I and see I as I have think of HIM and see HIM ! " 

 as she say those melancholy word , all the lose tenderness return to her voice , and all the lose beauty tremble back into her face . her eye rest as lovingly on the blighted , solitary , ill - omen view before we , as if they see the friendly hill of Cumberland in the dim and threatening sky . 

 " do not   speak of Walter any more , " I say , as soon as I could control myself . " oh , Laura , spare we both the wretchedness of talk of he now ! " 

 she rouse herself , and look at I tenderly . 

 " I would rather be silent about he for ever , " she answer , " than cause you a moment 's pain . " 

 " it be in your interest , " I plead ; " it be for your sake that I speak . if your husband hear you " 

 " it would not surprise he if he do hear I . " 

 she make that strange reply with a weary calmness and coldness . the change in her manner , when she give the answer , startle I almost as much as the answer itself . 

 " not surprise he ! " I repeat . " Laura ! remember what you be say you frighten I ! " 

 " it be true , " she say ; " it be what I want to tell you to - day , when we be talk in your room . my only secret when I open my heart to he at Limmeridge be a harmless secret , Marian you say so yourself . the name be all I keep from he , and he have discover it . " 

 I hear she , but I could say nothing . her last word have kill the little hope that still live in I . 

 " it happen at Rome , " she go on , as wearily calm and cold as ever . " we be at a little party give to the English by some friend of Sir Percival 's Mr. and Mrs. Markland . Mrs. Markland have the reputation of sketch very beautifully , and some of the guest prevail on she to show we her drawing . we all admire they , but something I say attract her attention particularly to I . ' surely you draw yourself ? ' she ask . ' I use to draw a little once , ' I answer , ' but I have give it up . ' ' if you have once draw , ' she say , ' you may take to it again one of these day , and if you do , I wish you would let I recommend you a master . ' I say nothing you know why , Marian and try to change the conversation . but Mrs. Markland persist . ' I have have all sort of teacher , ' she go on , ' but the good of all , the most intelligent and the most attentive , be a Mr. Hartright . if you ever take up your drawing again , do try he as a master . he be a young man modest and gentlemanlike I be sure you will like he . ' think of those word be speak to I publicly , in the presence of stranger stranger who have be invite to meet the bride and bridegroom ! I do all I could to control myself I say nothing , and look down close at the drawing . when I venture to raise my head again , my eye and my husband 's eye meet , and I know , by his look , that my face have betray I . ' we will see about Mr. Hartright , ' he say , look at I all the time , ' when we get back to England . I agree with you , Mrs. Markland I think Lady Glyde be sure to like he . ' he lay an emphasis on the last word which make my cheek burn , and set my heart beat as if it would stifle I . nothing more be say . we come away early . he be silent in the carriage drive back to the hotel . he help I out , and follow I upstairs as usual . but the moment we be in the drawing - room , he lock the door , push I down into a chair , and stand over I with his hand on my shoulder . ' ever since that morning when you make your audacious confession to I at Limmeridge , ' he say , ' I have want to find out the man , and I find he in your face to - night . your drawing - master be the man , and his name be Hartright . you shall repent it , and he shall repent it , to the last hour of your life . now go to bed and dream of he if you like , with the mark of my horsewhip on his shoulder . ' whenever he be angry with I now he refer to what I acknowledge to he in your presence with a sneer or a threat . I have no power to prevent he from put his own horrible construction on the confidence I place in he . I have no influence to make he believe I , or to keep he silent . you look surprised to - day when you hear he tell I that I have make a virtue of necessity in marry he . you will not be surprised again when you hear he repeat it , the next time he be out of temper Oh , Marian ! do not ! do not ! you hurt I ! " 

 I have catch she in my arm , and the sting and torment of my remorse have close they round she like a vice . yes ! my remorse . the white despair of Walter 's face , when my cruel word strike he to the heart in the summer - house at Limmeridge , rise before I in mute , unendurable reproach . my hand have point the way which lead the man my sister love , step by step , far from his country and his friend . between those two young heart I have stand , to sunder they for ever , the one from the other , and his life and her life lie waste before I alike in witness of the deed . I have do this , and do it for Sir Percival Glyde . 

 for Sir Percival Glyde . 


 I hear her speak , and I know by the tone of her voice that she be comfort I I , who deserve nothing but the reproach of her silence ! how long it be before I master the absorb misery of my own thought , I can not tell . I be first conscious that she be kiss I , and then my eye seem to wake on a sudden to their sense of outward thing , and I know that I be look mechanically straight before I at the prospect of the lake . 

 " it be late , " I hear her whisper . " it will be dark in the plantation . " she shake my arm and repeat , " Marian ! it will be dark in the plantation . " 

 " give I a minute long , " I say " a minute , to get well in . " 

 I be afraid to trust myself to look at she yet , and I keep my eye fix on the view . 

 it be late . the dense brown line of tree in the sky have fade in the gathering darkness to the faint resemblance of a long wreath of smoke . the mist over the lake below have stealthily enlarge , and advance on we . the silence be as breathless as ever , but the horror of it have go , and the solemn mystery of its stillness be all that remain . 

 " we be far from the house , " she whisper . " let we go back . " 

 she stop suddenly , and turn her face from I towards the entrance of the boat - house . 

 " Marian ! " she say , tremble violently . " do you see nothing ? look ! " 

 " where ? " 

 " down there , below we . " 

 she point . my eye follow her hand , and I see it too . 

 a living figure be move over the waste of heath in the distance . it cross our range of view from the boat - house , and pass darkly along the outer edge of the mist . it stop far off , in front of we wait and pass on ; move slowly , with the white cloud of mist behind it and above it slowly , slowly , till it glide by the edge of the boat - house , and we see it no more . 

 we be both unnerved by what have pass between we that evening . some minute elapse before Laura would venture into the plantation , and before I could make up my mind to lead she back to the house . 

 " be it a man or a woman ? " she ask in a whisper , as we move at last into the dark dampness of the outer air . 

 " I be not certain . " 

 " which do you think ? " 

 " it look like a woman . " 

 " I be afraid it be a man in a long cloak . " 

 " it may be a man . in this dim light it be not possible to be certain . " 

 " wait , Marian ! I be frightened I do not   see the path . suppose the figure should follow we ? " 

 " not at all likely , Laura . there be really nothing to be alarmed about . the shore of the lake be not far from the village , and they be free to any one to walk on by day or night . it be only wonderful we have see no living creature there before . " 

 we be now in the plantation . it be very dark so dark , that we find some difficulty in keep the path . I give Laura my arm , and we walk as fast as we could on our way back . 

 before we be half - way through she stop , and force I to stop with she . she be listen . 

 " hush , " she whisper . " I hear something behind we . " 

 " dead leaf , " I say to cheer she , " or a twig blow off the tree . " 

 " it be summer time , Marian , and there be not a breath of wind . listen ! " 

 I hear the sound too a sound like a light footstep follow we . 

 " no matter who it be , or what it be , " I say , " let we walk on . in another minute , if there be anything to alarm we , we shall be near enough to the house to be hear . " 

 we go on quickly so quickly , that Laura be breathless by the time we be nearly through the plantation , and within sight of the light window . 

 I wait a moment to give her breathing - time . just as we be about to proceed she stop I again , and sign to I with her hand to listen once more . we both hear distinctly a long , heavy sigh behind we , in the black depth of the tree . 

 " who be there ? " I call out . 

 there be no answer . 

 " who be there ? " I repeat . 

 an instant of silence follow , and then we hear the light fall of the footstep again , fainter and fainter sink away into the darkness sink , sink , sink till they be lose in the silence . 

 we hurry out from the tree to the open lawn beyond cross it rapidly ; and without another word pass between we , reach the house . 

 in the light of the hall - lamp Laura look at I , with white cheek and startled eye . 

 " I be half dead with fear , " she say . " who could it have be ? " 

 " we will try to guess to - morrow , " I reply . " in the meantime say nothing to any one of what we have hear and see . " 

 " why not ? " 

 " because silence be safe , and we have need of safety in this house . " 

 I send Laura upstairs immediately , wait a minute to take off my hat and put my hair smooth , and then go at once to make my first investigation in the library , on pretence of search for a book . 

 there sit the Count , fill out the large easy - chair in the house , smoking and read calmly , with his foot on an ottoman , his cravat across his knee , and his shirt collar wide open . and there sit Madame Fosco , like a quiet child , on a stool by his side , make cigarette . neither husband nor wife could , by any possibility , have be out late that evening , and have just get back to the house in a hurry . I feel that my object in visit the library be answer the moment I set eye on they . 

 Count Fosco rise in polite confusion and tie his cravat on when I enter the room . 

 " pray do not   let I disturb you , " I say . " I have only come here to get a book . " 

 " all unfortunate man of my size suffer from the heat , " say the Count , refresh himself gravely with a large green fan . " I wish I could change place with my excellent wife . she be as cool at this moment as a fish in the pond outside . " 

 the Countess allow herself to thaw under the influence of her husband 's quaint comparison . " I be never warm , Miss Halcombe , " she remark , with the modest air of a woman who be confess to one of her own merit . 

 " have you and Lady Glyde be out this evening ? " ask the Count , while I be take a book from the shelf to preserve appearance . 

 " yes , we go out to get a little air . " 

 " may I ask in what direction ? " 

 " in the direction of the lake as far as the boat - house . " 

 " Aha ? as far as the boat - house ? " 

 under other circumstance I might have resent his curiosity . but to - night I hail it as another proof that neither he nor his wife be connect with the mysterious appearance at the lake . 

 " no more adventure , I suppose , this evening ? " he go on . " no more discovery , like your discovery of the wound dog ? " 

 he fix his unfathomable grey eye on I , with that cold , clear , irresistible glitter in they which always force I to look at he , and always make I uneasy while I do look . an unutterable suspicion that his mind be pry into mine overcome I at these time , and it overcome I now . 

 " no , " I say shortly ; " no adventure no discovery . " 

 I try to look away from he and leave the room . strange as it seem , I hardly think I should have succeed in the attempt if Madame Fosco have not help I by cause he to move and look away first . 

 " count , you be keep Miss Halcombe standing , " she say . 

 the moment he turn round to get I a chair , I seize my opportunity thank he make my excuse and slip out . 

 an hour later , when Laura 's maid happen to be in her mistress 's room , I take occasion to refer to the closeness of the night , with a view to ascertain next how the servant have be pass their time . 

 " have you be suffer much from the heat downstairs ? " I ask . 

 " no , miss , " say the girl , " we have not feel it to speak of . " 

 " you have be out in the wood then , I suppose ? " 

 " some of we think of go , miss . but cook say she should take her chair into the cool court - yard , outside the kitchen door , and on second thought , all the rest of we take our chair out there too . " 

 the housekeeper be now the only person who remain to be account for . 

 " be Mrs. Michelson go to bed yet ? " I inquire . 

 " I should think not , miss , " say the girl , smile . " Mrs. Michelson be more likely to be get up just now than go to bed . " 

 " why ? what do you mean ? have Mrs. Michelson be take to her bed in the daytime ? " 

 " no , miss , not exactly , but the next thing to it . she be be asleep all the evening on the sofa in her own room . " 

 put together what I observe for myself in the library , and what I have just hear from Laura 's maid , one conclusion seem inevitable . the figure we see at the lake be not the figure of Madame Fosco , of her husband , or of any of the servant . the footstep we hear behind we be not the footstep of any one belong to the house . 

 who could it have be ? 

 it seem useless to inquire . I can not even decide whether the figure be a man 's or a woman 's . I can only say that I think it be a woman 's . 



 VI 

 June 18th . the misery of self - reproach which I suffer yesterday evening , on hear what Laura tell I in the boat - house , return in the loneliness of the night , and keep I wake and wretche for hour . 

 I light my candle at last , and search through my old journal to see what my share in the fatal error of her marriage have really be , and what I might have once do to save she from it . the result soothe I a little for it show that , however blindly and ignorantly I act , I act for the good . cry generally do I harm ; but it be not so last night I think it relieve I . I rise this morning with a settle resolution and a quiet mind . nothing Sir Percival can say or do shall ever irritate I again , or make I forget for one moment that I be stay here in defiance of mortification , insult , and threat , for Laura 's service and for Laura 's sake . 

 the speculation in which we might have indulge this morning , on the subject of the figure at the lake and the footstep in the plantation , have be all suspend by a trifling accident which have cause Laura great regret . she have lose the little brooch I give she for a keepsake on the day before her marriage . as she wear it when we go out yesterday evening we can only suppose that it must have drop from her dress , either in the boat - house or on our way back . the servant have be send to search , and have return unsuccessful . and now Laura herself have go to look for it . whether she find it or not the loss will help to excuse her absence from the house , if Sir Percival return before the letter from Mr. Gilmore 's partner be place in my hand . 

 one o'clock have just strike . I be consider whether I have well wait here for the arrival of the messenger from London , or slip away quietly , and watch for he outside the lodge gate . 

 my suspicion of everybody and everything in this house incline I to think that the second plan may be the good . the Count be safe in the breakfast - room . I hear he , through the door , as I run upstairs ten minute since , exercise his canary - bird at their trick : " come out on my little finger , my pret - pret - prettie ! come out , and hop upstairs ! one , two , three and up ! three , two , one and down ! one , two , three twit - twit - twit - tweet ! " the bird burst into their usual ecstasy of singing , and the Count chirrup and whistle at they in return , as if he be a bird himself . my room door be open , and I can hear the shrill singing and whistle at this very moment . if I be really to slip out without be observe , now be my time . 


 FOUR O'CLOCK . the three hour that have pass since I make my last entry have turn the whole march of event at Blackwater Park in a new direction . whether for good or for evil , I can not and dare not decide . 

 let I get back first to the place at which I leave off , or I shall lose myself in the confusion of my own thought . 

 I go out , as I have propose , to meet the messenger with my letter from London at the lodge gate . on the stair I see no one . in the hall I hear the Count still exercise his bird . but on cross the quadrangle outside , I pass Madame Fosco , walk by herself in her favourite circle , round and round the great fish - pond . I at once slacken my pace , so as to avoid all appearance of be in a hurry , and even go the length , for caution 's sake , of inquire if she think of go out before lunch . she smile at I in the friendly manner say she prefer remain near the house , nod pleasantly , and re - enter the hall . I look back , and see that she have close the door before I have open the wicket by the side of the carriage gate . 

 in less than a quarter of an hour I reach the lodge . 

 the lane outside take a sudden turn to the left , run on straight for a hundred yard or so , and then take another sharp turn to the right to join the high - road . between these two turn , hide from the lodge on one side , and from the way to the station on the other , I wait , walk backwards and forwards . high hedge be on either side of I , and for twenty minute , by my watch , I neither see nor hear anything . at the end of that time the sound of a carriage catch my ear , and I be meet , as I advance towards the second turning , by a fly from the railway . I make a sign to the driver to stop . as he obey I a respectable - look man put his head out of the window to see what be the matter . 

 " I beg your pardon , " I say , " but be I right in suppose that you be go to Blackwater Park ? " 

 " yes , ma'am . " 

 " with a letter for any one ? " 

 " with a letter for Miss Halcombe , ma'am . " 

 " you may give I the letter . I be Miss Halcombe . " 

 the man touch his hat , get out of the fly immediately , and give I the letter . 

 I open it at once and read these line . I copy they here , think it well to destroy the original for caution 's sake . 



 " DEAR MADAM , your letter receive this morning have cause I very great anxiety . I will reply to it as briefly and plainly as possible . 

 " my careful consideration of the statement make by yourself , and my knowledge of Lady Glyde 's position , as define in the settlement , lead I , I regret to say , to the conclusion that a loan of the trust money to Sir Percival   or , in other word , a loan of some portion of the twenty thousand pound of Lady Glyde 's fortune   be in contemplation , and that she be make a party to the deed , in order to secure her approval of a flagrant breach of trust , and to have her signature produce against she if she should complain hereafter . it be impossible , on any other supposition , to account , situate as she be , for her execution to a deed of any kind be want at all . 

 " in the event of Lady Glyde 's signing such a document , as I be compel to suppose the deed in question to be , her trustee would be at liberty to advance money to Sir Percival out of her twenty thousand pound . if the amount so lent should not be pay back , and if Lady Glyde should have child , their fortune will then be diminish by the sum , large or small , so advanced . in plainer term still , the transaction , for anything that Lady Glyde know to the contrary , may be a fraud upon her unborn child . 

 " under these serious circumstance , I would recommend Lady Glyde to assign as a reason for withhold her signature , that she wish the deed to be first submit to myself , as her family solicitor   in the absence of my partner , Mr. Gilmore . no reasonable objection can be make to take this course for , if the transaction be an honourable one , there will necessarily be no difficulty in my give my approval . 

 " sincerely assure you of my readiness to afford any additional help or advice that may be want , I beg to remain , Madam , your faithful servant , 

 " WILLIAM KYRLE . " 


 I read this kind and sensible letter very thankfully . it supply Laura with a reason for object to the signature which be unanswerable , and which we could both of we understand . the messenger wait near I while I be read to receive his direction when I have do . 

 " will you be good enough to say that I understand the letter , and that I be very much oblige ? " I say . " there be no other reply necessary at present . " 

 exactly at the moment when I be speak those word , hold the letter open in my hand , Count Fosco turn the corner of the lane from the high - road , and stand before I as if he have spring up out of the earth . 

 the suddenness of his appearance , in the very last place under heaven in which I should have expect to see he , take I completely by surprise . the messenger wish I good - morning , and get into the fly again . I could not say a word to he I be not even able to return his bow . the conviction that I be discover and by that man , of all other absolutely petrify I . 

 " be you go back to the house , Miss Halcombe ? " he inquire , without show the least surprise on his side , and without even look after the fly , which drive off while he be speak to I . 

 I collect myself sufficiently to make a sign in the affirmative . 

 " I be go back too , " he say . " pray allow I the pleasure of accompany you . will you take my arm ? you look surprised at see I ! " 

 I take his arm . the first of my scatter sens that come back be the sense that warn I to sacrifice anything rather than make an enemy of he . 

 " you look surprised at see I ! " he repeat in his quietly pertinacious way . 

 " I think , Count , I hear you with your bird in the breakfast - room , " I answer , as quietly and firmly as I could . 

 " surely . but my little feather child , dear lady , be only too like other child . they have their day of perversity , and this morning be one of they . my wife come in as I be put they back in their cage , and say she have leave you go out alone for a walk . you tell she so , do you not ? " 

 " certainly . " 

 " well , Miss Halcombe , the pleasure of accompany you be too great a temptation for I to resist . at my age there be no harm in confess so much as that , be there ? I seize my hat , and set off to offer myself as your escort . even so fat an old man as Fosco be surely well than no escort at all ? I take the wrong path I come back in despair , and here I be , arrive   may I say it ?   at the height of my wish . " 

 he talk on in this complimentary strain with a fluency which leave I no exertion to make beyond the effort of maintain my composure . he never refer in the most distant manner to what he have see in the lane , or to the letter which I still have in my hand . this ominous discretion help to convince I that he must have surprise , by the most dishonourable mean , the secret of my application in Laura 's interest to the lawyer ; and that , have now assure himself of the private manner in which I have receive the answer , he have discover enough to suit his purpose , and be only bent on try to quiet the suspicion which he know he must have arouse in my mind . I be wise enough , under these circumstance , not to attempt to deceive he by plausible explanation , and woman enough , notwithstanding my dread of he , to feel as if my hand be taint by rest on his arm . 

 on the drive in front of the house we meet the dog - cart be take round to the stable . Sir Percival have just return . he come out to meet we at the house - door . whatever other result his journey might have have , it have not end in soften his savage temper . 

 " oh ! here be two of you come back , " he say , with a lower face . " what be the meaning of the house be desert in this way ? where be Lady Glyde ? " 

 I tell he of the loss of the brooch , and say that Laura have go into the plantation to look for it . 

 " Brooch or no brooch , " he growl sulkily , " I recommend she not to forget her appointment in the library this afternoon . I shall expect to see she in half an hour . " 

 I take my hand from the Count 's arm , and slowly ascend the step . he honour I with one of his magnificent bow , and then address himself gaily to the scowling master of the house .

I have never have any doubt that his sudden journey by himself take he to Welmingham to question Mrs. Catherick but the experience of to - night have make I fear that the expedition be not undertake in vain , and that he have get the information which he unquestionably leave we to collect . if I know where Anne Catherick be to be find , I would be up to - morrow with sunrise and warn she . 

 while the aspect under which Sir Percival present himself to - night be unhappily but too familiar to I , the aspect under which the Count appear be , on the other hand , entirely new in my experience of he . he permit I , this evening , to make his acquaintance , for the first time , in the character of a Man of Sentiment of sentiment , as I believe , really feel , not assume for the occasion . 

 for instance , he be quiet and subdue his eye and his voice express a restrained sensibility . he wear   as if there be some hide connection between his showy finery and his deep feeling   the most magnificent waistcoat he have yet appear in it be make of pale sea - green silk , and delicately trim with fine silver braid . his voice sink into the tender inflection , his smile express a thoughtful , fatherly admiration , whenever he speak to Laura or to I . he press his wife 's hand under the table when she thank he for trifle little attention at dinner . he take wine with she . " your health and happiness , my angel ! " he say , with fond glisten eye . he eat little or nothing , and sigh , and say " Good Percival ! " when his friend laugh at he . after dinner , he take Laura by the hand , and ask she if she would be ' so sweet as to play to he . " she comply , through sheer astonishment . he sit by the piano , with his watch - chain resting in fold , like a golden serpent , on the sea - green protuberance of his waistcoat . his immense head lie languidly on one side , and he gently beat time with two of his yellow - white finger . he highly approve of the music , and tenderly admire Laura 's manner of play not as poor Hartright use to praise it , with an innocent enjoyment of the sweet sound , but with a clear , cultivate , practical knowledge of the merit of the composition , in the first place , and of the merit of the player 's touch in the second . as the evening close in , he beg that the lovely die light might not be profane , just yet , by the appearance of the lamp . he come , with his horribly silent tread , to the distant window at which I be stand , to be out of his way and to avoid the very sight of he he come to ask I to support his protest against the lamp . if any one of they could only have burn he up at that moment , I would have go down to the kitchen and fetch it myself . 

 " surely you like this modest , tremble english twilight ? " he say softly . " ah ! I love it . I feel my inborn admiration of all that be noble , and great , and good , purify by the breath of heaven on an evening like this . nature have such imperishable charm , such inextinguishable tenderness for I ! I be an old , fat man talk which would become your lip , Miss Halcombe , sound like a derision and a mockery on mine . it be hard to be laugh at in my moment of sentiment , as if my soul be like myself , old and overgrown . Observe , dear lady , what a light be die on the tree ! do it penetrate your heart , as it penetrate mine ? " 

 he pause , look at I , and repeat the famous line of Dante on the evening - time , with a melody and tenderness which add a charm of their own to the matchless beauty of the poetry itself . 

 " Bah ! " he cry suddenly , as the last cadence of those noble italian word die away on his lip ; " I make an old fool of myself , and only weary you all ! let we shut up the window in our bosom and get back to the matter - of - fact world . percival ! I sanction the admission of the lamp . Lady Glyde Miss Halcombe Eleanor , my good wife which of you will indulge I with a game at domino ? " 

 he address we all , but he look especially at Laura . 

 she have learn to feel my dread of offend he , and she accept his proposal . it be more than I could have do at that moment . I could not have sit down at the same table with he for any consideration . his eye seem to reach my inmost soul through the thickening obscurity of the twilight . his voice tremble along every nerve in my body , and turn I hot and cold alternately . the mystery and terror of my dream , which have haunt I at interval all through the evening , now oppress my mind with an unendurable foreboding and an unutterable awe . I see the white tomb again , and the veiled woman rise out of it by Hartright 's side . the thought of Laura well up like a spring in the depth of my heart , and fill it with water of bitterness , never , never know to it before . I catch she by the hand as she pass I on her way to the table , and kiss she as if that night be to part we for ever . while they be all gaze at I in astonishment , I run out through the low window which be open before I to the ground run out to hide from they in the darkness , to hide even from myself . 


 we separate that evening later than usual . towards midnight the summer silence be break by the shuddering of a low , melancholy wind among the tree . we all feel the sudden chill in the atmosphere , but the Count be the first to notice the stealthy rise of the wind . he stop while he be light my candle for I , and hold up his hand warningly 

 " listen ! " he say . " there will be a change to - morrow . " 



 VII 

 June 19th . the event of yesterday warn I to be ready , soon or later , to meet the bad . to - day be not yet at an end , and the bad have come . 

 judge by the close calculation of time that Laura and I could make , we arrive at the conclusion that Anne Catherick must have appear at the boat - house at half - past two o'clock on the afternoon of yesterday . I accordingly arrange that Laura should just show herself at the luncheon - table to - day , and should then slip out at the first opportunity , leave I behind to preserve appearance , and to follow she as soon as I could safely do so . this mode of proceeding , if no obstacle occur to thwart we , would enable she to be at the boat - house before half - past two , and   when I leave the table , in my turn   would take I to a safe position in the plantation before three . 

 the change in the weather , which last night 's wind warn we to expect , come with the morning . it be rain heavily when I get up , and it continue to rain until twelve o'clock when the cloud disperse , the blue sky appear , and the sun shine again with the bright promise of a fine afternoon . 

 my anxiety to know how Sir Percival and the Count would occupy the early part of the day be by no mean set at rest , so far as Sir Percival be concern , by his leave we immediately after breakfast , and go out by himself , in spite of the rain . he neither tell we where he be go nor when we might expect he back . we see he pass the breakfast - room window hastily , with his high boot and his waterproof coat on and that be all . 

 the Count pass the morning quietly indoor , some part of it in the library , some part in the drawing - room , play odd and end of music on the piano , and hum to himself . judge by appearance , the sentimental side of his character be persistently inclined to betray itself still . he be silent and sensitive , and ready to sigh and languish ponderously   as only fat man can sigh and languish   on the small provocation . 

 luncheon - time come and Sir Percival do not return . the Count take his friend 's place at the table , plaintively devour the great part of a fruit tart , submerge under a whole jugful of cream , and explain the full merit of the achievement to we as soon as he have do . " a taste for sweet , " he say in his soft tone and his tender manner , " be the innocent taste of woman and child . I love to share it with they it be another bond , dear lady , between you and I . " 

 Laura leave the table in ten minute ' time . I be sorely tempt to accompany she . but if we have both go out together we must have excite suspicion , and bad still , if we allow Anne Catherick to see Laura , accompany by a second person who be a stranger to she , we should in all probability forfeit her confidence from that moment , never to regain it again . 

 I wait , therefore , as patiently as I could , until the servant come in to clear the table . when I quit the room , there be no sign , in the house or out of it , of Sir Percival 's return . I leave the Count with a piece of sugar between his lip , and the vicious cockatoo scramble up his waistcoat to get at it , while Madame Fosco , sit opposite to her husband , watch the proceeding of his bird and himself as attentively as if she have never see anything of the sort before in her life . on my way to the plantation I keep carefully beyond the range of view from the luncheon - room window . nobody see I and nobody follow I . it be then a quarter to three o'clock by my watch . 

 once among the tree I walk rapidly , until I have advance more than half - way through the plantation . at that point I slacken my pace and proceed cautiously , but I see no one , and hear no voice . by little and little I come within view of the back of the boat - house stop and listen then go on , till I be close behind it , and must have hear any person who be talk inside . still the silence be unbroken still far and near no sign of a living creature appear anywhere . 

 after skirt round by the back of the building , first on one side and then on the other , and make no discovery , I venture in front of it , and fairly look in . the place be empty . 

 I call , " Laura ! " at first softly , then louder and louder . no one answer and no one appear . for all that I could see and hear , the only human creature in the neighbourhood of the lake and the plantation be myself . 

 my heart begin to beat violently , but I keep my resolution , and search , first the boat - house and then the ground in front of it , for any sign which might show I whether Laura have really reach the place or not . no mark of her presence appear inside the building , but I find trace of she outside it , in footstep on the sand . 

 I detect the footstep of two person large footstep like a man 's , and small footstep , which , by put my own foot into they and test their size in that manner , I feel certain be Laura 's . the ground be confusedly mark in this way just before the boat - house . close against one side of it , under shelter of the project roof , I discover a little hole in the sand a hole artificially make , beyond a doubt . I just notice it , and then turn away immediately to trace the footstep as far as I could , and to follow the direction in which they might lead I . 

 they lead I , start from the left - hand side of the boat - house , along the edge of the tree , a distance , I should think , of between two and three hundred yard , and then the sandy ground show no further trace of they . feel that the person whose course I be track must necessarily have enter the plantation at this point , I enter it too . at first I could find no path , but I discover one afterwards , just faintly trace among the tree , and follow it . it take I , for some distance , in the direction of the village , until I stop at a point where another foot - track cross it . the bramble grow thickly on either side of this second path . I stand look down it , uncertain which way to take next , and while I look I see on one thorny branch some fragment of fringe from a woman 's shawl . a close examination of the fringe satisfy I that it have be tear from a shawl of Laura 's , and I instantly follow the second path . it bring I out at last , to my great relief , at the back of the house . I say to my great relief , because I infer that Laura must , for some unknown reason , have return before I by this roundabout way . I go in by the court - yard and the office . the first person whom I meet in cross the servant ' hall be Mrs. Michelson , the housekeeper . 

 " do you know , " I ask , " whether Lady Glyde have come in from her walk or not ? "

 my heart sink within I . " you do not   mean an accident ? " I say faintly .

 Fanny be Laura 's maid a good affectionate girl who have be with she for year the only person in the house whose fidelity and devotion we could both depend upon .

 she could give I no explanation whatever of her sudden dismissal .  I feel directly that Fanny 's departure offer we a safe mean of communication with London and with Limmeridge House , of which it might be very important to avail ourselves . accordingly , I tell she that she might expect to hear from her mistress or from I in the course of the evening , and that she might depend on our both do all that lie in our power to help she , under the trial of leave we for the present . those word say , I shake hand with she and go upstairs . 

 the door which lead to Laura 's room be the door of an ante - chamber opening on to the passage . when I try it , it be bolt on the inside .

 on open the door she instantly step out to the threshold , and stand grin at I in stolid silence .

 she stretch out a great red hand and arm on each side of she , so as to bar the doorway , and slowly nod her addle head at I .

 the drawing - room and the breakfast - room be both empty . "

 " listen to I , my love , and try to think that you be not quite helpless so long as I be here with you . " 

 " I will think so I do think so . do not   altogether forget poor fanny in thinking of I . she want help and comfort too . " 

 " I will not forget she . I see she before I come up here , and I have arrange to communicate with she to - night . letter be not safe in the post - bag at Blackwater Park , and I shall have two to write to - day , in your interest , which must pass through no hand but Fanny 's . " 

 " what letter ? " 

 " I mean to write first , Laura , to Mr. Gilmore 's partner , who have offer to help we in any fresh emergency . little as I know of the law , I be certain that it can protect a woman from such treatment as that ruffian have inflict on you to - day . I will go into no detail about Anne Catherick , because I have no certain information to give . but the lawyer shall know of those bruise on your arm , and of the violence offer to you in this room he shall , before I rest to - night ! " 

 " but think of the exposure , Marian ! " 

 " I be calculate on the exposure . Sir Percival have more to dread from it than you have . the prospect of an exposure may bring he to term when nothing else will . " 

 I rise as I speak , but Laura entreat I not to leave she . " you will drive he to desperation , " she say , " and increase our danger tenfold . " 

 I feel the truth the disheartening truth of those word . but I could not bring myself plainly to acknowledge it to she . in our dreadful position there be no help and no hope for we but in risk the bad . I say so in guard term . she sigh bitterly , but do not contest the matter . she only ask about the second letter that I have propose writing . to whom be it to be address ? 

 " to Mr. Fairlie , " I say . " your uncle be your near male relative , and the head of the family . he must and shall interfere . " 

 Laura shake her head sorrowfully . 

 " yes , yes , " I go on , " your uncle be a weak , selfish , worldly man , I know , but he be not Sir Percival Glyde , and he have no such friend about he as Count Fosco . I expect nothing from his kindness or his tenderness of feeling towards you or towards I , but he will do anything to pamper his own indolence , and to secure his own quiet . let I only persuade he that his interference at this moment will save he inevitable trouble and wretchedness and responsibility hereafter , and he will bestir himself for his own sake . I know how to deal with he , Laura I have have some practice . " 

 " if you could only prevail on he to let I go back to Limmeridge for a little while and stay there quietly with you , Marian , I could be almost as happy again as I be before I be marry ! " 

 those word set I think in a new direction . would it be possible to place Sir Percival between the two alternative of either expose himself to the scandal of legal interference on his wife 's behalf , or of allow she to be quietly separate from he for a time under pretext of a visit to her uncle 's house ? and could he , in that case , be reckon on as likely to accept the last resource ? it be doubtful more than doubtful . and yet , hopeless as the experiment seem , surely it be worth try . I resolve to try it in sheer despair of know what well to do . 

 " your uncle shall know the wish you have just express , " I say , " and I will ask the lawyer 's advice on the subject as well . Good may come of it and will come of it , I hope . " 

 say that I rise again , and again Laura try to make I resume my seat . 

 " do not   leave I , " she say uneasily . " my desk be on that table . you can write here . " 

 it try I to the quick to refuse she , even in her own interest . but we have be too long shut up alone together already . our chance of see each other again might entirely depend on our not exciting any fresh suspicion . it be full time to show myself , quietly and unconcernedly , among the wretch who be at that very moment , perhaps , think of we and talk of we downstairs . I explain the miserable necessity to Laura , and prevail on she to recognise it as I do . 

 " I will come back again , love , in an hour or less , " I say . " the bad be over for to - day . keep yourself quiet and fear nothing . " 

 " be the key in the door , marian ? can I lock it on the inside ? " 

 " yes , here be the key . lock the door , and open it to nobody until I come upstairs again . " 

 I kiss she and leave she . it be a relief to I as I walk away to hear the key turn in the lock , and to know that the door be at her own command . 



 VIII 

 June 19th . I have only get as far as the top of the stair when the locking of Laura 's door suggest to I the precaution of also lock my own door , and keep the key safely about I while I be out of the room . my journal be already secure with other paper in the table drawer , but my writing material be leave out . these include a seal bear the common device of two dove drink out of the same cup , and some sheet of blot - paper , which have the impression on they of the closing line of my writing in these page trace during the past night . distort by the suspicion which have now become a part of myself , even such trifle as these look too dangerous to be trust without a guard even the locked table drawer seem to be not sufficiently protect in my absence until the mean of access to it have be carefully secure as well .

I lock the door , put the key in my pocket , and go downstairs . 

 Madame Fosco be alone in the hall look at the weather - glass . 

 " still fall , " she say . " I be afraid we must expect more rain . " 

 her face be compose again to its customary expression and its customary colour . but the hand with which she point to the dial of the weather - glass still tremble . 

 could she have tell her husband already that she have overhear Laura revile he , in my company , as a ' spy ? " my strong suspicion that she must have tell he , my irresistible dread   all the more overpowering from its very vagueness   of the consequence which might follow , my fixed conviction , derive from various little self - betrayal which woman notice in each other , that Madame Fosco , in spite of her well - assume external civility , have not forgive her niece for innocently stand between she and the legacy of ten thousand pound all rush upon my mind together , all impel I to speak in the vain hope of use my own influence and my own power of persuasion for the atonement of Laura 's offence . 

 " may I trust to your kindness to excuse I , Madame Fosco , if I venture to speak to you on an exceedingly painful subject ? " 

 she cross her hand in front of she and bow her head solemnly , without utter a word , and without take her eye off mine for a moment .

I will only venture to hope that you have not think it of sufficient importance to be mention to the count ? " 

 " I think it of no importance whatever , " say Madame Fosco sharply and suddenly . " but , " she add , resume her icy manner in a moment , " I have no secret from my husband even in trifle . when he notice just now that I look distressed , it be my painful duty to tell he why I be distressed , and I frankly acknowledge to you , Miss Halcombe , that I have tell he . " 

 I be prepared to hear it , and yet she turn I cold all over when she say those word . 

 " let I earnestly entreat you , Madame Fosco let I earnestly entreat the count to make some allowance for the sad position in which my sister be place . she speak while she be smart under the insult and injustice inflict on she by her husband , and she be not herself when she say those rash word . may I hope that they will be considerately and generously forgive ? " 

 " most assuredly , " say the Count 's quiet voice behind I . he have steal on we with his noiseless tread and his book in his hand from the library .

 I try to continue , but his eye be on I ; his deadly smile that hide everything be set , hard , and unwavere on his broad , smooth face . my distrust of his unfathomable falseness , my sense of my own degradation in stoop to conciliate his wife and himself , so disturbed and confuse I , that the next word fail on my lip , and I stand there in silence . 

 " I beg you on my knee to say no more , Miss Halcombe I be truly shocked that you should have think it necessary to say so much . " with that polite speech he take my hand oh , how I despise myself ! oh , how little comfort there be even in know that I submit to it for Laura 's sake ! he take my hand and put it to his poisonous lip . never do I know all my horror of he till then . that innocent familiarity turn my blood as if it have be the vile insult that a man could offer I . yet I hide my disgust from he I try to smile I , who once mercilessly despise deceit in other woman , be as false as the bad of they , as false as the Judas whose lip have touch my hand . 

 I could not have maintain my degrade self - control it be all that redeem I in my own estimation to know that I could not if he have still continue to keep his eye on my face . his wife 's tigerish jealousy come to my rescue and force his attention away from I the moment he possess himself of my hand . her cold blue eye catch light , her dull white cheek flush into bright colour , she look year young than her age in an instant . 

 " Count ! " she say . " your foreign form of politeness be not understand by Englishwomen . " 

 " Pardon I , my angel ! the good and dearest Englishwoman in the world understand they . " with those word he drop my hand and quietly raise his wife 's hand to his lip in place of it . 

 I run back up the stair to take refuge in my own room . if there have be time to think , my thought , when I be alone again , would have cause I bitter suffering . but there be no time to think . happily for the preservation of my calmness and my courage there be time for nothing but action . 

 the letter to the lawyer and to Mr. Fairlie be still to be write , and I sit down at once without a moment 's hesitation to devote myself to they . 

 there be no multitude of resource to perplex I there be absolutely no one to depend on , in the first instance , but myself . Sir Percival have neither friend nor relative in the neighbourhood whose intercession I could attempt to employ . he be on the cold term in some case on the bad term with the family of his own rank and station who live near he . we two woman have neither father nor brother to come to the house and take our part . there be no choice but to write those two doubtful letter , or to put Laura in the wrong and myself in the wrong , and to make all peaceable negotiation in the future impossible by secretly escape from Blackwater Park . nothing but the most imminent personal peril could justify our take that second course . the letter must be try first , and I write they . 

 I say nothing to the lawyer about Anne Catherick , because   as I have already hint to Laura   that topic be connect with a mystery which we could not yet explain , and which it would therefore be useless to write about to a professional man . I leave my correspondent to attribute Sir Percival 's disgraceful conduct , if he please , to fresh dispute about money matter , and simply consult he on the possibility of take legal proceeding for Laura 's protection in the event of her husband 's refusal to allow she to leave Blackwater Park for a time and return with I to Limmeridge . I refer he to Mr. Fairlie for the detail of this last arrangement I assure he that I write with Laura 's authority and I end by entreat he to act in her name to the utmost extent of his power and with the least possible loss of time . 

 the letter to Mr. Fairlie occupy I next . I appeal to he on the term which I have mention to Laura as the most likely to make he bestir himself ; I enclose a copy of my letter to the lawyer to show he how serious the case be , and I represent our removal to Limmeridge as the only compromise which would prevent the danger and distress of Laura 's present position from inevitably affect her uncle as well as herself at no very distant time . 

 when I have do , and have seal and direct the two envelope , I go back with the letter to Laura 's room , to show she that they be write . 

 " have anybody disturb you ? " I ask , when she open the door to I . 

 " nobody have knock , " she reply . " but I hear some one in the outer room . " 

 " be it a man or a woman ? " 

 " a woman . I hear the rustling of her gown . " 

 " a rustling like silk ? " 

 " yes , like silk . " 

 Madame Fosco have evidently be watch outside . the mischief she might do by herself be little to be fear . but the mischief she might do , as a willing instrument in her husband 's hand , be too formidable to be overlook . 

 " what become of the rustling of the gown when you no long hear it in the ante - room ? " I inquire . " do you hear it go past your wall , along the passage ? " 

 " yes . I keep still and listen , and just hear it . " 

 " which way do it go ? " 

 " towards your room . " 

 I consider again . the sound have not catch my ear . but I be then deeply absorb in my letter , and I write with a heavy hand and a quill pen , scrap and scratch noisily over the paper . it be more likely that Madame Fosco would hear the scraping of my pen than that I should hear the rustling of her dress . another reason   if I have want one   for not trust my letter to the post - bag in the hall . 

 Laura see I think . " More difficulty ! " she say wearily ; " more difficulty and more danger ! " 

 " no danger , " I reply . " some little difficulty , perhaps . I be think of the safe way of put my two letter into Fanny 's hand . " 

 " you have really write they , then ? oh , Marian , run no risk pray , pray run no risk ! " 

 " no , no no fear . let I see what o'clock be it now ? " 

 it be a quarter to six . there would be time for I to get to the village inn , and to come back again before dinner . if I wait till the evening I might find no second opportunity of safely leave the house . 

 " keep the key turn in the lock . Laura , " I say , " and do not   be afraid about I . if you hear any inquiry make , call through the door , and say that I be go out for a walk . "

 " before dinner , without fail . Courage , my love . by this time to - morrow you will have a clear - head , trustworthy man act for your good . Mr. Gilmore 's partner be our next good friend to Mr. Gilmore himself . " 

 a moment 's reflection , as soon as I be alone , convince I that I have well not appear in my walking - dress until I have first discover what be go on in the low part of the house . I have not ascertain yet whether Sir Percival be indoor or out . 

 the singing of the canary in the library , and the smell of tobacco - smoke that come through the door , which be not close , tell I at once where the Count be . I look over my shoulder as I pass the doorway , and see to my surprise that he be exhibit the docility of the bird in his most engagingly polite manner to the housekeeper . he must have specially invite she to see they for she would never have think of go into the library of her own accord . the man 's slight action have a purpose of some kind at the bottom of every one of they . what could be his purpose here ? 

 it be no time then to inquire into his motif . I look about for Madame Fosco next , and find she follow her favourite circle round and round the fish - pond .

I contrive to refer to he indirectly , and after a little fencing on either side she at last mention that he have go out . 

 " which of the horse have he take ? " I ask carelessly .

 " I do not , Countess . " 

 " be you go in ? " 

 " yes , I think so . I suppose it will soon be time to dress for dinner . " 

 we enter the house together . Madame Fosco stroll into the library , and close the door . I go at once to fetch my hat and shawl . every moment be of importance , if I be to get to Fanny at the inn and be back before dinner .

 on my way to the village I prepare myself for the possibility of meet Sir Percival . as long as I have he to deal with alone I feel certain of not lose my presence of mind . any woman who be sure of her own wit be a match at any time for a man who be not sure of his own temper . I have no such fear of Sir Percival as I have of the Count . instead of flutter , it have compose I , to hear of the errand on which he have go out . while the tracing of Anne Catherick be the great anxiety that occupy he , Laura and I might hope for some cessation of any active persecution at his hand . for our sake now , as well as for Anne 's , I hop and pray fervently that she might still escape he . 

 I walk on as briskly as the heat would let I till I reach the cross - road which lead to the village , look back from time to time to make sure that I be not follow by any one .

 I reach the inn without meet Sir Percival , and without notice anything more , and be glad to find that the landlady have receive Fanny with all possible kindness . the girl have a little parlour to sit in , away from the noise of the taproom , and a clean bedchamber at the top of the house . she begin cry again at the sight of I , and say , poor soul , truly enough , that it be dreadful to feel herself turn out into the world as if she have commit some unpardonable fault , when no blame could be lay at her door by anybody not even by her master , who have send she away . 

 " try to make the good of it , Fanny , " I say . " your mistress and I will stand your friend , and will take care that your character shall not suffer . now , listen to I . I have very little time to spare , and I be go to put a great trust in your hand . I wish you to take care of these two letter . the one with the stamp on it you be to put into the post when you reach London to - morrow . the other , direct to Mr. Fairlie , you be to deliver to he yourself as soon as you get home . keep both the letter about you and give they up to no one . they be of the last importance to your mistress 's interest . "

" and when you see the housekeeper at Limmeridge give she my compliment , and say that you be in my service until Lady Glyde be able to take you back . we may meet again soon than you think . so keep a good heart , and do not   miss the seven o'clock train . "

"

' will you alter your mind , and tell I the rest ? you shall ! soon or later I will wring it out of you . you know where Anne Catherick be at this moment . ' ' indeed , indeed , ' I say , ' I do not . ' ' you do ! ' he call back . ' I will crush your obstinacy mind that ! I will wring it out of you !  the dinner - bell rang and I hasten away . 

 Sir Percival take Madame Fosco into the dining - room , and the Count give I his arm . he be hot and flushed , and be not dress with his customary care and completeness . have he , too , be out before dinner , and be late in get back ? or be he only suffer from the heat a little more severely than usual ? 

 however this might be , he be unquestionably trouble by some secret annoyance or anxiety , which , with all his power of deception , he be not able entirely to conceal . through the whole of dinner he be almost as silent as Sir Percival himself , and he , every now and then , look at his wife with an expression of furtive uneasiness which be quite new in my experience of he . the one social obligation which he seem to be self - possess enough to perform as carefully as ever be the obligation of be persistently civil and attentive to I . what vile object he have in view I can not still discover , but be the design what it may , invariable politeness towards myself , invariable humility towards Laura , and invariable suppression   at any cost   of Sir Percival 's clumsy violence , have be the mean he have resolutely and impenetrably use to get to his end ever since he set foot in this house . I suspect it when he first interfere in our favour , on the day when the deed be produce in the library , and I feel certain of it now .

 " what be you go away for ? " ask Sir Percival " I mean you , Fosco . "
