 " civil ! " say Sir Percival savagely . " civil behaviour , upon my soul , to a man in his own house ! " 

 I have more than once see he look at the Count uneasily during dinner - time , and have observe that the Count carefully abstain from look at he in return . this circumstance , couple with the host 's anxiety for a little quiet talk over the wine , and the guest 's obstinate resolution not to sit down again at the table , revive in my memory the request which Sir Percival have vainly address to his friend early in the day to come out of the library and speak to he . the Count have defer grant that private interview , when it be first ask for in the afternoon , and have again defer grant it , when it be a second time ask for at the dinner - table . whatever the come subject of discussion between they might be , it be clearly an important subject in Sir Percival 's estimation and perhaps   judge from his evident reluctance to approach it   a dangerous subject as well , in the estimation of the Count . 

 these consideration occur to I while we be pass from the dining - room to the drawing - room . Sir Percival 's angry commentary on his friend 's desertion of he have not produce the slight effect . the Count obstinately accompany we to the tea - table wait a minute or two in the room go out into the hall and return with the post - bag in his hand . it be then eight o'clock the hour at which the letter be always despatch from Blackwater Park . 

 " have you any letter for the post , Miss Halcombe ? " he ask , approach I with the bag .

 " no , Count , thank you . no letter to - day . " 

 he give the bag to the servant , who be then in the room ; sit down at the piano , and play the air of the lively Neapolitan street - song , " La mia Carolina , " twice over . his wife , who be usually the most deliberate of woman in all her movement , make the tea as quickly as I could have make it myself finish her own cup in two minute , and quietly glide out of the room . 

 I rise to follow her example partly because I suspect she of attempt some treachery upstairs with Laura , partly because I be resolve not to remain alone in the same room with her husband . 

 before I could get to the door the Count stop I , by a request for a cup of tea . I give he the cup of tea , and try a second time to get away . he stop I again this time by go back to the piano , and suddenly appeal to I on a musical question in which he declare that the honour of his country be concern . 

 I vainly plead my own total ignorance of music , and total want of taste in that direction . he only appeal to I again with a vehemence which set all further protest on my part at defiance . " the English and the Germans   he indignantly declare   be always revile the Italians for their inability to cultivate the high kind of music . we be perpetually talk of our oratorio , and they be perpetually talk of their symphony . do we forget and do they forget his immortal friend and countryman , Rossini ? what be Moses in Egypt but a sublime oratorio , which be act on the stage instead of be coldly sing in a concert - room ? what be the overture to Guillaume Tell but a symphony under another name ? have I hear Moses in Egypt ? would I listen to this , and this , and this , and say if anything more sublimely sacred and grand have ever be compose by mortal man ? " and without wait for a word of assent or dissent on my part , look I hard in the face all the time , he begin thunder on the piano , and sing to it with loud and lofty enthusiasm only interrupt himself , at interval , to announce to I fiercely the title of the different piece of music : " Chorus of Egyptians in the Plague of Darkness , Miss Halcombe ! " " Recitativo of Moses with the table of the Law . " " Prayer of Israelites , at the passage of the Red Sea . Aha ! aha ! be that sacred ? be that sublime ? " the piano tremble under his powerful hand , and the teacup on the table rattle , as his big bass voice thunder out the note , and his heavy foot beat time on the floor . 

 there be something horrible something fierce and devilish in the outburst of his delight at his own singing and playing , and in the triumph with which he watch its effect upon I as I shrink near and near to the door . I be release at last , not by my own effort , but by Sir Percival 's interposition . he open the dining - room door , and call out angrily to know what " that infernal noise " mean . the Count instantly get up from the piano . " ah ! if Percival be come , " he say , " harmony and melody be both at an end . the Muse of Music , Miss Halcombe , desert we in dismay , and I , the fat old minstrel , exhale the rest of my enthusiasm in the open air ! " he stalk out into the verandah , put his hand in his pocket , and resume the Recitativo of Moses , sotto voce , in the garden . 

 I hear Sir Percival call after he from the dining - room window . but he take no notice he seem determined not to hear . that long - defer quiet talk between they be still to be put off , be still to wait for the Count 's absolute will and pleasure . 

 he have detain I in the drawing - room nearly half an hour from the time when his wife leave we . where have she be , and what have she be do in that interval ? 

 I go upstairs to ascertain , but I make no discovery , and when I question Laura , I find that she have not hear anything . nobody have disturb she , no faint rustling of the silk dress have be audible , either in the ante - room or in the passage . 

 it be then twenty minute to nine . after go to my room to get my journal , I return , and sit with Laura , sometimes write , sometimes stop to talk with she . nobody come near we , and nothing happen . we remain together till ten o'clock . I then rise , say my last cheering word , and wish her good - night . she lock her door again after we have arrange that I should come in and see she the first thing in the morning . 

 I have a few sentence more to add to my diary before go to bed myself , and as I go down again to the drawing - room after leave Laura for the last time that weary day , I resolve merely to show myself there , to make my excuse , and then to retire an hour early than usual for the night . 

 Sir Percival , and the Count and his wife , be sit together . Sir Percival be yawn in an easy - chair , the Count be read , Madame Fosco be fan herself . strange to say , her face be flush now . she , who never suffer from the heat , be most undoubtedly suffer from it to - night . 

 " I be afraid , Countess , you be not quite so well as usual ? " I say . 

 " the very remark I be about to make to you , " she reply . " you be look pale , my dear . " 

 my dear ! it be the first time she have ever address I with that familiarity ! there be an insolent smile too on her face when she say the word . 

 " I be suffer from one of my bad headache , " I answer coldly . 

 " ah , indeed ? want of exercise , I suppose ? a walk before dinner would have be just the thing for you . " she refer to the " walk " with a strange emphasis . have she see I go out ? no matter if she have . the letter be safe now in Fanny 's hand . 

 " come and have a smoke , Fosco , " say Sir Percival , rise , with another uneasy look at his friend . 

 " with pleasure , Percival , when the lady have go to bed , " reply the Count . 

 " excuse I , Countess , if I set you the example of retire , " I say . " the only remedy for such a headache as mine be go to bed . " 

 I take my leave . there be the same insolent smile on the woman 's face when I shake hand with she . Sir Percival pay no attention to I . he be look impatiently at Madame Fosco , who show no sign of leave the room with I . the Count smile to himself behind his book . there be yet another delay to that quiet talk with Sir Percival and the Countess be the impediment this time . 



 IX 

 June 19th . once safely shut into my own room , I open these page , and prepare to go on with that part of the day 's record which be still leave to write . 

 for ten minute or more I sit idle , with the pen in my hand , think over the event of the last twelve hour . when I at last address myself to my task , I find a difficulty in proceed with it which I have never experience before . in spite of my effort to fix my thought on the matter in hand , they wander away with the strange persistency in the one direction of Sir Percival and the Count , and all the interest which I try to concentrate on my journal centre instead in that private interview between they which have be put off all through the day , and which be now to take place in the silence and solitude of the night . 

 in this perverse state of my mind , the recollection of what have pass since the morning would not come back to I , and there be no resource but to close my journal and to get away from it for a little while . 

 I open the door which lead from my bedroom into my sitting - room , and have pass through , pull it to again , to prevent any accident in case of draught with the candle leave on the dressing - table . my sitting - room window be wide open , and I lean out listlessly to look at the night . 

 it be dark and quiet . neither moon nor star be visible . there be a smell like rain in the still , heavy air , and I put my hand out of window . no . the rain be only threaten , it have not come yet . 

 I remain lean on the window - sill for nearly a quarter of an hour , look out absently into the black darkness , and hear nothing , except now and then the voice of the servant , or the distant sound of a closing door , in the low part of the house . 

 just as I be turn away wearily from the window to go back to the bedroom and make a second attempt to complete the unfinished entry in my journal , I smell the odour of tobacco - smoke stealing towards I on the heavy night air . the next moment I see a tiny red spark advance from the farth end of the house in the pitch darkness . I hear no footstep , and I could see nothing but the spark . it travel along in the night , pass the window at which I be stand , and stop opposite my bedroom window , inside which I have leave the light burn on the dressing - table . 

 the spark remain stationary for a moment , then move back again in the direction from which it have advance . as I follow its progress I see a second red spark , large than the first , approach from the distance . the two meet together in the darkness . remembering who smoke cigarette and who smoke cigar , I infer immediately that the Count have come out first to look and listen under my window , and that Sir Percival have afterwards join he . they must both have be walk on the lawn or I should certainly have hear Sir Percival 's heavy footfall , though the Count 's soft step might have escape I , even on the gravel walk . 

 I wait quietly at the window , certain that they could neither of they see I in the darkness of the room . 

 " what be the matter ? " I hear Sir Percival say in a low voice . " why do not   you come in and sit down ? " 

 " I want to see the light out of that window , " reply the count softly . 

 " what harm do the light do ? " 

 " it show she be not in bed yet . she be sharp enough to suspect something , and bold enough to come downstairs and listen , if she can get the chance . Patience , Percival patience . " 

 " Humbug ! you be always talk of patience . " 

 " I shall talk of something else presently . my good friend , you be on the edge of your domestic precipice , and if I let you give the woman one other chance , on my sacred word of honour they will push you over it ! " 

 " what the devil do you mean ? " 

 " we will come to our explanation , Percival , when the light be out of that window , and when I have have one little look at the room on each side of the library , and a peep at the staircase as well . " 

 they slowly move away , and the rest of the conversation between they   which have be conduct throughout in the same low tone   cease to be audible . it be no matter . I have hear enough to determine I on justify the Count 's opinion of my sharpness and my courage . before the red spark be out of sight in the darkness I have make up my mind that there should be a listener when those two man sit down to their talk and that the listener , in spite of all the Count 's precaution to the contrary , should be myself . I want but one motive to sanction the act to my own conscience , and to give I courage enough for perform it and that motive I have . Laura 's honour , Laura 's happiness Laura 's life itself might depend on my quick ear and my faithful memory to - night . 

 I have hear the Count say that he mean to examine the room on each side of the library , and the staircase as well , before he enter on any explanation with Sir Percival . this expression of his intention be necessarily sufficient to inform I that the library be the room in which he propose that the conversation should take place . the one moment of time which be long enough to bring I to that conclusion be also the moment which show I a means of baffle his precaution or , in other word , of hear what he and Sir Percival say to each other , without the risk of descend at all into the low region of the house . 

 in speak of the room on the ground floor I have mention incidentally the verandah outside they , on which they all open by mean of french window , extend from the cornice to the floor . the top of this verandah be flat , the rain - water be carry off from it by pipe into tank which help to supply the house . on the narrow leaden roof , which run along past the bedroom , and which be rather less , I should think , than three foot below the sill of the window , a row of flower - pot be range , with wide interval between each pot the whole be protect from fall in high wind by an ornamental iron rail along the edge of the roof . 

 the plan which have now occur to I be to get out at my sitting - room window on to this roof , to creep along noiselessly till I reach that part of it which be immediately over the library window , and to crouch down between the flower - pot , with my ear against the outer railing . if Sir Percival and the Count sit and smoke to - night , as I have see they sit and smoke many night before , with their chair close at the open window , and their foot stretch on the zinc garden seat which be place under the verandah , every word they say to each other above a whisper   and no long conversation , as we all know by experience , can be carry on in a whisper   must inevitably reach my ear . if , on the other hand , they choose to - night to sit far back inside the room , then the chance be that I should hear little or nothing and in that case , I must run the far more serious risk of try to outwit they downstairs . 

 strongly as I be fortify in my resolution by the desperate nature of our situation , I hop most fervently that I might escape this last emergency . my courage be only a woman 's courage after all , and it be very near to fail I when I think of trust myself on the ground floor , at the dead of night , within reach of Sir Percival and the Count . 

 I go softly back to my bedroom to try the safe experiment of the verandah roof first . 

 a complete change in my dress be imperatively necessary for many reason . I take off my silk gown to begin with , because the slight noise from it on that still night might have betray I . I next remove the white and cumbersome part of my underclothing , and replace they by a petticoat of dark flannel . over this I put my black travelling cloak , and pull the hood on to my head . in my ordinary evening costume I take up the room of three man at least . in my present dress , when it be hold close about I , no man could have pass through the narrow space more easily than i. the little breadth leave on the roof of the verandah , between the flower - pot on one side and the wall and the window of the house on the other , make this a serious consideration . if I knock anything down , if I make the least noise , who could say what the consequence might be ? 

 I only wait to put the match near the candle before I extinguish it , and grope my way back into the sitting - room , I lock that door , as I have lock my bedroom door then quietly get out of the window , and cautiously set my foot on the leaden roof of the verandah . 

 my two room be at the inner extremity of the new wing of the house in which we all live , and I have five window to pass before I could reach the position it be necessary to take up immediately over the library . the first window belong to a spare room which be empty . the second and third window belong to Laura 's room . the fourth window belong to Sir Percival 's room . the fifth belong to the Countess 's room . the other , by which it be not necessary for I to pass , be the window of the Count 's dressing - room , of the bathroom , and of the second empty spare room . 

 no sound reach my ear the black blind darkness of the night be all round I when I first stand on the verandah , except at that part of it which Madame Fosco 's window overlook . there , at the very place above the library to which my course be direct there I see a gleam of light ! the Countess be not yet in bed . 

 it be too late to draw back it be no time to wait . I determine to go on at all hazard , and trust for security to my own caution and to the darkness of the night . " for Laura 's sake ! " I think to myself , as I take the first step forward on the roof , with one hand hold my cloak close round I , and the other groping against the wall of the house . it be well to brush close by the wall than to risk strike my foot against the flower - pot within a few inch of I , on the other side . 

 I pass the dark window of the spare room , try the leaden roof at each step with my foot before I risk rest my weight on it . I pass the dark window of Laura 's room   " God bless she and keep she to - night ! " . I pass the dark window of Sir Percival 's room . then I wait a moment , kneel down with my hand to support I , and so creep to my position , under the protection of the low wall between the bottom of the lighted window and the verandah roof . 

 when I venture to look up at the window itself I find that the top of it only be open , and that the blind inside be draw down . while I be look I see the shadow of Madame Fosco pass across the white field of the blind then pass slowly back again . thus far she could not have hear I , or the shadow would surely have stop at the blind , even if she have want courage enough to open the window and look out ? 

 I place myself sideways against the railing of the verandah first ascertaining , by touch they , the position of the flower - pot on either side of I . there be room enough for I to sit between they and no more . the sweet - scented leaf of the flower on my left hand just brush my cheek as I lightly rest my head against the railing . 

 the first sound that reach I from below be cause by the opening or closing   most probably the latter   of three door in succession the door , no doubt , lead into the hall and into the room on each side of the library , which the Count have pledge himself to examine . the first object that I see be the red spark again travel out into the night from under the verandah , move away towards my window , wait a moment , and then return to the place from which it have set out . 

 " the devil take your restlessness ! when do you mean to sit down ? " growl Sir Percival 's voice beneath I . 

 " ouf ! how hot it be ! " say the Count , sigh and puff wearily . 

 his exclamation be follow by the scraping of the garden chair on the tile pavement under the verandah the welcome sound which tell I they be go to sit close at the window as usual . so far the chance be mine . the clock in the turret strike the quarter to twelve as they settle themselves in their chair . I hear Madame Fosco through the open window yawning , and see her shadow pass once more across the white field of the blind . 

 meanwhile , Sir Percival and the Count begin talk together below , now and then drop their voice a little low than usual , but never sink they to a whisper . the strangeness and peril of my situation , the dread , which I could not master , of Madame Fosco 's lighted window , make it difficult , almost impossible , for I , at first , to keep my presence of mind , and to fix my attention solely on the conversation beneath . for some minute I could only succeed in gather the general substance of it . I understand the Count to say that the one window alight be his wife 's , that the ground floor of the house be quite clear , and that they might now speak to each other without fear of accident . Sir Percival merely answer by upbraid his friend with have unjustifiably slight his wish and neglect his interest all through the day . the Count thereupon defend himself by declare that he have be beset by certain trouble and anxiety which have absorb all his attention , and that the only safe time to come to an explanation be a time when they could feel certain of be neither interrupted nor overheard . " we be at a serious crisis in our affair , Percival , " he say , " and if we be to decide on the future at all , we must decide secretly to - night . " 

 that sentence of the Count 's be the first which my attention be ready enough to master exactly as it be speak . from this point , with certain break and interruption , my whole interest fix breathlessly on the conversation , and I follow it word for word . 

 " Crisis ? " repeat Sir Percival . " it be a bad crisis than you think for , I can tell you . " 

 " so I should suppose , from your behaviour for the last day or two , " return the other coolly . " but wait a little . before we advance to what I do not know , let we be quite certain of what I do know . let we first see if I be right about the time that be past , before I make any proposal to you for the time that be to come . " 

 " stop till I get the brandy and water . have some yourself . " 

 " thank you , Percival . the cold water with pleasure , a spoon , and the basin of sugar . Eau sucree , my friend nothing more . " 

 " sugar - and - water for a man of your age ! there ! mix your sickly mess . you foreigner be all alike . " 

 " now listen , Percival . I will put our position plainly before you , as I understand it , and you shall say if I be right or wrong . you and I both come back to this house from the Continent with our affair very seriously embarrassed " 

 " cut it short ! I want some thousand and you some hundred , and without the money we be both in a fair way to go to the dog together . there be the situation . make what you can of it . go on . " 

 " well , Percival , in your own solid english word , you want some thousand and I want some hundred , and the only way of get they be for you to raise the money for your own necessity   with a small margin beyond for my poor little hundred   by the help of your wife . what do I tell you about your wife on our way to England ? and what do I tell you again when we have come here , and when I have see for myself the sort of woman Miss Halcombe be ? " 

 " how should I know ? you talk nineteen to the dozen , I suppose , just as usual . " 

 " I say this : human ingenuity , my friend , have hitherto only discover two way in which a man can manage a woman . one way be to knock she down a method largely adopt by the brutal low order of the people , but utterly abhorrent to the refined and educate class above they . the other way   much long , much more difficult , but in the end not less certain   be never to accept a provocation at a woman 's hand . it hold with animal , it hold with child , and it hold with woman , who be nothing but child grow up . quiet resolution be the one quality the animal , the child , and the woman all fail in . if they can once shake this superior quality in their master , they get the well of HIM . if they can never succeed in disturb it , he get the well of they . I say to you , remember that plain truth when you want your wife to help you to the money . I say , remember it doubly and trebly in the presence of your wife 's sister , Miss Halcombe . have you remember it ? not once in all the implication that have twist themselves about we in this house . every provocation that your wife and her sister could offer to you , you instantly accept from they . your mad temper lose the signature to the deed , lose the ready money , set Miss Halcombe write to the lawyer for the first time . " 

 " first time ! have she write again ? " 

 " yes , she have write again to - day . " 

 a chair fall on the pavement of the verandah fall with a crash , as if it have be kick down . 

 it be well for I that the Count 's revelation rouse Sir Percival 's anger as it do . on hear that I have be once more discover I start so that the railing against which I lean crack again . have he follow I to the inn ? do he infer that I must have give my letter to Fanny when I tell he I have none for the post - bag . even if it be so , how could he have examine the letter when they have go straight from my hand to the bosom of the girl 's dress ? 

 " thank your lucky star , " I hear the Count say next , " that you have I in the house to undo the harm as fast as you do it . thank your lucky star that I say no when you be mad enough to talk of turn the key to - day on Miss Halcombe , as you turn it in your mischievous folly on your wife . where be your eye ? can you look at Miss Halcombe and not see that she have the foresight and the resolution of a man ? with that woman for my friend I would snap these finger of mine at the world . with that woman for my enemy , I , with all my brain and experience I , Fosco , cunning as the devil himself , as you have tell I a hundred time I walk , in your english phrase , upon egg - shell ! and this grand creature I drink her health in my sugar - and - water this grand creature , who stand in the strength of her love and her courage , firm as a rock , between we two and that poor , flimsy , pretty blonde wife of yours this magnificent woman , whom I admire with all my soul , though I oppose she in your interest and in mine , you drive to extremity as if she be no sharp and no bold than the rest of her sex . Percival ! Percival ! you deserve to fail , and you have fail . " 

 there be a pause . I write the villain 's word about myself because I mean to remember they because I hope yet for the day when I may speak out once for all in his presence , and cast they back one by one in his tooth . 

 Sir Percival be the first to break the silence again . 

 " yes , yes , bully and bluster as much as you like , " he say sulkily ; " the difficulty about the money be not the only difficulty . you would be for take strong measure with the woman yourself if you know as much as I do . " 

 " we will come to that second difficulty all in good time , " rejoin the Count . " you may confuse yourself , Percival , as much as you please , but you shall not confuse I . let the question of the money be settle first . have I convince your obstinacy ? have I show you that your temper will not let you help yourself ? or must I go back , and   as you put it in your dear straightforward English   bully and bluster a little more ? " 

 " Pooh ! it be easy enough to grumble at I . say what be to be do that be a little hard . " 

 " be it ? bah ! this be what be to be do : you give up all direction in the business from to - night you leave it for the future in my hand only . I be talk to a practical british man ha ? well , Practical , will that do for you ? " 

 " what do you propose if I leave it all to you ? " 

 " answer I first . be it to be in my hand or not ? " 

 " say it be in your hand what then ? " 

 " a few question , Percival , to begin with . I must wait a little yet , to let circumstance guide I , and I must know , in every possible way , what those circumstance be likely to be . there be no time to lose . I have tell you already that Miss Halcombe have write to the lawyer to - day for the second time . " 

 " how do you find it out ? what do she say ? " 

 " if I tell you , Percival , we should only come back at the end to where we be now . enough that I have find it out and the finding have cause that trouble and anxiety which make I so inaccessible to you all through to - day . now , to refresh my memory about your affair it be some time since I talk they over with you . the money have be raise , in the absence of your wife 's signature , by mean of bill at three month raise at a cost that make my poverty - stricken foreign hair stand on end to think of it ! when the bill be due , be there really and truly no earthly way of pay they but by the help of your wife ? " 

 " none . " 

 " what ! you have no money at the banker ? " 

 " a few hundred , when I want as many thousand . " 

 " have you no other security to borrow upon ? " 

 " not a shred . " 

 " what have you actually get with your wife at the present moment ? " 

 " nothing but the interest of her twenty thousand pound barely enough to pay our daily expense . " 

 " what do you expect from your wife ? " 

 " three thousand a year when her uncle die . " 

 " a fine fortune , Percival . what sort of a man be this uncle ? old ? " 

 " no neither old nor young . " 

 " a good - temper , freely - live man ? marry ? no I think my wife tell I , not marry . " 

 " of course not . if he be marry , and have a son , Lady Glyde would not be next heir to the property . I will tell you what he be . he be a maudlin , twaddling , selfish fool , and bore everybody who come near he about the state of his health . " 

 " man of that sort , Percival , live long , and marry malevolently when you least expect it . I do not   give you much , my friend , for your chance of the three thousand a year . be there nothing more that come to you from your wife ? " 

 " nothing . " 

 " absolutely nothing ? " 

 " absolutely nothing except in case of her death . " 

 " aha ! in the case of her death . " 

 there be another pause . the Count move from the verandah to the gravel walk outside . I know that he have move by his voice . " the rain have come at last , " I hear he say . it have come . the state of my cloak show that it have be fall thickly for some little time . 

 the Count go back under the verandah I hear the chair creak beneath his weight as he sit down in it again . 

 " well , Percival , " he say , " and in the case of Lady Glyde 's death , what do you get then ? " 

 " if she leave no child " 

 " which she be likely to do ? " 

 " which she be not in the least likely to do " 

 " yes ? " 

 " why , then I get she twenty thousand pound . " 

 " pay down ? " 

 " pay down . " 

 they be silent once more . as their voice cease Madame Fosco 's shadow darken the blind again . instead of pass this time , it remain , for a moment , quite still . I see her finger steal round the corner of the blind , and draw it on one side . the dim white outline of her face , look out straight over I , appear behind the window . I keep still , shroud from head to foot in my black cloak . the rain , which be fast wet I , drip over the glass , blur it , and prevent she from see anything . " More rain ! " I hear she say to herself . she drop the blind , and I breathe again freely . 

 the talk go on below I , the Count resume it this time . 

 " Percival ! do you care about your wife ? " 

 " Fosco ! that be rather a downright question . " 

 " I be a downright man , and I repeat it . " 

 " why the devil do you look at I in that way ? " 

 " you will not   answer I ? well , then , let we say your wife die before the summer be out " 

 " drop it , Fosco ! " 

 " let we say your wife die " 

 " drop it , I tell you ! " 

 " in that case , you would gain twenty thousand pound , and you would lose " 

 " I should lose the chance of three thousand a year . " 

 " the REMOTE chance , Percival the remote chance only . and you want money , at once . in your position the gain be certain the loss doubtful . " 

 " speak for yourself as well as for I . some of the money I want have be borrow for you . and if you come to gain , my wife 's death would be ten thousand pound in your wife 's pocket . sharp as you be , you seem to have conveniently forget Madame Fosco 's legacy . do not   look at I in that way ! I will not   have it ! what with your look and your question , upon my soul , you make my flesh creep ! " 

 " your flesh ? do flesh mean conscience in English ? I speak of your wife 's death as I speak of a possibility . why not ? the respectable lawyer who scribble - scrabble your deed and your will look the death of live people in the face . do lawyer make your flesh creep ? why should I ? it be my business to - night to clear up your position beyond the possibility of mistake , and I have now do it . here be your position . if your wife live , you pay those bill with her signature to the parchment . if your wife die , you pay they with her death . " 

 as he speak the light in Madame Fosco 's room be extinguish , and the whole second floor of the house be now sink in darkness . 

 " talk ! talk ! " grumble Sir Percival . " one would think , to hear you , that my wife 's signature to the deed be get already . " 

 " you have leave the matter in my hand , " retort the Count , " and I have more than two month before I to turn round in . say no more about it , if you please , for the present . when the bill be due , you will see for yourself if my ' talk ! talk ! ' be worth something , or if it be not . and now , Percival , have do with the money matter for to - night , I can place my attention at your disposal , if you wish to consult I on that second difficulty which have mix itself up with our little embarrassment , and which have so alter you for the bad , that I hardly know you again . speak , my friend and pardon I if I shock your fiery national taste by mix myself a second glass of sugar - and - water . " 

 " it be very well to say speak , " reply Sir Percival , in a far more quiet and more polite tone than he have yet adopt , " but it be not so easy to know how to begin . " 

 " shall I help you ? " suggest the Count . " shall I give this private difficulty of yours a name ? what if I call it Anne Catherick ? " 

 " look here , Fosco , you and I have know each other for a long time , and if you have help I out of one or two scrape before this , I have do the good I could to help you in return , as far as money would go . we have make as many friendly sacrifice , on both side , as man could , but we have have our secret from each other , of course have not   we ? " 

 " you have have a secret from I , Percival . there be a skeleton in your cupboard here at Blackwater Park that have peep out in these last few day at other people besides yourself . " 

 " well , suppose it have . if it do not   concern you , you need not   be curious about it , need you ? " 

 " do I look curious about it ? " 

 " yes , you do . " 

 " so ! so ! my face speak the truth , then ? what an immense foundation of good there must be in the nature of a man who arrive at my age , and whose face have not yet lose the habit of speak the truth ! Come , Glyde ! let we be candid one with the other . this secret of yours have seek I : I have not seek it . let we say I be curious do you ask I , as your old friend , to respect your secret , and to leave it , once for all , in your own keeping ? " 

 " yes that be just what I do ask . " 

 " then my curiosity be at an end . it die in I from this moment . " 

 " do you really mean that ? " 

 " what make you doubt I ? " 

 " I have have some experience , Fosco , of your roundabout way , and I be not so sure that you will not   worm it out of I after all . " 

 the chair below suddenly creak again I feel the trellis - work pillar under I shake from top to bottom . the Count have start to his foot , and have strike it with his hand in indignation . 

 " Percival ! Percival ! " he cry passionately , " do you know I no well than that ? have all your experience show you nothing of my character yet ? I be a man of the antique type ! I be capable of the most exalted act of virtue when I have the chance of perform they . it have be the misfortune of my life that I have have few chance . my conception of friendship be sublime ! be it my fault that your skeleton have peep out at I ? why do I confess my curiosity ? you poor superficial Englishman , it be to magnify my own self - control . I could draw your secret out of you , if I like , as I draw this finger out of the palm of my hand you know I could ! but you have appeal to my friendship , and the duty of friendship be sacred to I . see ! I trample my base curiosity under my foot . my exalted sentiment lift I above it . recognise they , Percival ! imitate they , Percival ! shake hand I forgive you . " 

 his voice falter over the last word falter , as if he be actually shed tear ! 

 Sir Percival confusedly attempt to excuse himself , but the Count be too magnanimous to listen to he . 

 " no ! " he say . " when my friend have wound I , I can pardon he without apology . tell I , in plain word , do you want my help ? " 

 " yes , badly enough . " 

 " and you can ask for it without compromise yourself ? " 

 " I can try , at any rate . " 

 " try , then . " 

 " well , this be how it stand : I tell you to - day that I have do my good to find Anne Catherick , and fail . " 

 " yes , you do . " 

 " Fosco ! I be a lost man if I don't find she . " 

 " ha ! be it so serious as that ? " 

 a little stream of light travel out under the verandah , and fall over the gravel - walk . the Count have take the lamp from the inner part of the room to see his friend clearly by the light of it . 

 " yes ! " he say . " your face speak the truth this time . serious , indeed as serious as the money matter themselves . " 

 " more serious . as true as I sit here , more serious ! " 

 the light disappear again and the talk go on . 

 " I show you the letter to my wife that Anne Catherick hide in the sand , " Sir Percival continue . " there be no boasting in that letter , Fosco she do know the Secret . " 

 " say as little as possible , Percival , in my presence , of the Secret . do she know it from you ? " 

 " no , from her mother . " 

 " two woman in possession of your private mind bad , bad , bad , my friend ! one question here , before we go any far . the motive of your shut up the daughter in the asylum be now plain enough to I , but the manner of her escape be not quite so clear . do you suspect the people in charge of she of close their eye purposely , at the instance of some enemy who could afford to make it worth their while ? " 

 " no , she be the well - behave patient they have and , like fool , they trust she . she be just mad enough to be shut up , and just sane enough to ruin I when she be at large if you understand that ? " 

 " I do understand it . now , Percival , come at once to the point , and then I shall know what to do . where be the danger of your position at the present moment ? " 

 " Anne Catherick be in this neighbourhood , and in communication with Lady Glyde there be the danger , plain enough . who can read the letter she hide in the sand , and not see that my wife be in possession of the Secret , deny it as she may ? " 

 " one moment , Percival . if Lady Glyde do know the Secret , she must know also that it be a compromise secret for you . as your wife , surely it be her interest to keep it ? " 

 " be it ? I be come to that . it might be her interest if she care two straw about I . but I happen to be an encumbrance in the way of another man . she be in love with he before she marry I she be in love with he now an infernal vagabond of a drawing - master , name Hartright . " 

 " my dear friend ! what be there extraordinary in that ? they be all in love with some other man . who get the first of a woman 's heart ? in all my experience I have never yet meet with the man who be number one . number two , sometimes . Number three , Four , five , often . number one , never ! he exist , of course but I have not meet with he . " 

 " wait ! I have not   do yet . who do you think help Anne Catherick to get the start , when the people from the mad - house be after she ? Hartright . who do you think see she again in Cumberland ? Hartright . both time he speak to she alone . stop ! do not   interrupt I . the scoundrel 's as sweet on my wife as she be on he . he know the Secret , and she know the Secret . once let they both get together again , and it be her interest and his interest to turn their information against I . " 

 " gently , Percival gently ! be you insensible to the virtue of Lady Glyde ? " 

 " that for the virtue of Lady Glyde ! I believe in nothing about she but her money . do not   you see how the case stand ? she might be harmless enough by herself ; but if she and that vagabond Hartright " 

 " yes , yes , I see . where be Mr. Hartright ? " 

 " out of the country . if he mean to keep a whole skin on his bone , I recommend he not to come back in a hurry . " 

 " be you sure he be out of the country ? " 

 " certain . I have he watch from the time he leave Cumberland to the time he sail . oh , I have   be careful , I can tell you ! Anne Catherick live with some people at a farmhouse near Limmeridge . I go there myself , after she have give I the slip , and make sure that they know nothing . I give her mother a form of letter to write to Miss Halcombe , exonerate I from any bad motive in put she under restraint . I have   spend , I be afraid to say how much , in try to trace she , and in spite of it all , she turn up here and escape I on my own property ! how do I know who else may see she , who else may speak to she ? that pry scoundrel , Hartright , may come back without my know it , and may make use of she to - morrow " 

 " not he , Percival ! while I be on the spot , and while that woman be in the neighbourhood , I will answer for our lay hand on she before Mr. Hartright even if he do come back . I see ! yes , yes , I see ! the finding of Anne Catherick be the first necessity make your mind easy about the rest . your wife be here , under your thumb Miss Halcombe be inseparable from she , and be , therefore , under your thumb also and Mr. Hartright be out of the country . this invisible Anne of yours be all we have to think of for the present . you have make your inquiry ? " 

 " yes . I have be to her mother , I have ransack the village and all to no purpose . " 

 " be her mother to be depend on ? " 

 " yes . " 

 " she have tell your secret once . " 

 " she will not   tell it again . " 

 " why not ? be her own interest concern in keep it , as well as your ? " 

 " yes deeply concerned . " 

 " I be glad to hear it , Percival , for your sake . do not   be discourage , my friend . our money matter , as I tell you , leave I plenty of time to turn round in , and I may search for Anne Catherick to - morrow to well purpose than you . one last question before we go to bed . " 

 " what be it ? " 

 " it be this . when I go to the boat - house to tell Lady Glyde that the little difficulty of her signature be put off , accident take I there in time to see a strange woman part in a very suspicious manner from your wife . but accident do not bring I near enough to see this same woman 's face plainly . I must know how to recognise our invisible Anne . what be she like ? " 

 " like ? come ! I will tell you in two word . she be a sickly likeness of my wife . " 

 the chair creak , and the pillar shake once more . the Count be on his foot again this time in astonishment . 

 " what ! ! ! " he exclaim eagerly . 

 " Fancy my wife , after a bad illness , with a touch of something wrong in her head and there be Anne Catherick for you , " answer Sir Percival . 

 " be they relate to each other ? " 

 " not a bit of it . " 

 " and yet so like ? " 

 " yes , so like . what be you laugh about ? " 

 there be no answer , and no sound of any kind . the Count be laugh in his smooth silent internal way . 

 " what be you laugh about ? " reiterate Sir Percival . 

 " perhaps at my own fancy , my good friend . allow I my italian humour do I not come of the illustrious nation which invent the exhibition of Punch ? well , well , well , I shall know Anne Catherick when I see she and so enough for to - night . make your mind easy , Percival . Sleep , my son , the sleep of the just , and see what I will do for you when daylight come to help we both . I have my project and my plan here in my big head . you shall pay those bill and find Anne Catherick my sacred word of honour on it , but you shall ! be I a friend to be treasure in the good corner of your heart , or be I not ? be I worth those loan of money which you so delicately remind I of a little while since ? whatever you do , never wound I in my sentiment any more . recognise they , Percival ! imitate they , Percival ! I forgive you again I shake hand again . good - night ! " 


 not another word be speak . I hear the Count close the library door . I hear Sir Percival bar up the window - shutter . it have be rain , rain all the time . I be cramp by my position and chill to the bone . when I first try to move , the effort be so painful to I that I be oblige to desist . I try a second time , and succeed in rise to my knee on the wet roof . 

 as I creep to the wall , and raise myself against it , I look back , and see the window of the Count 's dressing - room gleam into light . my sink courage flicker up in I again , and keep my eye fix on his window , as I steal my way back , step by step , past the wall of the house . 

 the clock strike the quarter after one , when I lay my hand on the window - sill of my own room . I have see nothing and hear nothing which could lead I to suppose that my retreat have be discover . 



 X 

 June 20th . eight o'clock . the sun be shin in a clear sky . I have not be near my bed I have not once close my weary wakeful eye . from the same window at which I look out into the darkness of last night , I look out now at the bright stillness of the morning . 

 I count the hour that have pass since I escape to the shelter of this room by my own sensation and those hour seem like week . 

 how short a time , and yet how long to I since I sink down in the darkness , here , on the floor drench to the skin , cramp in every limb , cold to the bone , a useless , helpless , panic - stricken creature . 

 I hardly know when I rouse myself . I hardly know when I grope my way back to the bedroom , and light the candle , and search   with a strange ignorance , at first , of where to look for they   for dry clothe to warm I . the doing of these thing be in my mind , but not the time when they be do . 

 can I even remember when the chill , cramp feel leave I , and the throb heat come in its place ? 

 surely it be before the sun rise ? yes , I hear the clock strike three . I remember the time by the sudden brightness and clearness , the feverish strain and excitement of all my faculty which come with it . I remember my resolution to control myself , to wait patiently hour after hour , till the chance offer of remove Laura from this horrible place , without the danger of immediate discovery and pursuit . I remember the persuasion settle itself in my mind that the word those two man have say to each other would furnish we , not only with our justification for leave the house , but with our weapon of defence against they as well . I recall the impulse that awaken in I to preserve those word in writing , exactly as they be speak , while the time be my own , and while my memory vividly retain they . all this I remember plainly : there be no confusion in my head yet . the come in here from the bedroom , with my pen and ink and paper , before sunrise the sitting down at the widely - open window to get all the air I could to cool I the ceaseless writing , fast and fast , hot and hotter , drive on more and more wakefully , all through the dreadful interval before the house be astir again how clearly I recall it , from the beginning by candle - light , to the end on the page before this , in the sunshine of the new day ! 

 why do I sit here still ? why do I weary my hot eye and my burn head by write more ? why not lie down and rest myself , and try to quench the fever that consume I , in sleep ? 

 I dare not attempt it . a fear beyond all other fear have get possession of I . I be afraid of this heat that parch my skin . I be afraid of the creeping and throb that I feel in my head . if I lie down now , how do I know that I may have the sense and the strength to rise again ? 

 oh , the rain , the rain the cruel rain that chill I last night ! 

 nine o'clock . be it nine strike , or eight ? nine , surely ? I be shiver again shiver , from head to foot , in the summer air . have I be sit here asleep ? I do not   know what I have be do . 

 oh , my God ! be I go to be ill ? 


 Ill , at such a time as this ! 

 my head I be sadly afraid of my head . I can write , but the line all run together . I see the word . Laura I can write Laura , and see I write it . eight or nine which be it ? 

 so cold , so cold oh , that rain last night ! and the stroke of the clock , the stroke I can not   count , keep strike in my head 

 note   at this place the entry in the Diary cease to be legible . the two or three line which follow contain fragment of word only , mingle with blot and scratch of the pen . the last mark on the paper bear some resemblance to the first two letter   L and a   of the name of Lady Glyde . 

 on the next page of the Diary , another entry appear . it be in a man 's handwriting , large , bold , and firmly regular , and the date be " June the 21st . " it contain these line  


 POSTSCRIPT by a SINCERE FRIEND 


 the illness of our excellent Miss Halcombe have afford I the opportunity of enjoy an unexpected intellectual pleasure . 

 I refer to the perusal   which I have just complete   of this interesting Diary . 

 there be many hundred page here . I can lay my hand on my heart , and declare that every page have charm , refresh , delight I . 

 to a man of my sentiment it be unspeakably gratify to be able to say this . 

 admirable woman ! 

 I allude to Miss Halcombe . 

 stupendous effort ! 

 I refer to the Diary . 

 yes ! these page be amazing . the tact which I find here , the discretion , the rare courage , the wonderful power of memory , the accurate observation of character , the easy grace of style , the charming outburst of womanly feeling , have all inexpressibly increase my admiration of this sublime creature , of this magnificent Marian . the presentation of my own character be masterly in the extreme . I certify , with my whole heart , to the fidelity of the portrait . I feel how vivid an impression I must have produce to have be paint in such strong , such rich , such massive colour as these . I lament afresh the cruel necessity which set our interest at variance , and oppose we to each other . under happy circumstance how worthy I should have be of Miss Halcombe how worthy Miss Halcombe would have be of I . 

 the sentiment which animate my heart assure I that the line I have just write express a Profound Truth . 

 those sentiment exalt I above all merely personal consideration . I bear witness , in the most disinterested manner , to the excellence of the stratagem by which this unparalleled woman surprise the private interview between Percival and myself also to the marvellous accuracy of her report of the whole conversation from its beginning to its end . 

 those sentiment have induce I to offer to the unimpressionable doctor who attend on she my vast knowledge of chemistry , and my luminous experience of the more subtle resource which medical and magnetic science have place at the disposal of mankind . he have hitherto decline to avail himself of my assistance . miserable man ! 

 finally , those sentiment dictate the line grateful , sympathetic , paternal line which appear in this place . I close the book . my strict sense of propriety restore it   by the hand of my wife   to its place on the writer 's table . event be hurry I away . circumstance be guide I to serious issue . vast perspective of success unroll themselves before my eye . I accomplish my destiny with a calmness which be terrible to myself . nothing but the homage of my admiration be my own . I deposit it with respectful tenderness at the foot of Miss Halcombe . 

 I breathe my wish for her recovery . 

 I condole with she on the inevitable failure of every plan that she have form for her sister 's benefit . at the same time , I entreat she to believe that the information which I have derive from her Diary will in no respect help I to contribute to that failure . it simply confirm the plan of conduct which I have previously arrange . I have to thank these page for awaken the fine sensibility in my nature nothing more . 

 to a person of similar sensibility this simple assertion will explain and excuse everything . 

 Miss Halcombe be a person of similar sensibility . 

 in that persuasion I sign myself , 
 Fosco . 



 the story continued BY FREDERICK FAIRLIE , ESQ . , of LIMMERIDGE HOUSE 2 


  2   the manner in which Mr. Fairlie 's Narrative and other Narratives that be shortly to follow it , be originally obtain , form the subject of an explanation which will appear at a later period . 


 it be the grand misfortune of my life that nobody will let I alone . 

 why I ask everybody why worry I ? nobody answer that question , and nobody let I alone . relative , friend , and stranger all combine to annoy I . what have I do ? I ask myself , I ask my servant , Louis , fifty time a day what have I do ? neither of we can tell . most extraordinary ! 

 the last annoyance that have assail I be the annoyance of be call upon to write this Narrative . be a man in my state of nervous wretchedness capable of write narrative ? when I put this extremely reasonable objection , I be tell that certain very serious event relate to my niece have happen within my experience , and that I be the fit person to describe they on that account . I be threaten if I fail to exert myself in the manner require , with consequence which I can not so much as think of without perfect prostration . there be really no need to threaten I . shatter by my miserable health and my family trouble , I be incapable of resistance . if you insist , you take your unjust advantage of I , and I give way immediately . I will endeavour to remember what I can   under protest , and to write what I can   also under protest , and what I can not   remember and can not   write , Louis must remember and write for I . he be an ass , and I be an invalid , and we be likely to make all sort of mistake between we . how humiliating ! 

 I be tell to remember date . good heaven ! I never do such a thing in my life how be I to begin now ? 

 I have ask Louis . he be not quite such an ass as I have hitherto suppose . he remember the date of the event , within a week or two and I remember the name of the person . the date be towards the end of June , or the beginning of July , and the name   in my opinion a remarkably vulgar one   be Fanny . 

 at the end of June , or the beginning of July , then , I be recline in my customary state , surround by the various object of art which I have collect about I to improve the taste of the barbarous people in my neighbourhood . that be to say , I have the photograph of my picture , and print , and coin , and so forth , all about I , which I intend , one of these day , to present   the photograph , I mean , if the clumsy english language will let I mean anything   to present to the institution at Carlisle   horrid place ! , with a view to improve the taste of the member   goth and Vandals to a man . it might be suppose that a gentleman who be in course of confer a great national benefit on his countryman be the last gentleman in the world to be unfeelingly worried about private difficulty and family affair . quite a mistake , I assure you , in my case . 

 however , there I be , recline , with my art - treasure about I , and want a quiet morning . because I want a quiet morning , of course Louis come in . it be perfectly natural that I should inquire what the deuce he mean by make his appearance when I have not ring my bell . I seldom swear it be such an ungentlemanlike habit but when Louis answer by a grin , I think it be also perfectly natural that I should damn he for grin . at any rate , I do . 

 this rigorous mode of treatment , I have observe , invariably bring person in the low class of life to their sens . it bring Louis to his sens . he be so obliging as to leave off grinning , and inform I that a Young Person be outside want to see I . he add   with the odious talkativeness of servant , that her name be Fanny . 

 " who be fanny ? " 

 " Lady Glyde 's maid , sir . " 

 " what do Lady Glyde 's maid want with I ? " 

 " a letter , sir " 

 " take it . " 

 " she refuse to give it to anybody but you , sir . " 

 " who send the letter ? " 

 " Miss Halcombe , sir . " 

 the moment I hear Miss Halcombe 's name I give up . it be a habit of mine always to give up to Miss Halcombe . I find , by experience , that it save noise . I give up on this occasion . Dear Marian ! 

 " let Lady Glyde 's maid come in , Louis . stop ! do her shoe creak ? " 

 I be oblige to ask the question . creak shoe invariably upset I for the day . I be resign to see the Young Person , but I be not resign to let the Young Person 's shoe upset I . there be a limit even to my endurance . 

 Louis affirm distinctly that her shoe be to be depend upon . I wave my hand . he introduce she . be it necessary to say that she express her sense of embarrassment by shut up her mouth and breathe through her nose ? to the student of female human nature in the low order , surely not . 

 let I do the girl justice . her shoe do not creak . but why do Young Persons in service all perspire at the hand ? why have they all get fat nose and hard cheek ? and why be their face so sadly unfinished , especially about the corner of the eyelid ? I be not strong enough to think deeply myself on any subject , but I appeal to professional man , who be . why have we no variety in our breed of Young Persons ? 

 " you have a letter for I , from Miss Halcombe ? put it down on the table , please , and do not   upset anything . how be Miss Halcombe ? " 

 " very well , thank you , sir . " 

 " and Lady Glyde ? " 

 I receive no answer . the Young Person 's face become more unfinished than ever , and I think she begin to cry . I certainly see something moist about her eye . tear or perspiration ? Louis   whom I have just consult   be incline to think , tear . he be in her class of life , and he ought to know well . let we say , tear . 

 except when the refining process of art judiciously remove from they all resemblance to nature , I distinctly object to tear . tear be scientifically describe as a secretion . I can understand that a secretion may be healthy or unhealthy , but I can not see the interest of a secretion from a sentimental point of view . perhaps my own secretion be all wrong together , I be a little prejudiced on the subject . no matter . I behave , on this occasion , with all possible propriety and feeling . I close my eye and say to Louis 

 " endeavour to ascertain what she mean . " 

 Louis endeavour , and the Young Person endeavour . they succeed in confuse each other to such an extent that I be bind in common gratitude to say , they really amuse I . I think I shall send for they again when I be in low spirit . I have just mention this idea to Louis . strange to say , it seem to make he uncomfortable . poor devil ! 

 surely I be not expect to repeat my niece 's maid 's explanation of her tear , interpret in the English of my swiss valet ? the thing be manifestly impossible . I can give my own impression and feeling perhaps . will that do as well ? please say , yes . 

 my idea be that she begin by tell I   through Louis   that her master have dismiss she from her mistress 's service .   Observe , throughout , the strange irrelevancy of the Young Person . be it my fault that she have lose her place ?   on her dismissal , she have go to the inn to sleep .   I do not   keep the inn why mention it to I ?   between six o'clock and seven Miss Halcombe have come to say good - bye , and have give she two letter , one for I , and one for a gentleman in London .   I be not a gentleman in London hang the gentleman in London !   she have carefully put the two letter into her bosom   what have I to do with her bosom ? ; she have be very unhappy , when Miss Halcombe have go away again ; she have not have the heart to put bit or drop between her lip till it be near bedtime , and then , when it be close on nine o'clock , she have think she should like a cup of tea .   be I responsible for any of these vulgar fluctuation , which begin with unhappiness and end with tea ?   just as she be warm the POT   I give the word on the authority of Louis , who say he know what they mean , and wish to explain , but I snub he on principle   just as she be warm the pot the door open , and she be strike of a heap   her own word again , and perfectly unintelligible this time to Louis , as well as to myself   by the appearance in the inn parlour of her ladyship the Countess . I give my niece 's maid 's description of my sister 's title with a sense of the high relish . my poor dear sister be a tiresome woman who marry a foreigner . to resume : the door open , her ladyship the Countess appear in the parlour , and the Young Person be strike of a heap . most remarkable ! 


 I must really rest a little before I can get on any far . when I have recline for a few minute , with my eye close , and when Louis have refresh my poor ache temple with a little eau - de - cologne , I may be able to proceed . 

 her ladyship the Countess 

 no . I be able to proceed , but not to sit up . I will recline and dictate . Louis have a horrid accent , but he know the language , and can write . how very convenient ! 


 her ladyship , the Countess , explain her unexpected appearance at the inn by tell Fanny that she have come to bring one or two little message which Miss Halcombe in her hurry have forget . the Young Person thereupon wait anxiously to hear what the message be , but the Countess seem disinclined to mention they   so like my sister 's tiresome way !   until Fanny have have her tea . her ladyship be surprisingly kind and thoughtful about it   extremely unlike my sister , and say , " I be sure , my poor girl , you must want your tea . we can let the message wait till afterwards . come , come , if nothing else will put you at your ease , I will make the tea and have a cup with you . " I think those be the word , as report excitably , in my presence , by the Young Person . at any rate , the Countess insist on make the tea , and carry her ridiculous ostentation of humility so far as to take one cup herself , and to insist on the girl 's take the other . the girl drink the tea , and accord to her own account , solemnise the extraordinary occasion five minute afterwards by faint dead away for the first time in her life . here again I use her own word . Louis think they be accompany by an increase secretion of tear . I can not   say myself . the effort of listen be quite as much as I could manage , my eye be close . 

 where do I leave off ? ah , yes she faint after drink a cup of tea with the Countess a proceeding which might have interest I if I have be her medical man , but be nothing of the sort I feel bored by hear of it , nothing more . when she come to herself in half an hour 's time she be on the sofa , and nobody be with she but the landlady . the Countess , find it too late to remain any long at the inn , have go away as soon as the girl show sign of recover , and the landlady have be good enough to help she upstairs to bed . 

 leave by herself , she have feel in her bosom   I regret the necessity of refer to this part of the subject a second time , and have find the two letter there quite safe , but strangely crumple . she have be giddy in the night , but have get up well enough to travel in the morning . she have put the letter address to that obtrusive stranger , the gentleman in London into the post , and have now deliver the other letter into my hand as she be tell . this be the plain truth , and though she could not blame herself for any intentional neglect , she be sadly trouble in her mind , and sadly in want of a word of advice . at this point Louis think the secretion appear again . perhaps they do , but it be of infinitely great importance to mention that at this point also I lose my patience , open my eye , and interfere . 

 " what be the purport of all this ? " I inquire . 

 my niece 's irrelevant maid star , and stand speechless . 

 " endeavour to explain , " I say to my servant . " translate I , Louis . " 

 Louis endeavour and translate . in other word , he descend immediately into a bottomless pit of confusion , and the Young Person follow he down . I really do not   know when I have be so amused . I leave they at the bottom of the pit as long as they divert I . when they cease to divert I , I exert my intelligence , and pull they up again . 

 it be unnecessary to say that my interference enable I , in due course of time , to ascertain the purport of the Young Person 's remark . 

 I discover that she be uneasy in her mind , because the train of event that she have just describe to I have prevent she from receive those supplementary message which Miss Halcombe have intrust to the Countess to deliver . she be afraid the message might have be of great importance to her mistress 's interest . her dread of Sir Percival have deter she from go to Blackwater Park late at night to inquire about they , and Miss Halcombe 's own direction to she , on no account to miss the train in the morning , have prevent she from wait at the inn the next day . she be most anxious that the misfortune of her fainting - fit should not lead to the second misfortune of make her mistress think her neglectful , and she would humbly beg to ask I whether I would advise she to write her explanation and excuse to Miss Halcombe , request to receive the message by letter , if it be not too late . I make no apology for this extremely prosy paragraph . I have be order to write it . there be people , unaccountable as it may appear , who actually take more interest in what my niece 's maid say to I on this occasion than in what I say to my niece 's maid . amusing perversity ! 

 " I should feel very much oblige to you , sir , if you would kindly tell I what I have well do , " remark the Young Person . 

 " let thing stop as they be , " I say , adapt my language to my listener . " I invariably let thing stop as they be . yes . be that all ? " 

 " if you think it would be a liberty in I , sir , to write , of course I would not   venture to do so . but I be so very anxious to do all I can to serve my mistress faithfully " 

 People in the low class of life never know when or how to go out of a room . they invariably require to be help out by their better . I think it high time to help the Young Person out . I do it with two judicious word 

 " good - morning . " 

 something outside or inside this singular girl suddenly creak . Louis , who be look at she   which I be not , say she creak when she curtsey . curious . be it her shoe , her stay , or her bone ? Louis think it be her stay . most extraordinary ! 



 as soon as I be leave by myself I have a little nap I really want it . when I awake again I notice dear Marian 's letter . if I have have the least idea of what it contain I should certainly not have attempt to open it . be , unfortunately for myself , quite innocent of all suspicion , I read the letter . it immediately upset I for the day . 

 I be , by nature , one of the most easy - temper creature that ever live I make allowance for everybody , and I take offence at nothing . but as I have before remark , there be limit to my endurance . I lay down Marian 's letter , and feel myself justly feel myself an injure man . 

 I be about to make a remark . it be , of course , applicable to the very serious matter now under notice , or I should not allow it to appear in this place . 

 nothing , in my opinion , set the odious selfishness of mankind in such a repulsively vivid light as the treatment , in all class of society , which the single people receive at the hand of the married people . when you have once show yourself too considerate and self - deny to add a family of your own to an already overcrowded population , you be vindictively mark out by your married friend , who have no similar consideration and no similar self - denial , as the recipient of half their conjugal trouble , and the bear friend of all their child . husband and wife talk of the care of matrimony , and bachelor and spinster bear they . take my own case . I considerately remain single , and my poor dear brother Philip inconsiderately marry . what do he do when he die ? he leave his daughter to I . she be a sweet girl she be also a dreadful responsibility . why lie she on my shoulder ? because I be bind , in the harmless character of a single man , to relieve my married connection of all their own trouble . I do my good with my brother 's responsibility I marry my niece , with infinite fuss and difficulty , to the man her father want she to marry . she and her husband disagree , and unpleasant consequence follow . what do she do with those consequence ? she transfer they to I . why transfer they to I ? because I be bind , in the harmless character of a single man , to relieve my married connection of all their own trouble . poor single people ! poor human nature ! 

 it be quite unnecessary to say that Marian 's letter threaten I . everybody threaten I . all sort of horror be to fall on my devoted head if I hesitate to turn Limmeridge House into an asylum for my niece and her misfortune . I do hesitate , nevertheless . 

 I have mention that my usual course , hitherto , have be to submit to dear Marian , and save noise . but on this occasion , the consequence involve in her extremely inconsiderate proposal be of a nature to make I pause . if I open Limmeridge House as an asylum to Lady Glyde , what security have I against Sir Percival Glyde 's follow she here in a state of violent resentment against I for harbour his wife ? I see such a perfect labyrinth of trouble involve in this proceeding that I determine to feel my ground , as it be . I write , therefore , to dear Marian to beg   as she have no husband to lay claim to she   that she would come here by herself , first , and talk the matter over with I . if she could answer my objection to my own perfect satisfaction , then I assure she that I would receive our sweet Laura with the great pleasure , but not otherwise . 

 I feel , of course , at the time , that this temporise on my part would probably end in bring Marian here in a state of virtuous indignation , bang door . but then , the other course of proceeding might end in bring Sir Percival here in a state of virtuous indignation , bang door also , and of the two indignation and banging I prefer Marian 's , because I be use to she . accordingly I despatch the letter by return of post . it gain I time , at all event and , oh dear I ! what a point that be to begin with . 

 when I be totally prostrate   do I mention that I be totally prostrate by Marian 's letter ?   it always take I three day to get up again . I be very unreasonable I expect three day of quiet . of course I do not   get they . 

 the third day 's post bring I a most impertinent letter from a person with whom I be totally unacquainted . he describe himself as the act partner of our man of business our dear , pig - head old Gilmore and he inform I that he have lately receive , by the post , a letter address to he in Miss Halcombe 's handwriting . on open the envelope , he have discover , to his astonishment , that it contain nothing but a blank sheet of note - paper . this circumstance appear to he so suspicious   as suggest to his restless legal mind that the letter have be tamper with   that he have at once write to Miss Halcombe , and have receive no answer by return of post . in this difficulty , instead of act like a sensible man and let thing take their proper course , his next absurd proceeding , on his own showing , be to pester I by write to inquire if I know anything about it . what the deuce should I know about it ? why alarm I as well as himself ? I write back to that effect . it be one of my keenest letter . I have produce nothing with a sharp epistolary edge to it since I tender his dismissal in write to that extremely troublesome person , Mr. Walter Hartright . 

 my letter produce its effect . I hear nothing more from the lawyer . 

 this perhaps be not altogether surprising . but it be certainly a remarkable circumstance that no second letter reach I from Marian , and that no warning sign appear of her arrival . her unexpected absence do I amazing good . it be so very soothing and pleasant to infer   as I do of course   that my married connection have make it up again . five day of undisturbed tranquillity , of delicious single blessedness , quite restore I . on the sixth day I feel strong enough to send for my photographer , and to set he at work again on the presentation copy of my art - treasure , with a view , as I have already mention , to the improvement of taste in this barbarous neighbourhood . I have just dismiss he to his workshop , and have just begin coquet with my coin , when Louis suddenly make his appearance with a card in his hand . 

 " another Young Person ? " I say . " I will not   see she . in my state of health Young Persons disagree with I . not at home . " 

 " it be a gentleman this time , sir . " 

 a gentleman of course make a difference . I look at the card . 

 Gracious Heaven ! my tiresome sister 's foreign husband , Count Fosco . 



 be it necessary to say what my first impression be when I look at my visitor 's card ? surely not ! my sister have marry a foreigner , there be but one impression that any man in his sens could possibly feel . of course the Count have come to borrow money of I . 

 " Louis , " I say , " do you think he would go away if you give he five shilling ? " 

 Louis look quite shocked . he surprise I inexpressibly by declare that my sister 's foreign husband be dress superbly , and look the picture of prosperity . under these circumstance my first impression alter to a certain extent . I now take it for grant that the Count have matrimonial difficulty of his own to contend with , and that he have come , like the rest of the family , to cast they all on my shoulder . 

 " do he mention his business ? " I ask . 

 " Count Fosco say he have come here , sir , because Miss Halcombe be unable to leave Blackwater Park . " 

 fresh trouble , apparently . not exactly his own , as I have suppose , but dear Marian 's . trouble , anyway . oh dear ! 

 " show he in , " I say resignedly . 

 the Count 's first appearance really startle I . he be such an alarmingly large person that I quite tremble . I feel certain that he would shake the floor and knock down my art - treasure . he do neither the one nor the other . he be refreshingly dress in summer costume his manner be delightfully self - possess and quiet he have a charming smile . my first impression of he be highly favourable . it be not creditable to my penetration as the sequel will show to acknowledge this , but I be a naturally candid man , and I do acknowledge it notwithstanding . 

 " allow I to present myself , Mr. Fairlie , " he say . " I come from Blackwater Park , and I have the honour and the happiness of be Madame Fosco 's husband . let I take my first and last advantage of that circumstance by entreat you not to make a stranger of I . I beg you will not disturb yourself I beg you will not move . " 

 " you be very good , " I reply . " I wish I be strong enough to get up . charm to see you at Limmeridge . please take a chair . " 

 " I be afraid you be suffer to - day , " say the Count . 

 " as usual , " I say . " I be nothing but a bundle of nerve dress up to look like a man . " 

 " I have study many subject in my time , " remark this sympathetic person . " among other the inexhaustible subject of nerve . may I make a suggestion , at once the simple and the most profound ? will you let I alter the light in your room ? " 

 " certainly if you will be so very kind as not to let any of it in on I . " 

 he walk to the window . such a contrast to dear Marian ! so extremely considerate in all his movement ! 

 " Light , " he say , in that delightfully confidential tone which be so soothing to an invalid , " be the first essential . light stimulate , nourishe , preserve . you can no more do without it , Mr. Fairlie , than if you be a flower . observe . here , where you sit , I close the shutter to compose you . there , where you do not sit , I draw up the blind and let in the invigorate sun . admit the light into your room if you can not bear it on yourself . Light , sir , be the grand decree of Providence . you accept Providence with your own restriction . accept light on the same term . " 

 I think this very convincing and attentive . he have take I in up to that point about the light , he have certainly take I in . 

 " you see I confuse , " he say , return to his place " on my word of honour , Mr. Fairlie , you see I confused in your presence . " 

 " shocked to hear it , I be sure . may I inquire why ? " 

 " Sir , can I enter this room   where you sit a sufferer , and see you surround by these admirable object of Art , without discover that you be a man whose feeling be acutely impressionable , whose sympathy be perpetually alive ? tell I , can I do this ? " 

 if I have be strong enough to sit up in my chair I should , of course , have bow . not be strong enough , I smile my acknowledgment instead . it do just as well , we both understand one another . 

 " pray follow my train of thought , " continue the Count . " I sit here , a man of refine sympathy myself , in the presence of another man of refined sympathy also . I be conscious of a terrible necessity for lacerate those sympathy by refer to domestic event of a very melancholy kind . what be the inevitable consequence ? I have do myself the honour of point it out to you already . I sit confused . " 

 be it at this point that I begin to suspect he be go to bore I ? I rather think it be . 

 " be it absolutely necessary to refer to these unpleasant matter ? " I inquire . " in our homely english phrase , Count Fosco , will not   they keep ? " 

 the Count , with the most alarming solemnity , sigh and shake his head . 

 " must I really hear they ? " 

 he shrug his shoulder   it be the first foreign thing he have do since he have be in the room , and look at I in an unpleasantly penetrate manner . my instinct tell I that I have well close my eye . I obey my instinct . 

 " please break it gently , " I plead . " anybody dead ? " 

 " dead ! " cry the Count , with unnecessary foreign fierceness . " Mr. Fairlie , your national composure terrify I . in the name of Heaven , what have I say or do to make you think I the messenger of death ? "
