 " pray accept my apology , " I answer . " you have say and do nothing . I make it a rule in these distressing case always to anticipate the bad . it break the blow by meet it half - way , and so on . inexpressibly relieve , I be sure , to hear that nobody be dead . anybody ill ? " 

 I open my eye and look at he . be he very yellow when he come in , or have he turn very yellow in the last minute or two ? I really can not   say , and I can not   ask Louis , because he be not in the room at the time . 

 " anybody ill ? " I repeat , observe that my national composure still appear to affect he . 

 " that be part of my bad news , Mr. Fairlie . yes . somebody be ill . " 

 " grieve , I be sure . which of they be it ? " 

 " to my profound sorrow , Miss Halcombe . perhaps you be in some degree prepare to hear this ? perhaps when you find that Miss Halcombe do not come here by herself , as you propose , and do not write a second time , your affectionate anxiety may have make you fear that she be ill ? " 

 I have no doubt my affectionate anxiety have lead to that melancholy apprehension at some time or other , but at the moment my wretched memory entirely fail to remind I of the circumstance . however , I say yes , in justice to myself . I be much shocked . it be so very uncharacteristic of such a robust person as dear Marian to be ill , that I could only suppose she have meet with an accident . a horse , or a false step on the stair , or something of that sort . 

 " be it serious ? " I ask . 

 " serious beyond a doubt , " he reply . " dangerous I hope and trust not . Miss Halcombe unhappily expose herself to be wet through by a heavy rain . the cold that follow be of an aggravated kind , and it have now bring with it the bad consequence fever . " 

 when I hear the word fever , and when I remember at the same moment that the unscrupulous person who be now address I have just come from Blackwater Park , I think I should have faint on the spot . 

 " Good God ! " I say . " be it infectious ? " 

 " not at present , " he answer , with detestable composure . " it may turn to infection but no such deplorable complication have take place when I leave Blackwater Park . I have feel the deep interest in the case , Mr. Fairlie I have endeavour to assist the regular medical attendant in watch it accept my personal assurance of the uninfectious nature of the fever when I last see it . " 

 accept his assurance ! I never be far from accept anything in my life . I would not have believe he on his oath . he be too yellow to be believe . he look like a walk - west - indian - epidemic . he be big enough to carry typhus by the ton , and to dye the very carpet he walk on with scarlet fever . in certain emergency my mind be remarkably soon make up . I instantly determine to get rid of he . 

 " you will kindly excuse an invalid , " I say " but long conference of any kind invariably upset I . may I beg to know exactly what the object be to which I be indebted for the honour of your visit ? " 

 I fervently hop that this remarkably broad hint would throw he off his balance confuse he reduce he to polite apology in short , get he out of the room . on the contrary , it only settle he in his chair . he become additionally solemn , and dignified , and confidential . he hold up two of his horrid finger and give I another of his unpleasantly penetrate look . what be I to do ? I be not strong enough to quarrel with he . conceive my situation , if you please . be language adequate to describe it ? I think not . 

 " the object of my visit , " he go on , quite irrepressibly , " be number on my finger . they be two . first , I come to bear my testimony , with profound sorrow , to the lamentable disagreement between Sir Percival and Lady Glyde . I be Sir Percival 's old friend I be relate to Lady Glyde by marriage I be an eye - witness of all that have happen at Blackwater Park . in those three capacity I speak with authority , with confidence , with honourable regret . Sir , I inform you , as the head of Lady Glyde 's family , that Miss Halcombe have exaggerate nothing in the letter which she write to your address . I affirm that the remedy which that admirable lady have propose be the only remedy that will spare you the horror of public scandal . a temporary separation between husband and wife be the one peaceable solution of this difficulty . part they for the present , and when all cause of irritation be remove , I , who have now the honour of address you I will undertake to bring Sir Percival to reason . Lady Glyde be innocent , Lady Glyde be injure , but follow my thought here ! she be , on that very account   I say it with shame , the cause of irritation while she remain under her husband 's roof . no other house can receive she with propriety but your . I invite you to open it . " 

 cool . here be a matrimonial hailstorm pour in the South of England , and I be invite , by a man with fever in every fold of his coat , to come out from the North of England and take my share of the pelting . I try to put the point forcibly , just as I have put it here . the Count deliberately lower one of his horrid finger , keep the other up , and go on rode over I , as it be , without even the common coach - manlike attention of cry " Hi ! " before he knock I down . 

 " follow my thought once more , if you please , " he resume . " my first object you have hear . my second object in come to this house be to do what Miss Halcombe 's illness have prevent she from do for herself . my large experience be consult on all difficult matter at Blackwater Park , and my friendly advice be request on the interesting subject of your letter to Miss Halcombe . I understand at once for my sympathy be your sympathy why you wish to see she here before you pledge yourself to invite Lady Glyde . you be most right , sir , in hesitate to receive the wife until you be quite certain that the husband will not exert his authority to reclaim she . I agree to that . I also agree that such delicate explanation as this difficulty involve be not explanation which can be properly dispose of by write only . my presence here   to my own great inconvenience   be the proof that I speak sincerely . as for the explanation themselves , I Fosco I , who know Sir Percival much well than Miss Halcombe know he , affirm to you , on my honour and my word , that he will not come near this house , or attempt to communicate with this house , while his wife be live in it . his affair be embarrassed . offer he his freedom by mean of the absence of Lady Glyde . I promise you he will take his freedom , and go back to the continent at the early moment when he can get away . be this clear to you as crystal ? yes , it be . have you question to address to I ? be it so , I be here to answer . Ask , Mr. Fairlie oblige I by ask to your heart 's content . " 

 he have say so much already in spite of I , and he look so dreadfully capable of say a great deal more also in spite of I , that I decline his amiable invitation in pure self - defence . 

 " many thank , " I reply . " I be sink fast . in my state of health I must take thing for grant . allow I to do so on this occasion . we quite understand each other . yes . much oblige , I be sure , for your kind interference . if I ever get well , and ever have a second opportunity of improve our acquaintance " 

 he get up . I think he be go . no . More talk , more time for the development of infectious influence in my room , too remember that , in my room ! 

 " one moment yet , " he say , " one moment before I take my leave . I ask permission at part to impress on you an urgent necessity . it be this , sir . you must not think of wait till Miss Halcombe recover before you receive Lady Glyde . Miss Halcombe have the attendance of the doctor , of the housekeeper at Blackwater Park , and of an experienced nurse as well three person for whose capacity and devotion I answer with my life . I tell you that . I tell you , also , that the anxiety and alarm of her sister 's illness have already affect the health and spirit of Lady Glyde , and have make she totally unfit to be of use in the sick - room . her position with her husband grow more and more deplorable and dangerous every day . if you leave she any long at Blackwater Park , you do nothing whatever to hasten her sister 's recovery , and at the same time , you risk the public scandal , which you and I , and all of we , be bind in the sacred interest of the family to avoid . with all my soul , I advise you to remove the serious responsibility of delay from your own shoulder by write to Lady Glyde to come here at once . do your affectionate , your honourable , your inevitable duty , and whatever happen in the future , no one can lay the blame on you . I speak from my large experience I offer my friendly advice . be it accept yes , or no ? " 

 I look at he merely look at he with my sense of his amazing assurance , and my dawn resolution to ring for Louis and have he show out of the room express in every line of my face . it be perfectly incredible , but quite true , that my face do not appear to produce the slight impression on he . bear without nerve evidently bear without nerve . 

 " you hesitate ? " he say . " Mr. Fairlie ! I understand that hesitation . you object see , sir , how my sympathy look straight down into your thought ! you object that Lady Glyde be not in health and not in spirit to take the long journey , from Hampshire to this place , by herself . her own maid be remove from she , as you know , and of other servant fit to travel with she , from one end of England to another , there be none at Blackwater Park . you object , again , that she can not comfortably stop and rest in London , on her way here , because she can not comfortably go alone to a public hotel where she be a total stranger . in one breath , I grant both objection in another breath , I remove they . follow I , if you please , for the last time . it be my intention , when I return to England with Sir Percival , to settle myself in the neighbourhood of London . that purpose have just be happily accomplish . I have take , for six month , a little furnished house in the quarter call St. John 's Wood . be so oblige as to keep this fact in your mind , and observe the programme I now propose . Lady Glyde travel to London   a short journey   I myself meet she at the station I take she to rest and sleep at my house , which be also the house of her aunt when she be restore I escort she to the station again she travel to this place , and her own maid   who be now under your roof   receive she at the carriage - door . here be comfort consult here be the interest of propriety consult here be your own duty duty of hospitality , sympathy , protection , to an unhappy lady in need of all three smooth and make easy , from the beginning to the end . I cordially invite you , sir , to second my effort in the sacred interest of the family . I seriously advise you to write , by my hand , offer the hospitality of your house   and heart , and the hospitality of my house   and heart , to that injured and unfortunate lady whose cause I plead to - day . " 

 he wave his horrid hand at I he strike his infectious breast he address I oratorically , as if I be lay up in the House of Commons . it be high time to take a desperate course of some sort . it be also high time to send for Louis , and adopt the precaution of fumigate the room . 

 in this try emergency an idea occur to I an inestimable idea which , so to speak , kill two intrusive bird with one stone . I determine to get rid of the Count 's tiresome eloquence , and of Lady Glyde 's tiresome trouble , by comply with this odious foreigner 's request , and write the letter at once . there be not the least danger of the invitation be accept , for there be not the least chance that Laura would consent to leave Blackwater Park while Marian be lie there ill . how this charmingly convenient obstacle could have escape the officious penetration of the Count , it be impossible to conceive but it have escape he . my dread that he might yet discover it , if I allow he any more time to think , stimulate I to such an amazing degree , that I struggle into a sit position seize , really seize , the write material by my side , and produce the letter as rapidly as if I have be a common clerk in an office . " Dearest Laura , please come , whenever you like . break the journey by sleep in London at your aunt 's house . grieve to hear of dear Marian 's illness . ever affectionately your . " I hand these line , at arm 's length , to the Count I sink back in my chair I say , " excuse I I be entirely prostrate I can do no more . will you rest and lunch downstairs ? love to all , and sympathy , and so on . good - morning . " 

 he make another speech the man be absolutely inexhaustible . I close my eye I endeavour to hear as little as possible . in spite of my endeavour I be oblige to hear a great deal . my sister 's endless husband congratulate himself , and congratulate I , on the result of our interview he mention a great deal more about his sympathy and mine he deplore my miserable health he offer to write I a prescription he impress on I the necessity of not forget what he have say about the importance of light he accept my oblige invitation to rest and lunch he recommend I to expect Lady Glyde in two or three day ' time he beg my permission to look forward to our next meeting , instead of pain himself and pain I , by say farewell he add a great deal more , which , I rejoice to think , I do not attend to at the time , and do not remember now . I hear his sympathetic voice travel away from I by degree but , large as he be , I never hear he . he have the negative merit of be absolutely noiseless . I do not   know when he open the door , or when he shut it . I venture to make use of my eye again , after an interval of silence and he be go . 

 I rang for Louis , and retire to my bathroom . tepid water , strengthen with aromatic vinegar , for myself , and copious fumigation for my study , be the obvious precaution to take , and of course I adopt they . I rejoice to say they prove successful . I enjoy my customary siesta . I awake moist and cool . 

 my first inquiry be for the Count . have we really get rid of he ? yes he have go away by the afternoon train . have he lunch , and if so , upon what ? entirely upon fruit - tart and cream . what a man ! what a digestion ! 



 be I expect to say anything more ? I believe not . I believe I have reach the limit assign to I . the shocking circumstance which happen at a later period do not , I be thankful to say , happen in my presence . I do beg and entreat that nobody will be so very unfeeling as to lay any part of the blame of those circumstance on I . I do everything for the good . I be not answerable for a deplorable calamity , which it be quite impossible to foresee . I be shatter by it I have suffer under it , as nobody else have suffer . my servant , Louis   who be really attach to I in his unintelligent way , think I shall never get over it . he see I dictate at this moment , with my handkerchief to my eye . I wish to mention , in justice to myself , that it be not my fault , and that I be quite exhausted and heartbroken . need I say more ? 




 the story continued BY ELIZA MICHELSON 

  Housekeeper at Blackwater Park 



 I 

 I be ask to state plainly what I know of the progress of Miss Halcombe 's illness and of the circumstance under which Lady Glyde leave Blackwater Park for London . 

 the reason give for make this demand on I be , that my testimony be want in the interest of truth . as the widow of a clergyman of the Church of England   reduce by misfortune to the necessity of accept a situation , I have be teach to place the claim of truth above all other consideration . I therefore comply with a request which I might otherwise , through reluctance to connect myself with distress family affair , have hesitate to grant . 

 I make no memorandum at the time , and I can not therefore be sure to a day of the date , but I believe I be correct in state that Miss Halcombe 's serious illness begin during the last fortnight or ten day in June . the breakfast hour be late at Blackwater Park sometimes as late as ten , never early than half - past nine . on the morning to which I be now refer , Miss Halcombe   who be usually the first to come down   do not make her appearance at the table . after the family have wait a quarter of an hour , the upper housemaid be send to see after she , and come run out of the room dreadfully frighten . I meet the servant on the stair , and go at once to Miss Halcombe to see what be the matter . the poor lady be incapable of tell I . she be walk about her room with a pen in her hand , quite light - head , in a state of burn fever . 

 Lady Glyde   be no long in Sir Percival 's service , I may , without impropriety , mention my former mistress by her name , instead of call she my lady   be the first to come in from her own bedroom . she be so dreadfully alarmed and distressed that she be quite useless . the Count Fosco , and his lady , who come upstairs immediately afterwards , be both most serviceable and kind . her ladyship assist I to get Miss Halcombe to her bed . his lordship the Count remain in the sitting - room , and have send for my medicine - chest , make a mixture for Miss Halcombe , and a cool lotion to be apply to her head , so as to lose no time before the doctor come . we apply the lotion , but we could not get she to take the mixture . Sir Percival undertake to send for the doctor . he despatch a groom , on horseback , for the near medical man , Mr. Dawson , of Oak Lodge . 

 Mr. Dawson arrive in less than an hour 's time . he be a respectable elderly man , well know all round the country , and we be much alarmed when we find that he consider the case to be a very serious one . 

 his lordship the Count affably enter into conversation with Mr. Dawson , and give his opinion with a judicious freedom . Mr. Dawson , not over - courteously , inquire if his lordship 's advice be the advice of a doctor , and be inform that it be the advice of one who have study medicine unprofessionally , reply that he be not accustomed to consult with amateur physician . the Count , with truly christian meekness of temper , smile and leave the room . before he go out he tell I that he might be find , in case he be want in the course of the day , at the boat - house on the bank of the lake . why he should have go there , I can not say . but he do go , remain away the whole day till seven o'clock , which be dinner - time . perhaps he wish to set the example of keep the house as quiet as possible . it be entirely in his character to do so . he be a most considerate nobleman . 

 Miss Halcombe pass a very bad night , the fever come and go , and get bad towards the morning instead of well . no nurse fit to wait on she be at hand in the neighbourhood , her ladyship the Countess and myself undertake the duty , relieve each other . Lady Glyde , most unwisely , insist on sit up with we . she be much too nervous and too delicate in health to bear the anxiety of Miss Halcombe 's illness calmly . she only do herself harm , without be of the least real assistance . a more gentle and affectionate lady never live but she cry , and she be frightened , two weakness which make she entirely unfit to be present in a sick - room . 

 Sir Percival and the Count come in the morning to make their inquiry . 

 Sir Percival   from distress , I presume , at his lady 's affliction and at Miss Halcombe 's illness   appear much confused and unsettle in his mind . his lordship testify , on the contrary , a become composure and interest . he have his straw hat in one hand , and his book in the other , and he mention to Sir Percival in my hearing that he would go out again and study at the lake . " let we keep the house quiet , " he say . " let we not smoke indoor , my friend , now Miss Halcombe be ill . you go your way , and I will go mine . when I study I like to be alone . good - morning , Mrs. Michelson . " 

 Sir Percival be not civil enough perhaps I ought in justice to say , not compose enough to take leave of I with the same polite attention . the only person in the house , indeed , who treat I , at that time or at any other , on the footing of a lady in distressed circumstance , be the Count . he have the manner of a true nobleman he be considerate towards every one . even the young person   Fanny by name   who attend on Lady Glyde be not beneath his notice . when she be send away by Sir Percival , his lordship   show I his sweet little bird at the time   be most kindly anxious to know what have become of she , where she be to go the day she leave Blackwater Park , and so on . it be in such little delicate attention that the advantage of aristocratic birth always show themselves . I make no apology for introduce these particular they be bring forward in justice to his lordship , whose character , I have reason to know , be view rather harshly in certain quarter . a nobleman who can respect a lady in distressed circumstance , and can take a fatherly interest in the fortune of an humble servant girl , show principle and feeling of too high an order to be lightly call in question . I advance no opinion I offer fact only . my endeavour through life be to judge not that I be not judge . one of my beloved husband 's fine sermon be on that text . I read it constantly in my own copy of the edition print by subscription , in the first day of my widowhood and at every fresh perusal I derive an increase of spiritual benefit and edification . 

 there be no improvement in Miss Halcombe , and the second night be even bad than the first . Mr. Dawson be constant in his attendance . the practical duty of nursing be still divide between the Countess and myself , Lady Glyde persist in sit up with we , though we both entreat she to take some rest . " my place be by Marian 's bedside , " be her only answer . " whether I be ill , or well , nothing will induce I to lose sight of she . " 

 towards midday I go downstairs to attend to some of my regular duty . an hour afterwards , on my way back to the sick - room , I see the Count   who have go out again early , for the third time   enter the hall , to all appearance in the high good spirit . Sir Percival , at the same moment , put his head out of the library door , and address his noble friend , with extreme eagerness , in these word 

 " have you find she ? " 

 his lordship 's large face become dimple all over with placid smile , but he make no reply in word . at the same time Sir Percival turn his head , observe that I be approach the stair , and look at I in the most rudely angry manner possible . 

 " come in here and tell I about it , " he say to the Count . " whenever there be woman in a house they be always sure to be go up or down stair . " 

 " my dear Percival , " observe his lordship kindly , " Mrs. Michelson have duty . pray recognise her admirable performance of they as sincerely as I do ! how be the sufferer , Mrs. Michelson ? " 

 " no well , my lord , I regret to say . " 

 " sad most sad ! " remark the Count . " you look fatigue , Mrs. Michelson . it be certainly time you and my wife have some help in nursing . I think I may be the mean of offer you that help . circumstance have happen which will oblige Madame Fosco to travel to London either to - morrow or the day after . she will go away in the morning and return at night , and she will bring back with she , to relieve you , a nurse of excellent conduct and capacity , who be now disengage . the woman be know to my wife as a person to be trust . before she come here say nothing about she , if you please , to the doctor , because he will look with an evil eye on any nurse of my providing . when she appear in this house she will speak for herself , and Mr. Dawson will be oblige to acknowledge that there be no excuse for not employ she . Lady Glyde will say the same . pray present my good respect and sympathy to Lady Glyde . " 

 I express my grateful acknowledgment for his lordship 's kind consideration . Sir Percival cut they short by call to his noble friend   use , I regret to say , a profane expression   to come into the library , and not to keep he wait there any long . 

 I proceed upstairs . we be poor err creature , and however well establish a woman 's principle may be she can not always keep on her guard against the temptation to exercise an idle curiosity . I be ashamed to say that an idle curiosity , on this occasion , get the well of my principle , and make I unduly inquisitive about the question which Sir Percival have address to his noble friend at the library door . who be the Count expect to find in the course of his studious morning ramble at Blackwater Park ? a woman , it be to be presume , from the term of Sir Percival 's inquiry . I do not suspect the Count of any impropriety I know his moral character too well . the only question I ask myself be have he find she ? 

 to resume . the night pass as usual without produce any change for the well in Miss Halcombe . the next day she seem to improve a little . the day after that her ladyship the Countess , without mention the object of her journey to any one in my hearing , proceed by the morning train to London her noble husband , with his customary attention , accompany she to the station . 

 I be now leave in sole charge of Miss Halcombe , with every apparent chance , in consequence of her sister 's resolution not to leave the bedside , of have Lady Glyde herself to nurse next . 

 the only circumstance of any importance that happen in the course of the day be the occurrence of another unpleasant meeting between the doctor and the Count . 

 his lordship , on return from the station , step up into Miss Halcombe 's sitting - room to make his inquiry . I go out from the bedroom to speak to he , Mr. Dawson and Lady Glyde be both with the patient at the time . the Count ask I many question about the treatment and the symptom . I inform he that the treatment be of the kind describe as ' saline , " and that the symptom , between the attack of fever , be certainly those of increase weakness and exhaustion . just as I be mention these last particular , Mr. Dawson come out from the bedroom . 

 " good - morning , sir , " say his lordship , step forward in the most urbane manner , and stop the doctor , with a high - breed resolution impossible to resist , " I greatly fear you find no improvement in the symptom to - day ? " 

 " I find decide improvement , " answer Mr. Dawson . 

 " you still persist in your lower treatment of this case of fever ? " continue his lordship . 

 " I persist in the treatment which be justify by my own professional experience , " say Mr. Dawson . 

 " permit I to put one question to you on the vast subject of professional experience , " observe the Count . " I presume to offer no more advice I only presume to make an inquiry . you live at some distance , sir , from the gigantic centre of scientific activity London and Paris . have you ever hear of the waste effect of fever be reasonably and intelligibly repair by fortify the exhausted patient with brandy , wine , ammonia , and quinine ? have that new heresy of the high medical authority ever reach your ear yes or no ? " 

 " when a professional man put that question to I I shall be glad to answer he , " say the doctor , open the door to go out . " you be not a professional man , and I beg to decline answer you . " 

 buffet in this inexcusably uncivil way on one cheek , the Count , like a practical christian , immediately turn the other , and say , in the sweetest manner , " good - morning , Mr. Dawson . " 

 if my late beloved husband have be so fortunate as to know his lordship , how highly he and the Count would have esteem each other ! 

 her ladyship the Countess return by the last train that night , and bring with she the nurse from London . I be instruct that this person 's name be Mrs. Rubelle . her personal appearance , and her imperfect English when she speak , inform I that she be a foreigner . 

 I have always cultivate a feeling of humane indulgence for foreigner . they do not possess our blessing and advantage , and they be , for the most part , bring up in the blind error of Popery . it have also always be my precept and practice , as it be my dear husband 's precept and practice before I   see Sermon XXIX . in the Collection by the late Rev. Samuel Michelson , M.A. , to do as I would be do by . on both these account I will not say that Mrs. Rubelle strike I as be a small , wiry , sly person , of fifty or thereabout , with a dark brown or Creole complexion and watchful light grey eye . nor will I mention , for the reason just allege , that I think her dress , though it be of the plain black silk , inappropriately costly in texture and unnecessarily refine in trim and finish , for a person in her position in life . I should not like these thing to be say of I , and therefore it be my duty not to say they of Mrs. Rubelle . I will merely mention that her manner be , not perhaps unpleasantly reserve , but only remarkably quiet and retire that she look about she a great deal , and say very little , which might have arise quite as much from her own modesty as from distrust of her position at Blackwater Park ; and that she decline to partake of supper   which be curious perhaps , but surely not suspicious ? , although I myself politely invite she to that meal in my own room . 

 at the Count 's particular suggestion   so like his lordship 's forgiving kindness ! , it be arrange that Mrs. Rubelle should not enter on her duty until she have be see and approve by the doctor the next morning . I sit up that night . Lady Glyde appear to be very unwilling that the new nurse should be employ to attend on Miss Halcombe . such want of liberality towards a foreigner on the part of a lady of her education and refinement surprise I . I venture to say , " my lady , we must all remember not to be hasty in our judgment on our inferior especially when they come from foreign part . " Lady Glyde do not appear to attend to I . she only sigh , and kiss Miss Halcombe 's hand as it lie on the counterpane . scarcely a judicious proceeding in a sick - room , with a patient whom it be highly desirable not to excite . but poor Lady Glyde know nothing of nurse nothing whatever , I be sorry to say . 

 the next morning Mrs. Rubelle be send to the sitting - room , to be approve by the doctor on his way through to the bedroom . 

 I leave Lady Glyde with Miss Halcombe , who be slumber at the time , and join Mrs. Rubelle , with the object of kindly prevent she from feel strange and nervous in consequence of the uncertainty of her situation . she do not appear to see it in that light . she seem to be quite satisfied , beforehand , that Mr. Dawson would approve of she , and she sit calmly look out of window , with every appearance of enjoy the country air . some people might have think such conduct suggestive of brazen assurance . I beg to say that I more liberally set it down to extraordinary strength of mind . 

 instead of the doctor come up to we , I be send for to see the doctor . I think this change of affair rather odd , but Mrs. Rubelle do not appear to be affect by it in any way . I leave she still calmly look out of the window , and still silently enjoy the country air . 

 Mr. Dawson be wait for I by himself in the breakfast - room . 

 " about this new nurse , Mrs. Michelson , " say the doctor . 

 " yes , sir ? " 

 " I find that she have be bring here from London by the wife of that fat old foreigner , who be always try to interfere with I . Mrs. Michelson , the fat old foreigner be a quack . " 

 this be very rude . I be naturally shock at it . 

 " be you aware , sir , " I say , " that you be talk of a nobleman ? " 

 " Pooh ! he be not   the first quack with a handle to his name . they be all count hang them ! " 

 " he would not be a friend of Sir Percival Glyde 's , sir , if he be not a member of the high aristocracy except the english aristocracy , of course . " 

 " very well , Mrs. Michelson , call he what you like , and let we get back to the nurse . I have be object to she already . " 

 " without have see she , sir ? " 

 " yes , without have see she . she may be the good nurse in existence , but she be not a nurse of my providing . I have put that objection to Sir Percival , as the master of the house . he do not   support I . he say a nurse of my providing would have be a stranger from London also , and he think the woman ought to have a trial , after his wife 's aunt have take the trouble to fetch she from London . there be some justice in that , and I can not   decently say no . but I have make it a condition that she be to go at once , if I find reason to complain of she . this proposal be one which I have some right to make , as medical attendant , Sir Percival have consent to it . now , Mrs. Michelson , I know I can depend on you , and I want you to keep a sharp eye on the nurse for the first day or two , and to see that she give Miss Halcombe no medicine but mine . this foreign nobleman of yours be die to try his quack remedy   mesmerism include   on my patient , and a nurse who be bring here by his wife may be a little too willing to help he . you understand ? very well , then , we may go upstairs . be the nurse there ? I will say a word to she before she go into the sick - room . " 

 we find Mrs. Rubelle still enjoy herself at the window . when I introduce she to Mr. Dawson , neither the doctor 's doubtful look nor the doctor 's search question appear to confuse she in the least . she answer he quietly in her broken English , and though he try hard to puzzle she , she never betray the least ignorance , so far , about any part of her duty . this be doubtless the result of strength of mind , as I say before , and not of brazen assurance , by any mean . 

 we all go into the bedroom . 

 Mrs. Rubelle look very attentively at the patient , curtsey to Lady Glyde , set one or two little thing right in the room , and sit down quietly in a corner to wait until she be want . her ladyship seem startled and annoy by the appearance of the strange nurse . no one say anything , for fear of rouse Miss Halcombe , who be still slumber , except the doctor , who whisper a question about the night . I softly answer , " much as usual , " and then Mr. Dawson go out . Lady Glyde follow he , I suppose to speak about Mrs. Rubelle . for my own part , I have make up my mind already that this quiet foreign person would keep her situation . she have all her wit about she , and she certainly understand her business . so far , I could hardly have do much well by the bedside myself . 

 remember Mr. Dawson 's caution to I , I subject Mrs. Rubelle to a severe scrutiny at certain interval for the next three or four day . I over and over again enter the room softly and suddenly , but I never find she out in any suspicious action . Lady Glyde , who watch she as attentively as I do , discover nothing either . I never detect a sign of the medicine bottle be tamper with , I never see Mrs. Rubelle say a word to the Count , or the Count to she . she manage Miss Halcombe with unquestionable care and discretion . the poor lady waver backwards and forwards between a sort of sleepy exhaustion , which be half faintness and half slumber , and attack of fever which bring with they more or less of wander in her mind . Mrs. Rubelle never disturb she in the first case , and never startle she in the second , by appear too suddenly at the bedside in the character of a stranger . honour to whom honour be due   whether foreign or English   and I give her privilege impartially to Mrs. Rubelle . she be remarkably uncommunicative about herself , and she be too quietly independent of all advice from experienced person who understand the duty of a sick - room but with these drawback , she be a good nurse , and she never give either Lady Glyde or Mr. Dawson the shadow of a reason for complain of she . 

 the next circumstance of importance that occur in the house be the temporary absence of the Count , occasion by business which take he to London . he go away   I think   on the morning of the fourth day after the arrival of Mrs. Rubelle , and at part he speak to Lady Glyde very seriously , in my presence , on the subject of Miss Halcombe . 

 " Trust Mr. Dawson , " he say , " for a few day more , if you please . but if there be not some change for the well in that time , send for advice from London , which this mule of a doctor must accept in spite of himself . offend Mr. Dawson , and save Miss Halcombe . I say this seriously , on my word of honour and from the bottom of my heart . " 

 his lordship speak with extreme feeling and kindness . but poor Lady Glyde 's nerve be so completely break down that she seem quite frightened at he . she tremble from head to foot , and allow he to take his leave without utter a word on her side . she turn to I when he have go , and say , " oh , Mrs. Michelson , I be heartbroken about my sister , and I have no friend to advise I ! do you think Mr. Dawson be wrong ? he tell I himself this morning that there be no fear , and no need to send for another doctor . " 

 " with all respect to Mr. Dawson , " I answer , " in your ladyship 's place I should remember the Count 's advice . " 

 Lady Glyde turn away from I suddenly , with an appearance of despair , for which I be quite unable to account . 

 " his advice ! " she say to herself . " God help we his advice ! " 


 the Count be away from Blackwater Park , as nearly as I remember , a week . 

 Sir Percival seem to feel the loss of his lordship in various way , and appear also , I think , much depressed and alter by the sickness and sorrow in the house . occasionally he be so very restless that I could not help notice it , come and go , and wander here and there and everywhere in the ground . his inquiry about Miss Halcombe , and about his lady   whose fail health seem to cause he sincere anxiety , be most attentive . I think his heart be much soften . if some kind clerical friend some such friend as he might have find in my late excellent husband have be near he at this time , cheer moral progress might have be make with Sir Percival . I seldom find myself mistaken on a point of this sort , have have experience to guide I in my happy married day . 

 her ladyship the Countess , who be now the only company for Sir Percival downstair , rather neglect he , as I consider or , perhaps , it might have be that he neglect she . a stranger might almost have suppose that they be bent , now they be leave together alone , on actually avoid one another . this , of course , could not be . but it do so happen , nevertheless , that the Countess make her dinner at luncheon - time , and that she always come upstairs towards evening , although Mrs. Rubelle have take the nursing duty entirely off her hand . Sir Percival din by himself , and William   the man out of livery   make the remark , in my hearing , that his master have put himself on half ration of food and on a double allowance of drink . I attach no importance to such an insolent observation as this on the part of a servant . I reprobate it at the time , and I wish to be understand as reprobate it once more on this occasion . 

 in the course of the next few day Miss Halcombe do certainly seem to all of we to be mend a little . our faith in Mr. Dawson revive . he appear to be very confident about the case , and he assure Lady Glyde , when she speak to he on the subject , that he would himself propose to send for a physician the moment he feel so much as the shadow of a doubt cross his own mind . 

 the only person among we who do not appear to be relieve by these word be the Countess . she say to I privately , that she could not feel easy about Miss Halcombe on Mr. Dawson 's authority , and that she should wait anxiously for her husband 's opinion on his return . that return , his letter inform she , would take place in three day ' time . the Count and Countess correspond regularly every morning during his lordship 's absence . they be in that respect , as in all other , a pattern to married people . 

 on the evening of the third day I notice a change in Miss Halcombe , which cause I serious apprehension . Mrs. Rubelle notice it too . we say nothing on the subject to Lady Glyde , who be then lie asleep , completely overpower by exhaustion , on the sofa in the sitting - room . 

 Mr. Dawson do not pay his evening visit till later than usual . as soon as he set eye on his patient I see his face alter . he try to hide it , but he look both confused and alarmed . a messenger be send to his residence for his medicine - chest , disinfect preparation be use in the room , and a bed be make up for he in the house by his own direction . " have the fever turn to infection ? " I whisper to he . " I be afraid it have , " he answer ; " we shall know well to - morrow morning . " 

 by Mr. Dawson 's own direction Lady Glyde be keep in ignorance of this change for the bad . he himself absolutely forbid she , on account of her health , to join we in the bedroom that night . she try to resist there be a sad scene but he have his medical authority to support he , and he carry his point . 

 the next morning one of the man - servant be send to London at eleven o'clock , with a letter to a physician in town , and with order to bring the new doctor back with he by the early possible train . half an hour after the messenger have go the Count return to Blackwater Park . 

 the Countess , on her own responsibility , immediately bring he in to see the patient . there be no impropriety that I could discover in she take this course . his lordship be a married man , he be old enough to be Miss Halcombe 's father , and he see she in the presence of a female relative , Lady Glyde 's aunt . Mr. Dawson nevertheless protest against his presence in the room , but I could plainly remark the doctor be too much alarmed to make any serious resistance on this occasion . 

 the poor suffering lady be past know any one about she . she seem to take her friend for enemy . when the Count approach she bedside her eye , which have be wander incessantly round and round the room before , settle on his face with a dreadful stare of terror , which I shall remember to my die day . the Count sit down by she , feel her pulse and her temple , look at she very attentively , and then turn round upon the doctor with such an expression of indignation and contempt in his face , that the word fail on Mr. Dawson 's lip , and he stand for a moment , pale with anger and alarm pale and perfectly speechless . 

 his lordship look next at I . 

 " when do the change happen ? " he ask . 

 I tell he the time . 

 " Has Lady Glyde be in the room since ? " 

 I reply that she have not . the doctor have absolutely forbid she to come into the room on the evening before , and have repeat the order again in the morning . 

 " have you and Mrs. Rubelle be make aware of the full extent of the mischief ? " be his next question . 

 we be aware , I answer , that the malady be consider infectious . he stop I before I could add anything more . 

 " it be typhus fever , " he say . 

 in the minute that pass , while these question and answer be go on , Mr. Dawson recover himself , and address the Count with his customary firmness . 

 " it be not typhus fever , " he remark sharply . " I protest against this intrusion , sir . no one have a right to put question here but I . I have do my duty to the good of my ability " 

 the Count interrupt he not by word , but only by point to the bed . Mr. Dawson seem to feel that silent contradiction to his assertion of his own ability , and to grow only the more angry under it . 

 " I say I have do my duty , " he reiterate . " a physician have be send for from London . I will consult on the nature of the fever with he , and with no one else . I insist on your leave the room . " 

 " I enter this room , sir , in the sacred interest of humanity , " say the Count . " and in the same interest , if the coming of the physician be delay , I will enter it again . I warn you once more that the fever have turn to typhus , and that your treatment be responsible for this lamentable change . if that unhappy lady die , I will give my testimony in a court of justice that your ignorance and obstinacy have be the cause of her death . " 

 before Mr. Dawson could answer , before the Count could leave we , the door be open from the sitting - room , and we see Lady Glyde on the threshold . 

 " I must and will come in , " she say , with extraordinary firmness . 

 instead of stop she , the Count move into the sitting - room , and make way for she to go in . on all other occasion he be the last man in the world to forget anything , but in the surprise of the moment he apparently forget the danger of infection from typhus , and the urgent necessity of force Lady Glyde to take proper care of herself . 

 to my astonishment Mr. Dawson show more presence of mind . he stop her ladyship at the first step she take towards the bedside . " I be sincerely sorry , I be sincerely grieve , " he say . " the fever may , I fear , be infectious . until I be certain that it be not , I entreat you to keep out of the room . " 

 she struggle for a moment , then suddenly drop her arm and sink forward . she have faint . the Countess and I take she from the doctor and carry she into her own room . the Count precede we , and wait in the passage till I come out and tell he that we have recover she from the swoon .

Sir Percival and the Count be together downstairs , and send up from time to time to make their inquiry . at last , between five and six o'clock , to our great relief , the physician come . 

 he be a young man than Mr. Dawson , very serious and very decide . what he think of the previous treatment I can not say , but it strike I as curious that he put many more question to myself and to Mrs. Rubelle than he put to the doctor , and that he do not appear to listen with much interest to what Mr. Dawson say , while he be examine Mr. Dawson 's patient . I begin to suspect , from what I observe in this way , that the Count have be right about the illness all the way through , and I be naturally confirm in that idea when Mr. Dawson , after some little delay , ask the one important question which the London doctor have be send for to set at rest . 

 " what be your opinion of the fever ? " he inquire . 

 " Typhus , " reply the physician " Typhus fever beyond all doubt . " 

 that quiet foreign person , Mrs. Rubelle , cross her thin brown hand in front of she , and look at I with a very significant smile . the Count himself could hardly have appear more gratified if he have be present in the room and have hear the confirmation of his own opinion . 

 after give we some useful direction about the management of the patient , and mention that he would come again in five day ' time , the physician withdraw to consult in private with Mr. Dawson . he would offer no opinion on Miss Halcombe 's chance of recovery he say it be impossible at that stage of the illness to pronounce one way or the other . 



 the five day pass anxiously . 

 Countess Fosco and myself take it by turn to relieve Mrs. Rubelle , Miss Halcombe 's condition grow bad and bad , and require our utmost care and attention . it be a terribly try time . Lady Glyde   support , as Mr. Dawson say , by the constant strain of her suspense on her sister 's account   rally in the most extraordinary manner , and show a firmness and determination for which I should myself never have give her credit . she insist on come into the sick - room two or three time every day , to look at Miss Halcombe with her own eye , promise not to go too close to the bed , if the doctor would consent to her wish so far . Mr. Dawson very unwillingly make the concession require of he I think he see that it be hopeless to dispute with she . she come in every day , and she self - denyingly keep her promise . I feel it personally so distress   as remind I of my own affliction during my husband 's last illness   to see how she suffer under these circumstance , that I must beg not to dwell on this part of the subject any long . it be more agreeable to I to mention that no fresh dispute take place between Mr. Dawson and the Count . his lordship make all his inquiry by deputy , and remain continually in company with Sir Percival downstairs . 

 on the fifth day the physician come again and give we a little hope . he say the tenth day from the first appearance of the typhus would probably decide the result of the illness , and he arrange for his third visit to take place on that date . the interval pass as before except that the Count go to London again one morning and return at night . 

 on the tenth day it please a merciful Providence to relieve our household from all further anxiety and alarm . the physician positively assure we that Miss Halcombe be out of danger . " she want no doctor now all she require be careful watching and nursing for some time to come , and that I see she have . " those be his own word . that evening I read my husband 's touching sermon on Recovery from Sickness , with more happiness and advantage   in a spiritual point of view   than I ever remember to have derive from it before . 

 the effect of the good news on poor Lady Glyde be , I grieve to say , quite overpower . she be too weak to bear the violent reaction , and in another day or two she sink into a state of debility and depression which oblige she to keep her room . rest and quiet , and change of air afterwards , be the good remedy which Mr. Dawson could suggest for her benefit . it be fortunate that matter be no bad , for , on the very day after she take to her room , the Count and the doctor have another disagreement and this time the dispute between they be of so serious a nature that Mr. Dawson leave the house . 

 I be not present at the time , but I understand that the subject of dispute be the amount of nourishment which it be necessary to give to assist Miss Halcombe 's convalescence after the exhaustion of the fever . Mr. Dawson , now that his patient be safe , be less inclined than ever to submit to unprofessional interference , and the Count   I can not imagine why   lose all the self - control which he have so judiciously preserve on former occasion , and taunt the doctor , over and over again , with his mistake about the fever when it change to typhus . the unfortunate affair end in Mr. Dawson 's appeal to Sir Percival , and threaten   now that he could leave without absolute danger to Miss Halcombe   to withdraw from his attendance at Blackwater Park if the Count 's interference be not peremptorily suppress from that moment . Sir Percival 's reply   though not designedly uncivil   have only result in make matter bad , and Mr. Dawson have thereupon withdraw from the house in a state of extreme indignation at Count Fosco 's usage of he , and have send in his bill the next morning . 

 we be now , therefore , leave without the attendance of a medical man . although there be no actual necessity for another doctor nursing and watch being , as the physician have observe , all that Miss Halcombe require I should still , if my authority have be consult , have obtain professional assistance from some other quarter , for form 's sake . 

 the matter do not seem to strike Sir Percival in that light . he say it would be time enough to send for another doctor if Miss Halcombe show any sign of a relapse . in the meanwhile we have the Count to consult in any minor difficulty , and we need not unnecessarily disturb our patient in her present weak and nervous condition by the presence of a stranger at her bedside . there be much that be reasonable , no doubt , in these consideration , but they leave I a little anxious nevertheless . nor be I quite satisfied in my own mind of the propriety of our conceal the doctor 's absence as we do from Lady Glyde . it be a merciful deception , I admit for she be in no state to bear any fresh anxiety . but still it be a deception , and , as such , to a person of my principle , at good a doubtful proceeding . 



 a second perplexing circumstance which happen on the same day , and which take I completely by surprise , add greatly to the sense of uneasiness that be now weigh on my mind . 

 I be send for to see Sir Percival in the library . the Count , who be with he when I go in , immediately rise and leave we alone together . Sir Percival civilly ask I to take a seat , and then , to my great astonishment , address I in these term 

 " I want to speak to you , Mrs. Michelson , about a matter which I decide on some time ago , and which I should have mention before , but for the sickness and trouble in the house . in plain word , I have reason for wish to break up my establishment immediately at this place leave you in charge , of course , as usual . as soon as Lady Glyde and Miss Halcombe can travel they must both have change of air . my friend , Count Fosco and the Countess , will leave we before that time to live in the neighbourhood of London , and I have reason for not open the house to any more company , with a view to economise as carefully as I can . I do not   blame you , but my expense here be a great deal too heavy . in short , I shall sell the horse , and get rid of all the servant at once . I never do thing by half , as you know , and I mean to have the house clear of a pack of useless people by this time to - morrow . " 

 I listen to he , perfectly aghast with astonishment . 

 " do you mean , Sir Percival , that I be to dismiss the indoor servant under my charge without the usual month 's warning ? " I ask . 

 " certainly I do . we may all be out of the house before another month , and I be not go to leave the servant here in idleness , with no master to wait on . " 

 " who be to do the cooking , Sir Percival , while you be still stay here ? " 

 " Margaret Porcher can roast and boil keep she . what do I want with a cook if I do not   mean to give any dinner - party ? " 

 " the servant you have mention be the most unintelligent servant in the house , Sir Percival . " 

 " keep she , I tell you , and have a woman in from the village to do the cleaning and go away again . my weekly expense must and shall be lower immediately . I do not   send for you to make objection , Mrs. Michelson I send for you to carry out my plan of economy . dismiss the whole lazy pack of indoor servant to - morrow , except Porcher . she be as strong as a horse and we will make her work like a horse . " 

 " you will excuse I for remind you , Sir Percival , that if the servant go to - morrow they must have a month 's wage in lieu of a month 's warning . " 

 " let they ! a month 's wage save a month 's waste and gluttony in the servant ' hall . " 

 this last remark convey an aspersion of the most offensive kind on my management . I have too much self - respect to defend myself under so gross an imputation . christian consideration for the helpless position of Miss Halcombe and Lady Glyde , and for the serious inconvenience which my sudden absence might inflict on they , alone prevent I from resign my situation on the spot . I rise immediately . it would have lower I in my own estimation to have permit the interview to continue a moment long . 

 " after that last remark , Sir Percival , I have nothing more to say . your direction shall be attend to . " pronounce those word , I bow my head with the most distant respect , and go out of the room . 

 the next day the servant leave in a body . Sir Percival himself dismiss the groom and stableman , send they , with all the horse but one , to London . of the whole domestic establishment , indoor and out , there now remain only myself , Margaret Porcher , and the gardener this last live in his own cottage , and be want to take care of the one horse that remain in the stable . 

 with the house leave in this strange and lonely condition with the mistress of it ill in her room with Miss Halcombe still as helpless as a child and with the doctor 's attendance withdraw from we in enmity it be surely not unnatural that my spirit should sink , and my customary composure be very hard to maintain . my mind be ill at ease . I wish the poor lady both well again , and I wish myself away from Blackwater Park . 



 II 

 the next event that occur be of so singular a nature that it might have cause I a feeling of superstitious surprise , if my mind have not be fortify by principle against any pagan weakness of that sort . the uneasy sense of something wrong in the family which have make I wish myself away from Blackwater Park , be actually follow , strange to say , by my departure from the house . it be true that my absence be for a temporary period only , but the coincidence be , in my opinion , not the less remarkable on that account . 

 my departure take place under the following circumstance 

 a day or two after the servant all leave I be again send for to see Sir Percival . the undeserved slur which he have cast on my management of the household do not , I be happy to say , prevent I from return good for evil to the good of my ability , by comply with his request as readily and respectfully as ever . it cost I a struggle with that fallen nature , which we all share in common , before I could suppress my feeling . be accustomed to self - discipline , I accomplish the sacrifice . 

 I find Sir Percival and Count Fosco sit together again . on this occasion his lordship remain present at the interview , and assist in the development of Sir Percival 's view . 

 the subject to which they now request my attention relate to the healthy change of air by which we all hop that Miss Halcombe and Lady Glyde might soon be enable to profit . Sir Percival mention that both the lady would probably pass the autumn   by invitation of Frederick Fairlie , Esquire   at Limmeridge House , Cumberland . but before they go there , it be his opinion , confirm by Count Fosco   who here take up the conversation and continue it to the end , that they would benefit by a short residence first in the genial climate of Torquay . the great object , therefore , be to engage lodging at that place , afford all the comfort and advantage of which they stand in need , and the great difficulty be to find an experienced person capable of choose the sort of residence which they want . in this emergency the Count beg to inquire , on Sir Percival 's behalf , whether I would object to give the lady the benefit of my assistance , by proceed myself to Torquay in their interest . 

 it be impossible for a person in my situation to meet any proposal , make in these term , with a positive objection . 

 I could only venture to represent the serious inconvenience of my leave Blackwater Park in the extraordinary absence of all the indoor servant , with the one exception of Margaret Porcher . but Sir Percival and his lordship declare that they be both willing to put up with inconvenience for the sake of the invalid . I next respectfully suggest write to an agent at Torquay , but I be meet here by be remind of the imprudence of take lodging without first see they . I be also inform that the Countess   who would otherwise have go to devonshire herself   could not , in Lady Glyde 's present condition , leave her niece , and that Sir Percival and the Count have business to transact together which would oblige they to remain at Blackwater Park . in short , it be clearly show I that if I do not undertake the errand , no one else could be trust with it . under these circumstance , I could only inform Sir Percival that my service be at the disposal of Miss Halcombe and Lady Glyde . 

 it be thereupon arrange that I should leave the next morning , that I should occupy one or two day in examine all the most convenient house in Torquay , and that I should return with my report as soon as I conveniently could . a memorandum be write for I by his lordship , state the requisite which the place I be send to take must be find to possess , and a note of the pecuniary limit assign to I be add by Sir Percival . 

 my own idea on read over these instruction be , that no such residence as I see describe could be find at any watering - place in England , and that , even if it could by chance be discover , it would certainly not be part with for any period on such term as I be permit to offer . I hint at these difficulty to both the gentleman , but Sir Percival   who undertake to answer I   do not appear to feel they . it be not for I to dispute the question . I say no more , but I feel a very strong conviction that the business on which I be send away be so beset by difficulty that my errand be almost hopeless at start . 

 before I leave I take care to satisfy myself that Miss Halcombe be go on favourably . 

 there be a painful expression of anxiety in her face which make I fear that her mind , on first recover itself , be not at ease . but she be certainly strengthen more rapidly than I could have venture to anticipate , and she be able to send kind message to Lady Glyde , say that she be fast get well , and entreat her ladyship not to exert herself again too soon . I leave she in charge of Mrs. Rubelle , who be still as quietly independent of every one else in the house as ever . when I knock at Lady Glyde 's door before go away , I be tell that she be still sadly weak and depressed , my informant be the Countess , who be then keep her company in her room . Sir Percival and the Count be walk on the road to the lodge as I be drive by in the chaise . I bow to they and quit the house , with not a live soul leave in the servant ' office but Margaret Porcher . 

 every one must feel what I have feel myself since that time , that these circumstance be more than unusual they be ! almost suspicious . let I , however , say again that it be impossible for I , in my dependent position , to act otherwise than I do . 

 the result of my errand at Torquay be exactly what I have foresee . no such lodging as I be instruct to take could be find in the whole place , and the term I be permit to give be much too low for the purpose , even if I have be able to discover what I want . I accordingly return to Blackwater Park , and inform Sir Percival , who meet I at the door , that my journey have be take in vain . he seem too much occupy with some other subject to care about the failure of my errand , and his first word inform I that even in the short time of my absence another remarkable change have take place in the house . 

 the Count and Countess Fosco have leave Blackwater Park for their new residence in St. John 's Wood . 

 I be not make aware of the motive for this sudden departure I be only tell that the Count have be very particular in leave his kind compliment to I . when I venture on ask Sir Percival whether Lady Glyde have any one to attend to her comfort in the absence of the Countess , he reply that she have Margaret Porcher to wait on she , and he add that a woman from the village have be send for to do the work downstairs . 

 the answer really shock I there be such a glaring impropriety in permit an under - housemaid to fill the place of confidential attendant on Lady Glyde . I go upstairs at once , and meet Margaret on the bedroom landing . her service have not be require   naturally enough , her mistress have sufficiently recover that morning to be able to leave her bed . I ask next after Miss Halcombe , but I be answer in a slouching , sulky way , which leave I no wise than I be before . 

 I do not choose to repeat the question , and perhaps provoke an impertinent reply . it be in every respect more become to a person in my position to present myself immediately in Lady Glyde 's room .

 the arrangement propose be so unquestionably the right and the proper one , that I could make no possible objection to it . much as I sympathise with Lady Glyde in other respect , I could not sympathise with she in her unjust prejudice against Count Fosco .  towards five o'clock on the afternoon of that same day , have a little time to myself in the midst of the household duty which now press upon I , I sit down alone in my own room , to try and compose my mind with the volume of my husband 's sermon . for the first time in my life I find my attention wander over those pious and cheer word . conclude that Lady Glyde 's departure must have disturb I far more seriously than I have myself suppose , I put the book aside , and go out to take a turn in the garden .  " at last ! " she say , with a shrug of her lean foreign shoulder . she lead the way into the inhabited side of the house , ascend the stair , and open with her key the door at the end of the passage , which communicate with the old Elizabethan room a door never previously use , in my time , at Blackwater Park . the room themselves I know well , have enter they myself on various occasion from the other side of the house . Mrs. Rubelle stop at the third door along the old gallery , hand I the key of it , with the key of the door of communication , and tell I I should find Miss Halcombe in that room . before I go in I think it desirable to make she understand that her attendance have cease . accordingly , I tell she in plain word that the charge of the sick lady henceforth devolve entirely on myself . 

 " I be glad to hear it , ma'am , " say Mrs. Rubelle . " I want to go very much . " 

 " do you leave to - day ? " I ask , to make sure of she . 

 " now that you have take charge , ma'am , I leave in half an hour 's time . Sir Percival have kindly place at my disposition the gardener , and the chaise , whenever I want they . I shall want they in half an hour 's time to go to the station . I be pack up in anticipation already . I wish you good - day , ma'am . " 

 she drop a brisk curtsey , and walk back along the gallery , hum a little tune , and keep time to it cheerfully with the nosegay in her hand . I be sincerely thankful to say that be the last I see of Mrs. Rubelle . 

 when I go into the room Miss Halcombe be asleep . I look at her anxiously , as she lie in the dismal , high , old - fashioned bed . she be certainly not in any respect alter for the bad since I have see she last . she have not be neglect , I be bind to admit , in any way that I could perceive . the room be dreary , and dusty , and dark , but the window   look on a solitary court - yard at the back of the house   be open to let in the fresh air , and all that could be do to make the place comfortable have be do . the whole cruelty of Sir Percival 's deception have fall on poor Lady Glyde . the only ill - usage which either he or Mrs. Rubelle have inflict on Miss Halcombe consist , so far as I could see , in the first offence of hide she away . 

 I steal back , leave the sick lady still peacefully asleep , to give the gardener instruction about bring the doctor . I beg the man , after he have take Mrs. Rubelle to the station , to drive round by Mr. Dawson 's , and leave a message in my name , ask he to call and see I . I know he would come on my account , and I know he would remain when he find Count Fosco have leave the house . 

 in due course of time the gardener return , and say that he have drive round by Mr. Dawson 's residence , after leave Mrs. Rubelle at the station . the doctor send I word that he be poorly in health himself , but that he would call , if possible , the next morning . 

 have deliver his message the gardener be about to withdraw , but I stop he to request that he would come back before dark , and sit up that night , in one of the empty bedroom , so as to be within call in case I want he . he understand readily enough my unwillingness to be leave alone all night in the most desolate part of that desolate house , and we arrange that he should come in between eight and nine . 

 he come punctually , and I find cause to be thankful that I have adopt the precaution of call he in . before midnight Sir Percival 's strange temper break out in the most violent and most alarming manner , and if the gardener have not be on the spot to pacify he on the instant , I be afraid to think what might have happen . 

 almost all the afternoon and evening he have be walk about the house and ground in an unsettled , excitable manner , have , in all probability , as I think , take an excessive quantity of wine at his solitary dinner . however that may be , I hear his voice call loudly and angrily in the new wing of the house , as I be take a turn backwards and forwards along the gallery the last thing at night . the gardener immediately run down to he , and I close the door of communication , to keep the alarm , if possible , from reach Miss Halcombe 's ear . it be full half an hour before the gardener come back . he declare that his master be quite out of his sens not through the excitement of drink , as I have suppose , but through a kind of panic or frenzy of mind , for which it be impossible to account . he have find Sir Percival walk backwards and forwards by himself in the hall , swearing , with every appearance of the most violent passion , that he would not stop another minute alone in such a dungeon as his own house , and that he would take the first stage of his journey immediately in the middle of the night . the gardener , on approach he , have be hunt out , with oath and threat , to get the horse and chaise ready instantly . in a quarter of an hour Sir Percival have join he in the yard , have jump into the chaise , and , lash the horse into a gallop , have drive himself away , with his face as pale as ash in the moonlight . the gardener have hear he shout and curse at the lodge - keeper to get up and open the gate have hear the wheel roll furiously on again in the still night , when the gate be unlocked and know no more . 

 the next day , or a day or two after , I forget which , the chaise be bring back from Knowlesbury , our near town , by the ostler at the old inn . Sir Percival have stop there , and have afterwards leave by the train for what destination the man could not tell . I never receive any further information , either from himself or from any one else , of Sir Percival 's proceeding , and I be not even aware , at this moment , whether he be in England or out of it . he and I have not meet since he drive away like an escape criminal from his own house , and it be my fervent hope and prayer that we may never meet again . 



 my own part of this sad family story be now draw to an end . 

 I have be inform that the particular of Miss Halcombe 's waking , and of what pass between we when she find I sit by her bedside , be not material to the purpose which be to be answer by the present narrative . it will be sufficient for I to say in this place , that she be not herself conscious of the mean adopt to remove she from the inhabit to the uninhabited part of the house . she be in a deep sleep at the time , whether naturally or artificially produce she could not say . in my absence at Torquay , and in the absence of all the resident servant except Margaret Porcher   who be perpetually eat , drinking , or sleeping , when she be not at work , the secret transfer of Miss Halcombe from one part of the house to the other be no doubt easily perform . Mrs. Rubelle   as I discover for myself , in look about the room   have provision , and all other necessary , together with the mean of heating water , broth , and so on , without kindle a fire , place at her disposal during the few day of her imprisonment with the sick lady . she have decline to answer the question which Miss Halcombe naturally put , but have not , in other respect , treat she with unkindness or neglect . the disgrace of lend herself to a vile deception be the only disgrace with which I can conscientiously charge Mrs. Rubelle . 

 I need write no particular   and I be relieve to know it   of the effect produce on Miss Halcombe by the news of Lady Glyde 's departure , or by the far more melancholy tiding which reach we only too soon afterwards at Blackwater Park . in both case I prepare her mind beforehand as gently and as carefully as possible , have the doctor 's advice to guide I , in the last case only , through Mr. Dawson 's be too unwell to come to the house for some day after I have send for he . it be a sad time , a time which it afflict I to think of or to write of now . the precious blessing of religious consolation which I endeavour to convey be long in reach Miss Halcombe 's heart , but I hope and believe they come home to she at last . I never leave she till her strength be restore . the train which take I away from that miserable house be the train which take she away also . we part very mournfully in London . I remain with a relative at Islington , and she go on to Mr. Fairlie 's house in Cumberland . 

 I have only a few line more to write before I close this painful statement . they be dictate by a sense of duty . 

 in the first place , I wish to record my own personal conviction that no blame whatever , in connection with the event which I have now relate , attache to Count Fosco . I be inform that a dreadful suspicion have be raise , and that some very serious construction be place upon his lordship 's conduct . my persuasion of the Count 's innocence remain , however , quite unshaken . if he assist Sir Percival in send I to Torquay , he assist under a delusion , for which , as a foreigner and a stranger , he be not to blame . if he be concern in bring Mrs. Rubelle to Blackwater Park , it be his misfortune and not his fault , when that foreign person be base enough to assist a deception plan and carry out by the master of the house . I protest , in the interest of morality , against blame be gratuitously and wantonly attach to the proceeding of the Count . 

 in the second place , I desire to express my regret at my own inability to remember the precise day on which Lady Glyde leave Blackwater Park for London . I be tell that it be of the last importance to ascertain the exact date of that lamentable journey , and I have anxiously tax my memory to recall it . the effort have be in vain . I can only remember now that it be towards the latter part of July . we all know the difficulty , after a lapse of time , of fix precisely on a past date unless it have be previously write down . that difficulty be greatly increase in my case by the alarming and confusing event which take place about the period of Lady Glyde 's departure . I heartily wish I have make a memorandum at the time . I heartily wish my memory of the date be as vivid as my memory of that poor lady 's face , when it look at I sorrowfully for the last time from the carriage window . 




 the STORY continue in several NARRATIVES 

 1 . the narrative of hester PINHORN , 
 COOK in the service of COUNT FOSCO 

  take down from her own statement 


 I be sorry to say that I have never learn to read or write . I have be a hard - work woman all my life , and have keep a good character . I know that it be a sin and wickedness to say the thing which be not , and I will truly beware of do so on this occasion . all that I know I will tell , and I humbly beg the gentleman who take this down to put my language right as he go on , and to make allowance for my be no scholar . 

 in this last summer I happen to be out of place   through no fault of my own , and I hear of a situation as plain cook , at Number five , Forest Road , St. John 's Wood . I take the place on trial . my master 's name be Fosco . my mistress be an english lady . he be Count and she be Countess . there be a girl to do housemaid 's work when I get there . she be not over - clean or tidy , but there be no harm in she . I and she be the only servant in the house . 

 our master and mistress come after we get in ; and as soon as they do come we be tell , downstair , that company be expect from the country . 

 the company be my mistress 's niece , and the back bedroom on the first floor be get ready for she . my mistress mention to I that Lady Glyde   that be her name   be in poor health , and that I must be particular in my cooking accordingly . she be to come that day , as well as I can remember but whatever you do , do not   trust my memory in the matter . I be sorry to say it be no use ask I about day of the month , and such - like . except sunday , half my time I take no heed of they , be a hard - work woman and no scholar . all I know be Lady Glyde come , and when she do come , a fine fright she give we all surely . I do not   know how master bring she to the house , be hard at work at the time . but he do bring she in the afternoon , I think , and the housemaid open the door to they , and show they into the parlour . before she have be long down in the kitchen again with I , we hear a hurry - skurry upstairs , and the parlour bell ring like mad , and my mistress 's voice call out for help . 

 we both run up , and there we see the lady lay on the sofa , with her face ghastly white , and her hand fast clench , and her head draw down to one side . she have be take with a sudden fright , my mistress say , and master he tell we she be in a fit of convulsion . I run out , know the neighbourhood a little well than the rest of they , to fetch the near doctor 's help . the near help be at Goodricke 's and Garth 's , who work together as partner , and have a good name and connection , as I have hear , all round St. John 's Wood . Mr. Goodricke be in , and he come back with I directly . 

 it be some time before he could make himself of much use . the poor unfortunate lady fall out of one fit into another , and go on so till she be quite weary out , and as helpless as a new - bear babe . we then get she to bed . Mr. Goodricke go away to his house for medicine , and come back again in a quarter of an hour or less . besides the medicine he bring a bit of hollow mahogany wood with he , shape like a kind of trumpet , and after wait a little while , he put one end over the lady 's heart and the other to his ear , and listen carefully . 

 when he have do he say to my mistress , who be in the room , " this be a very serious case , " he say , " I recommend you to write to Lady Glyde 's friend directly . " my mistress say to he , " be it heart - disease ? " and he say , " yes , heart - disease of a most dangerous kind . " he tell she exactly what he think be the matter , which I be not clever enough to understand . but I know this , he end by say that he be afraid neither his help nor any other doctor 's help be likely to be of much service . 

 my mistress take this ill news more quietly than my master . he be a big , fat , odd sort of elderly man , who keep bird and white mouse , and speak to they as if they be so many christian child . he seem terribly cut up by what have happen . " ah ! poor Lady Glyde ! poor dear Lady Glyde ! " he say , and go stalk about , wring his fat hand more like a play - actor than a gentleman . for one question my mistress ask the doctor about the lady 's chance of get round , he ask a good fifty at least . I declare he quite torment we all , and when he be quiet at last , out he go into the bit of back garden , pick trumpery little nosegay , and ask I to take they upstairs and make the sick - room look pretty with they . as if that do any good .
