I think he must have be , at time , a little soft in his head . but he be not a bad master he have a monstrous civil tongue of his own , and a jolly , easy , coax way with he . I like he a deal well than my mistress . she be a hard one , if ever there be a hard one yet . 

 towards night - time the lady rouse up a little . she have be so weary out , before that , by the convulsion , that she never stir hand or foot , or speak a word to anybody . she move in the bed now , and star about she at the room and we in it . she must have be a nice - look lady when well , with light hair , and blue eye and all that . her rest be trouble at night at least so I hear from my mistress , who sit up alone with she . I only go in once before go to bed to see if I could be of any use , and then she be talk to herself in a confused , ramble manner . she seem to want sadly to speak to somebody who be absent from she somewhere . I could not   catch the name the first time , and the second time master knock at the door , with his regular mouthful of question , and another of his trumpery nosegay . 

 when I go in early the next morning , the lady be clean wear out again , and lie in a kind of faint sleep . Mr. Goodricke bring his partner , Mr. Garth , with he to advise . they say she must not be disturb out of her rest on any account . they ask my mistress many question , at the other end of the room , about what the lady 's health have be in past time , and who have attend she , and whether she have ever suffer much and long together under distress of mind . I remember my mistress say " yes " to that last question . and Mr. Goodricke look at Mr. Garth , and shake his head ; and Mr. Garth look at Mr. Goodricke , and shake his head . they seem to think that the distress might have something to do with the mischief at the lady 's heart . she be but a frail thing to look at , poor creature ! very little strength at any time , I should say very little strength . 

 later on the same morning , when she wake , the lady take a sudden turn , and get seemingly a great deal well . I be not let in again to see she , no more be the housemaid , for the reason that she be not to be disturb by stranger . what I hear of her be well be through my master . he be in wonderful good spirit about the change , and look in at the kitchen window from the garden , with his great big curly - brim white hat on , to go out . 

 " Good Mrs. Cook , " say he , " Lady Glyde be well . my mind be more easy than it be , and I be go out to stretch my big leg with a sunny little summer walk . shall I order for you , shall I market for you , Mrs. Cook ? what be you make there ? a nice tart for dinner ? much crust , if you please much crisp crust , my dear , that melt and crumble delicious in the mouth . " that be his way . he be past sixty , and fond of pastry . just think of that ! 

 the doctor come again in the forenoon , and see for himself that Lady Glyde have wake up well . he forbid we to talk to she , or to let she talk to we , in case she be that way dispose , say she must be keep quiet before all thing , and encourage to sleep as much as possible . she do not seem to want to talk whenever I see she , except overnight , when I could not   make out what she be say she seem too much wear down . Mr. Goodricke be not nearly in such good spirit about she as master . he say nothing when he come downstairs , except that he would call again at five o'clock . 

 about that time   which be before master come home again   the bell ring hard from the bedroom , and my mistress run out into the landing , and call to I to go for Mr. Goodricke , and tell he the lady have faint . I get on my bonnet and shawl , when , as good luck would have it , the doctor himself come to the house for his promise visit . 

 I let he in , and go upstairs along with he . " Lady Glyde be just as usual , " say my mistress to he at the door ; ' she be awake , and look about she in a strange , forlorn manner , when I hear she give a sort of half cry , and she faint in a moment . " the doctor go up to the bed , and stoop down over the sick lady . he look very serious , all on a sudden , at the sight of she , and put his hand on her heart . 

 my mistress star hard in Mr. Goodricke 's face . " not dead ! " say she , whispering , and turn all of a tremble from head to foot . 

 " yes , " say the doctor , very quiet and grave . " dead . I be afraid it would happen suddenly when I examine her heart yesterday . " my mistress step back from the bedside while he be speak , and tremble and tremble again . " dead ! " she whisper to herself ; " dead so suddenly ! dead so soon ! what will the Count say ? " Mr. Goodricke advise she to go downstairs , and quiet herself a little . " you have be sit up all night , " say he , " and your nerve be shake . this person , " say he , mean I , " this person will stay in the room till I can send for the necessary assistance . " my mistress do as he tell she . " I must prepare the Count , " she say . " I must carefully prepare the Count . " and so she leave we , shake from head to foot , and go out . 

 " your master be a foreigner , " say Mr. Goodricke , when my mistress have leave we . " do he understand about register the death ? " " I can not   rightly tell , sir , " say I , " but I should think not . " the doctor consider a minute , and then say he , " I do not   usually do such thing , " say he , " but it may save the family trouble in this case if I register the death myself . I shall pass the district office in half an hour 's time , and I can easily look in . Mention , if you please , that I will do so . " " yes , sir , " say I , " with thank , I be sure , for your kindness in thinking of it . " " you do not   mind stay here till I can send you the proper person ? " say he . " no , sir , " say I ; " I will stay with the poor lady till then . I suppose nothing more could be do , sir , than be do ? " say I. " no , " say he , " nothing ; she must have suffer sadly before ever I see she the case be hopeless when I be call in . " " ah , dear I ! we all come to it , soon or later , do not   we , sir ? " say I. he give no answer to that he do not   seem to care about talk . he say , " good - day , " and go out . 

 I stop by the bedside from that time till the time when Mr. Goodricke send the person in , as he have promise . she be , by name , Jane Gould . I consider she to be a respectable - look woman . she make no remark , except to say that she understand what be want of she , and that she have wind a many of they in her time . 

 how master bear the news , when he first hear it , be more than I can tell , not have be present . when I do see he he look awfully overcome by it , to be sure . he sit quiet in a corner , with his fat hand hang over his thick knee , and his head down , and his eye look at nothing . he seem not so much sorry , as scared and daze like , by what have happen . my mistress manage all that be to be do about the funeral . it must have cost a sight of money the coffin , in particular , be most beautiful . the dead lady 's husband be away , as we hear , in foreign part . but my mistress   be her aunt   settle it with her friend in the country   Cumberland , I think   that she should be bury there , in the same grave along with her mother . everything be do handsomely , in respect of the funeral , I say again , and master go down to attend the burying in the country himself . he look grand in his deep mourning , with his big solemn face , and his slow walk , and his broad hatband that he do ! 

 in conclusion . I have to say , in answer to question put to I 

  1   that neither I nor my fellow - servant ever see my master give Lady Glyde any medicine himself . 

  2   that he be never , to my knowledge and belief , leave alone in the room with Lady Glyde . 

  3   that I be not able to say what cause the sudden fright , which my mistress inform I have seize the lady on her first come into the house . the cause be never explain , either to I or to my fellow - servant . 

 the above statement have be read over in my presence . I have nothing to add to it , or to take away from it . I say , on my oath as a christian woman , this be the truth . 

  sign   hester PINHORN , her    Mark . 



 2 . the narrative of the doctor 

 to the Registrar of the Sub - District in which the undermentioned death take place . I hereby certify that I attend Lady Glyde , age Twenty - one last birthday ; that I last see she on Thursday the 25th July 1850 ; that she die on the same day at no . 5 Forest Road , St. John 's Wood , and that the cause of her death be Aneurism . duration of disease not know . 

  sign   Alfred Goodricke . 

 Prof. Title . M.R.C.S. Eng . , L.S.A. Address , 12 Croydon Gardens 
 St. John 's Wood . 



 3 . the NARRATIVE of JANE GOULD 

 I be the person send in by Mr. Goodricke to do what be right and needful by the remain of a lady who have die at the house name in the certificate which precede this . I find the body in charge of the servant , Hester Pinhorn . I remain with it , and prepare it at the proper time for the grave . it be lay in the coffin in my presence , and I afterwards see the coffin screw down previous to its removal . when that have be do , and not before , I receive what be due to I and leave the house . I refer person who may wish to investigate my character to Mr. Goodricke . he will bear witness that I can be trust to tell the truth . 

  sign   JANE GOULD 



 4 . the narrative of the tombstone 


 Sacred to the Memory of Laura , Lady Glyde , wife of Sir Percival Glyde , Bart . , of Blackwater Park , Hampshire , and daughter of the late Philip Fairlie , Esq . , of Limmeridge House , in this parish . bear March 27th , 1829 ; married December 22nd , 1849 ; die July 25th , 1850 . 




 5 . the narrative of WALTER HARTRIGHT 

 early in the summer of 1850 I and my survive companion leave the wild and forest of Central America for home . arrive at the coast , we take ship there for England . the vessel be wreck in the Gulf of Mexico I be among the few save from the sea . it be my third escape from peril of death . death by disease , death by the Indians , death by drown all three have approach I ; all three have pass I by . 

 the survivor of the wreck be rescue by an american vessel bind for Liverpool . the ship reach her port on the thirteenth day of October 1850 . we land late in the afternoon , and I arrive in London the same night . 

 these page be not the record of my wandering and my danger away from home . the motif which lead I from my country and my friend to a new world of adventure and peril be know . from that self - impose exile I come back , as I have hop , pray , believe I should come back a change man . in the water of a new life I have temper my nature afresh . in the stern school of extremity and danger my will have learn to be strong , my heart to be resolute , my mind to rely on itself . I have go out to fly from my own future . I come back to face it , as a man should . 

 to face it with that inevitable suppression of myself which I know it would demand from I . I have part with the bad bitterness of the past , but not with my heart 's remembrance of the sorrow and the tenderness of that memorable time . I have not cease to feel the one irreparable disappointment of my life I have only learn to bear it . Laura Fairlie be in all my thought when the ship bear I away , and I look my last at England . Laura Fairlie be in all my thought when the ship bring I back , and the morning light show the friendly shore in view . 

 my pen trace the old letter as my heart go back to the old love . I write of she as Laura Fairlie still . it be hard to think of she , it be hard to speak of she , by her husband 's name . 

 there be no more word of explanation to add on my appearance for the second time in these page . this narrative , if I have the strength and the courage to write it , may now go on . 


 my first anxiety and first hope when the morning come centre in my mother and my sister . I feel the necessity of prepare they for the joy and surprise of my return , after an absence during which it have be impossible for they to receive any tiding of I for month past . early in the morning I send a letter to the Hampstead Cottage , and follow it myself in an hour 's time . 

 when the first meeting be over , when our quiet and composure of other day begin gradually to return to we , I see something in my mother 's face which tell I that a secret oppression lie heavy on her heart . there be more than love there be sorrow in the anxious eye that look on I so tenderly there be pity in the kind hand that slowly and fondly strengthen its hold on mine . we have no concealment from each other . she know how the hope of my life have be wreck she know why I have leave she . it be on my lip to ask as composedly as I could if any letter have come for I from Miss Halcombe , if there be any news of her sister that I might hear . but when I look in my mother 's face I lose courage to put the question even in that guard form . I could only say , doubtingly and restrainedly 

 " you have something to tell I . " 

 my sister , who have be sit opposite to we , rise suddenly without a word of explanation rise and leave the room . 

 my mother move close to I on the sofa and put her arm round my neck . those fond arm tremble the tear flow fast over the faithful love face . 

 " Walter ! " she whisper , " my own darling ! my heart be heavy for you . oh , my son ! my son ! try to remember that I be still leave ! " 

 my head sink on her bosom . she have say all in say those word . 

 it be the morning of the third day since my return the morning of the sixteenth of October . 

 I have remain with they at the cottage I have try hard not to embitter the happiness of my return to they as it be embitter to I . I have do all man could to rise after the shock , and accept my life resignedly to let my great sorrow come in tenderness to my heart , and not in despair . it be useless and hopeless . no tear soothe my ache eye , no relief come to I from my sister 's sympathy or my mother 's love . 

 on that third morning I open my heart to they . at last the word pass my lip which I have long to speak on the day when my mother tell I of her death . 

 " let I go away alone for a little while , " I say . " I shall bear it well when I have look once more at the place where I first see she when I have kneel and pray by the grave where they have lay she to rest . " 

 I depart on my journey my journey to the grave of Laura Fairlie . 

 it be a quiet autumn afternoon when I stop at the solitary station , and set forth alone on foot by the well - remember road . the wan sun be shin faintly through thin white cloud the air be warm and still the peacefulness of the lonely country be overshadow and sadden by the influence of the fall year . 

 I reach the moor I stand again on the brow of the hill I look on along the path and there be the familiar garden tree in the distance , the clear sweeping semicircle of the drive , the high white wall of Limmeridge House . the chance and change , the wandering and danger of month and month past , all shrank and shrivel to nothing in my mind . it be like yesterday since my foot have last trodden the fragrant heathy ground . I think I should see she come to meet I , with her little straw hat shade her face , her simple dress flutter in the air , and her well - fill sketch - book ready in her hand . 

 oh death , thou hast thy sting ! oh , grave , thou hast thy victory ! 

 I turn aside , and there below I in the glen be the lonesome grey church , the porch where I have wait for the coming of the woman in white , the hill encircle the quiet burial - ground , the brook bubble cold over its stony bed . there be the marble cross , fair and white , at the head of the tomb the tomb that now rise over mother and daughter alike . 

 I approach the grave . I cross once more the low stone stile , and bar my head as I touch the sacred ground . sacred to gentleness and goodness , sacred to reverence and grief . 

 I stop before the pedestal from which the cross rise . on one side of it , on the side near to I , the newly - cut inscription meet my eye the hard , clear , cruel black letter which tell the story of her life and death . I try to read they . I do read as far as the name . " sacred to the Memory of Laura " the kind blue eye dim with tear the fair head droop wearily the innocent parting word which implore I to leave she oh , for a happy last memory of she than this ; the memory I take away with I , the memory I bring back with I to her grave ! 

 a second time I try to read the inscription . I see at the end the date of her death , and above it 

 above it there be line on the marble there be a name among they which disturb my thought of she . I go round to the other side of the grave , where there be nothing to read , nothing of earthly vileness to force its way between her spirit and mine . 

 I kneel down by the tomb . I lay my hand , I lay my head on the broad white stone , and close my weary eye on the earth around , on the light above . I let she come back to I . oh , my love ! my love ! my heart may speak to you now ! it be yesterday again since we part yesterday , since your dear hand lie in mine yesterday , since my eye look their last on you . my love ! my love ! 

 Time have flow on , and silence have fall like thick night over its course . 

 the first sound that come after the heavenly peace rustle faintly like a pass breath of air over the grass of the burial - ground . I hear it near I slowly , until it come change to my ear come like footstep move onward then stop . 

 I look up . 

 the sunset be near at hand . the cloud have part the slanting light fall mellow over the hill . the last of the day be cold and clear and still in the quiet valley of the dead . 

 beyond I , in the burial - ground , stand together in the cold clearness of the low light , I see two woman . they be look towards the tomb , look towards I . 

 two . 

 they come a little on , and stop again . their veil be down , and hide their face from I . when they stop , one of they raise her veil . in the still evening light I see the face of Marian Halcombe . 

 change , change as if year have pass over it ! the eye large and wild , and look at I with a strange terror in they . the face wear and waste piteously . pain and fear and grief write on she as with a brand . 

 I take one step towards she from the grave . she never move she never speak . the veiled woman with she cry out faintly . I stop . the spring of my life fall low , and the shuddering of an unutterable dread creep over I from head to foot . 

 the woman with the veiled face move away from her companion , and come towards I slowly . leave by herself , stand by herself , Marian Halcombe speak . it be the voice that I remember the voice not change , like the frightened eye and the waste face . 

 " my dream ! my dream ! " I hear she say those word softly in the awful silence . she sink on her knee , and raise her clasp hand to heaven . " Father ! strengthen he . Father ! help he in his hour of need . " 

 the woman come on , slowly and silently come on . I look at she at she , and at none other , from that moment . 

 the voice that be pray for I falter and sink low then rise on a sudden , and call affrightedly , call despairingly to I to come away . 

 but the veiled woman have possession of I , body and soul . she stop on one side of the grave . we stand face to face with the tombstone between we . she be close to the inscription on the side of the pedestal . her gown touch the black letter . 

 the voice come near , and rise and rise more passionately still . " hide your face ! do not   look at she ! oh , for God 's sake , spare he " 

 the woman lift her veil . 


 " sacred to the Memory of Laura , Lady Glyde " 


 Laura , Lady Glyde , be stand by the inscription , and be look at I over the grave . 


  the Second Epoch of the Story close here . 




 the THIRD EPOCH 



 THE story continued BY WALTER HARTRIGHT . 


 I 

 I open a new page . I advance my narrative by one week . 

 the history of the interval which I thus pass over must remain unrecorded . my heart turn faint , my mind sink in darkness and confusion when I think of it . this must not be , if I who write be to guide , as I ought , you who read . this must not be , if the clue that lead through the winding of the story be to remain from end to end untangle in my hand . 

 a life suddenly change its whole purpose create afresh , its hope and fear , its struggle , its interest , and its sacrifice all turn at once and for ever into a new direction this be the prospect which now open before I , like the burst of view from a mountain 's top . I leave my narrative in the quiet shadow of Limmeridge church I resume it , one week later , in the stir and turmoil of a London street . 


 the street be in a populous and a poor neighbourhood . the ground floor of one of the house in it be occupy by a small newsvendor 's shop , and the first floor and the second be let as furnish lodging of the humble kind . 

 I have take those two floor in an assumed name . on the upper floor I live , with a room to work in , a room to sleep in . on the low floor , under the same assume name , two woman live , who be describe as my sister . I get my bread by draw and engrave on wood for the cheap periodical . my sister be suppose to help I by take in a little needle - work . our poor place of abode , our humble calling , our assumed relationship , and our assumed name , be all use alike as a means of hide we in the house - forest of London . we be number no long with the people whose life be open and know . I be an obscure , unnoticed man , without patron or friend to help I . Marian Halcombe be nothing now but my eld sister , who provide for our household want by the toil of her own hand . we two , in the estimation of other , be at once the dupe and the agent of a daring imposture . we be suppose to be the accomplice of mad Anne Catherick , who claim the name , the place , and the live personality of dead Lady Glyde . 

 that be our situation . that be the change aspect in which we three must appear , henceforth , in this narrative , for many and many a page to come . 

 in the eye of reason and of law , in the estimation of relative and friend , accord to every receive formality of civilised society , " Laura , Lady Glyde , " lie bury with her mother in Limmeridge churchyard . tear in her own lifetime from the list of the living , the daughter of Philip Fairlie and the wife of Percival Glyde might still exist for her sister , might still exist for I , but to all the world besides she be dead . dead to her uncle , who have renounce she ; dead to the servant of the house , who have fail to recognise she ; dead to the person in authority , who have transmit her fortune to her husband and her aunt ; dead to my mother and my sister , who believe I to be the dupe of an adventuress and the victim of a fraud ; socially , morally , legally dead . 

 and yet alive ! alive in poverty and in hiding . alive , with the poor drawing - master to fight her battle , and to win the way back for she to her place in the world of live being . 

 do no suspicion , excite by my own knowledge of Anne Catherick 's resemblance to she , cross my mind , when her face be first reveal to I ? not the shadow of a suspicion , from the moment when she lift her veil by the side of the inscription which record her death . 

 before the sun of that day have set , before the last glimpse of the home which be close against she have pass from our view , the farewell word I speak , when we part at Limmeridge House , have be recall by both of we repeat by I , recognise by she . " if ever the time come , when the devotion of my whole heart and soul and strength will give you a moment 's happiness , or spare you a moment 's sorrow , will you try to remember the poor drawing - master who have teach you ? " she , who now remember so little of the trouble and terror of a later time , remember those word , and lay her poor head innocently and trustingly on the bosom of the man who have speak they . in that moment , when she call I by my name , when she say , " they have try to make I forget everything , Walter ; but I remember Marian , and I remember you " in that moment , I , who have long since give she my love , give she my life , and thank God that it be mine to bestow on she . yes ! the time have come . from thousand on thousand of mile away through forest and wilderness , where companion strong than I have fall by my side , through peril of death thrice renew , and thrice escape , the Hand that lead man on the dark road to the future have lead I to meet that time . forlorn and disown , sorely try and sadly change her beauty fade , her mind cloud rob of her station in the world , of her place among living creature the devotion I have promise , the devotion of my whole heart and soul and strength , might be lay blamelessly now at those dear foot . in the right of her calamity , in the right of her friendlessness , she be mine at last ! mine to support , to protect , to cherish , to restore . mine to love and honour as father and brother both . mine to vindicate through all risk and all sacrifice through the hopeless struggle against Rank and Power , through the long fight with armed deceit and fortify success , through the waste of my reputation , through the loss of my friend , through the hazard of my life . 



 II 

 my position be define my motif be acknowledge . the story of Marian and the story of Laura must come next . 

 I shall relate both narrative , not in the word   often interrupt , often inevitably confuse   of the speaker themselves , but in the word of the brief , plain , studiously simple abstract which I commit to write for my own guidance , and for the guidance of my legal adviser . so the tangled web will be most speedily and most intelligibly unroll . 

 the story of Marian begin where the narrative of the housekeeper at Blackwater Park leave off . 


 on Lady Glyde 's departure from her husband 's house , the fact of that departure , and the necessary statement of the circumstance under which it have take place , be communicate to Miss Halcombe by the housekeeper . it be not till some day afterwards   how many day exactly , Mrs. Michelson , in the absence of any write memorandum on the subject , could not undertake to say   that a letter arrive from Madame Fosco announce Lady Glyde 's sudden death in Count Fosco 's house . the letter avoid mention date , and leave it to Mrs. Michelson 's discretion to break the news at once to Miss Halcombe , or to defer do so until that lady 's health should be more firmly establish . 

 have consult Mr. Dawson   who have be himself delay , by ill health , in resume his attendance at Blackwater Park , Mrs. Michelson , by the doctor 's advice , and in the doctor 's presence , communicate the news , either on the day when the letter be receive , or on the day after . it be not necessary to dwell here upon the effect which the intelligence of Lady Glyde 's sudden death produce on her sister . it be only useful to the present purpose to say that she be not able to travel for more than three week afterwards . at the end of that time she proceed to London accompany by the housekeeper . they part there Mrs. Michelson previously inform Miss Halcombe of her address , in case they might wish to communicate at a future period . 

 on part with the housekeeper Miss Halcombe go at once to the office of Messrs. Gilmore    Kyrle to consult with the latter gentleman in Mr. Gilmore 's absence . she mention to Mr. Kyrle what she have think it desirable to conceal from every one else   Mrs. Michelson include   her suspicion of the circumstance under which Lady Glyde be say to have meet her death . Mr. Kyrle , who have previously give friendly proof of his anxiety to serve Miss Halcombe , at once undertake to make such inquiry as the delicate and dangerous nature of the investigation propose to he would permit . 

 to exhaust this part of the subject before go far , it may be mention that Count Fosco offer every facility to Mr. Kyrle , on that gentleman 's state that he be send by Miss Halcombe to collect such particular as have not yet reach she of Lady Glyde 's decease . Mr. Kyrle be place in communication with the medical man , Mr. Goodricke , and with the two servant . in the absence of any mean of ascertain the exact date of Lady Glyde 's departure from Blackwater Park , the result of the doctor 's and the servant ' evidence , and of the volunteered statement of Count Fosco and his wife , be conclusive to the mind of Mr. Kyrle . he could only assume that the intensity of Miss Halcombe 's suffering , under the loss of her sister , have mislead her judgment in a most deplorable manner , and he write her word that the shocking suspicion to which she have allude in his presence be , in his opinion , destitute of the small fragment of foundation in truth . thus the investigation by Mr. Gilmore 's partner begin and end . 

 meanwhile , Miss Halcombe have return to Limmeridge House , and have there collect all the additional information which she be able to obtain . 

 Mr. Fairlie have receive his first intimation of his niece 's death from his sister , Madame Fosco , this letter also not contain any exact reference to date . he have sanction his sister 's proposal that the deceased lady should be lay in her mother 's grave in Limmeridge churchyard . Count Fosco have accompany the remain to Cumberland , and have attend the funeral at Limmeridge , which take place on the 30th of July . it be follow , as a mark of respect , by all the inhabitant of the village and the neighbourhood . on the next day the inscription   originally draw out , it be say , by the aunt of the deceased lady , and submit for approval to her brother , Mr. Fairlie   be engrave on one side of the monument over the tomb . 

 on the day of the funeral , and for one day after it , Count Fosco have be receive as a guest at Limmeridge House , but no interview have take place between Mr. Fairlie and himself , by the former gentleman 's desire . they have communicate by writing , and through this medium Count Fosco have make Mr. Fairlie acquaint with the detail of his niece 's last illness and death . the letter present this information add no new fact to the fact already know , but one very remarkable paragraph be contain in the postscript . it refer to Anne Catherick . 

 the substance of the paragraph in question be as follow 

 it first inform Mr. Fairlie that Anne Catherick   of whom he might hear full particular from Miss Halcombe when she reach Limmeridge   have be trace and recover in the neighbourhood of Blackwater Park , and have be for the second time place under the charge of the medical man from whose custody she have once escape . 

 this be the first part of the postscript . the second part warn Mr. Fairlie that Anne Catherick 's mental malady have be aggravate by her long freedom from control , and that the insane hatred and distrust of Sir Percival Glyde , which have be one of her most marked delusion in former time , still exist under a newly - acquire form . the unfortunate woman 's last idea in connection with Sir Percival be the idea of annoying and distress he , and of elevate herself , as she suppose , in the estimation of the patient and nurse , by assume the character of his deceased wife , the scheme of this personation have evidently occur to she after a steal interview which she have succeed in obtain with Lady Glyde , and at which she have observe the extraordinary accidental likeness between the deceased lady and herself . it be to the last degree improbable that she would succeed a second time in escape from the Asylum , but it be just possible she might find some mean of annoy the late Lady Glyde 's relative with letter , and in that case Mr. Fairlie be warn beforehand how to receive they . 

 the postscript , express in these term , be show to Miss Halcombe when she arrive at Limmeridge . there be also place in her possession the clothe Lady Glyde have wear , and the other effect she have bring with she to her aunt 's house . they have be carefully collect and send to Cumberland by Madame Fosco . 

 such be the posture of affair when Miss Halcombe reach Limmeridge in the early part of September . 

 shortly afterwards she be confine to her room by a relapse , her weaken physical energy give way under the severe mental affliction from which she be now suffer . on get strong again , in a month 's time , her suspicion of the circumstance describe as attend her sister 's death still remain unshaken . she have hear nothing in the interim of Sir Percival Glyde , but letter have reach she from Madame Fosco , make the most affectionate inquiry on the part of her husband and herself . instead of answer these letter , Miss Halcombe cause the house in St. John 's Wood , and the proceeding of its inmate , to be privately watch . 

 nothing doubtful be discover . the same result attend the next investigation , which be secretly institute on the subject of Mrs. Rubelle . she have arrive in London about six month before with her husband . they have come from Lyons , and they have take a house in the neighbourhood of Leicester Square , to be fit up as a boarding - house for foreigner , who be expect to visit England in large number to see the exhibition of 1851 . nothing be know against husband or wife in the neighbourhood . they be quiet people , and they have pay their way honestly up to the present time . the final inquiry relate to Sir Percival Glyde . he be settle in Paris , and live there quietly in a small circle of English and french friend . 

 foil at all point , but still not able to rest , Miss Halcombe next determine to visit the Asylum in which she then suppose Anne Catherick to be for the second time confine . she have feel a strong curiosity about the woman in former day , and she be now doubly interested first , in ascertain whether the report of Anne Catherick 's attempt personation of Lady Glyde be true , and secondly   if it prove to be true , in discover for herself what the poor creature 's real motif be for attempt the deceit . 

 although Count Fosco 's letter to Mr. Fairlie do not mention the address of the Asylum , that important omission cast no difficulty in Miss Halcombe 's way . when Mr. Hartright have meet Anne Catherick at Limmeridge , she have inform he of the locality in which the house be situate , and Miss Halcombe have note down the direction in her diary , with all the other particular of the interview exactly as she hear they from Mr. Hartright 's own lip . accordingly she look back at the entry and extract the address furnish herself with the Count 's letter to Mr. Fairlie as a species of credential which might be useful to she , and start by herself for the Asylum on the eleventh of October . 

 she pass the night of the eleventh in London . it have be her intention to sleep at the house inhabit by Lady Glyde 's old governess , but Mrs. Vesey 's agitation at the sight of her lose pupil 's near and dear friend be so distressing that Miss Halcombe considerately refrain from remain in her presence , and remove to a respectable boarding - house in the neighbourhood , recommend by Mrs. Vesey 's married sister . the next day she proceed to the Asylum , which be situate not far from London on the northern side of the metropolis . 

 she be immediately admit to see the proprietor . 

 at first he appear to be decidedly unwilling to let she communicate with his patient . but on she show he the postscript to Count Fosco 's letter on she remind he that she be the " Miss Halcombe " there refer to that she be a near relative of the deceased Lady Glyde and that she be therefore naturally interested , for family reason , in observe for herself the extent of Anne Catherick 's delusion in relation to her late sister the tone and manner of the owner of the Asylum alter , and he withdraw his objection . he probably feel that a continued refusal , under these circumstance , would not only be an act of discourtesy in itself , but would also imply that the proceeding in his establishment be not of a nature to bear investigation by respectable stranger . 

 Miss Halcombe 's own impression be that the owner of the Asylum have not be receive into the confidence of Sir Percival and the Count . his consent at all to let she visit his patient seem to afford one proof of this , and his readiness in make admission which could scarcely have escape the lip of an accomplice , certainly appear to furnish another . 

 for example , in the course of the introductory conversation which take place , he inform Miss Halcombe that Anne Catherick have be bring back to he with the necessary order and certificate by Count Fosco on the twenty - seventh of July the Count also produce a letter of explanation and instruction sign by Sir Percival Glyde . on receive his inmate again , the proprietor of the Asylum acknowledge that he have observe some curious personal change in she . such change no doubt be not without precedent in his experience of person mentally afflict . insane people be often at one time , outwardly as well as inwardly , unlike what they be at another the change from well to bad , or from bad to well , in the madness have a necessary tendency to produce alteration of appearance externally . he allow for these , and he allow also for the modification in the form of Anne Catherick 's delusion , which be reflect no doubt in her manner and expression . but he be still perplex at time by certain difference between his patient before she have escape and his patient since she have be bring back . those difference be too minute to be describe . he could not say of course that she be absolutely alter in height or shape or complexion , or in the colour of her hair and eye , or in the general form of her face the change be something that he feel more than something that he see . in short , the case have be a puzzle from the first , and one more perplexity be add to it now . 

 it can not be say that this conversation lead to the result of even partially prepare Miss Halcombe 's mind for what be to come . but it produce , nevertheless , a very serious effect upon she . she be so completely unnerved by it , that some little time elapse before she could summon composure enough to follow the proprietor of the Asylum to that part of the house in which the inmate be confine . 

 on inquiry , it turn out that the suppose Anne Catherick be then take exercise in the ground attach to the establishment . one of the nurse volunteer to conduct Miss Halcombe to the place , the proprietor of the Asylum remain in the house for a few minute to attend to a case which require his service , and then engage to join his visitor in the ground . 

 the nurse lead Miss Halcombe to a distant part of the property , which be prettily lay out , and after look about she a little , turn into a turf walk , shade by a shrubbery on either side . about half - way down this walk two woman be slowly approach . the nurse point to they and say , " there be Anne Catherick , ma'am , with the attendant who wait on she . the attendant will answer any question you wish to put . " with those word the nurse leave she to return to the duty of the house . 

 Miss Halcombe advance on her side , and the woman advance on theirs . when they be within a dozen pace of each other , one of the woman stop for an instant , look eagerly at the strange lady , shake off the nurse 's grasp on she , and the next moment rush into Miss Halcombe 's arm . in that moment Miss Halcombe recognise her sister recognise the dead - alive . 

 fortunately for the success of the measure take subsequently , no one be present at that moment but the nurse . she be a young woman , and she be so startled that she be at first quite incapable of interfere . when she be able to do so her whole service be require by Miss Halcombe , who have for the moment sink altogether in the effort to keep her own sens under the shock of the discovery . after wait a few minute in the fresh air and the cool shade , her natural energy and courage help she a little , and she become sufficiently mistress of herself to feel the necessity of recall her presence of mind for her unfortunate sister 's sake . 

 she obtain permission to speak alone with the patient , on condition that they both remain well within the nurse 's view . there be no time for question there be only time for Miss Halcombe to impress on the unhappy lady the necessity of control herself , and to assure she of immediate help and rescue if she do so . the prospect of escape from the Asylum by obedience to her sister 's direction be sufficient to quiet Lady Glyde , and to make she understand what be require of she . Miss Halcombe next return to the nurse , place all the gold she then have in her pocket   three sovereign   in the nurse 's hand , and ask when and where she could speak to she alone . 

 the woman be at first surprised and distrustful . but on Miss Halcombe 's declare that she only want to put some question which she be too much agitated to ask at that moment , and that she have no intention of mislead the nurse into any dereliction of duty , the woman take the money , and propose three o'clock on the next day as the time for the interview . she might then slip out for half an hour , after the patient have din , and she would meet the lady in a retired place , outside the high north wall which screen the ground of the house . Miss Halcombe have only time to assent , and to whisper to her sister that she should hear from she on the next day , when the proprietor of the Asylum join they . he notice his visitor 's agitation , which Miss Halcombe account for by say that her interview with Anne Catherick have a little startled she at first . she take her leave as soon after as possible that be to say , as soon as she could summon courage to force herself from the presence of her unfortunate sister . 

 a very little reflection , when the capacity to reflect return , convince she that any attempt to identify Lady Glyde and to rescue she by legal mean , would , even if successful , involve a delay that might be fatal to her sister 's intellect , which be shake already by the horror of the situation to which she have be consign . by the time Miss Halcombe have get back to London , she have determine to effect Lady Glyde 's escape privately , by mean of the nurse . 

 she go at once to her stockbroker , and sell out of the fund all the little property she possess , amount to rather less than seven hundred pound . determine , if necessary , to pay the price of her sister 's liberty with every farthing she have in the world , she repair the next day , have the whole sum about she in bank - note , to her appointment outside the Asylum wall . 

 the nurse be there . Miss Halcombe approach the subject cautiously by many preliminary question . she discover , among other particular , that the nurse who have in former time attend on the true Anne Catherick have be hold responsible   although she be not to blame for it   for the patient 's escape , and have lose her place in consequence . the same penalty , it be add , would attach to the person then speak to she , if the suppose Anne Catherick be miss a second time ; and , moreover , the nurse in this case have an especial interest in keep her place . she be engage to be marry , and she and her future husband be wait till they could save , together , between two and three hundred pound to start in business . the nurse 's wage be good , and she might succeed , by strict economy , in contribute her small share towards the sum require in two year ' time . 

 on this hint Miss Halcombe speak . she declare that the suppose Anne Catherick be nearly related to she , that she have be place in the Asylum under a fatal mistake , and that the nurse would be do a good and a christian action in be the mean of restore they to one another . before there be time to start a single objection , Miss Halcombe take four bank - note of a hundred pound each from her pocket - book , and offer they to the woman , as a compensation for the risk she be to run , and for the loss of her place . 

 the nurse hesitate , through sheer incredulity and surprise . Miss Halcombe press the point on she firmly . 

 " you will be do a good action , " she repeat ; " you will be help the most injured and unhappy woman alive . there be your marriage portion for a reward . bring she safely to I here , and I will put these four bank - note into your hand before I claim she . " 

 " will you give I a letter say those word , which I can show to my sweetheart when he ask how I get the money ? " inquire the woman . 

 " I will bring the letter with I , ready write and sign , " answer Miss Halcombe . 

 " then I will risk it , " say the nurse . 

 " when ? " 

 " to - morrow . " 

 it be hastily agree between they that Miss Halcombe should return early the next morning and wait out of sight among the tree always , however , keep near the quiet spot of ground under the north wall . the nurse could fix no time for her appearance , caution require that she should wait and be guide by circumstance . on that understanding they separate . 

 Miss Halcombe be at her place , with the promise letter and the promise bank - note , before ten the next morning . she wait more than an hour and a half . at the end of that time the nurse come quickly round the corner of the wall hold Lady Glyde by the arm . the moment they meet Miss Halcombe put the bank - note and the letter into her hand , and the sister be unite again . 

 the nurse have dress Lady Glyde , with excellent forethought , in a bonnet , veil , and shawl of her own . Miss Halcombe only detain she to suggest a mean of turn the pursuit in a false direction , when the escape be discover at the Asylum . she be to go back to the house , to mention in the hearing of the other nurse that Anne Catherick have be inquire latterly about the distance from London to Hampshire , to wait till the last moment , before discovery be inevitable , and then to give the alarm that Anne be miss . the suppose inquiry about Hampshire , when communicate to the owner of the Asylum , would lead he to imagine that his patient have return to Blackwater Park , under the influence of the delusion which make her persist in assert herself to be Lady Glyde , and the first pursuit would , in all probability , be turn in that direction . 

 the nurse consent to follow these suggestion , the more readily as they offer she the mean of secure herself against any bad consequence than the loss of her place , by remain in the Asylum , and so maintain the appearance of innocence , at least . she at once return to the house , and Miss Halcombe lose no time in take her sister back with she to London . they catch the afternoon train to Carlisle the same afternoon , and arrive at Limmeridge , without accident or difficulty of any kind , that night . 

 during the latter part of their journey they be alone in the carriage , and Miss Halcombe be able to collect such remembrance of the past as her sister 's confused and weakened memory be able to recall . the terrible story of the conspiracy so obtain be present in fragment , sadly incoherent in themselves , and widely detach from each other . imperfect as the revelation be , it must nevertheless be record here before this explanatory narrative close with the event of the next day at Limmeridge House . 


 Lady Glyde 's recollection of the event which follow her departure from Blackwater Park begin with her arrival at the London terminus of the South Western Railway . she have omit to make a memorandum beforehand of the day on which she take the journey . all hope of fix that important date by any evidence of her , or of Mrs. Michelson 's , must be give up for lost . 

 on the arrival of the train at the platform Lady Glyde find Count Fosco wait for she . he be at the carriage door as soon as the porter could open it . the train be unusually crowd , and there be great confusion in get the luggage . some person whom Count Fosco bring with he procure the luggage which belong to Lady Glyde . it be mark with her name . she drive away alone with the Count in a vehicle which she do not particularly notice at the time . 

 her first question , on leave the terminus , refer to Miss Halcombe . the Count inform she that Miss Halcombe have not yet go to Cumberland , after - consideration have cause he to doubt the prudence of she take so long a journey without some day ' previous rest . 

 Lady Glyde next inquire whether her sister be then stay in the Count 's house . her recollection of the answer be confused , her only distinct impression in relation to it be that the Count declare he be then take she to see Miss Halcombe . Lady Glyde 's experience of London be so limited that she could not tell , at the time , through what street they be drive . but they never leave the street , and they never pass any garden or tree . when the carriage stop , it stop in a small street behind a square a square in which there be shop , and public building , and many people . from these recollection   of which Lady Glyde be certain   it seem quite clear that Count Fosco do not take she to his own residence in the suburb of St. John 's Wood . 

 they enter the house , and go upstairs to a back room , either on the first or second floor . the luggage be carefully bring in . a female servant open the door , and a man with a dark beard , apparently a foreigner , meet they in the hall , and with great politeness show they the way upstairs . in answer to Lady Glyde 's inquiry , the Count assure she that Miss Halcombe be in the house , and that she should be immediately inform of her sister 's arrival . he and the foreigner then go away and leave she by herself in the room . it be poorly furnish as a sitting - room , and it look out on the back of house . 

 the place be remarkably quiet no footstep go up or down the stair she only hear in the room beneath she a dull , rumble sound of man 's voice talk . before she have be long leave alone the Count return , to explain that Miss Halcombe be then take rest , and could not be disturb for a little while . he be accompany into the room by a gentleman   an Englishman , whom he beg to present as a friend of his . 

 after this singular introduction in the course of which no name , to the good of Lady Glyde 's recollection , have be mention she be leave alone with the stranger . he be perfectly civil , but he startle and confuse she by some odd question about herself , and by look at she , while he ask they , in a strange manner . after remain a short time he go out , and a minute or two afterwards a second stranger also an Englishman come in . this person introduce himself as another friend of Count Fosco 's , and he , in his turn , look at she very oddly , and ask some curious question never , as well as she could remember , address she by name , and go out again , after a little while , like the first man . by this time she be so frightened about herself , and so uneasy about her sister , that she have thought of venture downstairs again , and claim the protection and assistance of the only woman she have see in the house the servant who answer the door . 

 just as she have rise from her chair , the Count come back into the room . 

 the moment he appear she ask anxiously how long the meeting between her sister and herself be to be still delay . at first he return an evasive answer , but on be press , he acknowledge , with great apparent reluctance , that Miss Halcombe be by no mean so well as he have hitherto represent she to be . his tone and manner , in make this reply , so alarmed Lady Glyde , or rather so painfully increase the uneasiness which she have feel in the company of the two stranger , that a sudden faintness overcome she , and she be oblige to ask for a glass of water . the Count call from the door for water , and for a bottle of smell - salt . both be bring in by the foreign - look man with the beard . the water , when Lady Glyde attempt to drink it , have so strange a taste that it increase her faintness , and she hastily take the bottle of salt from Count Fosco , and smell at it . her head become giddy on the instant . the Count catch the bottle as it drop out of her hand , and the last impression of which she be conscious be that he hold it to her nostril again . 

 from this point her recollection be find to be confuse , fragmentary , and difficult to reconcile with any reasonable probability . 

 her own impression be that she recover her sens later in the evening , that she then leave the house , that she go   as she have previously arrange to go , at Blackwater Park   to Mrs. Vesey 's that she drink tea there , and that she pass the night under Mrs. Vesey 's roof . she be totally unable to say how , or when , or in what company she leave the house to which Count Fosco have bring she . but she persist in assert that she have be to Mrs. Vesey 's , and still more extraordinary , that she have be help to undress and get to bed by Mrs. Rubelle ! she could not remember what the conversation be at Mrs. Vesey 's or whom she see there besides that lady , or why Mrs. Rubelle should have be present in the house to help she . 

 her recollection of what happen to she the next morning be still more vague and unreliable . 

 she have some dim idea of drive out   at what hour she could not say   with Count Fosco , and with Mrs. Rubelle again for a female attendant . but when , and why , she leave Mrs. Vesey she could not tell ; neither do she know what direction the carriage drive in , or where it set she down , or whether the Count and Mrs. Rubelle do or do not remain with she all the time she be out . at this point in her sad story there be a total blank . she have no impression of the faint kind to communicate no idea whether one day , or more than one day , have pass until she come to herself suddenly in a strange place , surround by woman who be all unknown to she . 

 this be the Asylum . here she first hear herself call by Anne Catherick 's name , and here , as a last remarkable circumstance in the story of the conspiracy , her own eye inform she that she have Anne Catherick 's clothe on . the nurse , on the first night in the Asylum , have show she the mark on each article of her underclothing as it be take off , and have say , not at all irritably or unkindly , " look at your own name on your own clothe , and do not   worry we all any more about be Lady Glyde . she be dead and bury , and you be alive and hearty . do look at your clothe now ! there it be , in good mark ink , and there you will find it on all your old thing , which we have keep in the house Anne Catherick , as plain as print ! " and there it be , when Miss Halcombe examine the linen her sister wear , on the night of their arrival at Limmeridge House . 


 these be the only recollection all of they uncertain , and some of they contradictory which could be extract from Lady Glyde by careful questioning on the journey to Cumberland . Miss Halcombe abstain from press she with any inquiry relate to event in the Asylum her mind be but too evidently unfit to bear the trial of revert to they . it be know , by the voluntary admission of the owner of the mad - house , that she be receive there on the twenty - seventh of July . from that date until the fifteenth of October   the day of her rescue   she have be under restraint , her identity with Anne Catherick systematically assert , and her sanity , from first to last , practically deny . faculty less delicately balanced , constitution less tenderly organise , must have suffer under such an ordeal as this . no man could have go through it and come out of it unchanged . 

 arrive at Limmeridge late on the evening of the fifteenth , Miss Halcombe wisely resolve not to attempt the assertion of Lady Glyde 's identity until the next day . 

 the first thing in the morning she go to Mr. Fairlie 's room , and use all possible caution and preparation beforehand , at last tell he in so many word what have happen . as soon as his first astonishment and alarm have subside , he angrily declare that Miss Halcombe have allow herself to be dupe by Anne Catherick . he refer she to Count Fosco 's letter , and to what she have herself tell he of the personal resemblance between Anne and his deceased niece , and he positively decline to admit to his presence , even for one minute only , a madwoman , whom it be an insult and an outrage to have bring into his house at all . 

 Miss Halcombe leave the room wait till the first heat of her indignation have pass away decide on reflection that Mr. Fairlie should see his niece in the interest of common humanity before he close his door on she as a stranger and thereupon , without a word of previous warning , take Lady Glyde with she to his room . the servant be post at the door to prevent their entrance , but Miss Halcombe insist on pass he , and make her way into Mr. Fairlie 's presence , lead her sister by the hand . 

 the scene that follow , though it only last for a few minute , be too painful to be describe Miss Halcombe herself shrink from refer to it . let it be enough to say that Mr. Fairlie declare , in the most positive term , that he do not recognise the woman who have be bring into his room that he see nothing in her face and manner to make he doubt for a moment that his niece lie bury in Limmeridge churchyard , and that he would call on the law to protect he if before the day be over she be not remove from the house . 

 take the very bad view of Mr. Fairlie 's selfishness , indolence , and habitual want of feeling , it be manifestly impossible to suppose that he be capable of such infamy as secretly recognise and openly disown his brother 's child . Miss Halcombe humanely and sensibly allow all due force to the influence of prejudice and alarm in prevent he from fairly exercise his perception , and account for what have happen in that way . but when she next put the servant to the test , and find that they too be , in every case , uncertain , to say the least of it , whether the lady present to they be their young mistress or Anne Catherick , of whose resemblance to she they have all hear , the sad conclusion be inevitable that the change produce in Lady Glyde 's face and manner by her imprisonment in the Asylum be far more serious than Miss Halcombe have at first suppose . the vile deception which have assert her death defied exposure even in the house where she be bear , and among the people with whom she have live . 

 in a less critical situation the effort need not have be give up as hopeless even yet . 

 for example , the maid , Fanny , who happen to be then absent from Limmeridge , be expect back in two day , and there would be a chance of gain her recognition to start with , see that she have be in much more constant communication with her mistress , and have be much more heartily attach to she than the other servant . again , Lady Glyde might have be privately keep in the house or in the village to wait until her health be a little recovered and her mind be a little steadied again . when her memory could be once more trust to serve she , she would naturally refer to person and event in the past with a certainty and a familiarity which no impostor could simulate , and so the fact of her identity , which her own appearance have fail to establish , might subsequently be prove , with time to help she , by the surer test of her own word . 

 but the circumstance under which she have regain her freedom render all recourse to such mean as these simply impracticable . the pursuit from the Asylum , divert to Hampshire for the time only , would infallibly next take the direction of Cumberland . the person appoint to seek the fugitive might arrive at Limmeridge House at a few hour ' notice , and in Mr. Fairlie 's present temper of mind they might count on the immediate exertion of his local influence and authority to assist they . the common consideration for Lady Glyde 's safety force on Miss Halcombe the necessity of resign the struggle to do her justice , and of remove she at once from the place of all other that be now most dangerous to she the neighbourhood of her own home . 

 an immediate return to London be the first and wise measure of security which suggest itself . in the great city all trace of they might be most speedily and most surely effaced . there be no preparation to make no farewell word of kindness to exchange with any one . on the afternoon of that memorable day of the sixteenth Miss Halcombe rouse her sister to a last exertion of courage , and without a live soul to wish they well at parting , the two take their way into the world alone , and turn their back for ever on Limmeridge House . 

 they have pass the hill above the churchyard , when Lady Glyde insist on turn back to look she last at her mother 's grave . Miss Halcombe try to shake her resolution , but , in this one instance , try in vain . she be immovable . her dim eye light with a sudden fire , and flash through the veil that hang over they her waste finger strengthen moment by moment round the friendly arm by which they have hold so listlessly till this time . I believe in my soul that the hand of God be point their way back to they , and that the most innocent and the most afflict of his creature be choose in that dread moment to see it . 

 they retrace their step to the burial - ground , and by that act seal the future of our three life . 



 III 

 this be the story of the past the story so far as we know it then . 

 two obvious conclusion present themselves to my mind after hear it . in the first place , I see darkly what the nature of the conspiracy have be , how chance have be watch , and how circumstance have be handle to ensure impunity to a daring and an intricate crime . while all detail be still a mystery to I , the vile manner in which the personal resemblance between the woman in white and Lady Glyde have be turn to account be clear beyond a doubt . it be plain that Anne Catherick have be introduce into Count Fosco 's house as Lady Glyde it be plain that Lady Glyde have take the dead woman 's place in the Asylum the substitution have be so manage as to make innocent people   the doctor and the two servant certainly , and the owner of the mad - house in all probability   accomplice in the crime . 

 the second conclusion come as the necessary consequence of the first . we three have no mercy to expect from Count Fosco and Sir Percival Glyde . the success of the conspiracy have bring with it a clear gain to those two man of thirty thousand pound twenty thousand to one , ten thousand to the other through his wife . they have that interest , as well as other interest , in ensure their impunity from exposure , and they would leave no stone unturne , no sacrifice unattempte , no treachery untried , to discover the place in which their victim be conceal , and to part she from the only friend she have in the world Marian Halcombe and myself . 

 the sense of this serious peril a peril which every day and every hour might bring nearer and near to we be the one influence that guide I in fix the place of our retreat . I choose it in the far east of London , where there be few idle people to lounge and look about they in the street . I choose it in a poor and a populous neighbourhood because the hard the struggle for existence among the man and woman about we , the less the risk of their have the time or take the pain to notice chance stranger who come among they . these be the great advantage I look to , but our locality be a gain to we also in another and a hardly less important respect . we could live cheaply by the daily work of my hand , and could save every farthing we possess to forward the purpose , the righteous purpose , of redress an infamous wrong which , from first to last , I now keep steadily in view . 

 in a week 's time Marian Halcombe and I have settle how the course of our new life should be direct . 

 there be no other lodger in the house , and we have the mean of go in and out without pass through the shop . I arrange , for the present at least , that neither Marian nor Laura should stir outside the door without my be with they , and that in my absence from home they should let no one into their room on any pretence whatever . this rule establish , I go to a friend whom I have know in former day a wood engraver in large practice to seek for employment , tell he , at the same time , that I have reason for wish to remain unknown . 

 he at once conclude that I be in debt , express his regret in the usual form , and then promise to do what he could to assist I . I leave his false impression undisturbed , and accept the work he have to give . he know that he could trust my experience and my industry . I have what he want , steadiness and facility , and though my earning be but small , they suffice for our necessity . as soon as we could feel certain of this , Marian Halcombe and I put together what we possess . she have between two and three hundred pound leave of her own property , and I have nearly as much remain from the purchase - money obtain by the sale of my drawing - master 's practice before I leave England . together we make up between we more than four hundred pound . I deposit this little fortune in a bank , to be keep for the expense of those secret inquiry and investigation which I be determined to set on foot , and to carry on by myself if I could find no one to help I . we calculate our weekly expenditure to the last farthing , and we never touch our little fund except in Laura 's interest and for Laura 's sake . 

 the house - work , which , if we have dare trust a stranger near we , would have be do by a servant , be take on the first day , take as her own right , by Marian Halcombe . " what a woman 's hand be fit for , " she say , " early and late , these hand of mine shall do . " they tremble as she hold they out . the waste arm tell their sad story of the past , as she turn up the sleeve of the poor plain dress that she wear for safety 's sake ; but the unquenchable spirit of the woman burn bright in she even yet . I see the big tear rise thick in her eye , and fall slowly over her cheek as she look at I . she dash they away with a touch of her old energy , and smile with a faint reflection of her old good spirit . " do not   doubt my courage , Walter , " she plead , " it be my weakness that cry , not I . the house - work shall conquer it if I can not . " and she keep her word the victory be win when we meet in the evening , and she sit down to rest . her large steady black eye look at I with a flash of their bright firmness of bygone day . " I be not quite break down yet , " she say . " I be worth trust with my share of the work . " before I could answer , she add in a whisper , " and worth trust with my share in the risk and the danger too . remember that , if the time come ! " 

 I do remember it when the time come . 


 as early as the end of October the daily course of our life have assume its settle direction , and we three be as completely isolate in our place of concealment as if the house we live in have be a desert island , and the great network of street and the thousand of our fellow - creature all round we the water of an illimitable sea . I could now reckon on some leisure time for consider what my future plan of action should be , and how I might arm myself most securely at the outset for the come struggle with Sir Percival and the Count . 

 I give up all hope of appeal to my recognition of Laura , or to Marian 's recognition of she , in proof of her identity . if we have love she less dearly , if the instinct implant in we by that love have not be far more certain than any exercise of reasoning , far keener than any process of observation , even we might have hesitate on first see she . 

 the outward change work by the suffering and the terror of the past have fearfully , almost hopelessly , strengthen the fatal resemblance between Anne Catherick and herself . in my narrative of event at the time of my residence in Limmeridge House , I have record , from my own observation of the two , how the likeness , strike as it be when view generally , fail in many important point of similarity when test in detail . in those former day , if they have both be see together side by side , no person could for a moment have mistake they one for the other as have happen often in the instance of twin . I could not say this now . the sorrow and suffering which I have once blame myself for associate even by a pass thought with the future of Laura Fairlie , had set their profane mark on the youth and beauty of her face ; and the fatal resemblance which I have once see and shudder at see , in idea only , be now a real and living resemblance which assert itself before my own eye . stranger , acquaintance , friend even who could not look at she as we look , if she have be show to they in the first day of her rescue from the Asylum , might have doubt if she be the Laura Fairlie they have once see , and doubt without blame . 

 the one remain chance , which I have at first thought might be trust to serve we the chance of appeal to her recollection of person and event with which no impostor could be familiar , be prove , by the sad test of our later experience , to be hopeless . every little caution that Marian and I practise towards she every little remedy we try , to strengthen and steady slowly the weaken , shake faculty , be a fresh protest in itself against the risk of turn her mind back on the troubled and the terrible past . 

 the only event of former day which we venture on encourage she to recall be the little trivial domestic event of that happy time at Limmeridge , when I first go there and teach she to draw . the day when I rouse those remembrance by show she the sketch of the summer - house which she have give I on the morning of our farewell , and which have never be separate from I since , be the birthday of our first hope . tenderly and gradually , the memory of the old walk and drive dawn upon she , and the poor weary pin eye look at Marian and at I with a new interest , with a falter thoughtfulness in they , which from that moment we cherish and keep alive . I buy she a little box of colour , and a sketch - book like the old sketch - book which I have see in her hand on the morning that we first meet . once again oh I , once again ! at spare hour save from my work , in the dull London light , in the poor London room , I sit by her side to guide the falter touch , to help the feeble hand . day by day I raise and raise the new interest till its place in the blank of her existence be at last assured till she could think of her drawing and talk of it , and patiently practise it by herself , with some faint reflection of the innocent pleasure in my encouragement , the grow enjoyment in her own progress , which belong to the lose life and the lose happiness of past day . 

 we help her mind slowly by this simple mean , we take she out between we to walk on fine day , in a quiet old City square near at hand , where there be nothing to confuse or alarm she we spar a few pound from the fund at the banker 's to get her wine , and the delicate strengthen food that she require we amuse she in the evening with child 's game at card , with scrap - book full of print which I borrow from the engraver who employ I by these , and other trifling attention like they , we compose she and steady she , and hop all thing , as cheerfully as we could from time and care , and love that never neglect and never despair of she . but to take she mercilessly from seclusion and repose to confront she with stranger , or with acquaintance who be little well than stranger to rouse the painful impression of her past life which we have so carefully hush to rest this , even in her own interest , we dare not do . whatever sacrifice it cost , whatever long , weary , heartbreaking delay it involve , the wrong that have be inflict on she , if mortal mean could grapple it , must be redress without her knowledge and without her help . 

 this resolution settle , it be next necessary to decide how the first risk should be venture , and what the first proceeding should be . 

 after consult with Marian , I resolve to begin by gather together as many fact as could be collect then to ask the advice of Mr. Kyrle   whom we know we could trust , and to ascertain from he , in the first instance , if the legal remedy lie fairly within our reach . I owe it to Laura 's interest not to stake her whole future on my own unaided exertion , so long as there be the faint prospect of strengthen our position by obtain reliable assistance of any kind . 

 the first source of information to which I apply be the journal keep at Blackwater Park by Marian Halcombe . there be passage in this diary relate to myself which she think it well that I should not see . accordingly , she read to I from the manuscript , and I take the note I want as she go on . we could only find time to pursue this occupation by sit up late at night . three night be devoted to the purpose , and be enough to put I in possession of all that Marian could tell . 

 my next proceeding be to gain as much additional evidence as I could procure from other people without exciting suspicion . I go myself to Mrs. Vesey to ascertain if Laura 's impression of have sleep there be correct or not . in this case , from consideration for Mrs. Vesey 's age and infirmity , and in all subsequent case of the same kind from consideration of caution , I keep our real position a secret , and be always careful to speak of Laura as " the late Lady Glyde . " 

 Mrs. Vesey 's answer to my inquiry only confirm the apprehension which I have previously feel . Laura have certainly write to say she would pass the night under the roof of her old friend but she have never be near the house . 

 her mind in this instance , and , as I fear , in other instance besides , confusedly present to her something which she have only intend to do in the false light of something which she have really do . the unconscious contradiction of herself be easy to account for in this way but it be likely to lead to serious result . it be a stumble on the threshold at start it be a flaw in the evidence which tell fatally against we . 

 when I next ask for the letter which Laura have write to Mrs. Vesey from Blackwater Park , it be give to I without the envelope , which have be throw into the wastepaper basket , and long since destroy . in the letter itself no date be mention not even the day of the week . it only contain these line : " Dearest Mrs. Vesey , I be in sad distress and anxiety , and I may come to your house to - morrow night , and ask for a bed . I can not   tell you what be the matter in this letter I write it in such fear of be find out that I can fix my mind on nothing . pray be at home to see I . I will give you a thousand kiss , and tell you everything . your affectionate Laura . " what help be there in those line ? none . 

 on return from Mrs. Vesey 's , I instruct Marian to write   observe the same caution which I practise myself   to Mrs. Michelson . she be to express , if she please , some general suspicion of Count Fosco 's conduct , and she be to ask the housekeeper to supply we with a plain statement of event , in the interest of truth . while we be wait for the answer , which reach we in a week 's time , I go to the doctor in St. John 's Wood , introduce myself as send by Miss Halcombe to collect , if possible , more particular of her sister 's last illness than Mr. Kyrle have find the time to procure . by Mr. Goodricke 's assistance , I obtain a copy of the certificate of death , and an interview with the woman   Jane Gould   who have be employ to prepare the body for the grave . through this person I also discover a mean of communicate with the servant , Hester Pinhorn . she have recently leave her place in consequence of a disagreement with her mistress , and she be lodge with some people in the neighbourhood whom Mrs. Gould know . in the manner here indicate I obtain the Narratives of the housekeeper , of the doctor , of Jane Gould , and of Hester Pinhorn , exactly as they be present in these page . 

 furnish with such additional evidence as these document afford , I consider myself to be sufficiently prepare for a consultation with Mr. Kyrle , and Marian write accordingly to mention my name to he , and to specify the day and hour at which I request to see he on private business . 

 there be time enough in the morning for I to take Laura out for her walk as usual , and to see she quietly settle at she draw afterwards . she look up at I with a new anxiety in her face as I rise to leave the room , and her finger begin to toy doubtfully , in the old way , with the brush and pencil on the table . 

 " you be not tired of I yet ? " she say . " you be not go away because you be tired of I ? I will try to do well I will try to get well . be you as fond of I , Walter as you use to be , now I be so pale and thin , and so slow in learn to draw ? " 

 she speak as a child might have speak , she show I her thought as a child might have show they . I wait a few minute long wait to tell she that she be dearer to I now than she have ever be in the past time . " try to get well again , " I say , encourage the new hope in the future which I see dawn in her mind , " try to get well again , for Marian 's sake and for mine . " 

 " yes , " she say to herself , return to her drawing . " I must try , because they be both so fond of I . " she suddenly look up again . " do not   be go long ! I can not   get on with my drawing , Walter , when you be not here to help I . " 

 " I shall soon be back , my darling soon be back to see how you be get on . " 

 my voice falter a little in spite of I . I force myself from the room . it be no time , then , for part with the self - control which might yet serve I in my need before the day be out . 

 as I open the door , I beckon to Marian to follow I to the stair . it be necessary to prepare she for a result which I feel might soon or later follow my show myself openly in the street . 

 " I shall , in all probability , be back in a few hour , " I say , " and you will take care , as usual , to let no one inside the door in my absence . but if anything happen " 

 " what can happen ? " she interpose quickly . " tell I plainly , Walter , if there be any danger , and I shall know how to meet it . " 

 " the only danger , " I reply , " be that Sir Percival Glyde may have be recall to London by the news of Laura 's escape . you be aware that he have I watch before I leave England , and that he probably know I by sight , although I do not   know he ? " 

 she lay her hand on my shoulder and look at I in anxious silence . I see she understand the serious risk that threaten we .
