 " it be not likely , " I say , " that I shall be see in London again so soon , either by Sir Percival himself or by the person in his employ . but it be barely possible that an accident may happen . in that case , you will not be alarmed if I fail to return to - night , and you will satisfy any inquiry of Laura 's with the good excuse that you can make for I ? if I find the least reason to suspect that I be watch , I will take good care that no spy follow I back to this house . do not   doubt my return , Marian , however it may be delay and fear nothing . " 

 " nothing ! " she answer firmly . " you shall not regret , Walter , that you have only a woman to help you . " she pause , and detain I for a moment long . " take care ! " she say , press my hand anxiously " take care ! " 

 I leave she , and set forth to pave the way for discovery the dark and doubtful way , which begin at the lawyer 's door . 



 IV 

 no circumstance of the slight importance happen on my way to the office of Messrs. Gilmore    Kyrle , in Chancery Lane . 

 while my card be be take in to Mr. Kyrle , a consideration occur to I which I deeply regret not have think of before . the information derive from Marian 's diary make it a matter of certainty that Count Fosco have open her first letter from Blackwater Park to Mr. Kyrle , and have , by mean of his wife , intercept the second . he be therefore well aware of the address of the office , and he would naturally infer that if Marian want advice and assistance , after Laura 's escape from the Asylum , she would apply once more to the experience of Mr. Kyrle . in this case the office in Chancery Lane be the very first place which he and Sir Percival would cause to be watch , and if the same person be choose for the purpose who have be employ to follow I , before my departure from England , the fact of my return would in all probability be ascertain on that very day . I have think , generally , of the chance of my be recognise in the street , but the special risk connect with the office have never occur to I until the present moment . it be too late now to repair this unfortunate error in judgment too late to wish that I have make arrangement for meet the lawyer in some place privately appoint beforehand . I could only resolve to be cautious on leave Chancery Lane , and not to go straight home again under any circumstance whatever . 

 after wait a few minute I be show into Mr. Kyrle 's private room . he be a pale , thin , quiet , self - possess man , with a very attentive eye , a very low voice , and a very undemonstrative manner not   as I judge   ready with his sympathy where stranger be concern , and not at all easy to disturb in his professional composure . a well man for my purpose could hardly have be find . if he commit himself to a decision at all , and if the decision be favourable , the strength of our case be as good as prove from that moment . 

 " before I enter on the business which bring I here , " I say , " I ought to warn you , Mr. Kyrle , that the short statement I can make of it may occupy some little time . " 

 " my time be at Miss Halcombe 's disposal , " he reply . " where any interest of her be concern , I represent my partner personally , as well as professionally . it be his request that I should do so , when he cease to take an active part in business . " 

 " may I inquire whether Mr. Gilmore be in England ? " 

 " he be not , he be live with his relative in Germany . his health have improve , but the period of his return be still uncertain . " 

 while we be exchange these few preliminary word , he have be search among the paper before he , and he now produce from they a seal letter . I think he be about to hand the letter to I , but , apparently change his mind , he place it by itself on the table , settle himself in his chair , and silently wait to hear what I have to say . 

 without waste a moment in prefatory word of any sort , I enter on my narrative , and put he in full possession of the event which have already be relate in these page . 

 Lawyer as he be to the very marrow of his bone , I startle he out of his professional composure . expression of incredulity and surprise , which he could not repress , interrupt I several time before I have do . I persevere , however , to the end , and as soon as I reach it , boldly ask the one important question 

 " what be your opinion , Mr. Kyrle ? " 

 he be too cautious to commit himself to an answer without take time to recover his self - possession first . 

 " before I give my opinion , " he say , " I must beg permission to clear the ground by a few question . " 

 he put the question sharp , suspicious , unbelieve question , which clearly show I , as they proceed , that he think I be the victim of a delusion , and that he might even have doubt , but for my introduction to he by Miss Halcombe , whether I be not attempt the perpetration of a cunningly - design fraud . 

 " do you believe that I have speak the truth , Mr. Kyrle ? " I ask , when he have do examine I . 

 " so far as your own conviction be concern , I be certain you have speak the truth , " he reply . " I have the high esteem for Miss Halcombe , and I have therefore every reason to respect a gentleman whose mediation she trust in a matter of this kind . I will even go far , if you like , and admit , for courtesy 's sake and for argument 's sake , that the identity of Lady Glyde as a live person be a proved fact to Miss Halcombe and yourself . but you come to I for a legal opinion . as a lawyer , and as a lawyer only , it be my duty to tell you , Mr. Hartright , that you have not the shadow of a case . " 

 " you put it strongly , Mr. Kyrle . " 

 " I will try to put it plainly as well . the evidence of Lady Glyde 's death be , on the face of it , clear and satisfactory . there be her aunt 's testimony to prove that she come to Count Fosco 's house , that she fall ill , and that she die . there be the testimony of the medical certificate to prove the death , and to show that it take place under natural circumstance . there be the fact of the funeral at Limmeridge , and there be the assertion of the inscription on the tomb . that be the case you want to overthrow . what evidence have you to support the declaration on your side that the person who die and be bury be not Lady Glyde ? let we run through the main point of your statement and see what they be worth . Miss Halcombe go to a certain private Asylum , and there see a certain female patient . it be know that a woman name Anne Catherick , and bear an extraordinary personal resemblance to Lady Glyde , escape from the asylum ; it be know that the person receive there last July be receive as Anne Catherick bring back ; it be know that the gentleman who bring she back warn Mr. Fairlie that it be part of her insanity to be bent on personate his dead niece ; and it be know that she do repeatedly declare herself in the Asylum   where no one believe she   to be Lady Glyde . these be all fact . what have you to set against they ? Miss Halcombe 's recognition of the woman , which recognition after - event invalidate or contradict . do Miss Halcombe assert her suppose sister 's identity to the owner of the Asylum , and take legal mean for rescue she ? no , she secretly bribe a nurse to let her escape . when the patient have be release in this doubtful manner , and be take to Mr. Fairlie , do he recognise she ? be he stagger for one instant in his belief of his niece 's death ? no . do the servant recognise she ? no . be she keep in the neighbourhood to assert her own identity , and to stand the test of further proceeding ? no , she be privately take to London . in the meantime you have recognise she also , but you be not a relative you be not even an old friend of the family . the servant contradict you , and Mr. Fairlie contradict Miss Halcombe , and the suppose Lady Glyde contradict herself . she declare she pass the night in London at a certain house . your own evidence show that she have never be near that house , and your own admission be that her condition of mind prevent you from produce she anywhere to submit to investigation , and to speak for herself . I pass over minor point of evidence on both side to save time , and I ask you , if this case be to go now into a court of law to go before a jury , bind to take fact as they reasonably appear where be your proof ? " 

 I be oblige to wait and collect myself before I could answer he . it be the first time the story of Laura and the story of Marian have be present to I from a stranger 's point of view the first time the terrible obstacle that lie across our path have be make to show themselves in their true character . 

 " there can be no doubt , " I say , " that the fact , as you have state they , appear to tell against we , but " 

 " but you think those fact can be explain away , " interpose Mr. Kyrle . " let I tell you the result of my experience on that point . when an english jury have to choose between a plain fact on the surface and a long explanation under the surface , it always take the fact in preference to the explanation . for example , Lady Glyde   I call the lady you represent by that name for argument 's sake   declare she have sleep at a certain house , and it be prove that she have not sleep at that house . you explain this circumstance by enter into the state of her mind , and deduce from it a metaphysical conclusion . I do not   say the conclusion be wrong I only say that the jury will take the fact of she contradict herself in preference to any reason for the contradiction that you can offer . " 

 " but be it not possible , " I urge , " by dint of patience and exertion , to discover additional evidence ? Miss Halcombe and I have a few hundred pound " 

 he look at I with a half - suppress pity , and shake his head . 

 " consider the subject , Mr. Hartright , from your own point of view , " he say . " if you be right about Sir Percival Glyde and Count Fosco   which I do not   admit , mind , every imaginable difficulty would be throw in the way of your get fresh evidence . every obstacle of litigation would be raise every point in the case would be systematically contest and by the time we have spend our thousand instead of our hundred , the final result would , in all probability , be against we . question of identity , where instance of personal resemblance be concern , be , in themselves , the hard of all question to settle the hard , even when they be free from the complication which beset the case we be now discuss . I really see no prospect of throw any light whatever on this extraordinary affair . even if the person bury in Limmeridge churchyard be not Lady Glyde , she be , in life , on your own showing , so like she , that we should gain nothing , if we apply for the necessary authority to have the body exhume . in short , there be no case , Mr. Hartright there be really no case . " 

 I be determined to believe that there be a case , and in that determination shift my ground , and appeal to he once more .

" the simple and sure of all proof , the proof by comparison of date , be , as I understand , altogether out of your reach . if you could show a discrepancy between the date of the doctor 's certificate and the date of Lady Glyde 's journey to London , the matter would wear a totally different aspect , and I should be the first to say , let we go on . " 

 " that date may yet be recover , Mr. Kyrle . "

 I consider . the housekeeper could not help we Laura could not help we Marian could not help we . in all probability , the only person in existence who know the date be Sir Percival and the Count .

 Mr. Kyrle 's calmly attentive face relaxed , for the first time , into a smile .

 " impel by honourable admiration honourable to myself , honourable to you I write , magnificent Marian , in the interest of your tranquillity , to say two consoling word 

 " fear nothing !

 " to blackwater ! "

 V 

 the story of my first inquiry in Hampshire be soon tell . 

 my early departure from London enable I to reach Mr. Dawson 's house in the forenoon . our interview , so far as the object of my visit be concern , lead to no satisfactory result . 

 Mr. Dawson 's book certainly show when he have resume his attendance on Miss Halcombe at Blackwater Park , but it be not possible to calculate back from this date with any exactness , without such help from Mrs. Michelson as I know she be unable to afford . she could not say from memory   who , in similar case , ever can ?   how many day have elapse between the renewal of the doctor 's attendance on his patient and the previous departure of Lady Glyde . she be almost certain of have mention the circumstance of the departure to Miss Halcombe , on the day after it happen but then she be no more able to fix the date of the day on which this disclosure take place , than to fix the date of the day before , when Lady Glyde have leave for London . neither could she calculate , with any nearer approach to exactness , the time that have pass from the departure of her mistress , to the period when the undated letter from Madame Fosco arrive . lastly , as if to complete the series of difficulty , the doctor himself , have be ill at the time , have omit to make his usual entry of the day of the week and month when the gardener from Blackwater Park have call on he to deliver Mrs. Michelson 's message . 

 hopeless of obtain assistance from Mr. Dawson , I resolve to try next if I could establish the date of Sir Percival 's arrival at Knowlesbury . 

 it seem like a fatality ! when I reach Knowlesbury the inn be shut up , and bill be post on the wall . the speculation have be a bad one , as I be inform , ever since the time of the railway . the new hotel at the station have gradually absorb the business , and the old inn   which we know to be the inn at which Sir Percival have put up , have be close about two month since . the proprietor have leave the town with all his good and chattel , and where he have go I could not positively ascertain from any one . the four people of whom I inquire give I four different account of his plan and project when he leave Knowlesbury . 

 there be still some hour to spare before the last train leave for London , and I drive back again in a fly from the Knowlesbury station to Blackwater Park , with the purpose of question the gardener and the person who keep the lodge . if they , too , prove unable to assist I , my resource for the present be at an end , and I might return to town . 

 I dismiss the fly a mile distant from the park , and get my direction from the driver , proceed by myself to the house . 

 as I turn into the lane from the high - road , I see a man , with a carpet - bag , walk before I rapidly on the way to the lodge . he be a little man , dress in shabby black , and wear a remarkably large hat . I set he down   as well as it be possible to judge   for a lawyer 's clerk , and stop at once to widen the distance between we . he have not hear I , and he walk on out of sight , without look back . when I pass through the gate myself , a little while afterwards , he be not visible he have evidently go on to the house .

"

 certain suspicion of his errand at Blackwater Park have already cross my mind . they be now increase by the gardener 's inability   or unwillingness   to tell I who the man be , and I determine to clear the way before I , if possible , by speak to he . the plain question I could put as a stranger would be to inquire if the house be allow to be show to visitor .  I be prepared to be watch on the way from Blackwater Park to the station , exactly as I have be watch in London the day before . but I could not discover at the time , whether I be really follow on this occasion or not . the man in black might have have mean of track I at his disposal of which I be not aware , but I certainly see nothing of he , in his own person , either on the way to the station , or afterwards on my arrival at the London terminus in the evening . I reach home on foot , take the precaution , before I approach our own door , of walk round by the lonely street in the neighbourhood , and there stop and look back more than once over the open space behind I . I have first learn to use this stratagem against suspect treachery in the wild of Central America and now I be practise it again , with the same purpose and with even great caution , in the heart of civilised London ! 

 nothing have happen to alarm Marian during my absence . she ask eagerly what success I have meet with . when I tell she she could not conceal her surprise at the indifference with which I speak of the failure of my investigation thus far . 

 the truth be , that the ill - success of my inquiry have in no sense daunt I . I have pursue they as a matter of duty , and I have expect nothing from they . in the state of my mind at that time , it be almost a relief to I to know that the struggle be now narrow to a trial of strength between myself and Sir Percival Glyde . the vindictive motive have mingle itself all along with my other and well motif , and I confess it be a satisfaction to I to feel that the sure way , the only way left , of serve Laura 's cause , be to fasten my hold firmly on the villain who have marry she . 

 while I acknowledge that I be not strong enough to keep my motif above the reach of this instinct of revenge , I can honestly say something in my own favour on the other side . no base speculation on the future relation of Laura and myself , and on the private and personal concession which I might force from Sir Percival if I once have he at my mercy , ever enter my mind . I never say to myself , " if I do succeed , it shall be one result of my success that I put it out of her husband 's power to take she from I again . " I could not look at she and think of the future with such thought as those . the sad sight of the change in she from her former self , make the one interest of my love an interest of tenderness and compassion which her father or her brother might have feel , and which I feel , God know , in my inmost heart . all my hope look no far on now than to the day of her recovery . there , till she be strong again and happy again there , till she could look at I as she have once look , and speak to I as she have once speak the future of my happy thought and my dearest wish end . 

 these word be write under no prompting of idle self - contemplation . passage in this narrative be soon to come which will set the mind of other in judgment on my conduct . it be right that the good and the bad of I should be fairly balanced before that time . 


 on the morning after my return from Hampshire I take marian upstairs into my working - room , and there lay before she the plan that I have mature thus far , for master the one assailable point in the life of Sir Percival Glyde . 

 the way to the Secret lie through the mystery , hitherto impenetrable to all of we , of the woman in white . the approach to that in its turn might be gain by obtain the assistance of Anne Catherick 's mother , and the only ascertainable mean of prevail on Mrs. Catherick to act or to speak in the matter depend on the chance of my discovering local particular and family particular first of all from Mrs. Clements . after think the subject over carefully , I feel certain that I could only begin the new inquiry by place myself in communication with the faithful friend and protectress of Anne Catherick . 

 the first difficulty then be to find Mrs. Clements . 

 I be indebted to Marian 's quick perception for meet this necessity at once by the good and simplest mean . she propose to write to the farm near Limmeridge   Todd 's Corner , to inquire whether Mrs. Clements have communicate with Mrs. Todd during the past few month . how Mrs. Clements have be separate from Anne it be impossible for we to say , but that separation once effect , it would certainly occur to Mrs. Clements to inquire after the miss woman in the neighbourhood of all other to which she be know to be most attach the neighbourhood of Limmeridge . I see directly that Marian 's proposal offer we a prospect of success , and she write to Mrs. Todd accordingly by that day 's post . 

 while we be wait for the reply , I make myself master of all the information Marian could afford on the subject of Sir Percival 's family , and of his early life . she could only speak on these topic from hearsay , but she be reasonably certain of the truth of what little she have to tell . 

 Sir Percival be an only child . his father , Sir Felix Glyde , have suffer from his birth under a painful and incurable deformity , and have shun all society from his early year . his sole happiness be in the enjoyment of music , and he have marry a lady with taste similar to his own , who be say to be a most accomplished musician . he inherit the Blackwater property while still a young man . neither he nor his wife after take possession , make advance of any sort towards the society of the neighbourhood , and no one endeavour to tempt they into abandon their reserve , with the one disastrous exception of the rector of the parish . 

 the rector be the bad of all innocent mischief - maker an over - zealous man . he have hear that Sir Felix have leave College with the character of be little well than a revolutionist in politic and an infidel in religion , and he arrive conscientiously at the conclusion that it be his bounden duty to summon the lord of the manor to hear sound view enunciate in the parish church . Sir Felix fiercely resent the clergyman 's well - mean but ill - direct interference , insult he so grossly and so publicly , that the family in the neighbourhood send letter of indignant remonstrance to the Park , and even the tenant of the Blackwater property express their opinion as strongly as they dare . the baronet , who have no country taste of any kind , and no attachment to the estate or to any one live on it , declare that society at Blackwater should never have a second chance of annoy he , and leave the place from that moment . 

 after a short residence in London he and his wife depart for the continent , and never return to England again . they live part of the time in France and part in Germany always keep themselves in the strict retirement which the morbid sense of his own personal deformity have make a necessity to Sir Felix . their son , Percival , have be bear abroad , and have be educate there by private tutor . his mother be the first of his parent whom he lose . his father have die a few year after she , either in 1825 or 1826 . Sir Percival have be in England , as a young man , once or twice before that period , but his acquaintance with the late Mr. Fairlie do not begin till after the time of his father 's death . they soon become very intimate , although Sir Percival be seldom , or never , at Limmeridge House in those day . Mr. Frederick Fairlie might have meet he once or twice in Mr. Philip Fairlie 's company , but he could have know little of he at that or at any other time . Sir Percival 's only intimate friend in the Fairlie family have be Laura 's father . 

 these be all the particular that I could gain from Marian . they suggest nothing which be useful to my present purpose , but I note they down carefully , in the event of their proving to be of importance at any future period . 

 Mrs. Todd 's reply   address , by our own wish , to a post - office at some distance from we   have arrive at its destination when I go to apply for it . the chance , which have be all against we hitherto , turn from this moment in our favour . Mrs. Todd 's letter contain the first item of information of which we be in search . 

 Mrs. Clements , it appear , have   as we have conjecture   write to Todd 's Corner , ask pardon in the first place for the abrupt manner in which she and Anne have leave their friend at the farm - house   on the morning after I have meet the woman in white in Limmeridge churchyard , and then inform Mrs. Todd of Anne 's disappearance , and entreat that she would cause inquiry to be make in the neighbourhood , on the chance that the lost woman might have stray back to Limmeridge . in make this request , Mrs. Clements have be careful to add to it the address at which she might always be hear of , and that address Mrs. Todd now transmit to Marian . it be in London , and within half an hour 's walk of our own lodging . 

 in the word of the proverb , I be resolve not to let the grass grow under my foot . the next morning I set forth to seek an interview with Mrs. Clements . this be my first step forward in the investigation . the story of the desperate attempt to which I now stand committed begin here . 



 VI 

 the address communicate by Mrs. Todd take I to a lodging - house situate in a respectable street near the Gray 's Inn Road . 

 when I knock the door be open by Mrs. Clements herself . she do not appear to remember I , and ask what my business be . I recall to she our meeting in Limmeridge churchyard at the close of my interview there with the woman in white , take special care to remind she that I be the person who assist Anne Catherick   as Anne have herself declare   to escape the pursuit from the Asylum . this be my only claim to the confidence of Mrs. Clements . she remember the circumstance the moment I speak of it , and ask I into the parlour , in the great anxiety to know if I have bring she any news of Anne . 

 it be impossible for I to tell she the whole truth without , at the same time , enter into particular on the subject of the conspiracy , which it would have be dangerous to confide to a stranger . I could only abstain most carefully from raise any false hope , and then explain that the object of my visit be to discover the person who be really responsible for Anne 's disappearance . I even add , so as to exonerate myself from any after - reproach of my own conscience , that I entertain not the least hope of be able to trace she that I believe we should never see she alive again and that my main interest in the affair be to bring to punishment two man whom I suspect to be concern in lure she away , and at whose hand I and some dear friend of mine have suffer a grievous wrong . with this explanation I leave it to Mrs. Clements to say whether our interest in the matter   whatever difference there might be in the motif which actuate we   be not the same , and whether she feel any reluctance to forward my object by give I such information on the subject of my inquiry as she happen to possess . 

 the poor woman be at first too much confused and agitated to understand thoroughly what I say to she . she could only reply that I be welcome to anything she could tell I in return for the kindness I have show to Anne ; but as she be not very quick and ready , at the good of time , in talk to stranger , she would beg I to put she in the right way , and to say where I wish she to begin . 

 know by experience that the plainest narrative attainable from person who be not accustomed to arrange their idea , be the narrative which go far enough back at the beginning to avoid all impediment of retrospection in its course , I ask Mrs. Clements to tell I first what have happen after she have leave Limmeridge , and so , by watchful questioning , carry she on from point to point , till we reach the period of Anne 's disappearance . 

 the substance of the information which I thus obtain be as follow : 

 on leave the farm at Todd 's Corner , Mrs. Clements and Anne have travel that day as far as Derby , and have remain there a week on Anne 's account . they have then go on to London , and have live in the lodging occupy by Mrs. Clements at that time for a month or more , when circumstance connect with the house and the landlord have oblige they to change their quarter . Anne 's terror of be discover in London or its neighbourhood , whenever they venture to walk out , have gradually communicate itself to Mrs. Clements , and she have determine on remove to one of the most out - of - the - way place in England to the town of Grimsby in Lincolnshire , where her deceased husband have pass all his early life . his relative be respectable people settle in the town they have always treat Mrs. Clements with great kindness , and she think it impossible to do well than go there and take the advice of her husband 's friend . Anne would not hear of return to her mother at Welmingham , because she have be remove to the Asylum from that place , and because Sir Percival would be certain to go back there and find she again . there be serious weight in this objection , and Mrs. Clements feel that it be not to be easily remove . 

 at Grimsby the first serious symptom of illness have show themselves in Anne . they appear soon after the news of Lady Glyde 's marriage have be make public in the newspaper , and have reach she through that medium . 

 the medical man who be send for to attend the sick woman discover at once that she be suffer from a serious affection of the heart . the illness last long , leave she very weak , and return at interval , though with mitigated severity , again and again . they remain at Grimsby , in consequence , during the first half of the new year , and there they might probably have stay much long , but for the sudden resolution which Anne take at this time to venture back to Hampshire , for the purpose of obtain a private interview with Lady Glyde . 

 Mrs. Clements do all in her power to oppose the execution of this hazardous and unaccountable project . no explanation of her motif be offer by Anne , except that she believe the day of her death be not far off , and that she have something on her mind which must be communicate to Lady Glyde , at any risk , in secret . her resolution to accomplish this purpose be so firmly settle that she declare her intention of go to Hampshire by herself if Mrs. Clements feel any unwillingness to go with she . the doctor , on be consult , be of opinion that serious opposition to her wish would , in all probability , produce another and perhaps a fatal fit of illness , and Mrs. Clements , under this advice , yield to necessity , and once more , with sad foreboding of trouble and danger to come , allow Anne Catherick to have her own way . 

 on the journey from London to Hampshire Mrs. Clements discover that one of their fellow - passenger be well acquainted with the neighbourhood of blackwater , and could give she all the information she need on the subject of locality . in this way she find out that the only place they could go to , which be not dangerously near to Sir Percival 's residence , be a large village call Sandon . the distance here from Blackwater Park be between three and four mile and that distance , and back again , Anne have walk on each occasion when she have appear in the neighbourhood of the lake . 

 for the few day during which they be at Sandon without be discover they have live a little away from the village , in the cottage of a decent widow - woman who have a bedroom to let , and whose discreet silence Mrs. Clements have do she good to secure , for the first week at least . she have also try hard to induce Anne to be content with writing to Lady Glyde , in the first instance ; but the failure of the warning contain in the anonymous letter send to Limmeridge have make Anne resolute to speak this time , and obstinate in the determination to go on her errand alone . 

 Mrs. Clements , nevertheless , follow she privately on each occasion when she go to the lake , without , however , venture near enough to the boat - house to be witness of what take place there . when Anne return for the last time from the dangerous neighbourhood , the fatigue of walking , day after day , distance which be far too great for her strength , add to the exhausting effect of the agitation from which she have suffer , produce the result which Mrs. Clements have dread all along . the old pain over the heart and the other symptom of the illness at Grimsby return , and Anne be confine to her bed in the cottage . 

 in this emergency the first necessity , as Mrs. Clements know by experience , be to endeavour to quiet Anne 's anxiety of mind , and for this purpose the good woman go herself the next day to the lake , to try if she could find Lady Glyde   who would be sure , as Anne say , to take her daily walk to the boat - house , and prevail on she to come back privately to the cottage near Sandon . on reach the outskirt of the plantation Mrs. Clements encounter , not Lady Glyde , but a tall , stout , elderly gentleman , with a book in his hand in other word , Count Fosco . 

 the Count , after look at she very attentively for a moment , ask if she expect to see any one in that place , and add , before she could reply , that he be wait there with a message from Lady Glyde , but that he be not quite certain whether the person then before he answer the description of the person with whom he be desire to communicate . 

 upon this Mrs. Clements at once confide her errand to he , and entreat that he would help to allay Anne 's anxiety by trust his message to she . the Count most readily and kindly comply with her request . the message , he say , be a very important one . Lady Glyde entreat Anne and her good friend to return immediately to London , as she feel certain that Sir Percival would discover they if they remain any long in the neighbourhood of Blackwater . she be herself go to London in a short time , and if Mrs. Clements and Anne would go there first , and would let she know what their address be , they should hear from she and see she in a fortnight or less . the Count add that he have already attempt to give a friendly warning to Anne herself , but that she have be too much startle by see that he be a stranger to let he approach and speak to she . 

 to this Mrs. Clements reply , in the great alarm and distress , that she ask nothing well than to take Anne safely to London , but that there be no present hope of remove she from the dangerous neighbourhood , as she lie ill in her bed at that moment . the Count inquire if Mrs. Clements have send for medical advice , and hear that she have hitherto hesitate to do so , from the fear of make their position publicly know in the village , inform she that he be himself a medical man , and that he would go back with she if she please , and see what could be do for Anne . Mrs. Clements   feel a natural confidence in the Count , as a person trust with a secret message from Lady Glyde   gratefully accept the offer , and they go back together to the cottage . 

 Anne be asleep when they get there . the Count start at the sight of she   evidently from astonishment at her resemblance to Lady Glyde . poor Mrs. Clements suppose that he be only shock to see how ill she be . he would not allow she to be awaken he be content with put question to Mrs. Clements about her symptom , with look at she , and with lightly touch her pulse . Sandon be a large enough place to have a grocer 's and druggist 's shop in it , and thither the Count go to write his prescription and to get the medicine make up . he bring it back himself , and tell Mrs. Clements that the medicine be a powerful stimulant , and that it would certainly give Anne strength to get up and bear the fatigue of a journey to London of only a few hour . the remedy be to be administer at state time on that day and on the day after . on the third day she would be well enough to travel , and he arrange to meet Mrs. Clements at the Blackwater station , and to see they off by the midday train . if they do not appear he would assume that Anne be bad , and would proceed at once to the cottage . 

 as event turn out , no such emergency as this occur . 

 this medicine have an extraordinary effect on Anne , and the good result of it be help by the assurance Mrs. Clements could now give she that she would soon see Lady Glyde in London . at the appoint day and time   when they have not be quite so long as a week in Hampshire altogether , they arrive at the station . the Count be wait there for they , and be talk to an elderly lady , who appear to be go to travel by the train to London also . he most kindly assist they , and put they into the carriage himself , beg Mrs. Clements not to forget to send her address to Lady Glyde . the elderly lady do not travel in the same compartment , and they do not notice what become of she on reach the London terminus . Mrs. Clements secure respectable lodging in a quiet neighbourhood , and then write , as she have engage to do , to inform Lady Glyde of the address . 

 a little more than a fortnight pass , and no answer come . 

 at the end of that time a lady   the same elderly lady whom they have see at the station   call in a cab , and say that she come from Lady Glyde , who be then at an hotel in London , and who wish to see Mrs. Clements , for the purpose of arrange a future interview with Anne . Mrs. Clements express her willingness   Anne be present at the time , and entreat she to do so   to forward the object in view , especially as she be not require to be away from the house for more than half an hour at the most . she and the elderly lady   clearly Madame Fosco   then leave in the cab . the lady stop the cab , after it have drive some distance , at a shop before they get to the hotel , and beg Mrs. Clements to wait for she for a few minute while she make a purchase that have be forget . she never appear again . 

 after wait some time Mrs. Clements become alarmed , and order the cabman to drive back to her lodging . when she get there , after an absence of rather more than half an hour , Anne be go . 

 the only information to be obtain from the people of the house be derive from the servant who wait on the lodger . she have open the door to a boy from the street , who have leave a letter for " the young woman who live on the second floor "   the part of the house which Mrs. Clements occupy . the servant have deliver the letter , have then go downstairs , and five minute afterwards have observe Anne open the front door and go out , dress in her bonnet and shawl . she have probably take the letter with she , for it be not to be find , and it be therefore impossible to tell what inducement have be offer to make she leave the house . it must have be a strong one , for she would never stir out alone in London of her own accord . if Mrs. Clements have not know this by experience nothing would have induce she to go away in the cab , even for so short a time as half an hour only . 

 as soon as she could collect her thought , the first idea that naturally occur to Mrs. Clements be to go and make inquiry at the Asylum , to which she dread that Anne have be take back . 

 she go there the next day , have be inform of the locality in which the house be situate by Anne herself . the answer she receive   her application have in all probability be make a day or two before the false Anne Catherick have really be consign to safe keeping in the Asylum   be , that no such person have be bring back there . she have then write to Mrs. Catherick at Welmingham to know if she have see or hear anything of her daughter , and have receive an answer in the negative . after that reply have reach she , she be at the end of her resource , and perfectly ignorant where else to inquire or what else to do . from that time to this she have remain in total ignorance of the cause of Anne 's disappearance and of the end of Anne 's story . 



 VII 

 thus far the information which I have receive from Mrs. Clements though it establish fact of which I have not previously be aware be of a preliminary character only . 

 it be clear that the series of deception which have remove Anne Catherick to London , and separate she from Mrs. Clements , have be accomplish solely by Count Fosco and the Countess , and the question whether any part of the conduct of husband or wife have be of a kind to place either of they within reach of the law might be well worthy of future consideration . but the purpose I have now in view lead I in another direction than this . the immediate object of my visit to Mrs. Clements be to make some approach at least to the discovery of Sir Percival 's secret , and she have say nothing as yet which advance I on my way to that important end . I feel the necessity of try to awaken her recollection of other time , person , and event than those on which her memory have hitherto be employ , and when I next speak I speak with that object indirectly in view . 

 " I wish I could be of any help to you in this sad calamity , " I say . " all I can do be to feel heartily for your distress . if Anne have be your own child , Mrs. Clements , you could have show she no truer kindness you could have make no readier sacrifice for her sake . " 

 " there be no great merit in that , sir , " say Mrs. Clements simply . " the poor thing be as good as my own child to I . I nurse she from a baby , sir , bring she up by hand and a hard job it be to rear she . it would not   go to my heart so to lose she if I have not   make her first short clothe and teach she to walk . I always say she be send to console I for never have chick or child of my own . and now she be lose the old time keep come back to my mind , and even at my age I can not   help cry about she I can not   indeed , sir ! " 

 I wait a little to give Mrs. Clements time to compose herself . be the light that I have be look for so long glimmering on I far off , as yet in the good woman 's recollection of Anne 's early life ? 

 " do you know Mrs. Catherick before Anne be bear ? " I ask . 

 " not very long , sir not above four month . we see a great deal of each other in that time , but we be never very friendly together . " 

 her voice be steady as she make that reply . painful as many of her recollection might be , I observe that it be unconsciously a relief to her mind to revert to the dimly - see trouble of the past , after dwell so long on the vivid sorrow of the present . 

 " be you and Mrs. Catherick neighbour ? " I inquire , lead her memory on as encouragingly as I could . 

 " yes , sir neighbour at Old Welmingham . " 

 " OLD Welmingham ? there be two place of that name , then , in Hampshire ? " 

 " well , sir , there use to be in those day well than three - and - twenty year ago . they build a new town about two mile off , convenient to the river and Old Welmingham , which be never much more than a village , get in time to be desert . the new town be the place they call Welmingham now but the old parish church be the parish church still . it stand by itself , with the house pull down or go to ruin all round it . I have   live to see sad change . it be a pleasant , pretty place in my time . " 

 " do you live there before your marriage , Mrs. Clements ? " 

 " no , sir I be a Norfolk woman . it be not   the place my husband belong to either . he be from Grimsby , as I tell you , and he serve his apprenticeship there . but have friend down south , and hearing of an opening , he get into business at Southampton . it be in a small way , but he make enough for a plain man to retire on , and settle at Old Welmingham . I go there with he when he marry I . we be neither of we young , but we live very happy together happy than our neighbour , Mr. Catherick , live along with his wife when they come to Old Welmingham a year or two afterwards . " 

 " be your husband acquaint with they before that ? " 

 " with Catherick , sir not with his wife . she be a stranger to both of we . some gentleman have make interest for Catherick , and he get the situation of clerk at Welmingham church , which be the reason of his come to settle in our neighbourhood . he bring his newly - marry wife along with he , and we hear in course of time she have be lady's - maid in a family that live at Varneck Hall , near Southampton . Catherick have find it a hard matter to get she to marry he , in consequence of she hold herself uncommonly high . he have ask and ask , and give the thing up at last , see she be so contrary about it . when he have give it up she turn contrary just the other way , and come to he of her own accord , without rhyme or reason seemingly . my poor husband always say that be the time to have give she a lesson . but Catherick be too fond of she to do anything of the sort he never check she either before they be married or after . he be a quick man in his feeling , let they carry he a deal too far , now in one way and now in another , and he would have spoil a well wife than Mrs. Catherick if a well have marry he . I do not   like to speak ill of any one , sir , but she be a heartless woman , with a terrible will of her own fond of foolish admiration and fine clothe , and not care to show so much as decent outward respect to Catherick , kindly as he always treat she . my husband say he think thing would turn out badly when they first come to live near we , and his word prove true . before they have be quite four month in our neighbourhood there be a dreadful scandal and a miserable break - up in their household . both of they be in fault I be afraid both of they be equally in fault . " 

 " you mean both husband and wife ? " 

 " oh , no , sir ! I do not   mean Catherick he be only to be pity . I mean his wife and the person " 

 " and the person who cause the scandal ? " 

 " yes , sir . a gentleman bear and bring up , who ought to have set a well example . you know he , sir and my poor dear Anne know he only too well . " 

 " Sir Percival Glyde ? " 

 " yes , Sir Percival Glyde . " 

 my heart beat fast I think I have my hand on the clue . how little I know then of the winding of the labyrinth which be still to mislead I ! 

 " do Sir Percival live in your neighbourhood at that time ? " I ask . 

 " no , sir . he come among we as a stranger . his father have die not long before in foreign part . I remember he be in mourning . he put up at the little inn on the river   they have pull it down since that time , where gentleman use to go to fish . he be not   much notice when he first come it be a common thing enough for gentleman to travel from all part of England to fish in our river . " 

 " do he make his appearance in the village before Anne be bear ? " 

 " yes , sir . Anne be bear in the June month of eighteen hundred and twenty - seven and I think he come at the end of April or the beginning of May . " 

 " come as a stranger to all of you ? a stranger to Mrs. Catherick as well as to the rest of the neighbour ? " 

 " so we think at first , sir . but when the scandal break out , nobody believe they be stranger . I remember how it happen as well as if it be yesterday . Catherick come into our garden one night , and wake we by throw up a handful of gravel from the walk at our window . I hear he beg my husband , for the Lord 's sake , to come down and speak to he . they be a long time together talk in the porch . when my husband come back upstairs he be all of a tremble . he sit down on the side of the bed and he say to I , ' Lizzie ! I always tell you that woman be a bad one I always say she would end ill , and I be afraid in my own mind that the end have come already . Catherick have find a lot of lace handkerchief , and two fine ring , and a new gold watch and chain , hide away in his wife 's drawer thing that nobody but a bear lady ought ever to have and his wife will not   say how she come by they . ' ' do he think she steal they ? ' say I. ' no , ' say he , ' stealing would be bad enough . but it be bad than that , she be have no chance of steal such thing as those , and she be not a woman to take they if she have . they be gift , Lizzie there be her own initial engrave inside the watch and Catherick have see she talk privately , and carry on as no married woman should , with that gentleman in mourning , Sir Percival Glyde . do not   you say anything about it I have   quiet Catherick for to - night . I have   tell he to keep his tongue to himself , and his eye and his ear open , and to wait a day or two , till he can be quite certain . ' ' I believe you be both of you wrong , ' say I. ' it be not in nature , comfortable and respectable as she be here , that Mrs. Catherick should take up with a chance strange like Sir Percival Glyde . ' ' ay , but be he a stranger to she ? ' say my husband . ' you forget how Catherick 's wife come to marry he . she go to he of her own accord , after say no over and over again when he ask she . there have be wicked woman before her time , Lizzie , who have use honest man who love they as a means of save their character , and I be sorely afraid this Mrs. Catherick be as wicked as the bad of they . we shall see , ' say my husband , ' we shall soon see . ' and only two day afterwards we do see . " 

 Mrs. Clements wait for a moment before she go on . even in that moment , I begin to doubt whether the clue that I think I have find be really lead I to the central mystery of the labyrinth after all . be this common , too common , story of a man 's treachery and a woman 's frailty the key to a secret which have be the lifelong terror of Sir Percival Glyde ? 

 " well , sir , Catherick take my husband 's advice and wait , " Mrs. Clements continue . " and as I tell you , he have not   long to wait . on the second day he find his wife and Sir Percival whisper together quite familiar , close under the vestry of the church . I suppose they think the neighbourhood of the vestry be the last place in the world where anybody would think of look after they , but , however that may be , there they be . Sir Percival , be seemingly surprised and confound , defend himself in such a guilty way that poor Catherick   whose quick temper I have tell you of already   fall into a kind of frenzy at his own disgrace , and strike Sir Percival . he be no match   and I be sorry to say it   for the man who have wrong he , and he be beat in the cruel manner , before the neighbour , who have come to the place on hear the disturbance , could run in to part they . all this happen towards evening , and before nightfall , when my husband go to Catherick 's house , he be go , nobody know where . no live soul in the village ever see he again . he know too well , by that time , what his wife 's vile reason have be for marry he , and he feel his misery and disgrace , especially after what have happen to he with Sir Percival , too keenly . the clergyman of the parish put an advertisement in the paper beg he to come back , and say that he should not lose his situation or his friend . but Catherick have too much pride and spirit , as some people say too much feeling , as I think , sir to face his neighbour again , and try to live down the memory of his disgrace . my husband hear from he when he have leave England , and hear a second time , when he be settle and do well in America . he be alive there now , as far as I know , but none of we in the old country his wicked wife least of all be ever likely to set eye on he again . " 

 " what become of Sir Percival ? " I inquire . " do he stay in the neighbourhood ? " 

 " not he , sir . the place be too hot to hold he . he be hear at high word with Mrs. Catherick the same night when the scandal break out , and the next morning he take himself off . " 

 " and Mrs. Catherick ? surely she never remain in the village among the people who know of her disgrace ? " 

 " she do , sir . she be hard enough and heartless enough to set the opinion of all her neighbour at flat defiance . she declare to everybody , from the clergyman downward , that she be the victim of a dreadful mistake , and that all the scandal - monger in the place should not drive she out of it , as if she be a guilty woman . all through my time she live at Old Welmingham , and after my time , when the new town be build , and the respectable neighbour begin move to it , she move too , as if she be determined to live among they and scandalise they to the very last . there she be now , and there she will stop , in defiance of the good of they , to her die day . " 

 " but how have she live through all these year ? " I ask . " be her husband able and willing to help she ? " 

 " both able and willing , sir , " say Mrs. Clements . " in the second letter he write to my good man , he say she have bear his name , and live in his home , and , wicked as she be , she must not starve like a beggar in the street . he could afford to make she some small allowance , and she might draw for it quarterly at a place in London . " 

 " do she accept the allowance ? " 

 " not a farthing of it , sir . she say she would never be beholden to Catherick for bit or drop , if she live to be a hundred . and she have keep her word ever since . when my poor dear husband die , and leave all to I , Catherick 's letter be put in my possession with the other thing , and I tell she to let I know if she be ever in want . ' I will let all England know I be in want , ' she say , ' before I tell Catherick , or any friend of Catherick 's . take that for your answer , and give it to HIM for an answer , if he ever write again . ' " 

 " do you suppose that she have money of her own ? " 

 " very little , if any , sir . it be say , and say truly , I be afraid , that her mean of living come privately from Sir Percival Glyde . " 


 after that last reply I wait a little , to reconsider what I have hear . if I unreservedly accept the story so far , it be now plain that no approach , direct or indirect , to the Secret have yet be reveal to I , and that the pursuit of my object have end again in leave I face to face with the most palpable and the most disheartening failure . 

 but there be one point in the narrative which make I doubt the propriety of accept it unreservedly , and which suggest the idea of something hide below the surface . 

 I could not account to myself for the circumstance of the clerk 's guilty wife voluntarily live out all her after - existence on the scene of her disgrace . the woman 's own report statement that she have take this strange course as a practical assertion of her innocence do not satisfy I . it seem , to my mind , more natural and more probable to assume that she be not so completely a free agent in this matter as she have herself assert . in that case , who be the likeli person to possess the power of compel she to remain at Welmingham ? the person unquestionably from whom she derive the mean of living . she have refuse assistance from her husband , she have no adequate resource of her own , she be a friendless , degraded woman from what source should she derive help but from the source at which report point Sir Percival Glyde ? 

 reasoning on these assumption , and always bear in mind the one certain fact to guide I , that Mrs. Catherick be in possession of the Secret , I easily understand that it be Sir Percival 's interest to keep she at Welmingham , because her character in that place be certain to isolate she from all communication with female neighbour , and to allow she no opportunity of talk incautiously in moment of free intercourse with inquisitive bosom friend . but what be the mystery to be conceal ? not Sir Percival 's infamous connection with Mrs. Catherick 's disgrace , for the neighbour be the very people who know of it not the suspicion that he be Anne 's father , for Welmingham be the place in which that suspicion must inevitably exist . if I accept the guilty appearance describe to I as unreservedly as other have accept they , if I draw from they the same superficial conclusion which Mr. Catherick and all his neighbour have draw , where be the suggestion , in all that I have hear , of a dangerous secret between Sir Percival and Mrs. Catherick , which have be keep hide from that time to this ? 

 and yet , in those steal meeting , in those familiar whispering between the clerk 's wife and " the gentleman in mourning , " the clue to discovery exist beyond a doubt . 

 be it possible that appearance in this case have point one way while the truth lie all the while unsuspected in another direction ? could Mrs. Catherick 's assertion , that she be the victim of a dreadful mistake , by any possibility be true ? or , assume it to be false , could the conclusion which associate Sir Percival with her guilt have be found in some inconceivable error ? have Sir Percival , by any chance , court the suspicion that be wrong for the sake of divert from himself some other suspicion that be right ? here if I could find it here be the approach to the secret , hide deep under the surface of the apparently unpromising story which I have just hear . 


 my next question be now direct to the one object of ascertain whether Mr. Catherick have or have not arrive truly at the conviction of his wife 's misconduct . the answer I receive from Mrs. Clements leave I in no doubt whatever on that point . Mrs. Catherick have , on the clear evidence , compromise her reputation , while a single woman , with some person unknown , and have marry to save her character . it have be positively ascertain , by calculation of time and place into which I need not enter particularly , that the daughter who bear her husband 's name be not her husband 's child . 

 the next object of inquiry , whether it be equally certain that Sir Percival must have be the father of Anne , be beset by far great difficulty . I be in no position to try the probability on one side or on the other in this instance by any well test than the test of personal resemblance . 

 " I suppose you often see Sir Percival when he be in your village ? " I say . 

 " yes , sir , very often , " reply Mrs. Clements . 

 " do you ever observe that Anne be like he ? " 

 " she be not at all like he , sir . " 

 " be she like her mother , then ? " 

 " not like her mother either , sir . Mrs. Catherick be dark , and full in the face . " 

 not like her mother and not like she   suppose   father . I know that the test by personal resemblance be not to be implicitly trust , but , on the other hand , it be not to be altogether reject on that account . be it possible to strengthen the evidence by discover any conclusive fact in relation to the life of Mrs. Catherick and Sir Percival before they either of they appear at Old Welmingham ? when I ask my next question I put they with this view . 

 " when Sir Percival first arrive in your neighbourhood , " I say , " do you hear where he have come from last ? " 

 " no , sir . some say from Blackwater Park , and some say from Scotland but nobody know . " 

 " be Mrs. Catherick live in service at Varneck Hall immediately before her marriage ? " 

 " yes , sir . " 

 " and have she be long in her place ? " 

 " three or four year , sir ; I be not quite certain which . " 

 " do you ever hear the name of the gentleman to whom Varneck Hall belong at that time ? " 

 " yes , sir . his name be Major Donthorne . " 

 " do Mr. Catherick , or do any one else you know , ever hear that Sir Percival be a friend of Major Donthorne 's , or ever see Sir Percival in the neighbourhood of Varneck Hall ? " 

 " Catherick never do , sir , that I can remember nor any one else either , that I know of . " 

 I note down Major Donthorne 's name and address , on the chance that he might still be alive , and that it might be useful at some future time to apply to he . meanwhile , the impression on my mind be now decidedly adverse to the opinion that Sir Percival be Anne 's father , and decidedly favourable to the conclusion that the secret of his steal interview with Mrs. Catherick be entirely unconnected with the disgrace which the woman have inflict on her husband 's good name . I could think of no further inquiry which I might make to strengthen this impression I could only encourage Mrs. Clements to speak next of Anne 's early day , and watch for any chance - suggestion which might in this way offer itself to I . 

 " I have not hear yet , " I say , " how the poor child , bear in all this sin and misery , come to be trust , Mrs. Clements , to your care . " 

 " there be nobody else , sir , to take the little helpless creature in hand , " reply Mrs. Clements . " the wicked mother seem to hate it as if the poor baby be in fault ! from the day it be bear . my heart be heavy for the child , and I make the offer to bring it up as tenderly as if it be my own . " 

 " do Anne remain entirely under your care from that time ? " 

 " not quite entirely , sir . Mrs. Catherick have her whim and fancy about it at time , and use now and then to lay claim to the child , as if she want to spite I for bring it up . but these fit of hers never last for long . poor little Anne be always return to I , and be always glad to get back though she lead but a gloomy life in my house , have no playmate , like other child , to brighten she up . our long separation be when her mother take she to Limmeridge . just at that time I lose my husband , and I feel it be as well , in that miserable affliction , that Anne should not be in the house . she be between ten and eleven year old then , slow at her lesson , poor soul , and not so cheerful as other child but as pretty a little girl to look at as you would wish to see . I wait at home till her mother bring she back , and then I make the offer to take she with I to London the truth be , sir , that I could not find it in my heart to stop at Old Welmingham after my husband 's death , the place be so changed and so dismal to I . " 

 " and do Mrs. Catherick consent to your proposal ? " 

 " no , sir . she come back from the north hard and bitterer than ever . folk do say that she have be oblige to ask Sir Percival 's leave to go , to begin with ; and that she only go to nurse her die sister at Limmeridge because the poor woman be report to have save money the truth be that she hardly leave enough to bury she . these thing may have sour Mrs. Catherick likely enough , but however that may be , she would not   hear of my take the child away . she seem to like distress we both by part we . all I could do be to give Anne my direction , and to tell she privately , if she be ever in trouble , to come to I . but year pass before she be free to come . I never see she again , poor soul , till the night she escape from the mad - house . " 

 " you know , Mrs. Clements , why Sir Percival Glyde shut she up ? " 

 " I only know what Anne herself tell I , sir . the poor thing use to ramble and wander about it sadly . she say her mother have get some secret of Sir Percival 's to keep , and have let it out to she long after I leave Hampshire and when Sir Percival find she know it , he shut she up . but she never could say what it be when I ask she . all she could tell I be , that her mother might be the ruin and destruction of Sir Percival if she choose . Mrs. Catherick may have let out just as much as that , and no more . I be next to certain I should have hear the whole truth from Anne , if she have really know it as she pretend to do , and as she very likely fancy she do , poor soul . " 

 this idea have more than once occur to my own mind . I have already tell Marian that I doubt whether Laura be really on the point of make any important discovery when she and Anne Catherick be disturb by Count Fosco at the boat - house . it be perfectly in character with Anne 's mental affliction that she should assume an absolute knowledge of the secret on no well ground than vague suspicion , derive from hint which her mother have incautiously let drop in her presence . Sir Percival 's guilty distrust would , in that case , infallibly inspire he with the false idea that Anne know all from her mother , just as it have afterwards fix in his mind the equally false suspicion that his wife know all from Anne . 

 the time be pass , the morning be wear away . it be doubtful , if I stay long , whether I should hear anything more from Mrs. Clements that would be at all useful to my purpose . I have already discover those local and family particular , in relation to Mrs. Catherick , of which I have be in search , and I have arrive at certain conclusion , entirely new to I , which might immensely assist in direct the course of my future proceeding . I rise to take my leave , and to thank Mrs. Clements for the friendly readiness she have show in afford I information . 

 " I be afraid you must have think I very inquisitive , " I say . " I have trouble you with more question than many people would have care to answer . " 

 " you be heartily welcome , sir , to anything I can tell you , " answer Mrs. Clements . she stop and look at I wistfully . " but I do wish , " say the poor woman , " you could have tell I a little more about Anne , sir . I think I see something in your face when you come in which look as if you could . you can not   think how hard it be not even to know whether she be live or dead . I could bear it well if I be only certain . you say you never expect we should see she alive again . do you know , sir do you know for truth that it have please God to take she ? " 

 I be not proof against this appeal , it would have be unspeakably mean and cruel of I if I have resist it . 

 " I be afraid there be no doubt of the truth , " I answer gently ; " I have the certainty in my own mind that her trouble in this world be over . " 

 the poor woman drop into her chair and hide her face from I . " oh , sir , " she say , " how do you know it ? who can have tell you ? " 

 " no one have tell I , Mrs. Clements . but I have reason for feel sure of it reason which I promise you shall know as soon as I can safely explain they . I be certain she be not neglect in her last moment I be certain the heart complaint from which she suffer so sadly be the true cause of her death . you shall feel as sure of this as I do , soon you shall know , before long , that she be bury in a quiet country churchyard in a pretty , peaceful place , which you might have choose for her yourself . " 

 " dead ! " say Mrs. Clements , " dead so young , and I be leave to hear it ! I make her first short frock . I teach she to walk . the first time she ever say Mother she say it to I and now I be leave and Anne be take ! do you say , sir , " say the poor woman , remove the handkerchief from her face , and look up at I for the first time , " do you say that she have be nicely bury ? be it the sort of funeral she might have have if she have really be my own child ? " 

 I assure she that it be . she seem to take an inexplicable pride in my answer to find a comfort in it which no other and high consideration could afford . " it would have break my heart , " she say simply , " if Anne have not be nicely bury but how do you know it , sir ? who tell you ? " I once more entreat she to wait until I could speak to she unreservedly . " you be sure to see I again , " I say , " for I have a favour to ask when you be a little more composed perhaps in a day or two . " 

 " do not   keep it wait , sir , on my account , " say Mrs. Clements . " never mind my cry if I can be of use . if you have anything on your mind to say to I , sir , please to say it now . " 

 " I only wish to ask you one last question , " I say . " I only want to know Mrs. Catherick 's address at Welmingham . " 

 my request so startle Mrs. Clements , that , for the moment , even the tiding of Anne 's death seem to be drive from her mind . her tear suddenly cease to flow , and she sit look at I in blank amazement . 

 " for the Lord 's sake , sir ! " she say , " what do you want with Mrs. Catherick ! " 

 " I want this , Mrs. Clements , " I reply , " I want to know the secret of those private meeting of hers with Sir Percival Glyde . there be something more in what you have tell I of that woman 's past conduct , and of that man 's past relation with she , than you or any of your neighbour ever suspect . there be a secret we none of we know between those two , and I be go to Mrs. Catherick with the resolution to find it out . " 

 " think twice about it , sir ! " say Mrs. Clements , rise in her earnestness and lay her hand on my arm . " she be an awful woman you do not   know she as I do . think twice about it . " 

 " I be sure your warning be kindly mean , Mrs. Clements . but I be determined to see the woman , whatever come of it . " 

 Mrs. Clements look I anxiously in the face . 

 " I see your mind be make up , sir , " she say . " I will give you the address . " 

 I write it down in my pocket - book and then take her hand to say farewell . 

 " you shall hear from I soon , " I say ; " you shall know all that I have promise to tell you . " 

 Mrs. Clements sigh and shake her head doubtfully . 

 " an old woman 's advice be sometimes worth take , sir , " she say . " think twice before you go to Welmingham . " 



 VIII 

 when I reach home again after my interview with Mrs. Clements , I be strike by the appearance of a change in Laura . 

 the unvarying gentleness and patience which long misfortune have try so cruelly and have never conquer yet , seem now to have suddenly fail she . insensible to all Marian 's attempt to soothe and amuse she , she sit , with her neglect drawing push away on the table , her eye resolutely cast down , her finger twin and untwine themselves restlessly in her lap . Marian rise when I come in , with a silent distress in her face , wait for a moment to see if Laura would look up at my approach , whisper to I , " try if you can rouse she , " and leave the room . 

 I sit down in the vacant chair gently unclasp the poor , worn , restless finger , and take both her hand in mine . 

 " what be you think of , Laura ? tell I , my darling try and tell I what it be . " 

 she struggle with herself , and raise her eye to mine . " I can not   feel happy , " she say , " I can not   help think " she stop , bent forward a little , and lay her head on my shoulder , with a terrible mute helplessness that strike I to the heart . 

 " try to tell I , " I repeat gently ; " try to tell I why you be not happy . " 

 " I be so useless I be such a burden on both of you , " she answer , with a weary , hopeless sigh . " you work and get money , Walter , and Marian help you . why be there nothing I can do ? you will end in like marian well than you like I you will , because I be so helpless ! oh , do not , do not , do not   treat I like a child ! " 

 I raise her head , and smooth away the tangled hair that fall over her face , and kiss she my poor , faded flower ! my lost , afflict sister ! " you shall help we , Laura , " I say , " you shall begin , my darling , to - day . " 

 she look at I with a feverish eagerness , with a breathless interest , that make I tremble for the new life of hope which I have call into be by those few word . 

 I rise , and set her drawing material in order , and place they near she again . 

 " you know that I work and get money by draw , " I say . " now you have take such pain , now you be so much improve , you shall begin to work and get money too . try to finish this little sketch as nicely and prettily as you can . when it be do I will take it away with I , and the same person will buy it who buy all that I do . you shall keep your own earning in your own purse , and Marian shall come to you to help we , as often as she come to I . think how useful you be go to make yourself to both of we , and you will soon be as happy , Laura , as the day be long . " 

 her face grow eager , and brighten into a smile . in the moment while it last , in the moment when she again take up the pencil that have be lay aside , she almost look like the Laura of past day . 

 I have rightly interpret the first sign of a new growth and strength in her mind , unconsciously express themselves in the notice she have take of the occupation which fill her sister 's life and mine . marian   when I tell she what have pass   saw , as I see , that she be long to assume her own little position of importance , to raise herself in her own estimation and in ours and , from that day , we tenderly help the new ambition which give promise of the hopeful , happy future , that might now not be far off . her drawing , as she finish they , or try to finish they , be place in my hand . Marian take they from I and hide they carefully , and I set aside a little weekly tribute from my earning , to be offer to she as the price pay by stranger for the poor , faint , valueless sketch , of which I be the only purchaser . it be hard sometimes to maintain our innocent deception , when she proudly bring out her purse to contribute her share towards the expense , and wonder with serious interest , whether I or she have earn the most that week . I have all those hide drawing in my possession still they be my treasure beyond price the dear remembrance that I love to keep alive the friend in past adversity that my heart will never part from , my tenderness never forget . 

 be I trifle , here , with the necessity of my task ? be I look forward to the happy time which my narrative have not yet reach ? yes . back again back to the day of doubt and dread , when the spirit within I struggle hard for its life , in the icy stillness of perpetual suspense . I have pause and rest for a while on my forward course . it be not , perhaps , time waste , if the friend who read these page have pause and rest too . 


 I take the first opportunity I could find of speak to Marian in private , and of communicate to she the result of the inquiry which I have make that morning . she seem to share the opinion on the subject of my propose journey to Welmingham , which Mrs. Clements have already express to I . 

 " surely , Walter , " she say , " you hardly know enough yet to give you any hope of claim Mrs. Catherick 's confidence ? be it wise to proceed to these extremity , before you have really exhaust all safe and simple mean of attain your object ? when you tell I that Sir Percival and the Count be the only two people in existence who know the exact date of Laura 's journey , you forget , and I forget , that there be a third person who must surely know it I mean Mrs. Rubelle . would it not be far easy , and far less dangerous , to insist on a confession from she , than to force it from Sir Percival ? " 

 " it might be easy , " I reply , " but we be not aware of the full extent of Mrs. Rubelle 's connivance and interest in the conspiracy , and we be therefore not certain that the date have be impress on her mind , as it have be assuredly impressed on the mind of Sir Percival and the Count . it be too late , now , to waste the time on Mrs. Rubelle , which may be all - important to the discovery of the one assailable point in Sir Percival 's life . be you think a little too seriously , Marian , of the risk I may run in return to Hampshire ? be you begin to doubt whether Sir Percival Glyde may not in the end be more than a match for I ? " 

 " he will not be more than your match , " she reply decidedly , " because he will not be help in resist you by the impenetrable wickedness of the Count . " 

 " what have lead you to that conclusion ? " I reply , in some surprise . 

 " my own knowledge of Sir Percival 's obstinacy and impatience of the Count 's control , " she answer . " I believe he will insist on meet you single - handed just as he insist at first on act for himself at Blackwater Park . the time for suspect the Count 's interference will be the time when you have Sir Percival at your mercy . his own interest will then be directly threaten , and he will act , Walter , to terrible purpose in his own defence . " 

 " we may deprive he of his weapon beforehand , " I say . " some of the particular I have hear from Mrs. Clements may yet be turn to account against he , and other mean of strengthen the case may be at our disposal . there be passage in Mrs. Michelson 's narrative which show that the Count find it necessary to place himself in communication with Mr. Fairlie , and there may be circumstance which compromise he in that proceeding . while I be away , Marian , write to Mr. Fairlie and say that you want an answer describe exactly what pass between the Count and himself , and inform you also of any particular that may have come to his knowledge at the same time in connection with his niece . tell he that the statement you request will , soon or later , be insist on , if he show any reluctance to furnish you with it of his own accord . " 

 " the letter shall be write , Walter . but be you really determined to go to Welmingham ? " 

 " absolutely determined . I will devote the next two day to earn what we want for the week to come , and on the third day I go to Hampshire . " 

 when the third day come I be ready for my journey . 

 as it be possible that I might be absent for some little time , I arrange with Marian that we be to correspond every day of course address each other by assume name , for caution 's sake . as long as I hear from she regularly , I should assume that nothing be wrong . but if the morning come and bring I no letter , my return to London would take place , as a matter of course , by the first train . I contrive to reconcile Laura to my departure by tell she that I be go to the country to find new purchaser for her drawing and for mine , and I leave she occupy and happy . Marian follow I downstairs to the street door . 

 " remember what anxious heart you leave here , " she whisper , as we stand together in the passage . " remember all the hope that hang on your safe return . if strange thing happen to you on this journey if you and Sir Percival meet " 

 " what make you think we shall meet ? " I ask . 

 " I do not   know I have fear and fancy that I can not account for . Laugh at they , Walter , if you like but , for God 's sake , keep your temper if you come in contact with that man ! " 

 " never fear , Marian ! I answer for my self - control . " 

 with those word we part . 

 I walk briskly to the station . there be a glow of hope in I . there be a grow conviction in my mind that my journey this time would not be take in vain . it be a fine , clear , cold morning . my nerve be firmly string , and I feel all the strength of my resolution stir in I vigorously from head to foot . 

 as I cross the railway platform , and look right and leave among the people congregate on it , to search for any face among they that I know , the doubt occur to I whether it might not have be to my advantage if I have adopt a disguise before set out for Hampshire . but there be something so repellent to I in the idea something so meanly like the common herd of spy and informer in the mere act of adopt a disguise that I dismiss the question from consideration almost as soon as it have rise in my mind . even as a mere matter of expediency the proceeding be doubtful in the extreme . if I try the experiment at home the landlord of the house would soon or later discover I , and would have his suspicion arouse immediately . if I try it away from home the same person might see I , by the commonest accident , with the disguise and without it , and I should in that way be invite the notice and distrust which it be my most pressing interest to avoid . in my own character I have act thus far and in my own character I be resolve to continue to the end . 

 the train leave I at Welmingham early in the afternoon . 


 be there any wilderness of sand in the desert of Arabia , be there any prospect of desolation among the ruin of Palestine , which can rival the repel effect on the eye , and the depressing influence on the mind , of an english country town in the first stage of its existence , and in the transition state of its prosperity ?
