I ask myself that question as I pass through the clean desolation , the neat ugliness , the prim torpor of the street of Welmingham . and the tradesman who star after I from their lonely shop the tree that droop helpless in their arid exile of unfinished crescent and square the dead house - carcass that wait in vain for the vivifying human element to animate they with the breath of life every creature that I see , every object that I pass , seem to answer with one accord : the desert of Arabia be innocent of our civilised desolation the ruin of Palestine be incapable of our modern gloom ! 

 I inquire my way to the quarter of the town in which Mrs. Catherick live , and on reach it find myself in a square of small house , one story high . there be a bare little plot of grass in the middle , protect by a cheap wire fence . an elderly nursemaid and two child be stand in a corner of the enclosure , look at a lean goat tethered to the grass . two foot - passenger be talk together on one side of the pavement before the house , and an idle little boy be lead an idle little dog along by a string on the other . I hear the dull tinkling of a piano at a distance , accompany by the intermittent knocking of a hammer nearer at hand . these be all the sight and sound of life that encounter I when I enter the square . 

 I walk at once to the door of Number Thirteen the number of Mrs. Catherick 's house and knock , without wait to consider beforehand how I might well present myself when I get in . the first necessity be to see Mrs. Catherick . I could then judge , from my own observation , of the safe and easy manner of approach the object of my visit . 

 the door be open by a melancholy middle - aged woman servant . I give she my card , and ask if I could see Mrs. Catherick . the card be take into the front parlour , and the servant return with a message request I to mention what my business be . 

 " say , if you please , that my business relate to Mrs. Catherick 's daughter , " I reply . this be the good pretext I could think of , on the spur of the moment , to account for my visit . 

 the servant again retire to the parlour , again return , and this time beg I , with a look of gloomy amazement , to walk in . 

 I enter a little room , with a flaring paper of the large pattern on the wall . chair , table , cheffonier , and sofa , all gleam with the glutinous brightness of cheap upholstery . on the large table , in the middle of the room , stand a smart Bible , place exactly in the centre on a red and yellow woollen mat and at the side of the table near to the window , with a little knitting - basket on her lap , and a wheezing , blear - eyed old spaniel crouch at her foot , there sit an elderly woman , wear a black net cap and a black silk gown , and have slate - colour mitten on her hand . her iron - grey hair hang in heavy band on either side of her face her dark eye look straight forward , with a hard , defiant , implacable stare . she have full square cheek , a long , firm chin , and thick , sensual , colourless lip . her figure be stout and sturdy , and her manner aggressively self - possess . this be Mrs. Catherick . 

 " you have come to speak to I about my daughter , " she say , before I could utter a word on my side . " be so good as to mention what you have to say . " 

 the tone of her voice be as hard , as defiant , as implacable as the expression of her eye . she point to a chair , and look I all over attentively , from head to foot , as I sit down in it . I see that my only chance with this woman be to speak to she in her own tone , and to meet she , at the outset of our interview , on her own ground . 

 " you be aware , " I say , " that your daughter have be lose ? " 

 " I be perfectly aware of it . " 

 " have you feel any apprehension that the misfortune of her loss might be follow by the misfortune of her death ? " 

 " yes . have you come here to tell I she be dead ? " 

 " I have . " 

 " why ? " 

 she put that extraordinary question without the slight change in her voice , her face , or her manner . she could not have appear more perfectly unconcerned if I have tell she of the death of the goat in the enclosure outside . 

 " why ? " I repeat . " do you ask why I come here to tell you of your daughter 's death ? " 

 " yes . what interest have you in I , or in she ? how do you come to know anything about my daughter ? " 

 " in this way . I meet she on the night when she escape from the Asylum , and I assist she in reach a place of safety . " 

 " you do very wrong . " 

 " I be sorry to hear her mother say so . " 

 " her mother do say so . how do you know she be dead ? " 

 " I be not at liberty to say how I know it but I do know it . " 

 " be you at liberty to say how you find out my address ? " 

 " certainly . I get your address from Mrs. Clements . " 

 " Mrs. Clements be a foolish woman . do she tell you to come here ? " 

 " she do not . " 

 " then , I ask you again , why do you come ? " 

 as she be determined to have her answer , I give it to she in the plain possible form . 

 " I come , " I say , " because I think Anne Catherick 's mother might have some natural interest in know whether she be alive or dead . " 

 " just so , " say Mrs. Catherick , with additional self - possession . " have you no other motive ? " 

 I hesitate . the right answer to that question be not easy to find at a moment 's notice . 

 " if you have no other motive , " she go on , deliberately take off her slate - colour mitten , and roll they up , " I have only to thank you for your visit , and to say that I will not detain you here any long . your information would be more satisfactory if you be willing to explain how you become possessed of it . however , it justify I , I suppose , in go into mourning . there be not much alteration necessary in my dress , as you see . when I have change my mitten , I shall be all in black . " 

 she search in the pocket of her gown , draw out a pair of black lace mitten , put they on with the stony and steady composure , and then quietly cross her hand in her lap . 

 " I wish you good morning , " she say . 

 the cool contempt of her manner irritate I into directly avow that the purpose of my visit have not be answer yet . 

 " I have another motive in come here , " I say . 

 " ah ! I think so , " remark Mrs. Catherick . 

 " your daughter 's death " 

 " what do she die of ? " 

 " of disease of the heart . " 

 " yes . go on . " 

 " your daughter 's death have be make the pretext for inflict serious injury on a person who be very dear to I . two man have be concern , to my certain knowledge , in do that wrong . one of they be Sir Percival Glyde . " 

 " indeed ! " 

 I look attentively to see if she flinch at the sudden mention of that name . not a muscle of she stir the hard , defiant , implacable stare in her eye never waver for an instant . 

 " you may wonder , " I go on , " how the event of your daughter 's death can have be make the mean of inflict injury on another person . " 

 " no , " say Mrs. Catherick ; " I do not   wonder at all . this appear to be your affair . you be interested in my affair . I be not interested in your . " 

 " you may ask , then , " I persist , " why I mention the matter in your presence . " 

 " yes , I do ask that . " 

 " I mention it because I be determined to bring Sir Percival Glyde to account for the wickedness he have commit . " 

 " what have I to do with your determination ? " 

 " you shall hear . there be certain event in Sir Percival 's past life which it be necessary for my purpose to be fully acquaint with . you know they and for that reason I come to you . " 

 " what event do you mean ? " 

 " event that occur at Old Welmingham when your husband be parish - clerk at that place , and before the time when your daughter be bear . " 

 I have reach the woman at last through the barrier of impenetrable reserve that she have try to set up between we . I see her temper smoulder in her eye as plainly as I see her hand grow restless , then unclasp themselves , and begin mechanically smooth her dress over her knee . 

 " what do you know of those event ? " she ask . 

 " all that Mrs. Clements could tell I , " I answer . 

 there be a momentary flush on her firm square face , a momentary stillness in her restless hand , which seem to betoken a come outburst of anger that might throw she off her guard . but no she master the rise irritation , lean back in her chair , cross her arm on her broad bosom , and with a smile of grim sarcasm on her thick lip , look at I as steadily as ever . 

 " ah ! I begin to understand it all now , " she say , her tame and disciplined anger only express itself in the elaborate mockery of her tone and manner . " you have get a grudge of your own against Sir Percival Glyde , and I must help you to wreak it . I must tell you this , that , and the other about Sir Percival and myself , must I ? yes , indeed ? you have be pry into my private affair . you think you have find a lost woman to deal with , who live here on sufferance , and who will do anything you ask for fear you may injure she in the opinion of the town' - people . I see through you and your precious speculation I do ! and it amuse I . ha ! ha ! " 

 she stop for a moment , her arm tighten over her bosom , and she laugh to herself a hard , harsh , angry laugh . 

 " you do not   know how I have live in this place , and what I have do in this place , Mr. What's - your - name , " she go on . " I will tell you , before I ring the bell and have you show out . I come here a wronged woman I come here rob of my character and determine to claim it back . I have   be year and year about it and I have claim it back . I have match the respectable people fairly and openly on their own ground . if they say anything against I now they must say it in secret they can not   say it , they dare not   say it , openly . I stand high enough in this town to be out of your reach . the CLERGYMAN BOWS to I . aha ! you do not   bargain for that when you come here . go to the church and inquire about I you will find Mrs. Catherick have she sit like the rest of they , and pay the rent on the day it be due . go to the town - hall . there be a petition lie there a petition of the respectable inhabitant against allow a circus to come and perform here and corrupt our moral yes ! our moral . I sign that petition this morning . go to the bookseller 's shop . the clergyman 's Wednesday evening Lectures on Justification by Faith be publish there by subscription I be down on the list . the doctor 's wife only put a shilling in the plate at our last charity sermon I put half - a - crown . Mr. Churchwarden Soward hold the plate , and bow to I . ten year ago he tell Pigrum the chemist I ought to be whip out of the town at the cart 's tail . be your mother alive ? have she get a well Bible on her table than I have get on mine ? do she stand well with her trade - people than I do with mine ? have she always live within her income ? I have always live within mine . ah ! there be the clergyman come along the square . look , Mr. What's - your - name look , if you please ! " 

 she start up with the activity of a young woman , go to the window , wait till the clergyman pass , and bow to he solemnly . the clergyman ceremoniously raise his hat , and walk on . Mrs. Catherick return to her chair , and look at I with a grimmer sarcasm than ever . 

 " there ! " she say . " what do you think of that for a woman with a lost character ? how do your speculation look now ? " 

 the singular manner in which she have choose to assert herself , the extraordinary practical vindication of her position in the town which she have just offer , have so perplex I that I listen to she in silent surprise . I be not the less resolve , however , to make another effort to throw she off her guard . if the woman 's fierce temper once get beyond her control , and once flame out on I , she might yet say the word which would put the clue in my hand . 

 " how do your speculation look now ? " she repeat . 

 " exactly as it look when I first come in , " I answer . " I do not   doubt the position you have gain in the town , and I do not   wish to assail it even if I could . I come here because Sir Percival Glyde be , to my certain knowledge , your enemy , as well as mine . if I have a grudge against he , you have a grudge against he too . you may deny it if you like , you may distrust I as much as you please , you may be as angry as you will but , of all the woman in England , you , if you have any sense of injury , be the woman who ought to help I to crush that man . " 

 " crush he for yourself , " she say ; " then come back here , and see what I say to you . " 

 she speak those word as she have not speak yet , quickly , fiercely , vindictively . I have stir in its lair the serpent - hatred of year , but only for a moment . like a lurking reptile it leap up at I as she eagerly bent forward towards the place in which I be sit . like a lurking reptile it drop out of sight again as she instantly resume her former position in the chair . 

 " you will not   trust I ? " I say . 

 " no . " 

 " you be afraid ? " 

 " do I look as if I be ? " 

 " you be afraid of Sir Percival Glyde ? " 

 " be I ? " 

 her colour be rise , and her hand be at work again smooth her gown . I press the point far and far home , I go on without allow she a moment of delay . 

 " Sir Percival have a high position in the world , " I say ; " it would be no wonder if you be afraid of he . Sir Percival be a powerful man , a baronet , the possessor of a fine estate , the descendant of a great family " 

 she amaze I beyond expression by suddenly burst out laugh . 

 " yes , " she repeat , in tone of the bitter , steady contempt . " a baronet , the possessor of a fine estate , the descendant of a great family . yes , indeed ! a great family especially by the mother 's side . " 

 there be no time to reflect on the word that have just escape she , there be only time to feel that they be well worth think over the moment I leave the house . 

 " I be not here to dispute with you about family question , " I say . " I know nothing of Sir Percival 's mother " 

 " and you know as little of Sir Percival himself , " she interpose sharply . 

 " I advise you not to be too sure of that , " I rejoin . " I know some thing about he , and I suspect many more . " 

 " what do you suspect ? " 

 " I will tell you what I don't suspect . I don't suspect he of be Anne 's father . " 

 she start to her foot , and come close up to I with a look of fury . 

 " how dare you talk to I about Anne 's father ! how dare you say who be her father , or who be not ! " she break out , her face quiver , her voice tremble with passion . 

 " the secret between you and Sir Percival be not that secret , " I persist . " the mystery which darken Sir Percival 's life be not bear with your daughter 's birth , and have not die with your daughter 's death . " 

 she draw back a step . " go ! " she say , and point sternly to the door . 

 " there be no thought of the child in your heart or in his , " I go on , determined to press she back to her last defence . " there be no bond of guilty love between you and he when you hold those steal meeting , when your husband find you whisper together under the vestry of the church . " 

 her point hand instantly drop to her side , and the deep flush of anger fade from her face while I speak . I see the change pass over she I see that hard , firm , fearless , self - possess woman quail under a terror which her utmost resolution be not strong enough to resist when I say those five last word , " the vestry of the church . " 

 for a minute or more we stand look at each other in silence . I speak first . 

 " do you still refuse to trust I ? " I ask . 

 she could not call the colour that have leave it back to her face , but she have steady her voice , she have recover the defiant self - possession of her manner when she answer I . 

 " I do refuse , " she say . 

 " do you still tell I to go ? " 

 " yes . go and never come back . " 

 I walk to the door , wait a moment before I open it , and turn round to look at she again . 

 " I may have news to bring you of Sir Percival which you do not   expect , " I say , " and in that case I shall come back . " 

 " there be no news of Sir Percival that I do not   expect , except " 

 she stop , her pale face darken , and she steal back with a quiet , stealthy , cat - like step to her chair . 

 " except the news of his death , " she say , sit down again , with the mockery of a smile just hover on her cruel lip , and the furtive light of hatred lurk deep in her steady eye . 

 as I open the door of the room to go out , she look round at I quickly . the cruel smile slowly widen her lip she eye I , with a strange stealthy interest , from head to foot an unutterable expectation show itself wickedly all over her face . be she speculate , in the secrecy of her own heart , on my youth and strength , on the force of my sense of injury and the limit of my self - control , and be she consider the length to which they might carry I , if Sir Percival and I ever chance to meet ? the bare doubt that it might be so drive I from her presence , and silence even the common form of farewell on my lip . without a word more , on my side or on her , I leave the room . 

 as I open the outer door , I see the same clergyman who have already pass the house once , about to pass it again , on his way back through the square . I wait on the door - step to let he go by , and look round , as I do so , at the parlour window . 

 Mrs. Catherick have hear his footstep approach , in the silence of that lonely place , and she be on her foot at the window again , wait for he . not all the strength of all the terrible passion I have rouse in that woman 's heart , could loosen her desperate hold on the one fragment of social consideration which year of resolute effort have just drag within her grasp . there she be again , not a minute after I have leave she , place purposely in a position which make it a matter of common courtesy on the part of the clergyman to bow to she for a second time . he raise his hat once more . I see the hard ghastly face behind the window soften , and light up with gratified pride I see the head with the grim black cap bend ceremoniously in return . the clergyman have bow to she , and in my presence , twice in one day ! 



 IX 

 I leave the house , feel that Mrs. Catherick have help I a step forward , in spite of herself . before I have reach the turning which lead out of the square , my attention be suddenly arouse by the sound of a closing door behind I .

 the train be on the point of starting , and two or three passenger who be late be cluster round the small opening through which the ticket be issue .  there be only one interpretation that I could place on what I have just see and hear . I have unquestionably observe the man leave a house which closely adjoin Mrs. Catherick 's residence . he have be probably place there , by Sir Percival 's direction , as a lodger , in anticipation of my inquiry lead I , soon or later , to communicate with Mrs. Catherick . he have doubtless see I go in and come out , and he have hurry away by the first train to make his report at Blackwater Park , to which place Sir Percival would naturally betake himself   know what he evidently know of my movement , in order to be ready on the spot , if I return to Hampshire . before many day be over , there seem every likelihood now that he and I might meet . 

 whatever result event might be destine to produce , I resolve to pursue my own course , straight to the end in view , without stop or turn aside for Sir Percival or for any one . the great responsibility which weigh on I heavily in London the responsibility of so guide my slight action as to prevent they from lead accidentally to the discovery of Laura 's place of refuge be remove , now that I be in Hampshire . I could go and come as I please at Welmingham , and if I chance to fail in observe any necessary precaution , the immediate result , at least , would affect no one but myself . 

 when I leave the station the winter evening be begin to close in . there be little hope of continue my inquiry after dark to any useful purpose in a neighbourhood that be strange to I . accordingly , I make my way to the near hotel , and order my dinner and my bed . this do , I write to Marian , to tell she that I be safe and well , and that I have fair prospect of success . I have direct she , on leave home , to address the first letter she write to I   the letter I expect to receive the next morning   to " the Post - Office , Welmingham , " and I now beg she to send her second day 's letter to the same address . 

 I could easily receive it by write to the postmaster if I happen to be away from the town when it arrive . 

 the coffee - room of the hotel , as it grow late in the evening , become a perfect solitude .  the vestry of Old Welmingham church be the starting - point from which my mind slowly work its way back through all that I have hear Mrs. Catherick say , and through all I have see Mrs. Catherick do . 

 at the time when the neighbourhood of the vestry be first refer to in my presence by Mrs. Clements , I have think it the strange and most unaccountable of all place for Sir Percival to select for a clandestine meeting with the clerk 's wife . influence by this impression , and by no other , I have mention " the vestry of the church " before Mrs. Catherick on pure speculation it represent one of the minor peculiarity of the story which occur to I while I be speak . I have long before associate Sir Percival 's secret with the concealment of a serious crime which Mrs. Catherick know of , but I have go no far than this . now the woman 's paroxysm of terror associate the crime , either directly or indirectly , with the vestry , and convince I that she have be more than the mere witness of it she be also the accomplice , beyond a doubt . 

 what have be the nature of the crime ? surely there be a contemptible side to it , as well as a dangerous side , or Mrs. Catherick would not have repeat my own word , refer to Sir Percival 's rank and power , with such marked disdain as she have certainly display . it be a contemptible crime then and a dangerous crime , and she have share in it , and it be associate with the vestry of the church . 

 the next consideration to be dispose of lead I a step far from this point . 

 Mrs. Catherick 's undisguised contempt for Sir Percival plainly extend to his mother as well . she have refer with the bitter sarcasm to the great family he have descend from " especially by the mother 's side . " what do this mean ? 

 there appear to be only two explanation of it . either his mother 's birth have be low , or his mother 's reputation be damage by some hide flaw with which Mrs. Catherick and Sir Percival be both privately acquaint ? I could only put the first explanation to the test by look at the register of her marriage , and so ascertain her maiden name and her parentage as a preliminary to further inquiry . 

 on the other hand , if the second case suppose be the true one , what have be the flaw in her reputation ? remember the account which Marian have give I of Sir Percival 's father and mother , and of the suspiciously unsocial seclude life they have both lead , I now ask myself whether it might not be possible that his mother have never be marry at all . here again the register might , by offer write evidence of the marriage , prove to I , at any rate , that this doubt have no foundation in truth . but where be the register to be find ? at this point I take up the conclusion which I have previously form , and the same mental process which have discover the locality of the conceal crime , now lodge the register also in the vestry of Old Welmingham church . 

 these be the result of my interview with Mrs. Catherick these be the various consideration , all steadily converge to one point , which decide the course of my proceeding on the next day . 


 the morning be cloudy and lower , but no rain fall . I leave my bag at the hotel to wait there till I call for it , and , after inquire the way , set forth on foot for Old Welmingham church . 

 it be a walk of rather more than two mile , the ground rise slowly all the way . 

 on the high point stand the church an ancient , weather - beat building , with heavy buttress at its side , and a clumsy square tower in front . the vestry at the back be build out from the church , and seem to be of the same age . round the building at interval appear the remain of the village which Mrs. Clements have describe to I as her husband 's place of abode in former year , and which the principal inhabitant have long since desert for the new town . some of the empty house have be dismantle to their outer wall , some have be leave to decay with time , and some be still inhabit by person evidently of the poor class . it be a dreary scene , and yet , in the bad aspect of its ruin , not so dreary as the modern town that I have just leave . here there be the brown , breezy sweep of surround field for the eye to repose on here the tree , leafless as they be , still vary the monotony of the prospect , and help the mind to look forward to summer - time and shade . 

 as I move away from the back of the church , and pass some of the dismantle cottage in search of a person who might direct I to the clerk , I see two man saunter out after I from behind a wall . the tall of the two a stout muscular man in the dress of a gamekeeper be a stranger to I . the other be one of the man who have follow I in London on the day when I leave Mr. Kyrle 's office . I have take particular notice of he at the time ; and I feel sure that I be not mistake in identify the fellow on this occasion .

" I should have be away in ten minute more . parish business , sir , and a goodish long trot before it be all do for a man at my age . but , bless you , I be strong on my leg still ! as long as a man do not   give at his leg , there be a deal of work leave in he . do not   you think so yourself , sir ? " 

 he take his key down while he be talk from a hook behind the fireplace , and lock his cottage door behind we . 

 " nobody at home to keep house for I , " say the clerk , with a cheerful sense of perfect freedom from all family encumbrance . " my wife 's in the churchyard there , and my child be all married . a wretched place this , be not   it , sir ? but the parish be a large one every man could not   get through the business as I do . it be learn do it , and I have   have my share , and a little more . I can talk the Queen 's English   God bless the Queen ! , and that be more than most of the people about here can do . you be from London , I suppose , sir ? I have   be in London a matter of five - and - twenty year ago . what be the news there now , if you please ? " 

 chatter on in this way , he lead I back to the vestry . I look about to see if the two spy be still in sight . they be not visible anywhere . after have discover my application to the clerk , they have probably conceal themselves where they could watch my next proceeding in perfect freedom . 

 the vestry door be of stout old oak , stud with strong nail , and the clerk put his large heavy key into the lock with the air of a man who know that he have a difficulty to encounter , and who be not quite certain of creditably conquer it . 

 " I be oblige to bring you this way , sir , " he say , " because the door from the vestry to the church be bolt on the vestry side . we might have get in through the church otherwise . this be a perverse lock , if ever there be one yet . it be big enough for a prison - door it be be hamper over and over again , and it ought to be change for a new one . I have   mention that to the churchwarden fifty time over at least he be always say , ' I will see about it ' and he never do see . ah , it be a sort of lose corner , this place . not like London be it , sir ? bless you , we be all asleep here ! we do not   march with the time . " 

 after some twisting and turning of the key , the heavy lock yield , and he open the door . 

 the vestry be large than I should have suppose it to be , judge from the outside only . it be a dim , mouldy , melancholy old room , with a low , rafter ceiling . round two side of it , the side near to the interior of the church , run heavy wooden press , worm - eat and gap with age . hooked to the inner corner of one of these press hang several surplice , all bulge out at their low end in an irreverent - look bundle of limp drapery . below the surplice , on the floor , stand three packing - case , with the lid half off , half on , and the straw profusely burst out of their crack and crevice in every direction . behind they , in a corner , be a litter of dusty paper , some large and roll up like architect ' plan , some loosely string together on file like bill or letter . the room have once be light by a small side window , but this have be bricke up , and a lantern skylight be now substitute for it . the atmosphere of the place be heavy and mouldy , be render additionally oppressive by the closing of the door which lead into the church . this door also be compose of solid oak , and be bolt at the top and bottom on the vestry side . 

 " we might be tidy , might not   we , sir ? " say the cheerful clerk ; " but when you be in a lost corner of a place like this , what be you to do ? why , look here now , just look at these packing - case . there they have   be , for a year or more , ready to go down to London there they be , litter the place , and there they will stop as long as the nail hold they together . I will tell you what , sir , as I say before , this be not London . we be all asleep here . bless you , we do not   march with the time ! " 

 " what be there in the packing - case ? " I ask . 

 " bit of old wood carving from the pulpit , and panel from the chancel , and image from the organ - loft , " say the clerk . " portrait of the twelve apostle in wood , and not a whole nose among them . all break , and worm - eat , and crumble to dust at the edge . as brittle as crockery , sir , and as old as the church , if not old . " 

 " and why be they go to London ? to be repair ? " 

 " that be it , sir , to be repair , and where they be past repair , to be copy in sound wood . but , bless you , the money fall short , and there they be , wait for new subscription , and nobody to subscribe . it be all do a year ago , sir . six gentleman din together about it , at the hotel in the new town . they make speech , and pass resolution , and put their name down , and print off thousand of prospectus . beautiful prospectus , sir , all flourish over with Gothic device in red ink , say it be a disgrace not to restore the church and repair the famous carving , and so on . there be the prospectus that could not   be distribute , and the architect 's plan and estimate , and the whole correspondence which set everybody at loggerhead and end in a dispute , all down together in that corner , behind the packing - case . the money dribble in a little at first but what can you expect out of London ? there be just enough , you know , to pack the broken carving , and get the estimate , and pay the printer 's bill , and after that there be not   a halfpenny leave . there the thing be , as I say before . we have nowhere else to put they nobody in the new town care about accommodate we we be in a lost corner and this be an untidy vestry and who be to help it ? that be what I want to know . " 

 my anxiety to examine the register do not dispose I to offer much encouragement to the old man 's talkativeness . I agree with he that nobody could help the untidiness of the vestry , and then suggest that we should proceed to our business without more delay .

 Marian have inform I of Sir Percival 's age at the time when we have speak together of his marriage engagement with Laura . she have then describe he as be forty - five year old . calculate back from this , and make due allowance for the year that have pass since I have gain my information , I find that he must have be bear in eighteen hundred and four , and that I might safely start on my search through the register from that date . 

 " I want to begin with the year eighteen hundred and four , " I say . 

 " which way after that , sir ? " ask the clerk . " Forwards to our time or backwards away from we ? " 

 " backwards from eighteen hundred and four . " 

 he open the door of one of the press the press from the side of which the surplice be hang and produce a large volume bind in greasy brown leather . " those be the very word my old master be always say year and year ago , when I be a lad . ' why be not   the register '   mean this register here , under my hand   ' why be not   it keep in an iron safe ? ' if I have   hear he say that once , I have   hear he say it a hundred time . he be the solicitor in those day , sir , who have the appointment of vestry - clerk to this church . a fine hearty old gentleman , and the most particular man breathing . as long as he live he keep a copy of this book in his office at Knowlesbury , and have it post up regular , from time to time , to correspond with the fresh entry here . you would hardly think it , but he have his own appoint day , once or twice in every quarter , for rid over to this church on his old white pony , to check the copy , by the register , with his own eye and hand . ' how do I know ? '   he use to say   ' how do I know that the register in this vestry may not be steal or destroy ? why be not   it keep in an iron safe ? why can not   I make other people as careful as I be myself ? some of these day there will be an accident happen , and when the register 's lose , then the parish will find out the value of my copy . ' he use to take his pinch of snuff after that , and look about he as bold as a lord . ah ! the like of he for do business be not   easy to find now . you may go to London and not match he , even there . which year do you say , sir ? eighteen hundred and what ? "

 the clerk put on his spectacle , and turn over the leaf of the register , carefully wet his finger and thumb at every third page . " there it be , sir , " say he , with another cheerful smack on the open volume . " there be the year you want . " 

 as I be ignorant of the month in which Sir Percival be bear , I begin my backward search with the early part of the year . the register - book be of the old - fashioned kind , the entry be all make on blank page in manuscript , and the division which separate they be indicate by ink line draw across the page at the close of each entry . 

 I reach the beginning of the year eighteen hundred and four without encounter the marriage , and then travel back through December eighteen hundred and three through November and October through 

 no ! not through September also . under the heading of that month in the year I find the marriage . 

 I look carefully at the entry . it be at the bottom of a page , and be for want of room compress into a small space than that occupy by the marriage above . the marriage immediately before it be impressed on my attention by the circumstance of the bridegroom 's christian name be the same as my own . the entry immediately follow it   on the top of the next page   be noticeable in another way from the large space it occupy , the record in this case register the marriage of two brother at the same time . the register of the marriage of Sir Felix Glyde be in no respect remarkable except for the narrowness of the space into which it be compress at the bottom of the page . the information about his wife be the usual information give in such case . she be describe as " Cecilia Jane Elster , of Park - View Cottages , Knowlesbury , only daughter of the late Patrick Elster , Esq . , formerly of Bath . " 

 I note down these particular in my pocket - book , feel as I do so both doubtful and dishearten about my next proceeding . the Secret which I have believe until this moment to be within my grasp seem now far from my reach than ever . 

 what suggestion of any mystery unexplained have arise out of my visit to the vestry ? I see no suggestion anywhere . what progress have I make towards discover the suspect stain on the reputation of Sir Percival 's mother ? the one fact I have ascertain vindicate her reputation . fresh doubt , fresh difficulty , fresh delay begin to open before I in interminable prospect . what be I to do next ? the one immediate resource leave to I appear to be this .  " have you find what you want , sir ? " say the clerk , as I close the register - book .

 " no , no , sir , he be dead three or four year before I come here , and that be as long ago as the year twenty - seven . I get this place , sir , " persist my talkative old friend , " through the clerk before I leave it . they say he be drive out of house and home by his wife and she be live still down in the new town there .  " you say just now he be vestry - clerk , like his father before he . I be not quite sure that I know what a vestry - clerk be . " 

 " do not   you indeed , sir ? and you come from London too ! every parish church , you know , have a vestry - clerk and a parish - clerk . the parish - clerk be a man like I   except that I have   get a deal more learning than most of they though I do not   boast of it . the vestry - clerk be a sort of an appointment that the lawyer get , and if there be any business to be do for the vestry , why there they be to do it . it be just the same in London . every parish church there have get its vestry - clerk and you may take my word for it he be sure to be a lawyer . "

" 

 " how far be it to Knowlesbury from this place ? " 

 " a long stretch , sir , " say the clerk , with that exaggerated idea of distance , and that vivid perception of difficulty in get from place to place , which be peculiar to all country people . " Nigh on five mile , I can tell you ! " 

 it be still early in the forenoon . there be plenty of time for a walk to Knowlesbury and back again to Welmingham ; and there be no person probably in the town who be fitter to assist my inquiry about the character and position of Sir Percival 's mother before her marriage than the local solicitor . resolve to go at once to Knowlesbury on foot , I lead the way out of the vestry . 

 " thank you kindly , sir , " say the clerk , as I slip my little present into his hand . " be you really go to walk all the way to Knowlesbury and back ? well ! you be strong on your leg , too and what a blessing that be , be not   it ? there be the road , you can not   miss it . I wish I be go your way it be pleasant to meet with gentleman from London in a lost corner like this . one hear the news . wish you good - morning , sir , and thank you kindly once more . " 

 we part . as I leave the church behind I I look back , and there be the two man again on the road below , with a third in their company , that third person be the short man in black whom I have trace to the railway the evening before .

 X 

 once out of sight of the church , I press forward briskly on my way to Knowlesbury . 

 the road be , for the most part , straight and level . whenever I look back over it I see the two spy steadily follow I . for the great part of the way they keep at a safe distance behind . but once or twice they quicken their pace , as if with the purpose of overtake I , then stop , consult together , and fall back again to their former position . they have some special object evidently in view , and they seem to be hesitate or differ about the good mean of accomplish it . I could not guess exactly what their design might be , but I feel serious doubt of reach Knowlesbury without some mischance happen to I on the way . these doubt be realise . 

 I have just enter on a lonely part of the road , with a sharp turn at some distance ahead , and have just conclude   calculate by time   that I must be get near to the town , when I suddenly hear the step of the man close behind I .

I have be more irritated by the manner in which he and his companion have dog my step all the way from Old Welmingham than I be myself aware of , and I unfortunately push the fellow away smartly with my open hand . he instantly shout for help . his companion , the tall man in the gamekeeper 's clothe , spring to my right side , and the next moment the two scoundrel hold I pinion between they in the middle of the road . 

 the conviction that a trap have be lay for I , and the vexation of know that I have fall into it , fortunately restrain I from make my position still bad by an unavaile struggle with two man , one of whom would , in all probability , have be more than a match for I single - handed .  a labourer be at work in an adjoining field who must have witness all that have pass .
