I be cool enough and wise enough now to make no opposition . " drop your hold of my arm , " I say , " and I will go with you to the town . " the man in the gamekeeper 's dress roughly refuse . but the short man be sharp enough to look to consequence , and not to let his companion commit himself by unnecessary violence . he make a sign to the other , and I walk on between they with my arm free . 

 we reach the turning in the road , and there , close before we , be the suburb of Knowlesbury . one of the local policeman be walk along the path by the roadside . the man at once appeal to he . he reply that the magistrate be then sit at the town - hall , and recommend that we should appear before he immediately . 

 we go on to the town - hall . the clerk make out a formal summon , and the charge be prefer against I , with the customary exaggeration and the customary perversion of the truth on such occasion . the magistrate   an ill - temper man , with a sour enjoyment in the exercise of his own power   inquire if any one on or near the road have witness the assault , and , greatly to my surprise , the complainant admit the presence of the labourer in the field . I be enlighten , however , as to the object of the admission by the magistrate 's next word . he remand I at once for the production of the witness , express , at the same time , his willingness to take bail for my reappearance if I could produce one responsible surety to offer it . if I have be know in the town he would have liberate I on my own recognisance , but as I be a total stranger it be necessary that I should find responsible bail . 

 the whole object of the stratagem be now disclose to I . it have be so manage as to make a remand necessary in a town where I be a perfect stranger , and where I could not hope to get my liberty on bail . the remand merely extend over three day , until the next sitting of the magistrate . but in that time , while I be in confinement , Sir Percival might use any mean he please to embarrass my future proceeding perhaps to screen himself from detection altogether without the slight fear of any hindrance on my part . at the end of the three day the charge would , no doubt , be withdraw , and the attendance of the witness would be perfectly useless . 

 my indignation , I may almost say , my despair , at this mischievous check to all further progress so base and trifle in itself , and yet so disheartening and so serious in its probable result quite unfit I at first to reflect on the good mean of extricate myself from the dilemma in which I now stand . I have the folly to call for write material , and to think of privately communicate my real position to the magistrate . the hopelessness and the imprudence of this proceeding fail to strike I before I have actually write the opening line of the letter . it be not till I have push the paper away not till , I be ashamed to say , I have almost allow the vexation of my helpless position to conquer I that a course of action suddenly occur to my mind , which Sir Percival have probably not anticipate , and which might set I free again in a few hour . I determine to communicate the situation in which I be place to Mr. Dawson , of Oak Lodge . 

 I have visit this gentleman 's house , it may be remember , at the time of my first inquiry in the Blackwater Park neighbourhood , and I have present to he a letter of introduction from Miss Halcombe , in which she recommend I to his friendly attention in the strong term . I now write , refer to this letter , and to what I have previously tell Mr. Dawson of the delicate and dangerous nature of my inquiry . I have not reveal to he the truth about Laura , have merely describe my errand as be of the utmost importance to private family interest with which Miss Halcombe be concern . use the same caution still , I now account for my presence at Knowlesbury in the same manner , and I put it to the doctor to say whether the trust repose in I by a lady whom he well know , and the hospitality I have myself receive in his house , justify I or not in ask he to come to my assistance in a place where I be quite friendless . 

 I obtain permission to hire a messenger to drive away at once with my letter in a conveyance which might be use to bring the doctor back immediately . Oak Lodge be on the Knowlesbury side of Blackwater . the man declare he could drive there in forty minute , and could bring Mr. Dawson back in forty more . I direct he to follow the doctor wherever he might happen to be , if he be not at home , and then sit down to wait for the result with all the patience and all the hope that I could summon to help I . 

 it be not quite half - past one when the messenger depart . before half - past three he return , and bring the doctor with he . Mr. Dawson 's kindness , and the delicacy with which he treat his prompt assistance quite as a matter of course , almost overpower I . the bail require be offer , and accept immediately . before four o'clock , on that afternoon , I be shake hand warmly with the good old doctor a free man again in the street of Knowlesbury . 

 Mr. Dawson hospitably invite I to go back with he to Oak Lodge , and take up my quarter there for the night . I could only reply that my time be not my own , and I could only ask he to let I pay my visit in a few day , when I might repeat my thank , and offer to he all the explanation which I feel to be only his due , but which I be not then in a position to make . we part with friendly assurance on both side , and I turn my step at once to Mr. Wansborough 's office in the High Street . 

 Time be now of the last importance . 

 the news of my be free on bail would reach Sir Percival , to an absolute certainty , before night . if the next few hour do not put I in a position to justify his bad fear , and to hold he helpless at my mercy , I might lose every inch of the ground I have gain , never to recover it again . the unscrupulous nature of the man , the local influence he possess , the desperate peril of exposure with which my blindfold inquiry threaten he all warn I to press on to positive discovery , without the useless waste of a single minute . I have find time to think while I be wait for Mr. Dawson 's arrival , and I have well employ it . certain portion of the conversation of the talkative old clerk , which have weary I at the time , now recur to my memory with a new significance , and a suspicion cross my mind darkly which have not occur to I while I be in the vestry . on my way to Knowlesbury , I have only propose to apply to Mr. Wansborough for information on the subject of Sir Percival 's mother . my object now be to examine the duplicate register of Old Welmingham Church . 

 Mr. Wansborough be in his office when I inquire for he . 

 he be a jovial , red - faced , easy - look man more like a country squire than a lawyer and he seem to be both surprised and amuse by my application . he have hear of his father 's copy of the register , but have not even see it himself . it have never be inquire after , and it be no doubt in the strong room among other paper that have not be disturb since his father 's death . it be a pity   Mr. Wansborough say   that the old gentleman be not alive to hear his precious copy ask for at last . he would have ride his favourite hobby hard than ever now . how have I come to hear of the copy ? be it through anybody in the town ? 

 I parry the question as well as I could . it be impossible at this stage of the investigation to be too cautious , and it be just as well not to let Mr. Wansborough know prematurely that I have already examine the original register . I describe myself , therefore , as pursue a family inquiry , to the object of which every possible saving of time be of great importance . I be anxious to send certain particular to London by that day 's post , and one look at the duplicate register   pay , of course , the necessary fee   might supply what I require , and save I a further journey to Old Welmingham . I add that , in the event of my subsequently require a copy of the original register , I should make application to Mr. Wansborough 's office to furnish I with the document . 

 after this explanation no objection be make to produce the copy . a clerk be send to the strong room , and after some delay return with the volume . it be of exactly the same size as the volume in the vestry , the only difference be that the copy be more smartly bind . I take it with I to an unoccupied desk . my hand be tremble my head be burn hot I feel the necessity of conceal my agitation as well as I could from the person about I in the room , before I venture on open the book . 

 on the blank page at the beginning , to which I first turn , be trace some line in faded ink . they contain these word 

 " Copy of the Marriage Register of Welmingham Parish Church . execute under my order , and afterwards compare , entry by entry , with the original , by myself .   sign   Robert Wansborough , vestry - clerk . " below this note there be a line add , in another handwriting , as follow : " extend from the first of January , 1800 , to the thirtieth of June , 1815 . " 

 I turn to the month of September , eighteen hundred and three . I find the marriage of the man whose christian name be the same as my own . I find the double register of the marriage of the two brother . and between these entry , at the bottom of the page ? 

 nothing ! not a vestige of the entry which record the marriage of Sir Felix Glyde and Cecilia Jane Elster in the register of the church ! 

 my heart give a great bound , and throb as if it would stifle I . I look again I be afraid to believe the evidence of my own eye . no ! not a doubt . the marriage be not there . the entry on the copy occupy exactly the same place on the page as the entry in the original . the last entry on one page record the marriage of the man with my christian name . below it there be a blank space a space evidently leave because it be too narrow to contain the entry of the marriage of the two brother , which in the copy , as in the original , occupy the top of the next page . that space tell the whole story ! there it must have remain in the church register from eighteen hundred and three   when the marriage have be solemnise and the copy have be make   to eighteen hundred and twenty - seven , when Sir Percival appear at Old Welmingham . here , at Knowlesbury , be the chance of commit the forgery show to I in the copy , and there , at Old Welmingham , be the forgery commit in the register of the church . 

 my head turn giddy I hold by the desk to keep myself from fall . of all the suspicion which have strike I in relation to that desperate man , not one have be near the truth . 

 the idea that he be not Sir Percival Glyde at all , that he have no more claim to the baronetcy and to Blackwater Park than the poor labourer who work on the estate , have never once occur to my mind . at one time I have think he might be Anne Catherick 's father at another time I have think he might have be Anne Catherick 's husband the offence of which he be really guilty have be , from first to last , beyond the wide reach of my imagination . 

 the paltry mean by which the fraud have be effect , the magnitude and daring of the crime that it represent , the horror of the consequence involve in its discovery , overwhelm I . who could wonder now at the brute - restlessness of the wretch 's life at his desperate alternation between abject duplicity and reckless violence at the madness of guilty distrust which have make he imprison Anne Catherick in the Asylum , and have give he over to the vile conspiracy against his wife , on the bare suspicion that the one and the other know his terrible secret ? the disclosure of that secret might , in past year , have hang he might now transport he for life . the disclosure of that secret , even if the sufferer by his deception spar he the penalty of the law , would deprive he at one blow of the name , the rank , the estate , the whole social existence that he have usurp . this be the Secret , and it be mine ! a word from I , and house , land , baronetcy , be go from he for ever a word from I , and he be drive out into the world , a nameless , penniless , friendless outcast ! the man 's whole future hang on my lip and he know it by this time as certainly as I do ! 

 that last thought steady I . interest far more precious than my own depend on the caution which must now guide my slight action . there be no possible treachery which Sir Percival might not attempt against I . in the danger and desperation of his position he would be stagger by no risk , he would recoil at no crime he would literally hesitate at nothing to save himself . 

 I consider for a minute . my first necessity be to secure positive evidence in writing of the discovery that I have just make , and in the event of any personal misadventure happen to I , to place that evidence beyond Sir Percival 's reach . the copy of the register be sure to be safe in Mr. Wansborough 's strong room . but the position of the original in the vestry be , as I have see with my own eye , anything but secure . 

 in this emergency I resolve to return to the church , to apply again to the clerk , and to take the necessary extract from the register before I sleep that night . I be not then aware that a legally - certify copy be necessary , and that no document merely draw out by myself could claim the proper importance as a proof . I be not aware of this , and my determination to keep my present proceeding a secret prevent I from ask any question which might have procure the necessary information . my one anxiety be the anxiety to get back to Old Welmingham . I make the good excuse I could for the discomposure in my face and manner which Mr. Wansborough have already notice , lay the necessary fee on his table , arrange that I should write to he in a day or two , and leave the office , with my head in a whirl and my blood throb through my vein at fever heat . 

 it be just get dark . the idea occur to I that I might be follow again and attack on the high - road . 

 my walking - stick be a light one , of little or no use for purpose of defence . I stop before leave Knowlesbury and buy a stout country cudgel , short , and heavy at the head . with this homely weapon , if any one man try to stop I I be a match for he . if more than one attack I I could trust to my heel . in my school - day I have be a noted runner , and I have not want for practice since in the later time of my experience in Central America . 

 I start from the town at a brisk pace , and keep the middle of the road . 

 a small misty rain be fall , and it be impossible for the first half of the way to make sure whether I be follow or not . but at the last half of my journey , when I suppose myself to be about two mile from the church , I see a man run by I in the rain , and then hear the gate of a field by the roadside shut to sharply . I keep straight on , with my cudgel ready in my hand , my ear on the alert , and my eye strain to see through the mist and the darkness . before I have advance a hundred yard there be a rustling in the hedge on my right , and three man spring out into the road . 

 I draw aside on the instant to the footpath . the two foremost man be carry beyond I before they could check themselves . the third be as quick as lightning . he stop , half turn , and strike at I with his stick . the blow be aim at hazard , and be not a severe one . it fall on my left shoulder . I return it heavily on his head . he stagger back and jostle his two companion just as they be both rush at I . this circumstance give I a moment 's start . I slip by they , and take to the middle of the road again at the top of my speed . 

 the two unhurt man pursue I . they be both good runner the road be smooth and level , and for the first five minute or more I be conscious that I do not gain on they . it be perilous work to run for long in the darkness . I could barely see the dim black line of the hedge on either side , and any chance obstacle in the road would have throw I down to a certainty . Ere long I feel the ground change it descend from the level at a turn , and then rise again beyond . downhill the man rather gain on I , but uphill I begin to distance they . the rapid , regular thump of their foot grow fainter on my ear , and I calculate by the sound that I be far enough in advance to take to the field with a good chance of their pass I in the darkness . diverge to the footpath , I make for the first break that I could guess at , rather than see , in the hedge . it prove to be a closed gate . I vault over , and find myself in a field , keep across it steadily with my back to the road . I hear the man pass the gate , still run , then in a minute more hear one of they call to the other to come back . it be no matter what they do now , I be out of their sight and out of their hearing . I keep straight across the field , and when I have reach the farth extremity of it , wait there for a minute to recover my breath . 

 it be impossible to venture back to the road , but I be determine nevertheless to get to Old Welmingham that evening . 

 neither moon nor star appear to guide I . I only know that I have keep the wind and rain at my back on leave Knowlesbury , and if I now keep they at my back still , I might at least be certain of not advance altogether in the wrong direction . 

 proceed on this plan , I cross the country meet with no bad obstacle than hedge , ditch , and thicket , which every now and then oblige I to alter my course for a little while until I find myself on a hill - side , with the ground slop away steeply before I . I descend to the bottom of the hollow , squeeze my way through a hedge , and get out into a lane . have turn to the right on leave the road , I now turn to the left , on the chance of regain the line from which I have wander . after follow the muddy winding of the lane for ten minute or more , I see a cottage with a light in one of the window . the garden gate be open to the lane , and I go in at once to inquire my way . 

 before I could knock at the door it be suddenly open , and a man come run out with a lighted lantern in his hand . he stop and hold it up at the sight of I . we both start as we see each other . my wandering have lead I round the outskirt of the village , and have bring I out at the low end of it . I be back at Old Welmingham , and the man with the lantern be no other than my acquaintance of the morning , the parish clerk . 

 his manner appear to have alter strangely in the interval since I have last see he . he look suspicious and confuse his ruddy cheek be deeply flushed and his first word , when he speak , be quite unintelligible to I . 

 " where be the key ? " he ask . " have you take they ? " 

 " what key ? " I repeat . " I have this moment come from Knowlesbury . what key do you mean ? " 

 " the key of the vestry . Lord save we and help we ! what shall I do ? the key be go ! do you hear ? " cry the old man , shake the lantern at I in his agitation , " the key be go ! " 

 " how ? when ? who can have take they ? " 

 " I do not   know , " say the clerk , star about he wildly in the darkness . " I have   only just get back . I tell you I have a long day 's work this morning I lock the door and shut the window down it be open now , the window 's open . look ! somebody have get in there and take the key . " 

 he turn to the casement window to show I that it be wide open . the door of the lantern come loose from its fastening as he sway it round , and the wind blow the candle out instantly . 

 " get another light , " I say , " and let we both go to the vestry together . quick ! quick ! " 

 I hurry he into the house . the treachery that I have every reason to expect , the treachery that might deprive I of every advantage I have gain , be at that moment , perhaps , in process of accomplishment . my impatience to reach the church be so great that I could not remain inactive in the cottage while the clerk light the lantern again . I walk out , down the garden path , into the lane . 

 before I have advance ten pace a man approach I from the direction lead to the church . he speak respectfully as we meet . I could not see his face , but judge by his voice only , he be a perfect stranger to I . 

 " I beg your pardon , Sir Percival " he begin . 

 I stop he before he could say more . 

 " the darkness mislead you , " I say . " I be not Sir Percival . " 

 the man draw back directly . 

 " I think it be my master , " he mutter , in a confused , doubtful way . 

 " you expect to meet your master here ? " 

 " I be tell to wait in the lane . " 

 with that answer he retrace his step . I look back at the cottage and see the clerk come out , with the lantern light once more . I take the old man 's arm to help he on the more quickly . we hasten along the lane , and pass the person who have accost I . as well as I could see by the light of the lantern , he be a servant out of livery . 

 " who be that ? " whisper the clerk . " do he know anything about the key ? " 

 " we will not   wait to ask he , " I reply . " we will go on to the vestry first . " 

 the church be not visible , even by daytime , until the end of the lane be reach . as we mount the rise ground which lead to the building from that point , one of the village child a boy come close up to we , attract by the light we carry , and recognise the clerk . 

 " I say , measter , " say the boy , pull officiously at the clerk 's coat , " there be summun up yander in the church . I heerd un lock the door on hisself I heerd un strike a loight wi ' a match . " 

 the clerk tremble and lean against I heavily . 

 " Come ! come ! " I say encouragingly . " we be not too late . we will catch the man , whoever he be . keep the lantern , and follow I as fast as you can . " 

 I mount the hill rapidly . the dark mass of the church - tower be the first object I discern dimly against the night sky . as I turn aside to get round to the vestry , I hear heavy footstep close to I . the servant have ascend to the church after we . " I do not   mean any harm , " he say , when I turn round on he , " I be only look for my master . " the tone in which he speak betray unmistakable fear . I take no notice of he and go on . 

 the instant I turn the corner and come in view of the vestry , I see the lantern - skylight on the roof brilliantly light up from within . it shine out with dazzle brightness against the murky , starless sky . 

 I hurry through the churchyard to the door . 

 as I get near there be a strange smell steal out on the damp night air . I hear a snapping noise inside I see the light above grow brighter and brighter a pane of the glass crack I run to the door and put my hand on it . the vestry be on fire ! 

 before I could move , before I could draw my breath after that discovery , I be horror - strike by a heavy thump against the door from the inside . I hear the key work violently in the lock I hear a man 's voice behind the door , raise to a dreadful shrillness , scream for help . 

 the servant who have follow I stagger back shuddering , and drop to his knee . " oh , my God ! " he say , " it be Sir Percival ! " 

 as the word pass his lip the clerk join we , and at the same moment there be another and a last grating turn of the key in the lock . 

 " the Lord have mercy on his soul ! " say the old man . " he be doom and dead . he have hamper the lock . " 

 I rush to the door . the one absorb purpose that have fill all my thought , that have control all my action , for week and week past , vanish in an instant from my mind . all remembrance of the heartless injury the man 's crime have inflict of the love , the innocence , the happiness he have pitilessly lay waste of the oath I have swear in my own heart to summon he to the terrible reckoning that he deserve pass from my memory like a dream . I remember nothing but the horror of his situation . I feel nothing but the natural human impulse to save he from a frightful death . 

 " try the other door ! " I shout . " try the door into the church ! the lock 's hamper . you be a dead man if you waste another moment on it . " 

 there have be no renewed cry for help when the key be turn for the last time . there be no sound now of any kind , to give token that he be still alive . I hear nothing but the quicken crackle of the flame , and the sharp snap of the glass in the skylight above . 

 I look round at my two companion . the servant have rise to his foot he have take the lantern , and be hold it up vacantly at the door . Terror seem to have strike he with downright idiocy he wait at my heel , he follow I about when I move like a dog . the clerk sit crouch up on one of the tombstone , shivering , and moan to himself . the one moment in which I look at they be enough to show I that they be both helpless . 

 hardly know what I do , act desperately on the first impulse that occur to I , I seize the servant and push he against the vestry wall . " stoop ! " I say , " and hold by the stone . I be go to climb over you to the roof I be go to break the skylight , and give he some air ! " 

 the man tremble from head to foot , but he hold firm . I get on his back , with my cudgel in my mouth , seize the parapet with both hand , and be instantly on the roof . in the frantic hurry and agitation of the moment , it never strike I that I might let out the flame instead of let in the air . I strike at the skylight , and batter in the crack , loosen glass at a blow . the fire leap out like a wild beast from its lair . if the wind have not chance , in the position I occupy , to set it away from I , my exertion might have end then and there . I crouch on the roof as the smoke pour out above I with the flame . the gleam and flash of the light show I the servant 's face star up vacantly under the wall the clerk rise to his foot on the tombstone , wring his hand in despair and the scanty population of the village , haggard man and terrified woman , cluster beyond in the churchyard all appear and disappear , in the red of the dreadful glare , in the black of the choke smoke . and the man beneath my foot ! the man , suffocating , burning , die so near we all , so utterly beyond our reach ! 

 the thought half madden I . I lower myself from the roof , by my hand , and drop to the ground . 

 " the key of the church ! " I shout to the clerk . " we must try it that way we may save he yet if we can burst open the inner door . " 

 " no , no , no ! " cry the old man . " no hope ! the church key and the vestry key be on the same ring both inside there ! oh , sir , he be past save he be dust and ash by this time ! " 

 " they will see the fire from the town , " say a voice from among the man behind I . " there be a ingine in the town . they will save the church . " 

 I call to that man he have his wit about he I call to he to come and speak to I . it would be a quarter of an hour at least before the town engine could reach we . the horror of remain inactive all that time be more than I could face . in defiance of my own reason I persuade myself that the doomed and lose wretch in the vestry might still be lie senseless on the floor , might not be dead yet . if we break open the door , might we save he ? I know the strength of the heavy lock I know the thickness of the nailed oak I know the hopelessness of assail the one and the other by ordinary mean . but surely there be beam still leave in the dismantle cottage near the church ? what if we get one , and use it as a battering - ram against the door ? 

 the thought leap through I like the fire leap out of the shattered skylight . I appeal to the man who have speak first of the fire - engine in the town . " have you get your pickax handy ? " yes , they have . " and a hatchet , and a saw , and a bit of rope ? " yes ! yes ! yes ! I run down among the villager , with the lantern in my hand . " five shilling apiece to every man who help I ! " they start into life at the word . that ravenous second hunger of poverty the hunger for money rouse they into tumult and activity in a moment . " two of you for more lantern , if you have they ! two of you for the pickax and the tool ! the rest after I to find the beam ! " they cheer with shrill starveling voice they cheer . the woman and the child flee back on either side . we rush in a body down the churchyard path to the first empty cottage . not a man be leave behind but the clerk the poor old clerk stand on the flat tombstone sob and wail over the church . the servant be still at my heel his white , helpless , panic - stricken face be close over my shoulder as we push into the cottage . there be rafter from the tear - down floor above , lie loose on the ground but they be too light . a beam run across over our head , but not out of reach of our arm and our pickax a beam fast at each end in the ruin wall , with ceiling and floor all rip away , and a great gap in the roof above , open to the sky . we attack the beam at both end at once . God ! how it hold how the brick and mortar of the wall resist we ! we strike , and tug , and tore . the beam give at one end it come down with a lump of brickwork after it . there be a scream from the woman all huddle in the doorway to look at we a shout from the man two of they down but not hurt . another tug all together and the beam be loose at both end . we raise it , and give the word to clear the doorway . now for the work ! now for the rush at the door ! there be the fire stream into the sky , stream brighter than ever to light we ! Steady along the churchyard path steady with the beam for a rush at the door . one , two , three and off . out ring the cheering again , irrepressibly . we have shake it already , the hinge must give if the lock will not . another run with the beam ! one , two , three and off . it be loose ! the stealthy fire dart at we through the crevice all round it . another , and a last rush ! the door fall in with a crash . a great hush of awe , a stillness of breathless expectation , possess every live soul of we . we look for the body . the scorch heat on our face drive we back : we see nothing above , below , all through the room , we see nothing but a sheet of living fire . 


 " where be he ? " whisper the servant , star vacantly at the flame . 

 " he be dust and ash , " say the clerk . " and the book be dust and ash and oh , sir ! the church will be dust and ash soon . " 

 those be the only two who speak . when they be silent again , nothing stir in the stillness but the bubble and the crackle of the flame . 

 Hark ! 

 a harsh rattle sound in the distance then the hollow beat of horse ' hoof at full gallop then the low roar , the all - predominant tumult of hundred of human voice clamour and shout together . the engine at last . 

 the people about I all turn from the fire , and run eagerly to the brow of the hill . the old clerk try to go with the rest , but his strength be exhaust . I see he hold by one of the tombstone . " save the church ! " he cry out faintly , as if the fireman could hear he already . 

 save the church ! 

 the only man who never move be the servant . there he stand , his eye still fasten on the flame in a changeless , vacant stare . I speak to he , I shake he by the arm . he be past rousing . he only whisper once more , " where be he ? " 

 in ten minute the engine be in position , the well at the back of the church be feed it , and the hose be carry to the doorway of the vestry . if help have be want from I I could not have afford it now . my energy of will be go my strength be exhaust the turmoil of my thought be fearfully and suddenly still , now I know that he be dead . 

 I stand useless and helpless look , look , look into the burn room . 

 I see the fire slowly conquer . the brightness of the glare fade the steam rise in white cloud , and the smoulder heap of ember show red and black through it on the floor . there be a pause then an advance all together of the fireman and the police which block up the doorway then a consultation in low voice and then two man be detach from the rest , and send out of the churchyard through the crowd . the crowd draw back on either side in dead silence to let they pass . 

 after a while a great shudder run through the people , and the live lane widen slowly . the man come back along it with a door from one of the empty house . they carry it to the vestry and go in . the police close again round the doorway , and man steal out from among the crowd by two and three and stand behind they to be the first to see . other wait near to be the first to hear . woman and child be among these last . 

 the tiding from the vestry begin to flow out among the crowd they drop slowly from mouth to mouth till they reach the place where I be stand . I hear the question and answer repeat again and again in low , eager tone all round I . 

 " have they find he ? " " yes . " " where ? " " against the door , on his face . " " which door ? " " the door that go into the church . his head be against it he be down on his face . " " be his face burn ? " " no . " " yes , it be . " " no , scorch , not burn he lie on his face , I tell you . " " who be he ? a lord , they say . " " no , not a lord . SIR Something ; Sir mean Knight . " " and Baronight , too . " " no . " " yes , it do . " " what do he want in there ? " " no good , you may depend on it . " " do he do it on purpose ? " " burn himself on purpose ! " " I do not   mean himself , I mean the vestry . " " be he dreadful to look at ? " " dreadful ! " " not about the face , though ? " " no , no , not so much about the face . do not   anybody know he ? " " there be a man say he do . " " who ? " " a servant , they say . but he be strike stupid - like , and the police do not   believe he . " " do not   anybody else know who it be ? " " hush ! " 

 the loud , clear voice of a man in authority silence the low hum of talk all round I in an instant . 

 " where be the gentleman who try to save he ? " say the voice . 

 " here , sir here he be ! " dozen of eager face press about I dozen of eager arm part the crowd . the man in authority come up to I with a lantern in his hand . 

 " this way , sir , if you please , " he say quietly . 

 I be unable to speak to he , I be unable to resist he when he take my arm . I try to say that I have never see the dead man in his lifetime that there be no hope of identify he by mean of a stranger like I . but the word fail on my lip . I be faint , and silent , and helpless . 

 " do you know he , sir ? " 

 I be stand inside a circle of man . three of they opposite to I be hold lantern low down to the ground . their eye , and the eye of all the rest , be fix silently and expectantly on my face . I know what be at my foot I know why they be hold the lantern so low to the ground . 

 " can you identify he , sir ? " 

 my eye drop slowly . at first I see nothing under they but a coarse canvas cloth . the dripping of the rain on it be audible in the dreadful silence . I look up , along the cloth , and there at the end , stark and grim and black , in the yellow light there be his dead face . 

 so , for the first and last time , I see he . so the Visitation of God rule it that he and I should meet . 



 XI 

 the inquest be hurry for certain local reason which weigh with the coroner and the town authority . it be hold on the afternoon of the next day . I be necessarily one among the witness summon to assist the object of the investigation . 

 my first proceeding in the morning be to go to the post - office , and inquire for the letter which I expect from Marian . no change of circumstance , however extraordinary , could affect the one great anxiety which weigh on my mind while I be away from London . the morning 's letter , which be the only assurance I could receive that no misfortune have happen in my absence , be still the absorbing interest with which my day begin . 

 to my relief , the letter from Marian be at the office wait for I . 

 nothing have happen they be both as safe and as well as when I have leave they . Laura send her love , and beg that I would let she know of my return a day beforehand . her sister add , in explanation of this message , that she have save " nearly a sovereign " out of her own private purse , and that she have claim the privilege of order the dinner and give the dinner which be to celebrate the day of my return . I read these little domestic confidence in the bright morning with the terrible recollection of what have happen the evening before vivid in my memory . the necessity of spar Laura any sudden knowledge of the truth be the first consideration which the letter suggest to I . I write at once to Marian to tell she what I have tell in these page present the tiding as gradually and gently as I could , and warn she not to let any such thing as a newspaper fall in Laura 's way while I be absent . in the case of any other woman , less courageous and less reliable , I might have hesitate before I venture on unreservedly disclose the whole truth . but I owe it to Marian to be faithful to my past experience of she , and to trust she as I trust herself . 

 my letter be necessarily a long one . it occupy I until the time come for proceed to the inquest . 

 the object of the legal inquiry be necessarily beset by peculiar complication and difficulty . besides the investigation into the manner in which the deceased have meet his death , there be serious question to be settle relate to the cause of the fire , to the abstraction of the key , and to the presence of a stranger in the vestry at the time when the flame break out . even the identification of the dead man have not yet be accomplish . the helpless condition of the servant have make the police distrustful of his asserted recognition of his master . they have send to Knowlesbury overnight to secure the attendance of witness who be well acquainted with the personal appearance of Sir Percival Glyde , and they have communicate , the first thing in the morning , with Blackwater Park . these precaution enable the coroner and jury to settle the question of identity , and to confirm the correctness of the servant 's assertion ; the evidence offer by competent witness , and by the discovery of certain fact , be subsequently strengthen by an examination of the dead man 's watch . the crest and the name of Sir Percival Glyde be engrave inside it . 

 the next inquiry relate to the fire . 

 the servant and I , and the boy who have hear the light strike in the vestry , be the first witness call . the boy give his evidence clearly enough , but the servant 's mind have not yet recover the shock inflict on it he be plainly incapable of assist the object of the inquiry , and he be desire to stand down . 

 to my own relief , my examination be not a long one . I have not know the deceased I have never see he I be not aware of his presence at Old Welmingham and I have not be in the vestry at the finding of the body . all I could prove be that I have stop at the clerk 's cottage to ask my way that I have hear from he of the loss of the key that I have accompany he to the church to render what help I could that I have see the fire that I have hear some person unknown , inside the vestry , try vainly to unlock the door and that I have do what I could , from motif of humanity , to save the man . other witness , who have be acquaint with the deceased , be ask if they could explain the mystery of his presume abstraction of the key , and his presence in the burn room . but the coroner seem to take it for grant , naturally enough , that I , as a total stranger in the neighbourhood , and a total stranger to Sir Percival Glyde , could not be in a position to offer any evidence on these two point . 

 the course that I be myself bind to take , when my formal examination have close , seem clear to I . I do not feel call on to volunteer any statement of my own private conviction ; in the first place , because my do so could serve no practical purpose , now that all proof in support of any surmise of mine be burn with the burn register ; in the second place , because I could not have intelligibly state my opinion my unsupported opinion without disclose the whole story of the conspiracy , and produce beyond a doubt the same unsatisfactory effect an the mind of the coroner and the jury , which I have already produce on the mind of Mr. Kyrle . 

 in these page , however , and after the time that have now elapse , no such caution and restraint as be here describe need fetter the free expression of my opinion . I will state briefly , before my pen occupy itself with other event , how my own conviction lead I to account for the abstraction of the key , for the outbreak of the fire , and for the death of the man . 

 the news of my be free on bail drive Sir Percival , as I believe , to his last resource . the attempt attack on the road be one of those resource , and the suppression of all practical proof of his crime , by destroy the page of the register on which the forgery have be commit , be the other , and the sure of the two . if I could produce no extract from the original book to compare with the certify copy at Knowlesbury , I could produce no positive evidence , and could threaten he with no fatal exposure . all that be necessary to the attainment of his end be , that he should get into the vestry unperceive , that he should tear out the page in the register , and that he should leave the vestry again as privately as he have enter it . 

 on this supposition , it be easy to understand why he wait until nightfall before he make the attempt , and why he take advantage of the clerk 's absence to possess himself of the key . necessity would oblige he to strike a light to find his way to the right register , and common caution would suggest his lock the door on the inside in case of intrusion on the part of any inquisitive stranger , or on my part , if I happen to be in the neighbourhood at the time . 

 I can not believe that it be any part of his intention to make the destruction of the register appear to be the result of accident , by purposely set the vestry on fire . the bare chance that prompt assistance might arrive , and that the book might , by the remotest possibility , be save , would have be enough , on a moment 's consideration , to dismiss any idea of this sort from his mind . remember the quantity of combustible object in the vestry the straw , the paper , the packing - case , the dry wood , the old worm - eat press all the probability , in my estimation , point to the fire as the result of an accident with his match or his light . 

 his first impulse , under these circumstance , be doubtless to try to extinguish the flame , and fail in that , his second impulse   ignorant as he be of the state of the lock   have be to attempt to escape by the door which have give he entrance . when I have call to he , the flame must have reach across the door lead into the church , on either side of which the press extend , and close to which the other combustible object be place . in all probability , the smoke and flame   confine as they be to the room   have be too much for he when he try to escape by the inner door . he must have drop in his death - swoon he must have sink in the place where he be find just as I get on the roof to break the skylight window . even if we have be able , afterwards , to get into the church , and to burst open the door from that side , the delay must have be fatal . he would have be past saving , long past saving , by that time . we should only have give the flame free ingress into the church the church , which be now preserve , but which , in that event , would have share the fate of the vestry . there be no doubt in my mind , there can be no doubt in the mind of any one , that he be a dead man before ever we get to the empty cottage , and work with might and main to tear down the beam . 

 this be the near approach that any theory of mine can make towards account for a result which be visible matter of fact . as I have describe they , so event pass to we outside . as I have relate it , so his body be find . 



 the inquest be adjourn over one day no explanation that the eye of the law could recognise have be discover thus far to account for the mysterious circumstance of the case . 

 it be arrange that more witness should be summon , and that the London solicitor of the deceased should be invite to attend . a medical man be also charge with the duty of report on the mental condition of the servant , which appear at present to debar he from give any evidence of the least importance . he could only declare , in a daze way , that he have be order , on the night of the fire , to wait in the lane , and that he know nothing else , except that the decease be certainly his master . 

 my own impression be , that he have be first use   without any guilty knowledge on his own part   to ascertain the fact of the clerk 's absence from home on the previous day , and that he have be afterwards order to wait near the church   but out of sight of the vestry   to assist his master , in the event of my escape the attack on the road , and of a collision occur between Sir Percival and myself . it be necessary to add , that the man 's own testimony be never obtain to confirm this view . the medical report of he declare that what little mental faculty he possess be seriously shake ; nothing satisfactory be extract from he at the adjourned inquest , and for aught I know to the contrary , he may never have recover to this day . 

 I return to the hotel at Welmingham so jade in body and mind , so weakened and depress by all that I have go through , as to be quite unfit to endure the local gossip about the inqu , and to answer the trivial question that the talker address to I in the coffee - room . I withdraw from my scanty dinner to my cheap garret - chamber to secure myself a little quiet , and to think undisturbed of Laura and Marian . 

 if I have be a rich man I would have go back to London , and would have comfort myself with a sight of the two dear face again that night . but I be bind to appear , if call on , at the adjourn inquest , and doubly bind to answer my bail before the magistrate at Knowlesbury . our slender resource have suffer already , and the doubtful future more doubtful than ever now make I dread decrease our mean unnecessarily by allow myself an indulgence even at the small cost of a double railway journey in the carriage of the second class . 

 the next day the day immediately follow the inquest be leave at my own disposal . I begin the morning by again apply at the post - office for my regular report from Marian . it be wait for I as before , and it be write throughout in good spirit . I read the letter thankfully , and then set forth with my mind at ease for the day to go to Old Welmingham , and to view the scene of the fire by the morning light . 

 what change meet I when I get there ! 

 through all the way of our unintelligible world the trivial and the terrible walk hand in hand together . the irony of circumstance hold no mortal catastrophe in respect . when I reach the church , the trample condition of the burial - ground be the only serious trace leave to tell of the fire and the death . a rough hoarding of board have be knock up before the vestry doorway . rude caricature be scrawl on it already , and the village child be fight and shout for the possession of the good peep - hole to see through . on the spot where I have hear the cry for help from the burn room , on the spot where the panic - stricken servant have drop on his knee , a fussy flock of poultry be now scramble for the first choice of worm after the rain ; and on the ground at my foot , where the door and its dreadful burden have be lay , a workman 's dinner be wait for he , tie up in a yellow basin , and his faithful cur in charge be yelp at I for come near the food . the old clerk , look idly at the slow commencement of the repair , have only one interest that he could talk about now the interest of escape all blame for his own part on account of the accident that have happen . one of the village woman , whose white wild face I remember the picture of terror when we pull down the beam , be giggle with another woman , the picture of inanity , over an old washing - tub . there be nothing serious in mortality ! Solomon in all his glory be Solomon with the element of the contemptible lurking in every fold of his robe and in every corner of his palace . 

 as I leave the place , my thought turn , not for the first time , to the complete overthrow that all present hope of establish Laura 's identity have now suffer through Sir Percival 's death . he be go and with he the chance be go which have be the one object of all my labour and all my hope . 

 could I look at my failure from no truer point of view than this ? 

 suppose he have live , would that change of circumstance have alter the result ? could I have make my discovery a marketable commodity , even for Laura 's sake , after I have find out that robbery of the right of other be the essence of Sir Percival 's crime ? could I have offer the price of MY silence for his confession of the conspiracy , when the effect of that silence must have be to keep the right heir from the estate , and the right owner from the name ? impossible ! if Sir Percival have live , the discovery , from which   in my ignorance of the true nature of the Secret   I have hop so much , could not have be mine to suppress or to make public , as I think well , for the vindication of Laura 's right . in common honesty and common honour I must have go at once to the stranger whose birthright have be usurped I must have renounce the victory at the moment when it be mine by place my discovery unreservedly in that stranger 's hand and I must have face afresh all the difficulty which stand between I and the one object of my life , exactly as I be resolve in my heart of heart to face they now ! 

 I return to Welmingham with my mind compose , feel more sure of myself and my resolution than I have feel yet . 

 on my way to the hotel I pass the end of the square in which Mrs. Catherick live . should I go back to the house , and make another attempt to see she . no . that news of Sir Percival 's death , which be the last news she ever expect to hear , must have reach her hour since . all the proceeding at the inquest have be report in the local paper that morning there be nothing I could tell she which she do not know already . my interest in make her speak have slacken . I remember the furtive hatred in her face when she say , " there be no news of Sir Percival that I do not   expect except the news of his death . " I remember the stealthy interest in her eye when they settle on I at part , after she have speak those word . some instinct , deep in my heart , which I feel to be a true one , make the prospect of again enter her presence repulsive to I I turn away from the square , and go straight back to the hotel . 

 some hour later , while I be rest in the coffee - room , a letter be place in my hand by the waiter . it be address to I by name , and I find on inquiry that it have be leave at the bar by a woman just as it be near dusk , and just before the gas be light . she have say nothing , and she have go away again before there be time to speak to she , or even to notice who she be . 

 I open the letter . it be neither date nor sign , and the handwriting be palpably disguise . before I have read the first sentence , however , I know who my correspondent be Mrs. Catherick . 

 the letter run as follow I copy it exactly , word for word : 




 the story continue by MRS . CATHERICK 

 SIR , you have not come back , as you say you would . no matter I know the news , and I write to tell you so . do you see anything particular in my face when you leave I ? I be wonder , in my own mind , whether the day of his downfall have come at last , and whether you be the choose instrument for work it . you be , and you have work it . 

 you be weak enough , as I have hear , to try and save his life . if you have succeed , I should have look upon you as my enemy . now you have fail , I hold you as my friend . your inquiry frighten he into the vestry by night your inquiry , without your privity and against your will , have serve the hatred and wreak the vengeance of three - and - twenty year . thank you , sir , in spite of yourself . 

 I owe something to the man who have do this . how can I pay my debt ? if I be a young woman still I might say , " come , put your arm round my waist , and kiss I , if you like . " I should have be fond enough of you even to go that length , and you would have accept my invitation you would , sir , twenty year ago ! but I be an old woman now . well ! I can satisfy your curiosity , and pay my debt in that way . you had a great curiosity to know certain private affair of mine when you come to see I private affair which all your sharpness could not look into without my help private affair which you have not discover , even now . you shall discover they your curiosity shall be satisfied . I will take any trouble to please you , my estimable young friend ! 

 you be a little boy , I suppose , in the year twenty - seven ? I be a handsome young woman at that time , live at Old Welmingham . I have a contemptible fool for a husband . I have also the honour of be acquaint   never mind how   with a certain gentleman   never mind whom . I shall not call he by his name . why should I ? it be not his own . he never have a name : you know that , by this time , as well as I do . 

 it will be more to the purpose to tell you how he work himself into my good grace . I be bear with the taste of a lady , and he gratify they in other word , he admire I , and he make I present . no woman can resist admiration and present especially present , provide they happen to be just the thing she want . he be sharp enough to know that most man be . naturally he want something in return all man do . and what do you think be the something ? the mere trifle . nothing but the key of the vestry , and the key of the press inside it , when my husband 's back be turn . of course he lie when I ask he why he wish I to get he the key in that private way . he might have save himself the trouble I do not   believe he . but I like my present , and I want more . so I get he the key , without my husband 's knowledge , and I watch he , without his own knowledge . once , twice , four time I watch he , and the fourth time I find he out . 

 I be never over - scrupulous where other people 's affair be concern , and I be not over - scrupulous about his add one to the marriage in the register on his own account . 

 of course I know it be wrong , but it do no harm to I , which be one good reason for not make a fuss about it . and I have not get a gold watch and chain , which be another , still well and he have promise I one from London only the day before , which be a third , good of all . if I have know what the law consider the crime to be , and how the law punish it , I should have take proper care of myself , and have expose he then and there . but I know nothing , and I long for the gold watch . all the condition I insist on be that he should take I into his confidence and tell I everything . I be as curious about his affair then as you be about mine now . he grant my condition why , you will see presently . 

 this , put in short , be what I hear from he . he do not willingly tell I all that I tell you here . I draw some of it from he by persuasion and some of it by question . I be determined to have all the truth , and I believe I get it . 

 he know no more than any one else of what the state of thing really be between his father and mother till after his mother 's death . then his father confess it , and promise to do what he could for his son . he die have do nothing not have even make a will . the son   who can blame he ?   wisely provide for himself . he come to England at once , and take possession of the property . there be no one to suspect he , and no one to say he nay . his father and mother have always live as man and wife none of the few people who be acquaint with they ever suppose they to be anything else . the right person to claim the property   if the truth have be know   be a distant relation , who have no idea of ever get it , and who be away at sea when his father die . he have no difficulty so far he take possession , as a matter of course . but he could not borrow money on the property as a matter of course . there be two thing want of he before he could do this . one be a certificate of his birth , and the other be a certificate of his parent ' marriage . the certificate of his birth be easily get he be bear abroad , and the certificate be there in due form . the other matter be a difficulty , and that difficulty bring he to Old Welmingham . 

 but for one consideration he might have go to Knowlesbury instead . 

 his mother have be live there just before she meet with his father live under her maiden name , the truth be that she be really a married woman , marry in Ireland , where her husband have ill - use she , and have afterwards go off with some other person . I give you this fact on good authority Sir Felix mention it to his son as the reason why he have not marry . you may wonder why the son , know that his parent have meet each other at Knowlesbury , do not play his first trick with the register of that church , where it might have be fairly presume his father and mother be marry . the reason be that the clergyman who do duty at Knowlesbury church , in the year eighteen hundred and three   when , accord to his birth certificate , his father and mother ought to have be marry , be alive still when he take possession of the property in the New Year of eighteen hundred and twenty - seven . this awkward circumstance force he to extend his inquiry to our neighbourhood . there no such danger exist , the former clergyman at our church have be dead for some year . 

 Old Welmingham suit his purpose as well as Knowlesbury . his father have remove his mother from Knowlesbury , and have live with she at a cottage on the river , a little distance from our village . People who have know his solitary way when he be single do not wonder at his solitary way when he be suppose to be marry . if he have not be a hideous creature to look at , his retired life with the lady might have raise suspicion ; but , as thing be , his hide his ugliness and his deformity in the strict privacy surprised nobody . he live in our neighbourhood till he come in possession of the Park . after three or four and twenty year have pass , who be to say   the clergyman be dead   that his marriage have not be as private as the rest of his life , and that it have not take place at Old Welmingham church ? 

 so , as I tell you , the son find our neighbourhood the sure place he could choose to set thing right secretly in his own interest . it may surprise you to hear that what he really do to the marriage register be do on the spur of the moment do on second thought . 

 his first notion be only to tear the leaf out   in the right year and month , to destroy it privately , to go back to London , and to tell the lawyer to get he the necessary certificate of his father 's marriage , innocently refer they of course to the date on the leaf that be go . nobody could say his father and mother have not be marry after that , and whether , under the circumstance , they would stretch a point or not about lend he the money   he think they would , he have his answer ready at all event , if a question be ever raise about his right to the name and the estate . 

 but when he come to look privately at the register for himself , he find at the bottom of one of the page for the year eighteen hundred and three a blank space leave , seemingly through there be no room to make a long entry there , which be make instead at the top of the next page . the sight of this chance alter all his plan . it be an opportunity he have never hop for , or thought of and he take it you know how . the blank space , to have exactly tally with his birth certificate , ought to have occur in the July part of the register . it occur in the September part instead . however , in this case , if suspicious question be ask , the answer be not hard to find . he have only to describe himself as a seven month ' child . 

 I be fool enough , when he tell I his story , to feel some interest and some pity for he which be just what he calculate on , as you will see . I think he hardly use . it be not his fault that his father and mother be not marry , and it be not his father 's and mother 's fault either . a more scrupulous woman than I be a woman who have not set her heart on a gold watch and chain would have find some excuse for he . at all event , I hold my tongue , and help to screen what he be about . 

 he be some time get the ink the right colour   mix it over and over again in pot and bottle of mine , and some time afterwards in practise the handwriting . but he succeed in the end , and make an honest woman of his mother after she be dead in her grave ! so far , I do not   deny that he behave honourably enough to myself . he give I my watch and chain , and spar no expense in buy they ; both be of superior workmanship , and very expensive . I have get they still the watch go beautifully . 

 you say the other day that Mrs. Clements have tell you everything she know . in that case there be no need for I to write about the trumpery scandal by which I be the sufferer the innocent sufferer , I positively assert . you must know as well as I do what the notion be which my husband take into his head when he find I and my fine - gentleman acquaintance meet each other privately and talk secret together . but what you do not   know be how it end between that same gentleman and myself . you shall read and see how he behave to I . 

 the first word I say to he , when I see the turn thing have take , be , " do I justice clear my character of a stain on it which you know I do not   deserve . I do not   want you to make a clean breast of it to my husband only tell he , on your word of honour as a gentleman , that he be wrong , and that I be not to blame in the way he think I be . do I that justice , at least , after all I have do for you . " he flatly refuse , in so many word . he tell I plainly that it be his interest to let my husband and all my neighbour believe the falsehood because , as long as they do so they be quite certain never to suspect the truth . I have a spirit of my own , and I tell he they should know the truth from my lip . his reply be short , and to the point . if I speak , I be a lost woman , as certainly as he be a lost man . 

 yes ! it have come to that . he have deceive I about the risk I run in help he . he have practise on my ignorance , he have tempt I with his gift , he have interest I with his story and the result of it be that he make I his accomplice . he own this coolly , and he end by tell I , for the first time , what the frightful punishment really be for his offence , and for any one who help he to commit it . in those day the law be not so tender - hearted as I hear it be now . murderer be not the only people liable to be hang , and woman convict be not treat like lady in undeserved distress . I confess he frighten I the mean impostor ! the cowardly blackguard ! do you understand now how I hat he ? do you understand why I be take all this trouble thankfully take it to gratify the curiosity of the meritorious young gentleman who hunt he down ? 

 well , to go on . he be hardly fool enough to drive I to downright desperation . I be not the sort of woman whom it be quite safe to hunt into a corner he know that , and wisely quiet I with proposal for the future . 

 I deserve some reward   he be kind enough to say   for the service I have do he , and some compensation   he be so obliging as to add   for what I have suffer . he be quite willing generous scoundrel ! to make I a handsome yearly allowance , payable quarterly , on two condition . first , I be to hold my tongue in my own interest as well as in his . secondly , I be not to stir away from Welmingham without first let he know , and wait till I have obtain his permission . in my own neighbourhood , no virtuous female friend would tempt I into dangerous gossiping at the tea - table . in my own neighbourhood , he would always know where to find I . a hard condition , that second one but I accept it . 

 what else be I to do ? I be leave helpless , with the prospect of a come incumbrance in the shape of a child . what else be I to do ? cast myself on the mercy of my runaway idiot of a husband who have raise the scandal against I ? I would have die first . besides , the allowance be a handsome one . I have a well income , a well house over my head , well carpet on my floor , than half the woman who turn up the white of their eye at the sight of I . the dress of Virtue , in our part , be cotton print . I have silk . 

 so I accept the condition he offer I , and make the good of they , and fight my battle with my respectable neighbour on their own ground , and win it in course of time as you see yourself . how I keep his Secret   and mine   through all the year that have pass from that time to this , and whether my late daughter , Anne , ever really creep into my confidence , and get the keeping of the Secret too be question , I dare say , to which you be curious to find an answer . well ! my gratitude refuse you nothing . I will turn to a fresh page and give you the answer immediately . but you must excuse one thing you must excuse my beginning , Mr. Hartright , with an expression of surprise at the interest which you appear to have feel in my late daughter . it be quite unaccountable to I . if that interest make you anxious for any particular of her early life , I must refer you to Mrs. Clements , who know more of the subject than I do . pray understand that I do not profess to have be at all overfond of my late daughter . she be a worry to I from first to last , with the additional disadvantage of be always weak in the head . you like candour , and I hope this satisfy you . 

 there be no need to trouble you with many personal particular relate to those past time . it will be enough to say that I observe the term of the bargain on my side , and that I enjoy my comfortable income in return , pay quarterly . 

 now and then I get away and change the scene for a short time , always ask leave of my lord and master first , and generally get it . he be not , as I have already tell you , fool enough to drive I too hard , and he could reasonably rely on my hold my tongue for my own sake , if not for his . one of my long trip away from home be the trip I take to Limmeridge to nurse a half - sister there , who be die . she be report to have save money , and I think it as well   in case any accident happen to stop my allowance   to look after my own interest in that direction . as thing turn out , however , my pain be all throw away , and I get nothing , because nothing be to be have . 

 I have take Anne to the north with I , have my whim and fancy , occasionally , about my child , and get , at such time , jealous of Mrs. Clements ' influence over she . I never like Mrs. Clements . she be a poor , empty - headed , spiritless woman what you call a bear drudge and I be now and then not averse to plague she by take Anne away . not know what else to do with my girl while I be nurse in Cumberland , I put she to school at Limmeridge . the lady of the manor , Mrs. Fairlie   a remarkably plain - look woman , who have entrap one of the handsome man in England into marry she , amuse I wonderfully by take a violent fancy to my girl . the consequence be , she learn nothing at school , and be pet and spoil at Limmeridge House . among other whim and fancy which they teach she there , they put some nonsense into her head about always wear white . hat white and like colour myself , I determine to take the nonsense out of her head as soon as we get home again . 

 strange to say , my daughter resolutely resist I . when she have get a notion once fix in her mind she be , like other half - witted people , as obstinate as a mule in keep it . we quarrel finely , and Mrs. Clements , not like to see it , I suppose , offer to take Anne away to live in London with she . I should have say yes , if Mrs. Clements have not side with my daughter about she dress herself in white . but be determine she should not dress herself in white , and dislike Mrs. Clements more than ever for take part against I , I say no , and mean no , and stick to no . the consequence be , my daughter remain with I , and the consequence of that , in its turn , be the first serious quarrel that happen about the Secret . 

 the circumstance take place long after the time I have just be write of . I have my feeling as a mother , and my character to preserve in the town , and I will submit to nothing but a private Establishment , of the sort which my genteel neighbour would choose for afflicted relative of their own .  have I satisfy your curiosity ?  perhaps , after what I have just say , you will see the necessity of write I an apology . do so , and I will willingly receive it . I will , afterwards , if your wish point to a second interview with I , go a step far , and receive you . my circumstance only enable I to invite you to tea not that they be at all alter for the bad by what have happen . I propose to join the Dorcas Society , and I mean to make the clergyman 's wife bow to I next . 

 if you favour I with your company , pray understand that the conversation must be entirely on general subject . any attempt reference to this letter will be quite useless I be determined not to acknowledge have write it . the evidence have be destroy in the fire , I know , but I think it desirable to err on the side of caution , nevertheless .

 the story continued BY WALTER HARTRIGHT 



 I 

 my first impulse , after read Mrs. Catherick 's extraordinary narrative , be to destroy it . the hardened shameless depravity of the whole composition , from begin to end the atrocious perversity of mind which persistently associate I with a calamity for which I be in no sense answerable , and with a death which I have risk my life in try to avert so disgusted I , that I be on the point of tear the letter , when a consideration suggest itself which warn I to wait a little before I destroy it . 

 this consideration be entirely unconnected with Sir Percival . the information communicate to I , so far as it concern he , do little more than confirm the conclusion at which I have already arrive . 

 he have commit his offence , as I have suppose he to have commit it , and the absence of all reference , on Mrs. Catherick 's part , to the duplicate register at Knowlesbury , strengthen my previous conviction that the existence of the book , and the risk of detection which it imply , must have be necessarily unknown to Sir Percival . my interest in the question of the forgery be now at an end , and my only object in keep the letter be to make it of some future service in clear up the last mystery that still remain to baffle I the parentage of Anne Catherick on the father 's side . there be one or two sentence drop in her mother 's narrative , which it might be useful to refer to again , when matter of more immediate importance allow I leisure to search for the missing evidence . I do not despair of still find that evidence , and I have lose none of my anxiety to discover it , for I have lose none of my interest in trace the father of the poor creature who now lay at rest in Mrs. Fairlie 's grave . 

 accordingly , I seal up the letter and put it away carefully in my pocket - book , to be refer to again when the time come . 

 the next day be my last in Hampshire . when I have appear again before the magistrate at Knowlesbury , and when I have attend at the adjourn inquest , I should be free to return to London by the afternoon or the evening train .

 the news which those line contain news which I instantly associate with some attempt treachery on the part of Count Fosco fairly overwhelm I .  I hardly know to what forgetfulness of my obligation anxiety and alarm might not have tempt I , but for the quieting influence of my faith in Marian . my absolute reliance on she be the one earthly consideration which help I to restrain myself , and give I courage to wait . the inquest be the first of the impediment in the way of my freedom of action . I attend it at the appoint time , the legal formality require my presence in the room , but as it turn out , not call on I to repeat my evidence . this useless delay be a hard trial , although I do my good to quiet my impatience by follow the course of the proceeding as closely as I could . 

 the London solicitor of the deceased   Mr. Merriman   be among the person present . but he be quite unable to assist the object of the inquiry . he could only say that he be inexpressibly shock and astonish , and that he could throw no light whatever on the mysterious circumstance of the case . at interval during the adjourned investigation , he suggest question which the Coroner put , but which lead to no result . after a patient inquiry , which last nearly three hour , and which exhaust every available source of information , the jury pronounce the customary verdict in case of sudden death by accident . they add to the formal decision a statement , that there have be no evidence to show how the key have be abstract , how the fire have be cause , or what the purpose be for which the deceased have enter the vestry . this act close the proceeding . the legal representative of the dead man be leave to provide for the necessity of the interment , and the witness be free to retire . 

 resolve not to lose a minute in get to Knowlesbury , I pay my bill at the hotel , and hire a fly to take I to the town . a gentleman who hear I give the order , and who see that I be go alone , inform I that he live in the neighbourhood of Knowlesbury , and ask if I would have any objection to his get home by share the fly with I . I accept his proposal as a matter of course . 

 our conversation during the drive be naturally occupy by the one absorb subject of local interest . 

 my new acquaintance have some knowledge of the late Sir Percival 's solicitor , and he and Mr. Merriman have be discuss the state of the deceased gentleman 's affair and the succession to the property . Sir Percival 's embarrassment be so well know all over the county that his solicitor could only make a virtue of necessity and plainly acknowledge they . he have die without leave a will , and he have no personal property to bequeath , even if he have make one , the whole fortune which he have derive from his wife have be swallow up by his creditor . the heir to the estate   Sir Percival have leave no issue   be a son of Sir Felix Glyde 's first cousin , an officer in command of an East Indiaman . he would find his unexpected inheritance sadly encumber , but the property would recover with time , and , if " the captain " be careful , he might be a rich man yet before he die . 

 absorb as I be in the one idea of get to London , this information   which event prove to be perfectly correct   have an interest of its own to attract my attention . I think it justify I in keep secret my discovery of Sir Percival 's fraud . the heir , whose right he have usurp , be the heir who would now have the estate . the income from it , for the last three - and - twenty year , which should properly have be his , and which the dead man have squander to the last farthing , be go beyond recall . if I speak , my speaking would confer advantage on no one . if I keep the secret , my silence conceal the character of the man who have cheat Laura into marry he . for her sake , I wish to conceal it for her sake , still , I tell this story under feigned name . 

 I part with my chance companion at Knowlesbury , and go at once to the town - hall . as I have anticipate , no one be present to prosecute the case against I the necessary formality be observe , and I be discharge . on leave the court a letter from Mr. Dawson be put into my hand . it inform I that he be absent on professional duty , and it reiterate the offer I have already receive from he of any assistance which I might require at his hand . I write back , warmly acknowledge my obligation to his kindness , and apologise for not express my thank personally , in consequence of my immediate recall on press business to town . 

 half an hour later I be speed back to London by the express train . 



 II 

 it be between nine and ten o'clock before I reach Fulham , and find my way to Gower 's Walk .

' if one can think at all , in serious difficulty , one think quick . " 

 " you remember what he say ? " 

 " I can not   repeat it , Walter . you shall know directly what he say about you -but I can not   repeat what he say to I . it be bad than the polite insolence of his letter . my hand tingle to strike he , as if I have be a man !  " yes ? " 

 " it be hard to acknowledge it , Walter , and yet I must . I be the only consideration . no word can say how degraded I feel in my own estimation when I think of it , but the one weak point in that man 's iron character be the horrible admiration he feel for I . " 

 " I believe it , Marian . the good man be not consistent in good why should the bad man be consistent in evil ? at the same time , I suspect he of merely attempt to frighten you , by threaten what he can not really do . " 

 " he speak last of you . his eye brighten and harden , and his manner change to what I remember it in past time to that mixture of pitiless resolution and mountebank mockery which make it so impossible to fathom he . ' warn Mr. Hartright ! ' he say in his lofty manner . ' he have a man of brain to deal with , a man who snap his big finger at the law and convention of society , when he measure himself with I . if my lamented friend have take my advice , the business of the inquest would have be with the body of Mr. Hartright . but my lamented friend be obstinate . see !  " without return ? without say more last word ? " 

 " he turn at the corner of the street , and wave his hand , and then strike it theatrically on his breast . " 

 " what make you think of come to this place ? " 

 " my ignorance of other locality in the neighbourhood of London . "

 " not many week have pass , Marian , " I answer , ' since my interview with Mr. Kyrle . when he and I part , the last word I say to he about Laura be these : ' her uncle 's house shall open to receive she , in the presence of every soul who follow the false funeral to the grave ; the lie that record her death shall be publicly erase from the tombstone by the authority of the head of the family , and the two man who have wrong she shall answer for their crime to I , though the justice that sit in tribunal be powerless to pursue they . ' one of those man be beyond mortal reach . the other remain , and my resolution remain . " 

 her eye light up her colour rise . she say nothing , but I see all her sympathy gather to mine in her face .
