I be not rash enough to measure myself against such a man as the Count before I be well prepared for he . I have learn patience I can wait my time . let he believe that his message have produce its effect let he know nothing of we , and hear nothing of we let we give he full time to feel secure his own boastful nature , unless I seriously mistake he , will hasten that result . this be one reason for waiting , but there be another more important still . my position , Marian , towards you and towards Laura ought to be a strong one than it be now before I try our last chance . " 

 she lean near to I , with a look of surprise . 

 " how can it be strong ? " she ask .

I may be silent about it to Laura for ever I must be silent now , even to you , till I see for myself that I can harmlessly and honourably speak . let we leave that subject . there be another which have more pressing claim on our attention . you have keep Laura , mercifully keep she , in ignorance of her husband 's death " 

 " oh , Walter , surely it must be long yet before we tell she of it ? " 

 " no , Marian . well that you should reveal it to she now , than that accident , which no one can guard against , should reveal it to she at some future time . spare she all the detail break it to her very tenderly , but tell she that he be dead . " 

 " you have a reason , Walter , for wish she to know of her husband 's death besides the reason you have just mention ? " 

 " I have . " 

 " a reason connect with that subject which must not be mention between we yet ? which may never be mention to Laura at all ? " 

 she dwelt on the last word meaningly . when I answer she in the affirmative , I dwelt on they too . 

 her face grow pale . for a while she look at I with a sad , hesitate interest . an unaccustomed tenderness tremble in her dark eye and soften her firm lip , as she glance aside at the empty chair in which the dear companion of all our joy and sorrow have be sit .

 she sigh , and hold my hand fast for a moment then drop it abruptly , and leave the room . on the next day Laura know that his death have release she , and that the error and the calamity of her life lie bury in his tomb . 


 his name be mention among we no more . Thenceforward , we shrink from the slight approach to the subject of his death , and in the same scrupulous manner , Marian and I avoid all further reference to that other subject , which , by her consent and mine , be not to be mention between we yet . it be not the less present in our mind it be rather keep alive in they by the restraint which we have impose on ourselves . we both watch Laura more anxiously than ever , sometimes wait and hop , sometimes wait and fear , till the time come . 

 by degree we return to our accustomed way of life . I resume the daily work , which have be suspend during my absence in Hampshire . our new lodging cost we more than the small and less convenient room which we have leave , and the claim thus imply on my increase exertion be strengthen by the doubtfulness of our future prospect . emergency might yet happen which would exhaust our little fund at the banker 's , and the work of my hand might be , ultimately , all we have to look to for support . more permanent and more lucrative employment than have yet be offer to I be a necessity of our position a necessity for which I now diligently set myself to provide . 

 it must not be suppose that the interval of rest and seclusion of which I be now write , entirely suspend , on my part , all pursuit of the one absorb purpose with which my thought and action be associate in these page . that purpose be , for month and month yet , never to relax its claim on I . the slow ripening of it still leave I a measure of precaution to take , an obligation of gratitude to perform , and a doubtful question to solve . 

 the measure of precaution relate , necessarily , to the Count . it be of the last importance to ascertain , if possible , whether his plan commit he to remain in England or , in other word , to remain within my reach . I contrive to set this doubt at rest by very simple mean . his address in St. John 's Wood be know to I , I inquire in the neighbourhood , and have find out the agent who have the disposal of the furnished house in which he live , I ask if number five , Forest Road , be likely to be let within a reasonable time . the reply be in the negative . I be inform that the foreign gentleman then reside in the house have renew his term of occupation for another six month , and would remain in possession until the end of June in the following year . we be then at the beginning of December only . I leave the agent with my mind relieve from all present fear of the Count 's escape I . 

 the obligation I have to perform take I once more into the presence of Mrs. Clements . I have promise to return , and to confide to she those particular relate to the death and burial of Anne Catherick which I have be oblige to withhold at our first interview . change as circumstance now be , there be no hindrance to my trust the good woman with as much of the story of the conspiracy as it be necessary to tell . I have every reason that sympathy and friendly feeling could suggest to urge on I the speedy performance of my promise , and I do conscientiously and carefully perform it . there be no need to burden these page with any statement of what pass at the interview . it will be more to the purpose to say , that the interview itself necessarily bring to my mind the one doubtful question still remain to be solve the question of Anne Catherick 's parentage on the father 's side . 

 a multitude of small consideration in connection with this subject trifle enough in themselves , but strikingly important when mass together have latterly lead my mind to a conclusion which I resolve to verify . I obtain Marian 's permission to write to Major Donthorne , of Varneck Hall   where Mrs. Catherick have live in service for some year previous to her marriage , to ask he certain question . I make the inquiry in Marian 's name , and describe they as relate to matter of personal history in her family , which might explain and excuse my application . when I write the letter I have no certain knowledge that Major Donthorne be still alive I despatch it on the chance that he might be live , and able and willing to reply . 

 after a lapse of two day proof come , in the shape of a letter , that the Major be live , and that he be ready to help we . 

 the idea in my mind when I write to he , and the nature of my inquiry will be easily infer from his reply . his letter answer my question by communicate these important fact 

 in the first place , " the late Sir Percival Glyde , of Blackwater Park , " have never set foot in Varneck Hall . the deceased gentleman be a total stranger to Major Donthorne , and to all his family . 

 in the second place , " the late Mr. Philip Fairlie , of Limmeridge House , " have be , in his young day , the intimate friend and constant guest of Major Donthorne . have refresh his memory by look back to old letter and other paper , the Major be in a position to say positively that Mr. Philip Fairlie be stay at Varneck Hall in the month of August , eighteen hundred and twenty - six , and that he remain there for the shooting during the month of September and part of October follow . he then leave , to the good of the Major 's belief , for Scotland , and do not return to Varneck Hall till after a lapse of time , when he reappear in the character of a newly - marry man . 

 take by itself , this statement be , perhaps , of little positive value , but take in connection with certain fact , every one of which either Marian or I know to be true , it suggest one plain conclusion that be , to our mind , irresistible . 

 Knowing , now , that Mr. Philip Fairlie have be at Varneck Hall in the autumn of eighteen hundred and twenty - six , and that Mrs. Catherick have be live there in service at the same time , we know also first , that Anne have be bear in June , eighteen hundred and twenty - seven ; secondly , that she have always present an extraordinary personal resemblance to Laura ; and , thirdly , that Laura herself be strikingly like her father . Mr. Philip Fairlie have be one of the notoriously handsome man of his time . in disposition entirely unlike his brother Frederick , he be the spoilt darling of society , especially of the woman an easy , light - hearte , impulsive , affectionate man generous to a fault constitutionally lax in his principle , and notoriously thoughtless of moral obligation where woman be concern . such be the fact we know such be the character of the man . surely the plain inference that follow need no pointing out ? 

 Read by the new light which have now break upon I , even Mrs. Catherick 's letter , in despite of herself , render its mite of assistance towards strengthen the conclusion at which I have arrive . she have describe Mrs. Fairlie   in write to I   as " plain - look , " and as have " entrap the handsome man in England into marry she . " both assertion be gratuitously make , and both be false . jealous dislike   which , in such a woman as Mrs. Catherick , would express itself in petty malice rather than not express itself at all   appear to I to be the only assignable cause for the peculiar insolence of her reference to Mrs. Fairlie , under circumstance which do not necessitate any reference at all . 

 the mention here of Mrs. Fairlie 's name naturally suggest one other question . do she ever suspect whose child the little girl bring to she at Limmeridge might be ? 

 Marian 's testimony be positive on this point . Mrs. Fairlie 's letter to her husband , which have be read to I in former day the letter describe Anne 's resemblance to Laura , and acknowledge her affectionate interest in the little stranger have be write , beyond all question , in perfect innocence of heart . it even seem doubtful , on consideration , whether Mr. Philip Fairlie himself have be near than his wife to any suspicion of the truth . the disgracefully deceitful circumstance under which Mrs. Catherick have marry , the purpose of concealment which the marriage be intend to answer , might well keep she silent for caution 's sake , perhaps for her own pride 's sake also , even assume that she have the mean , in his absence , of communicate with the father of her unborn child . 

 as this surmise float through my mind , there rise on my memory the remembrance of the Scripture denunciation which we have all thought of in our time with wonder and with awe : " the sin of the father shall be visit on the child . " but for the fatal resemblance between the two daughter of one father , the conspiracy of which Anne have be the innocent instrument and Laura the innocent victim could never have be plan . with what unerring and terrible directness the long chain of circumstance lead down from the thoughtless wrong commit by the father to the heartless injury inflict on the child ! 

 these thought come to I , and other with they , which draw my mind away to the little Cumberland churchyard where Anne Catherick now lie bury . I think of the bygone day when I have meet she by Mrs. Fairlie 's grave , and meet she for the last time . I think of her poor helpless hand beat on the tombstone , and her weary , yearn word , murmur to the dead remain of her protectress and her friend : " oh , if I could die , and be hide and at rest with you ! " little more than a year have pass since she breathe that wish ; and how inscrutably , how awfully , it have be fulfil ! the word she have speak to Laura by the shore of the lake , the very word have now come true . " oh , if I could only be bury with your mother ! if I could only wake at her side when the angel 's trumpet sound and the graf give up their dead at the resurrection ! " through what mortal crime and horror , through what darkest winding of the way down to death the lose creature have wander in God 's lead to the last home that , live , she never hop to reach ! in that sacred rest I leave she in that dread companionship let she remain undisturbed . 


 so the ghostly figure which have haunt these page , as it haunt my life , go down into the impenetrable gloom . like a shadow she first come to I in the loneliness of the night . like a shadow she pass away in the loneliness of the dead . 



 III 

 four month elapse . April come the month of spring the month of change . 

 the course of time have flow through the interval since the winter peacefully and happily in our new home . I have turn my long leisure to good account , have largely increase my source of employment , and have place our mean of subsistence on surer ground . free from the suspense and the anxiety which have try she so sorely and hang over her so long , Marian 's spirit rally , and her natural energy of character begin to assert itself again , with something , if not all , of the freedom and the vigour of former time . 

 more pliable under change than her sister , Laura show more plainly the progress make by the heal influence of her new life . the worn and waste look which have prematurely age her face be fast leave it , and the expression which have be the first of its charm in past day be the first of its beauty that now return . my close observation of she detect but one serious result of the conspiracy which have once threaten her reason and her life . her memory of event , from the period of she leave Blackwater Park to the period of our meeting in the burial - ground of Limmeridge Church , be lose beyond all hope of recovery . at the slight reference to that time she change and tremble still , her word become confused , her memory wander and lose itself as helplessly as ever . here , and here only , the trace of the past lie deep too deep to be efface . 

 in all else she be now so far on the way to recovery that , on her good and bright day , she sometimes look and speak like the Laura of old time . the happy change work its natural result in we both . from their long slumber , on her side and on mine , those imperishable memory of our past life in Cumberland now awake , which be one and all alike , the memory of our love . 

 gradually and insensibly our daily relation towards each other become constrain . the fond word which I have speak to she so naturally , in the day of her sorrow and her suffering , falter strangely on my lip . in the time when my dread of lose she be most present to my mind , I have always kiss she when she leave I at night and when she meet I in the morning . the kiss seem now to have drop between we to be lose out of our life . our hand begin to tremble again when they meet . we hardly ever look long at one another out of Marian 's presence . the talk often flag between we when we be alone . when I touch she by accident I feel my heart beat fast , as it use to beat at Limmeridge House I see the lovely answer flush glowing again in her cheek , as if we be back among the Cumberland Hills in our past character of master and pupil once more . she have long interval of silence and thoughtfulness , and deny she have be think when Marian ask she the question . I surprise myself one day neglect my work to dream over the little water - colour portrait of she which I have take in the summer - house where we first meet just as I use to neglect Mr. Fairlie 's drawing to dream over the same likeness when it be newly finish in the bygone time . change as all the circumstance now be , our position towards each other in the golden day of our first companionship seem to be revive with the revival of our love . it be as if Time have drift we back on the wreck of our early hope to the old familiar shore ! 

 to any other woman I could have speak the decisive word which I still hesitate to speak to she . the utter helplessness of her position her friendless dependence on all the forbear gentleness that I could show she my fear of touch too soon some secret sensitiveness in she which my instinct as a man might not have be fine enough to discover these consideration , and other like they , keep I self - distrustfully silent . and yet I know that the restraint on both side must be end , that the relation in which we stand towards one another must be alter in some settle manner for the future , and that it rest with I , in the first instance , to recognise the necessity for a change . 

 the more I think of our position , the hard the attempt to alter it appear , while the domestic condition on which we three have be live together since the winter remain undisturbed . I can not account for the capricious state of mind in which this feeling originate , but the idea nevertheless possess I that some previous change of place and circumstance , some sudden break in the quiet monotony of our life , so manage as to vary the home aspect under which we have be accustom to see each other , might prepare the way for I to speak , and might make it easy and less embarrassing for Laura and Marian to hear . 

 with this purpose in view , I say , one morning , that I think we have all earn a little holiday and a change of scene . after some consideration , it be decide that we should go for a fortnight to the sea - side . 

 on the next day we leave Fulham for a quiet town on the south coast . at that early season of the year we be the only visitor in the place . the cliff , the beach , and the walk inland be all in the solitary condition which be most welcome to we . the air be mild the prospect over hill and wood and down be beautifully vary by the shift April light and shade , and the restless sea leapt under our window , as if it feel , like the land , the glow and freshness of spring . 

 I owe it to Marian to consult she before I speak to Laura , and to be guide afterwards by her advice . 

 on the third day from our arrival I find a fit opportunity of speak to she alone . the moment we look at one another , her quick instinct detect the thought in my mind before I could give it expression . with her customary energy and directness she speak at once , and speak first . 

 " you be think of that subject which be mention between we on the evening of your return from Hampshire , " she say . " I have be expect you to allude to it for some time past . there must be a change in our little household , Walter , we can not go on much long as we be now . I see it as plainly as you do as plainly as Laura see it , though she say nothing . how strangely the old time in Cumberland seem to have come back ! you and I be together again , and the one subject of interest between we be Laura once more . I could almost fancy that this room be the summer - house at Limmeridge , and that those wave beyond we be beat on our sea - shore . " 

 " I be guide by your advice in those past day , " I say , " and now , Marian , with reliance tenfold great I will be guide by it again . " 

 she answer by press my hand . I see that she be deeply touch by my reference to the past . we sit together near the window , and while I speak and she listen , we look at the glory of the sunlight shin on the majesty of the sea . 

 " whatever come of this confidence between we , " I say , " whether it end happily or sorrowfully for I , Laura 's interest will still be the interest of my life . when we leave this place , on whatever term we leave it , my determination to wrest from Count Fosco the confession which I fail to obtain from his accomplice , go back with I to London , as certainly as I go back myself . neither you nor I can tell how that man may turn on I , if I bring he to bay ; we only know , by his own word and action , that he be capable of strike at I through Laura , without a moment 's hesitation , or a moment 's remorse . in our present position I have no claim on she which society sanction , which the law allow , to strengthen I in resist he , and in protect she . this place I at a serious disadvantage . if I be to fight our cause with the Count , strong in the consciousness of Laura 's safety , I must fight it for my Wife . do you agree to that , Marian , so far ? " 

 " to every word of it , " she answer . 

 " I will not plead out of my own heart , " I go on ; " I will not appeal to the love which have survive all change and all shock I will rest my only vindication of myself for thinking of she , and speak of she as my wife , on what I have just say . if the chance of force a confession from the Count be , as I believe it to be , the last chance leave of publicly establish the fact of Laura 's existence , the least selfish reason that I can advance for our marriage be recognise by we both . but I may be wrong in my conviction other mean of achieve our purpose may be in our power , which be less uncertain and less dangerous . I have search anxiously , in my own mind , for those mean , and I have not find they . have you ? " 

 " no . I have think about it too , and think in vain . " 

 " in all likelihood , " I continue , " the same question have occur to you , in consider this difficult subject , which have occur to I . ought we to return with she to Limmeridge , now that she be like herself again , and trust to the recognition of she by the people of the village , or by the child at the school ? ought we to appeal to the practical test of her handwriting ? suppose we do so . suppose the recognition of she obtain , and the identity of the handwriting establish . would success in both those case do more than supply an excellent foundation for a trial in a court of law ? would the recognition and the handwriting prove her identity to Mr. Fairlie and take she back to Limmeridge House , against the evidence of her aunt , against the evidence of the medical certificate , against the fact of the funeral and the fact of the inscription on the tomb ? no ! we could only hope to succeed in throw a serious doubt on the assertion of her death , a doubt which nothing short of a legal inquiry can settle . I will assume that we possess   what we have certainly not get   money enough to carry this inquiry on through all its stage . I will assume that Mr. Fairlie 's prejudice might be reason away that the false testimony of the Count and his wife , and all the rest of the false testimony , might be confute that the recognition could not possibly be ascribe to a mistake between Laura and Anne Catherick , or the handwriting be declare by our enemy to be a clever fraud all these be assumption which , more or less , set plain probability at defiance ; but let they pass and let we ask ourselves what would be the first consequence or the first question put to Laura herself on the subject of the conspiracy . we know only too well what the consequence would be , for we know that she have never recover her memory of what happen to she in London . examine she privately , or examine she publicly , she be utterly incapable of assist the assertion of her own case . if you do not   see this , Marian , as plainly as I see it , we will go to Limmeridge and try the experiment to - morrow . " 

 " I do see it , Walter . even if we have the mean of pay all the law expense , even if we succeed in the end , the delay would be unendurable , the perpetual suspense , after what we have suffer already , would be heartbreaking . you be right about the hopelessness of go to Limmeridge . I wish I could feel sure that you be right also in determine to try that last chance with the Count . be it a chance at all ? " 

 " beyond a doubt , yes . it be the chance of recover the lost date of Laura 's journey to London . without return to the reason I give you some time since , I be still as firmly persuade as ever that there be a discrepancy between the date of that journey and the date on the certificate of death . there lie the weak point of the whole conspiracy it crumble to piece if we attack it in that way , and the mean of attack it be in possession of the Count . if I succeed in wrest they from he , the object of your life and mine be fulfil . if I fail , the wrong that Laura have suffer will , in this world , never be redress . " 

 " do you fear failure yourself , Walter ? " 

 " I dare not anticipate success , and for that very reason , Marian , I speak openly and plainly as I have speak now . in my heart and my conscience I can say it , Laura 's hope for the future be at their low ebb . I know that her fortune be go I know that the last chance of restore she to her place in the world lie at the mercy of her bad enemy , of a man who be now absolutely unassailable , and who may remain unassailable to the end . with every worldly advantage go from she , with all prospect of recover her rank and station more than doubtful , with no clear future before she than the future which her husband can provide , the poor drawing - master may harmlessly open his heart at last . in the day of her prosperity , Marian , I be only the teacher who guide her hand I ask for it , in her adversity , as the hand of my wife ! " 

 Marian 's eye meet mine affectionately I could say no more . my heart be full , my lip be tremble . in spite of myself I be in danger of appeal to her pity . I get up to leave the room . she rise at the same moment , lay her hand gently on my shoulder , and stop I . 

 " Walter ! " she say , " I once part you both , for your good and for her . wait here , my brother ! wait , my dearest , good friend , till Laura come , and tell you what I have do now ! " 

 for the first time since the farewell morning at Limmeridge she touch my forehead with her lip . a tear drop on my face as she kiss I . she turn quickly , point to the chair from which I have rise , and leave the room . 

 I sit down alone at the window to wait through the crisis of my life . my mind in that breathless interval feel like a total blank . I be conscious of nothing but a painful intensity of all familiar perception . the sun grow blind bright , the white sea bird chase each other far beyond I seem to be flit before my face , the mellow murmur of the wave on the beach be like thunder in my ear . 

 the door open , and Laura come in alone . so she have enter the breakfast - room at Limmeridge House on the morning when we part . slowly and falteringly , in sorrow and in hesitation , she have once approach I . now she come with the haste of happiness in her foot , with the light of happiness radiant in her face . of their own accord those dear arm clasp themselves round I , of their own accord the sweet lip come to meet mine . " my darling ! " she whisper , " we may own we love each other now ? " her head nestle with a tender contentedness on my bosom . " oh , " she say innocently , " I be so happy at last ! " 


 ten day later we be happy still . we be marry . 



 IV 

 the course of this narrative , steadily flow on , bear I away from the morning - time of our married life , and carry I forward to the end . 

 in a fortnight more we three be back in London , and the shadow be steal over we of the struggle to come . 

 Marian and I be careful to keep Laura in ignorance of the cause that have hurry we back the necessity of make sure of the Count . it be now the beginning of May , and his term of occupation at the house in Forest Road expire in June . if he renew it   and I have reason , shortly to be mention , for anticipate that he would , I might be certain of his not escape I . but if by any chance he disappoint my expectation and leave the country , then I have no time to lose in arm myself to meet he as I good might . 

 in the first fulness of my new happiness , there have be moment when my resolution falter moment when I be tempt to be safely content , now that the dear aspiration of my life be fulfil in the possession of Laura 's love . for the first time I think faint - heartedly of the greatness of the risk , of the adverse chance array against I , of the fair promise of our new life , and of the peril in which I might place the happiness which we have so hardly earn . yes ! let I own it honestly . for a brief time I wander , in the sweet guiding of love , far from the purpose to which I have be true under sterner discipline and in dark day . innocently Laura have tempt I aside from the hard path innocently she be destine to lead I back again . 

 at time , dream of the terrible past still disconnectedly recall to she , in the mystery of sleep , the event of which her wake memory have lose all trace . one night   barely two week after our marriage , when I be watch she at rest , I see the tear come slowly through her closed eyelid , I hear the faint murmuring word escape she which tell I that her spirit be back again on the fatal journey from Blackwater Park . that unconscious appeal , so touching and so awful in the sacredness of her sleep , run through I like fire . the next day be the day we come back to London the day when my resolution return to I with tenfold strength . 

 the first necessity be to know something of the man . thus far , the true story of his life be an impenetrable mystery to I . 

 I begin with such scanty source of information as be at my own disposal . the important narrative write by Mr. Frederick Fairlie   which Marian have obtain by follow the direction I have give to she in the winter   prove to be of no service to the special object with which I now look at it . while read it I reconsider the disclosure reveal to I by Mrs. Clements of the series of deception which have bring Anne Catherick to London , and which have there devote she to the interest of the conspiracy . here , again , the Count have not openly commit himself here , again , he be , to all practical purpose , out of my reach . 

 I next return to Marian 's journal at Blackwater Park . at my request she read to I again a passage which refer to her past curiosity about the Count , and to the few particular which she have discover relate to he . 

 the passage to which I allude occur in that part of her journal which delineate his character and his personal appearance . she describe he as " not have cross the frontier of his native country for year past " as " anxious to know if any italian gentleman be settle in the near town to Blackwater Park " as " receive letter with all sort of odd stamp on they , and one with a large official - look seal on it . " she be inclined to consider that his long absence from his native country may be account for by assume that he be a political exile . but she be , on the other hand , unable to reconcile this idea with the reception of the letter from abroad bear " the large official - look seal " letter from the continent address to political exile be usually the last to court attention from foreign post - office in that way . 

 the consideration thus present to I in the diary , join to certain surmise of my own that grow out of they , suggest a conclusion which I wonder I have not arrive at before . I now say to myself what Laura have once say to Marian at Blackwater Park , what Madame Fosco have overhear by listen at the door the Count be a spy ! 

 Laura have apply the word to he at hazard , in natural anger at his proceeding towards herself . I apply it to he with the deliberate conviction that his vocation in life be the vocation of a spy . on this assumption , the reason for his extraordinary stay in England so long after the object of the conspiracy have be gain , became , to my mind , quite intelligible . 

 the year of which I be now write be the year of the famous Crystal Palace Exhibition in Hyde Park . foreigner in unusually large number have arrive already , and be still arrive in England . man be among we by hundred whom the ceaseless distrustfulness of their government have follow privately , by mean of appoint agent , to our shore . my surmise do not for a moment class a man of the Count 's ability and social position with the ordinary rank and file of foreign spy . I suspect he of hold a position of authority , of be entrust by the government which he secretly serve with the organisation and management of agent specially employ in this country , both man and woman , and I believe Mrs. Rubelle , who have be so opportunely find to act as nurse at Blackwater Park , to be , in all probability , one of the number . 

 assume that this idea of mine have a foundation in truth , the position of the Count might prove to be more assailable than I have hitherto venture to hope . to whom could I apply to know something more of the man 's history and of the man himself than I know now ? 

 in this emergency it naturally occur to my mind that a countryman of his own , on whom I could rely , might be the fit person to help I . the first man whom I think of under these circumstance be also the only italian with whom I be intimately acquaint my quaint little friend , Professor Pesca . 


 the professor have be so long absent from these page that he have run some risk of be forget altogether . 

 it be the necessary law of such a story as mine that the person concern in it only appear when the course of event take they up they come and go , not by favour of my personal partiality , but by right of their direct connection with the circumstance to be detail . for this reason , not Pesca alone , but my mother and sister as well , have be leave far in the background of the narrative . my visit to the Hampstead cottage , my mother 's belief in the denial of Laura 's identity which the conspiracy have accomplish , my vain effort to overcome the prejudice on her part and on my sister 's to which , in their jealous affection for I , they both continue to adhere , the painful necessity which that prejudice impose on I of conceal my marriage from they till they have learn to do justice to my wife all these little domestic occurrence have be leave unrecorded because they be not essential to the main interest of the story . it be nothing that they add to my anxiety and embitter my disappointment the steady march of event have inexorably pass they by . 

 for the same reason I have say nothing here of the consolation that I find in Pesca 's brotherly affection for I , when I see he again after the sudden cessation of my residence at Limmeridge House . I have not record the fidelity with which my warm - hearted little friend follow I to the place of embarkation when I sail for Central America , or the noisy transport of joy with which he receive I when we next meet in London . if I have feel justify in accept the offer of service which he make to I on my return , he would have appear again long ere this . but , though I know that his honour and his courage be to be implicitly rely on , I be not so sure that his discretion be to be trust , and , for that reason only , I follow the course of all my inquiry alone . it will now be sufficiently understand that Pesca be not separate from all connection with I and my interest , although he have hitherto be separate from all connection with the progress of this narrative . he be as true and as ready a friend of mine still as ever he have be in his life . 


 before I summon Pesca to my assistance it be necessary to see for myself what sort of man I have to deal with . up to this time I have never once set eye on Count Fosco . 

 three day after my return with Laura and Marian to London , I set forth alone for Forest Road , St. John 's Wood , between ten and eleven o'clock in the morning . it be a fine day I have some hour to spare and I think it likely , if I wait a little for he , that the Count might be tempt out . I have no great reason to fear the chance of his recognise I in the daytime , for the only occasion when I have be see by he be the occasion on which he have follow I home at night . 

 no one appear at the window in the front of the house . I walk down a turning which run past the side of it , and look over the low garden wall . one of the back window on the low floor be throw up and a net be stretch across the opening . I see nobody , but I hear , in the room , first a shrill whistling and singing of bird , then the deep ringing voice which Marian 's description have make familiar to I . " come out on my little finger , my pret - pret - prettie ! " cry the voice . " come out and hop upstairs ! one , two , three and up ! three , two , one and down ! one , two , three twit - twit - twit - tweet ! " the Count be exercise his canary as he use to exercise they in Marian 's time at Blackwater Park . 

 I wait a little while , and the singing and the whistling cease . " come , kiss I , my prettie ! " say the deep voice . there be a responsive twittering and chirp a low , oily laugh a silence of a minute or so , and then I hear the opening of the house door . I turn and retrace my step . the magnificent melody of the Prayer in Rossini 's Moses , sing in a sonorous bass voice , rise grandly through the suburban silence of the place . the front garden gate open and close . the Count have come out . 

 he cross the road and walk towards the western boundary of the Regent 's Park . I keep on my own side of the way , a little behind he , and walk in that direction also . 

 Marian have prepare I for his high stature , his monstrous corpulence , and his ostentatious mourning garment , but not for the horrible freshness and cheerfulness and vitality of the man . he carry his sixty year as if they have be few than forty . he saunter along , wear his hat a little on one side , with a light jaunty step , swing his big stick , hum to himself , look up from time to time at the house and garden on either side of he with superb , smile patronage . if a stranger have be tell that the whole neighbourhood belong to he , that stranger would not have be surprised to hear it . he never look back , he pay no apparent attention to I , no apparent attention to any one who pass he on his own side of the road , except now and then , when he smile and smirk , with an easy paternal good humour , at the nursery - maid and the child whom he meet . in this way he lead I on , till we reach a colony of shop outside the western terrace of the Park . 

 here he stop at a pastrycook 's , go in   probably to give an order , and come out again immediately with a tart in his hand . an Italian be grind an organ before the shop , and a miserable little shrivelled monkey be sit on the instrument . the Count stop , bit a piece for himself out of the tart , and gravely hand the rest to the monkey . " my poor little man ! " he say , with grotesque tenderness , " you look hungry . in the sacred name of humanity , I offer you some lunch ! " the organ - grinder piteously put in his claim to a penny from the benevolent stranger . the Count shrug his shoulder contemptuously , and pass on . 

 we reach the street and the well class of shop between the New Road and Oxford Street . the Count stop again and enter a small optician 's shop , with an inscription in the window announce that repair be neatly execute inside . he come out again with an opera - glass in his hand , walk a few pace on , and stop to look at a bill of the opera place outside a music - seller 's shop . he read the bill attentively , consider a moment , and then hail an empty cab as it pass he . " Opera Box - office , " he say to the man , and be drive away . 

 I cross the road , and look at the bill in my turn . the performance announce be Lucrezia Borgia , and it be to take place that evening . the opera - glass in the Count 's hand , his careful reading of the bill , and his direction to the cabman , all suggest that he propose make one of the audience . I have the mean of get an admission for myself and a friend to the pit by apply to one of the scene - painter attach to the theatre , with whom I have be well acquaint in past time . there be a chance at least that the Count might be easily visible among the audience to I and to any one with I , and in this case I have the mean of ascertain whether Pesca know his countryman or not that very night . 

 this consideration at once decide the disposal of my evening . I procure the ticket , leave a note at the Professor 's lodging on the way . at a quarter to eight I call to take he with I to the theatre . my little friend be in a state of the high excitement , with a festive flower in his button - hole , and the large opera - glass I ever see hug up under his arm . 

 " be you ready ? " I ask . 

 " right - all - right , " say Pesca . 

 we start for the theatre .

 the last note of the introduction to the opera be be play , and the seat in the pit be all fill , when Pesca and I reach the theatre .

I place myself exactly on a line with he . Pesca stand by my side . the Professor be not yet aware of the purpose for which I have bring he to the theatre , and he be rather surprised that we do not move near to the stage . 

 the curtain rise , and the opera begin . 

 throughout the whole of the first act we remain in our position the Count , absorb by the orchestra and the stage , never cast so much as a chance glance at we . not a note of Donizetti 's delicious music be lose on he . there he sit , high above his neighbour , smile , and nod his great head enjoyingly from time to time . when the people near he applaud the close of an air   as an english audience in such circumstance always will applaud , without the least consideration for the orchestral movement which immediately follow it , he look round at they with an expression of compassionate remonstrance , and hold up one hand with a gesture of polite entreaty . at the more refined passage of the singing , at the more delicate phase of the music , which pass unapplauded by other , his fat hand , adorn with perfectly - fitting black kid glove , softly pat each other , in token of the cultivate appreciation of a musical man . at such time , his oily murmur of approval , " Bravo ! Bra - a - a - a ! " hum through the silence , like the purring of a great cat . his immediate neighbour on either side hearty , ruddy - face people from the country , bask amazedly in the sunshine of fashionable London see and hear he , begin to follow his lead . many a burst of applause from the pit that night start from the soft , comfortable patting of the black - gloved hand . the man 's voracious vanity devour this imply tribute to his local and critical supremacy with an appearance of the high relish . smile ripple continuously over his fat face . he look about he , at the pause in the music , serenely satisfied with himself and his fellow - creature . " yes ! yes ! these barbarous english people be learn something from I . here , there , and everywhere , I Fosco be an influence that be feel , a man who sit supreme ! " if ever face speak , his face speak then , and that be its language .

 he rise with the rest , and survey the occupant of the box grandly with his opera - glass . at first his back be towards we , but he turn round in time , to our side of the theatre , and look at the box above we , use his glass for a few minute then remove it , but still continue to look up . this be the moment I choose , when his full face be in view , for direct Pesca 's attention to he .

 " which man , my friend ? " 

 " the tall , fat man , stand there , with his face towards we . "

 the eye of the two Italians meet .

 know he , and more surprising still fear he as well ! there be no mistake the change that pass over the villain 's face . the leaden hue that alter his yellow complexion in a moment , the sudden rigidity of all his feature , the furtive scrutiny of his cold grey eye , the motionless stillness of he from head to foot tell their own tale . a mortal dread have master he body and soul and his own recognition of Pesca be the cause of it !

 I walk on rapidly without answer . the circumstance under which the Count have leave the theatre suggest to I that his extraordinary anxiety to escape Pesca might carry he to further extremity still . he might escape I , too , by leave London . I doubt the future if I allow he so much as a day 's freedom to act as he please . and I doubt that foreign stranger , who have get the start of we , and whom I suspect of intentionally follow he out .

I only ask you to consult your own recollection , and to say if they suggest no past cause for the terror which the first sight of you produce in that man . " 

 to my unutterable surprise , these word , harmless as they appear to I , produce the same astounding effect on Pesca which the sight of Pesca have produce on the Count . the rosy face of my little friend whiten in an instant , and he draw back from I slowly , tremble from head to foot .

 he speak in a whisper he look at I as if I have suddenly reveal to he some hide danger to both of we . in less than one minute of time he be so altered from the easy , lively , quaint little man of all my past experience , that if I have meet he in the street , change as I see he now , I should most certainly not have know he again .

 " there be no other way , " I answer .

 the trembling earnestness with which he utter this extraordinary warning , carry with it , to my mind , the conviction that he speak the truth .

"

  3   it be only right to mention here , that I repeat Pesco 's statement to I with the careful suppression and alteration which the serious nature of the subject and my own sense of duty to my friend demand . my first and last concealment from the reader be those which caution render absolutely necessary in this portion of the narrative . 

 " you know nothing of my motive for leave Italy , " he begin , " except that it be for political reason . if I have be drive to this country by the persecution of my government , I should not have keep those reason a secret from you or from any one . I have conceal they because no government authority have pronounce the sentence of my exile . you have hear , Walter , of the political society that be hide in every great city on the continent of Europe ? to one of those society I belong in Italy and belong still in England . when I come to this country , I come by the direction of my chief . I be over - zealous in my young time I run the risk of compromise myself and other . for those reason I be order to emigrate to England and to wait . I emigrate I have wait I wait still . to - morrow I may be call away ten year hence I may be call away . it be all one to I I be here , I support myself by teaching , and I wait . I violate no oath   you shall hear why presently   in make my confidence complete by tell you the name of the society to which I belong . all I do be to put my life in your hand . if what I say to you now be ever know by other to have pass my lip , as certainly as we two sit here , I be a dead man . " 

 he whisper the next word in my ear . I keep the secret which he thus communicate . the society to which he belong will be sufficiently individualise for the purpose of these page , if I call it " the Brotherhood , " on the few occasion when any reference to the subject will be need in this place . 

 " the object of the Brotherhood , " Pesca go on , " be , briefly , the object of other political society of the same sort the destruction of tyranny and the assertion of the right of the people . the principle of the Brotherhood be two . so long as a man 's life be useful , or even harmless only , he have the right to enjoy it . but , if his life inflict injury on the well - being of his fellow - man , from that moment he forfeit the right , and it be not only no crime , but a positive merit , to deprive he of it . it be not for I to say in what frightful circumstance of oppression and suffer this society take its rise . it be not for you to say you Englishmen , who have conquer your freedom so long ago , that you have conveniently forget what blood you shed , and what extremity you proceed to in the conquering it be not for you to say how far the bad of all exasperation may , or may not , carry the maddened man of an enslaved nation . the iron that have enter into our soul have go too deep for you to find it . leave the refugee alone ! laugh at he , distrust he , open your eye in wonder at that secret self which smoulder in he , sometimes under the everyday respectability and tranquillity of a man like I sometimes under the grind poverty , the fierce squalor , of man less lucky , less pliable , less patient than I be but judge we not ! in the time of your first Charles you might have do we justice the long luxury of your own freedom have make you incapable of do we justice now . " 

 all the deep feeling of his nature seem to force themselves to the surface in those word all his heart be pour out to I for the first time in our life but still his voice never rise , still his dread of the terrible revelation he be make to I never leave he . 

 " so far , " he resume , " you think the society like other society . its object   in your english opinion   be anarchy and revolution . it take the life of a bad king or a bad minister , as if the one and the other be dangerous wild beast to be shoot at the first opportunity . I grant you this . but the law of the Brotherhood be the law of no other political society on the face of the earth . the member be not know to one another . there be a president in Italy ; there be president abroad . each of these have his secretary . the president and the secretary know the member , but the member , among themselves , be all stranger , until their chief see fit , in the political necessity of the time , or in the private necessity of the society , to make they know to each other . with such a safeguard as this there be no oath among we on admittance . we be identify with the Brotherhood by a secret mark , which we all bear , which last while our life last . we be tell to go about our ordinary business , and to report ourselves to the president , or the secretary , four time a year , in the event of our service be require . we be warn , if we betray the Brotherhood , or if we injure it by serve other interest , that we die by the principle of the Brotherhood die by the hand of a stranger who may be send from the other end of the world to strike the blow or by the hand of our own bosom - friend , who may have be a member unknown to we through all the year of our intimacy . sometimes the death be delay sometimes it follow close on the treachery . it be our first business to know how to wait our second business to know how to obey when the word be speak . some of we may wait our life through , and may not be want . some of we may be call to the work , or to the preparation for the work , the very day of our admission . I myself the little , easy , cheerful man you know , who , of his own accord , would hardly lift up his handkerchief to strike down the fly that buzz about his face I , in my young time , under provocation so dreadful that I will not tell you of it , enter the Brotherhood by an impulse , as I might have kill myself by an impulse . I must remain in it now it have get I , whatever I may think of it in my well circumstance and my cooler manhood , to my die day . while I be still in Italy I be choose secretary , and all the member of that time , who be bring face to face with my president , be bring face to face also with I . " 

 I begin to understand he I see the end towards which his extraordinary disclosure be now tend . he wait a moment , watch I earnestly watch till he have evidently guess what be pass in my mind before he resume .

 he sign to I not to answer he rise remove his coat and roll up the shirt - sleeve on his left arm .

" whatever come of it you shall not reproach I with have hide anything from you which it be necessary to your interest to know . I have say that the Brotherhood identify its member by a mark that last for life . see the place , and the mark on it for yourself . " 

 he raise his bare arm , and show I , high on the upper part of it and in the inner side , a brand deeply burn in the flesh and stain of a bright blood - red colour . I abstain from describe the device which the brand represent . it will be sufficient to say that it be circular in form , and so small that it would have be completely cover by a shill coin .

 " good - night , my friend . " 



 VI 

 my first conviction as soon as I find myself outside the house , be that no alternative be leave I but to act at once on the information I have receive to make sure of the Count that night , or to risk the loss , if I only delay till the morning , of Laura 's last chance . I look at my watch it be ten o'clock . 

 not the shadow of a doubt cross my mind of the purpose for which the Count have leave the theatre . his escape from we , that evening , be beyond all question the preliminary only to his escape from London . the mark of the Brotherhood be on his arm I feel as certain of it as if he have show I the brand ; and the betrayal of the Brotherhood be on his conscience I have see it in his recognition of Pesca . 

 it be easy to understand why that recognition have not be mutual . a man of the Count 's character would never risk the terrible consequence of turn spy without look to his personal security quite as carefully as he look to his golden reward . the shaven face , which I have point out at the Opera , might have be cover by a beard in Pesca 's time his dark brown hair might be a wig his name be evidently a false one . the accident of time might have help he as well his immense corpulence might have come with his later year . there be every reason why Pesca should not have know he again every reason also why he should have know Pesca , whose singular personal appearance make a marked man of he , go where he might . 

 I have say that I feel certain of the purpose in the Count 's mind when he escape we at the theatre . how could I doubt it , when I see , with my own eye , that he believe himself , in spite of the change in his appearance , to have be recognise by Pesca , and to be therefore in danger of his life ? if I could get speech of he that night , if I could show he that I , too know of the mortal peril in which he stand , what result would follow ? plainly this . one of we must be master of the situation one of we must inevitably be at the mercy of the other . 

 I owe it to myself to consider the chance against I before I confront they . I owe it to my wife to do all that lie in my power to lessen the risk . 

 the chance against I want no reckoning up they be all merge in one . if the count discover , by my own avowal , that the direct way to his safety lie through my life , he be probably the last man in existence who would shrink from throw I off my guard and take that way , when he have I alone within his reach . the only mean of defence against he on which I could at all rely to lessen the risk , present themselves , after a little careful thinking , clearly enough . before I make any personal acknowledgment of my discovery in his presence , I must place the discovery itself where it would be ready for instant use against he , and safe from any attempt at suppression on his part . if I lay the mine under his foot before I approach he , and if I leave instruction with a third person to fire it on the expiration of a certain time , unless direction to the contrary be previously receive under my own hand , or from my own lip in that event the Count 's security be absolutely dependent upon mine , and I might hold the vantage ground over he securely , even in his own house . 

 this idea occur to I when I be close to the new lodging which we have take on return from the sea - side . I go in without disturb any one , by the help of my key . a light be in the hall , and I steal up with it to my workroom to make my preparation , and absolutely to commit myself to an interview with the Count , before either Laura or Marian could have the slight suspicion of what I intend to do . 

 a letter address to Pesca represent the sure measure of precaution which it be now possible for I to take . I write as follow 

 " the man whom I point out to you at the Opera be a member of the Brotherhood , and have be false to his trust . put both these assertion to the test instantly . you know the name he go by in England . his address be no . 5 Forest Road , St. John 's Wood . on the love you once bear I , use the power entrust to you without mercy and without delay against that man . I have risk all and lose all and the forfeit of my failure have be pay with my life . " 

 I sign and date these line , enclose they in an envelope , and seal it up . on the outside I write this direction : " keep the enclosure unopened until nine o'clock to - morrow morning . if you do not hear from I , or see I , before that time , break the seal when the clock strike , and read the content . " I add my initial , and protect the whole by enclose it in a second seal envelope , address to Pesca at his lodging . 

 nothing remain to be do after this but to find the mean of send my letter to its destination immediately . I should then have accomplish all that lay in my power . if anything happen to I in the Count 's house , I have now provide for his answer it with his life . 

 that the mean of prevent his escape , under any circumstance whatever , be at Pesca 's disposal , if he choose to exert they , I do not for an instant doubt . the extraordinary anxiety which he have express to remain unenlightened as to the Count 's identity or , in other word , to be leave uncertain enough about fact to justify he to his own conscience in remain passive betray plainly that the mean of exercise the terrible justice of the Brotherhood be ready to his hand , although , as a naturally humane man , he have shrink from plainly say as much in my presence . the deadly certainty with which the vengeance of foreign political society can hunt down a traitor to the cause , hide himself where he may , have be too often exemplified , even in my superficial experience , to allow of any doubt . consider the subject only as a reader of newspaper , case recur to my memory , both in London and in Paris , of foreigner find stab in the street , whose assassin could never be trace of body and part of body throw into the Thames and the Seine , by hand that could never be discover of death by secret violence which could only be account for in one way . I have disguise nothing relate to myself in these page , and I do not disguise here that I believe I have write Count Fosco 's death - warrant , if the fatal emergency happen which authorise Pesca to open my enclosure . 

 I leave my room to go down to the ground floor of the house , and speak to the landlord about find I a messenger . he happen to be ascend the stair at the time , and we meet on the landing . his son , a quick lad , be the messenger he propose to I on hear what I want . we have the boy upstairs , and I give he his direction . he be to take the letter in a cab , to put it into Professor Pesca 's own hand , and to bring I back a line of acknowledgment from that gentleman return in the cab , and keep it at the door for my use . it be then nearly half - past ten . I calculate that the boy might be back in twenty minute , and that I might drive to St. John 's Wood , on his return , in twenty minute more . 

 when the lad have depart on his errand I return to my own room for a little while , to put certain paper in order , so that they might be easily find in case of the bad . the key of the old - fashioned bureau in which the paper be keep I seal up , and leave it on my table , with Marian 's name write on the outside of the little packet . this do , I go downstairs to the sitting - room , in which I expect to find Laura and Marian await my return from the Opera . I feel my hand tremble for the first time when I lay it on the lock of the door . 

 no one be in the room but Marian . she be read , and she look at her watch , in surprise , when I come in . 

 " how early you be back ! " she say . " you must have come away before the Opera be over . " 

 " yes , " I reply , " neither Pesca nor I wait for the end . where be Laura ? " 

 " she have one of her bad headache this evening , and I advise she to go to bed when we have do tea . " 

 I leave the room again on the pretext of wish to see whether Laura be asleep . Marian 's quick eye be begin to look inquiringly at my face Marian 's quick instinct be begin to discover that I have something weigh on my mind . 

 when I enter the bedchamber , and softly approach the bedside by the dim flicker of the night - lamp , my wife be asleep . 

 we have not be marry quite a month yet . if my heart be heavy , if my resolution for a moment falter again , when I look at her face turn faithfully to my pillow in her sleep when I see her hand rest open on the coverlid , as if it be wait unconsciously for mine surely there be some excuse for I ? " God bless and keep you , my darling ! " I whisper , and leave she . 

 Marian be at the stairhead wait for I . she have a fold slip of paper in her hand .

 " your letter be receive . if I do not   see you before the time you mention , I will break the seal when the clock strike . " 

 I place the paper in my pocket - book , and make for the door . Marian meet I on the threshold , and push I back into the room , where the candle - light fall full on my face . she hold I by both hand , and her eye fasten searchingly on mine . 

 " I see ! " she say , in a low eager whisper . " you be try the last chance to - night . " 

 " yes , the last chance and the good , " I whisper back . 

 " not alone ! oh , Walter , for God 's sake , not alone ! let I go with you . do not   refuse I because I be only a woman . I must go ! I will go ! I will wait outside in the cab ! "

 I dare not allow her time to say a word more . she try to hold I again . I unclasp her hand , and be out of the room in a moment . the boy below hear I on the stair , and open the hall - door . I jump into the cab before the driver could get off the box . " Forest Road , St. John 's Wood , " I call to he through the front window . " double fare if you get there in a quarter of an hour . " " I will do it , sir . " I look at my watch . eleven o'clock . not a minute to lose . 

 the rapid motion of the cab , the sense that every instant now be bring I near to the Count , the conviction that I be embark at last , without let or hindrance , on my hazardous enterprise , heat I into such a fever of excitement that I shout to the man to go fast and fast . as we leave the street , and cross St. John 's Wood Road , my impatience so completely overpower I that I stand up in the cab and stretch my head out of the window , to see the end of the journey before we reach it . just as a church clock in the distance strike the quarter past , we turn into the Forest Road . I stop the driver a little away from the Count 's house , pay and dismiss he , and walk on to the door . 

 as I approach the garden gate , I see another person advance towards it also from the direction opposite to mine . we meet under the gas lamp in the road , and look at each other . I instantly recognise the light - haired foreigner with the scar on his cheek , and I think he recognise I . he say nothing , and instead of stop at the house , as I do , he slowly walk on . be he in the Forest Road by accident ? or have he follow the Count home from the Opera ? 

 I do not pursue those question . after wait a little till the foreigner have slowly pass out of sight , I ring the gate bell . it be then twenty minute past eleven late enough to make it quite easy for the Count to get rid of I by the excuse that he be in bed . 

 the only way of provide against this contingency be to send in my name without ask any preliminary question , and to let he know , at the same time , that I have a serious motive for wish to see he at that late hour . accordingly , while I be wait , I take out my card and write under my name " on important business . " the maid - servant answer the door while I be write the last word in pencil , and ask I distrustfully what I " pleased to want . "

 in a minute or so she reappear . " her master 's compliment , and would I be so obliging as to say what my business be ? " " take my compliment back , " I reply , " and say that the business can not be mention to any one but your master . " she leave I again , again return , and this time ask I to walk in . 

 I follow she at once . in another moment I be inside the Count 's house . 



 VII 

 there be no lamp in the hall , but by the dim light of the kitchen candle , which the girl have bring upstairs with she , I see an elderly lady steal noiselessly out of a back room on the ground floor . she cast one viperish look at I as I enter the hall , but say nothing , and go slowly upstairs without return my bow . my familiarity with Marian 's journal sufficiently assure I that the elderly lady be Madame Fosco .

 he be still in his evening dress , except his coat , which he have throw across a chair . his shirt - sleeve be turn up at the wrist , but no high . a carpet - bag be on one side of he , and a box on the other . book , paper , and article of wear apparel be scatter about the room . on a table , at one side of the door , stand the cage , so well know to I by description , which contain his white mouse . the canary and the cockatoo be probably in some other room . he be seat before the box , pack it , when I go in , and rise with some paper in his hand to receive I . his face still betray plain trace of the shock that have overwhelm he at the Opera . his fat cheek hang loose , his cold grey eye be furtively vigilant , his voice , look , and manner be all sharply suspicious alike , as he advance a step to meet I , and request , with distant civility , that I would take a chair .

 the unconcealed curiosity , with which he look hard in my face while he speak , convince I that I have pass unnoticed by he at the Opera . he have see Pesca first , and from that moment till he leave the theatre he have evidently see nothing else . my name would necessarily suggest to he that I have not come into his house with other than a hostile purpose towards himself , but he appear to be utterly ignorant thus far of the real nature of my errand . 

 " I be fortunate in find you here to - night , " I say . " you seem to be on the point of take a journey ? "

 " in some degree . " 

 " in what degree ? do you know where I be go to ? "

 he slip by I with the quickness of thought , lock the door , and put the key in his pocket . 

 " you and I , Mr. Hartright , be excellently well acquainted with one another by reputation , " he say . " do it , by any chance , occur to you when you come to this house that I be not the sort of man you could trifle with ? "

 I walk far into the room , and stand opposite to he on the rug before the fireplace . he draw a chair in front of the door , and sit down on it , with his left arm rest on the table . the cage with the white mouse be close to he , and the little creature scamper out of their sleep - place as his heavy arm shake the table , and peer at he through the gap in the smartly paint wire .

 " what I say . " 

 the perspiration break out thickly on his broad forehead . his left hand steal over the edge of the table . there be a drawer in it , with a lock , and the key be in the lock . his finger and thumb close over the key , but do not turn it .

 the same livid leaden change pass over his face which I have see pass over it at the theatre . the deadly glitter in his eye shine steady and straight into mine . he say nothing . but his left hand slowly open the table - drawer , and softly slip into it . the harsh grating noise of something heavy that he be move unseen to I sound for a moment , then cease . the silence that follow be so intense that the faint tick nibble of the white mouse at their wire be distinctly audible where I stand . 

 my life hang by a thread , and I know it . at that final moment I think with his mind , I feel with his finger I be as certain as if I have see it of what he keep hide from I in the drawer . 

 " wait a little , " I say . " you have get the door lock you see I do not   move you see my hand be empty . wait a little . I have something more to say . "

 " perhaps I do . " 

 " I be think , " he remark quietly , " whether I shall add to the disorder in this room by scatter your brain about the fireplace . " 

 if I have move at that moment , I see in his face that he would have do it .

 he read the line aloud : " your letter be receive . if I do not   hear from you before the time you mention , I will break the seal when the clock strike . " 

 another man in his position would have need some explanation of those word the Count feel no such necessity . one reading of the note show he the precaution that I have take as plainly as if he have be present at the time when I adopt it . the expression of his face change on the instant , and his hand come out of the drawer empty .

" 

 " I do , and I mean to have it . " 

 " on condition ? " 

 " on no condition . " 

 his hand drop into the drawer again .

I summon you to answer three question before you open your lip again . hear they they be necessary to this interview . answer they they be necessary to I . " he hold up one finger of his right hand . I note that circumstance for future use in my memory , which forget nothing , and proceed . " he hold up another finger .  " a man whom I have every reason to depend on , and whom you have every reason to fear . " 

 my answer reach he to some purpose . his left hand tremble audibly in the drawer .

"

 he smile with the most supreme composure , and carelessly wave his right hand .

 he look at I with the first honest expression that have cross his face in my presence an expression of blank amazement . I could see that I sink in his estimation as a dangerous man from that moment . he shut up the drawer at once , fold his arm over his breast , and listen to I with a smile of satirical attention .

"

I prefer to pass it over . I invite you to resume the discussion of your term . what do you demand ? "

 he raise his finger again . " one ! " he say , check I off with the steady attention of a practical man .

 " good ! you have mention your term , now listen to mine . the responsibility to myself of admit what you be pleased to call the ' conspiracy ' be less , perhaps , upon the whole , than the responsibility of lay you dead on that hearthrug . let we say that I meet your proposal on my own condition . the statement you demand of I shall be write , and the plain proof shall be produce . you call a letter from my late lamented friend inform I of the day and hour of his wife 's arrival in London , write , sign , and date by himself , a proof , I suppose ? I can give you this . I can also send you to the man of whom I hire the carriage to fetch my visitor from the railway , on the day when she arrive his order - book may help you to your date , even if his coachman who drive I prove to be of no use . these thing I can do , and will do , on condition . I recite they . first condition ! Madame Fosco and I leave this house when and how we please , without interference of any kind on your part . second condition ! you wait here , in company with I , to see my agent , who be come at seven o'clock in the morning to regulate my affair . you give my agent a write order to the man who have get your sealed letter to resign his possession of it . you wait here till my agent place that letter unopened in my hand , and you then allow I one clear half - hour to leave the house after which you resume your own freedom of action and go where you please . third condition ! you give I the satisfaction of a gentleman for your intrusion into my private affair , and for the language you have allow yourself to use to I at this conference . the time and place , abroad , to be fix in a letter from my hand when I be safe on the continent , and that letter to contain a strip of paper measure accurately the length of my sword . those be my term . inform I if you accept they yes or no . " 


 the extraordinary mixture of prompt decision , far - sight cunning , and mountebank bravado in this speech , stagger I for a moment and only for a moment . the one question to consider be , whether I be justify or not in possess myself of the mean of establish Laura 's identity at the cost of allow the scoundrel who have rob she of it to escape I with impunity . I know that the motive of secure the just recognition of my wife in the birthplace from which she have be drive out as an impostor , and of publicly erase the lie that still profane her mother 's tombstone , be far purer , in its freedom from all taint of evil passion , than the vindictive motive which have mingle itself with my purpose from the first . and yet I can not honestly say that my own moral conviction be strong enough to decide the struggle in I by themselves . they be help by my remembrance of Sir Percival 's death . how awfully , at the last moment , have the working of the retribution there be snatch from my feeble hand ! what right have I to decide , in my poor mortal ignorance of the future , that this man , too , must escape with impunity because he escape I ? I think of these thing perhaps with the superstition inherent in my nature , perhaps with a sense worthy of I than superstition . it be hard , when I have fasten my hold on he at last , to loosen it again of my own accord but I force myself to make the sacrifice . in plainer word , I determine to be guide by the one high motive of which I be certain , the motive of serve the cause of Laura and the cause of Truth . 


 " I accept your condition , " I say . " with one reservation on my part . " 

 " what reservation may that be ? " he ask .

 my object in make this stipulation be simply to prevent he from carry away write evidence of the nature of my communication with Pesca . the fact of my communication he would necessarily discover , when I give the address to his agent in the morning . but he could make no use of it on his own unsupported testimony even if he really venture to try the experiment which need excite in I the slight apprehension on Pesca 's account . 

 " I grant your reservation , " he reply , after consider the question gravely for a minute or two . " it be not worth dispute the letter shall be destroy when it come into my hand . " 

 he rise , as he speak , from the chair in which he have be sit opposite to I up to this time . with one effort he appear to free his mind from the whole pressure on it of the interview between we thus far . " Ouf ! " he cry , stretch his arm luxuriously , " the skirmish be hot while it last . take a seat , Mr. Hartright . we meet as mortal enemy hereafter let we , like gallant gentleman , exchange polite attention in the meantime . permit I to take the liberty of call for my wife . " 

 he unlock and open the door . " eleanor ! " he call out in his deep voice . the lady of the viperish face come in " Madame Fosco Mr. Hartright , " say the Count , introduce we with easy dignity . " my angel , " he go on , address his wife , " will your labour of pack up allow you time to make I some nice strong coffee ? I have write business to transact with Mr. Hartright and I require the full possession of my intelligence to do justice to myself . "

 the Count walk to a writing - table near the window , open his desk , and take from it several quire of paper and a bundle of quill pen . he scatter the pen about the table , so that they might lie ready in all direction to be take up when want , and then cut the paper into a heap of narrow slip , of the form use by professional writer for the press . " I shall make this a remarkable document , " he say , look at I over his shoulder . " habit of literary composition be perfectly familiar to I . one of the rare of all the intellectual accomplishment that a man can possess be the grand faculty of arrange his idea . immense privilege ! I possess it . do you ? " 

 he march backwards and forwards in the room , until the coffee appear , hum to himself , and mark the place at which obstacle occur in the arrangement of his idea , by strike his forehead from time to time with the palm of his hand . the enormous audacity with which he seize on the situation in which I place he , and make it the pedestal on which his vanity mount for the one cherish purpose of self - display , master my astonishment by main force . sincerely as I loathe the man , the prodigious strength of his character , even in its most trivial aspect , impress I in spite of myself .

 " may I offer you some coffee , Mr. Hartright ? " he say , before he sit down . 

 I decline .

" Bravo ! " he cry , spring to his foot with the activity of a young man , and look I straight in the face with a smile of superb triumph . 

 " do , Mr. Hartright ! " he announce with a self - renovate thump of his fist on his broad breast . " do , to my own profound satisfaction to your profound astonishment , when you read what I have write . the subject be exhaust : the man Fosco be not . I proceed to the arrangement of my slip to the revision of my slip to the reading of my slip address emphatically to your private ear . four o'clock have just strike . good ! arrangement , revision , reading , from four to five . short snooze of restoration for myself from five to six . final preparation from six to seven . affair of agent and seal letter from seven to eight . at eight , en route . behold the programme ! " 

 he sit down cross - legged on the floor among his paper , string they together with a bodkin and a piece of string revise they , write all the title and honour by which he be personally distinguish at the head of the first page , and then read the manuscript to I with loud theatrical emphasis and profuse theatrical gesticulation . the reader will have an opportunity , ere long , of form his own opinion of the document . it will be sufficient to mention here that it answer my purpose . 

 he next write I the address of the person from whom he have hire the fly , and hand I Sir Percival 's letter . it be date from Hampshire on the 25th of July , and it announce the journey of " Lady Glyde " to London on the 26th . thus , on the very day   the 25th   when the doctor 's certificate declare that she have die in St. John 's Wood , she be alive , by Sir Percival 's own showing , at Blackwater and , on the day after , she be to take a journey ! when the proof of that journey be obtain from the flyman , the evidence would be complete . 

 " a quarter - past five , " say the Count , look at his watch . " time for my restorative snooze . I personally resemble Napoleon the Great , as you may have remark , Mr. Hartright I also resemble that immortal man in my power of command sleep at will . excuse I one moment .  with those word she open her book , and never look at I or speak to I from that time till the time when her husband wake .

" he look about the room , and notice the cage with his white mouse in it . " ah !  " number one . cockatoo of transcendent plumage : attraction , of himself , to all visitor of taste . number two . canary of unrivalled vivacity and intelligence : worthy of the garden of Eden , worthy also of the garden in the Regent 's Park . homage to British Zoology . offer by Fosco . " 

 the pen splutter again , and the flourish be attach to his signature . 

 " Count ! you have not include the mouse , " say Madame Fosco 

 he leave the table , take her hand , and place it on his heart .
