 " admirable tenderness ! " say Madame Fosco , admire her husband , with a last viperish look in my direction . she take up the cage carefully , and leave the room . 

 the Count look at his watch . in spite of his resolute assumption of composure , he be get anxious for the agent 's arrival . the candle have long since be extinguish , and the sunlight of the new morning pour into the room . it be not till five minute past seven that the gate bell rang , and the agent make his appearance . he be a foreigner with a dark beard . 

 " Mr. Hartright Monsieur Rubelle , " say the Count , introduce we . he take the agent   a foreign spy , in every line of his face , if ever there be one yet   into a corner of the room , whisper some direction to he , and then leave we together . " Monsieur Rubelle , " as soon as we be alone , suggest with great politeness that I should favour he with his instruction . I write two line to Pesca , authorise he to deliver my seal letter " to the bearer , " direct the note , and hand it to Monsieur Rubelle . 

 the agent wait with I till his employer return , equip in travel costume . the Count examine the address of my letter before he dismiss the agent . " I think so ! " he say , turn on I with a dark look , and alter again in his manner from that moment . 

 he complete his packing , and then sit consult a travel map , make entry in his pocket - book , and look every now and then impatiently at his watch . not another word , address to myself , pass his lip . the near approach of the hour for his departure , and the proof he have see of the communication establish between Pesca and myself , have plainly recall his whole attention to the measure that be necessary for secure his escape . 

 a little before eight o'clock , Monsieur Rubelle come back with my unopened letter in his hand . the Count look carefully at the superscription and the seal , light a candle , and burn the letter . " I perform my promise , " he say , " but this matter , Mr. Hartright , shall not end here . " 

 the agent have keep at the door the cab in which he have return . he and the maid - servant now busy themselves in remove the luggage . Madame Fosco come downstairs , thickly veil , with the travel cage of the white mouse in her hand . she neither speak to I nor look towards I . her husband escort she to the cab . " follow I as far as the passage , " he whisper in my ear ; " I may want to speak to you at the last moment . "

 " remember the third condition ! " he whisper . " you shall hear from I , Mr. Hartright I may claim from you the satisfaction of a gentleman soon than you think for . " he catch my hand before I be aware of he , and wring it hard then turn to the door , stop , and come back to I again .

  Count of the Holy Roman Empire , Knight Grand Cross of the Order of the Brazen Crown , Perpetual Arch - Master of the Rosicrucian Masons of Mesopotamia ; attach   in Honorary Capacities   to Societies Musical , Societies Medical , Societies Philosophical , and Societies General Benevolent , throughout Europe ; etc . etc . etc . 


 the count 's narrative 

 in the summer of eighteen hundred and fifty I arrive in England , charge with a delicate political mission from abroad . confidential person be semi - officially connect with I , whose exertion I be authorise to direct , Monsieur and Madame Rubelle be among the number . some week of spare time be at my disposal , before I enter on my function by establish myself in the suburb of London . Curiosity may stop here to ask for some explanation of those function on my part . I entirely sympathise with the request . I also regret that diplomatic reserve forbid I to comply with it . 

 I arrange to pass the preliminary period of repose , to which I have just refer , in the superb mansion of my late lamented friend , Sir Percival Glyde . he arrive from the continent with his wife . I arrive from the continent with mine . England be the land of domestic happiness how appropriately we enter it under these domestic circumstance ! 

 the bond of friendship which unite Percival and myself be strengthen , on this occasion , by a touching similarity in the pecuniary position on his side and on mine . we both want money . immense necessity ! universal want ! be there a civilised human being who do not feel for we ? how insensible must that man be ! or how rich ! 

 I enter into no sordid particular , in discuss this part of the subject . my mind recoil from they . with a roman austerity , I show my empty purse and Percival 's to the shrink public gaze . let we allow the deplorable fact to assert itself , once for all , in that manner , and pass on . 

 we be receive at the mansion by the magnificent creature who be inscribe on my heart as " Marian , " who be know in the colder atmosphere of society as " Miss Halcombe . " 

 just Heaven ! with what inconceivable rapidity I learn to adore that woman . at sixty , I worship she with the volcanic ardour of eighteen . all the gold of my rich nature be pour hopelessly at her foot . my wife poor angel ! my wife , who adore I , get nothing but the shilling and the penny . such be the World , such man , such love . what be we   I ask   but puppet in a show - box ? oh , omnipotent Destiny , pull our string gently ! dance we mercifully off our miserable little stage ! 

 the precede line , rightly understand , express an entire system of philosophy . it be mine .

 the interest interest , breathless and immense ! with which I be here concern , begin with the deplorable calamity of Marian 's illness .

Chemistry especially have always have irresistible attraction for I from the enormous , the illimitable power which the knowledge of it confer . chemist I assert it emphatically might sway , if they please , the destiny of humanity . let I explain this before I go far . 

 mind , they say , rule the world . but what rule the mind ? the body   follow I closely here   lie at the mercy of the most omnipotent of all potentate the Chemist . give I fosco chemistry ; and when Shakespeare have conceive Hamlet , and sit down to execute the conception with a few grain of powder drop into his daily food , I will reduce his mind , by the action of his body , till his pen pour out the most abject drivel that have ever degrade paper . under similar circumstance , revive I the illustrious Newton . I guarantee that when he see the apple fall he shall eat it , instead of discover the principle of gravitation . Nero 's dinner shall transform Nero into the mild of man before he have do digest it , and the morning draught of Alexander the great shall make Alexander run for his life at the first sight of the enemy the same afternoon . on my sacred word of honour it be lucky for Society that modern chemist be , by incomprehensible good fortune , the most harmless of mankind . the mass be worthy father of family , who keep shop . the few be philosopher besotte with admiration for the sound of their own lecturing voice , visionary who waste their life on fantastic impossibility , or quack whose ambition soar no higher than our corn . thus Society escape , and the illimitable power of Chemistry remain the slave of the most superficial and the most insignificant end .

 at the ripe age of sixty , I make this unparalleled confession . youth ! I invoke your sympathy . Maidens ! I claim your tear . 

 a word more , and the attention of the reader   concentrate breathlessly on myself   shall be release .

I might answer this by simply refer to my own character , and by ask , in my turn , where , in the history of the world , have a man of my order ever be find without a woman in the background self - immolate on the altar of his life ? but I remember that I be write in England , I remember that I be marry in England , and I ask if a woman 's marriage obligation in this country provide for her private opinion of her husband 's principle ? no ! they charge she unreservedly to love , honour , and obey he . that be exactly what my wife have do . I stand here on a supreme moral elevation , and I loftily assert her accurate performance of her conjugal duty . Silence , Calumny ! your sympathy , Wives of England , for Madame Fosco !

I should have open the door of the Prison of Life , and have extend to the captive   incurably afflict in mind and body both   a happy release . 

 third question . on a calm revision of all the circumstance be my conduct worthy of any serious blame ? most emphatically , no ! have I not carefully avoid expose myself to the odium of commit unnecessary crime ? with my vast resource in chemistry , I might have take Lady Glyde 's life . at immense personal sacrifice I follow the dictate of my own ingenuity , my own humanity , my own caution , and take her identity instead . judge I by what I might have do . how comparatively innocent ! how indirectly virtuous I appear in what I really do !

 the STORY CONCLUDED BY WALTER HARTRIGHT 



 I 

 when I close the last leaf of the Count 's manuscript the half - hour during which I have engage to remain at Forest Road have expire . Monsieur Rubelle look at his watch and bow . I rise immediately , and leave the agent in possession of the empty house . I never see he again I never hear more of he or of his wife . out of the dark byway of villainy and deceit they have crawl across our path into the same byway they crawl back secretly and be lose . 

 in a quarter of an hour after leave Forest Road I be at home again . 

 but few word suffice to tell Laura and Marian how my desperate venture have end , and what the next event in our life be likely to be . I leave all detail to be describe later in the day , and hasten back to St. John 's Wood , to see the person of whom Count Fosco have order the fly , when he go to meet Laura at the station . 

 the address in my possession lead I to some " livery stable , " about a quarter of a mile distant from Forest Road . the proprietor prove to be a civil and respectable man . when I explain that an important family matter oblige I to ask he to refer to his book for the purpose of ascertain a date with which the record of his business transaction might supply I , he offer no objection to grant my request . the book be produce , and there , under the date of " July 26th , 1850 , " the order be enter in these word 

 " Brougham to Count Fosco , 5 Forest Road . two o'clock .   John Owen . " 

 I find on inquiry that the name of " John Owen , " attach to the entry , refer to the man who have be employ to drive the fly . he be then at work in the stable - yard , and be send for to see I at my request . 

 " do you remember drive a gentleman , in the month of July last , from Number five Forest Road to the Waterloo Bridge station ? " I ask . 

 " well , sir , " say the man , " I can not   exactly say I do . " 

 " perhaps you remember the gentleman himself ? can you call to mind drive a foreigner last summer a tall gentleman and remarkably fat ? " the man 's face brighten directly . 

 " I remember he , sir ! the fat gentleman as ever I see , and the heavy customer as ever I drive . yes , yes I call he to mind , sir ! we do go to the station , and it be from Forest Road . there be a parrot , or summat like it , screech in the window . the gentleman be in a mortal hurry about the lady 's luggage , and he give I a handsome present for look sharp and get the box . " 

 get the box ! I recollect immediately that Laura 's own account of herself on her arrival in London describe her luggage as be collect for she by some person whom Count Fosco bring with he to the station . this be the man . 

 " do you see the lady ? " I ask . " what do she look like ? be she young or old ? " 

 " well , sir , what with the hurry and the crowd of people push about , I can not   rightly say what the lady look like . I can not   call nothing to mind about she that I know of except her name . " 

 " you remember her name ? " 

 " yes , sir . her name be Lady Glyde . " 

 " how do you come to remember that , when you have forget what she look like ? " 

 the man smile , and shift his foot in some little embarrassment . 

 " why , to tell you the truth , sir , " he say , " I have not   be long marry at that time , and my wife 's name , before she change it for mine , be the same as the lady 's meaning the name of Glyde , sir . the lady mention it herself . ' be your name on your box , ma'am ? ' say I. ' yes , ' say she , ' my name be on my luggage it be Lady Glyde . ' ' come ! ' I say to myself , ' I have   a bad head for gentlefolk ' name in general but this one come like an old friend , at any rate . ' I can not   say nothing about the time , sir , it might be nigh on a year ago , or it might not . but I can swear to the stout gentleman , and swear to the lady 's name . " 

 there be no need that he should remember the time the date be positively establish by his master 's order - book . I feel at once that the mean be now in my power of strike down the whole conspiracy at a blow with the irresistible weapon of plain fact . without a moment 's hesitation , I take the proprietor of the livery stable aside and tell he what the real importance be of the evidence of his order - book and the evidence of his driver . an arrangement to compensate he for the temporary loss of the man 's service be easily make , and a copy of the entry in the book be take by myself , and certify as true by the master 's own signature . I leave the livery stable , have settle that John Owen be to hold himself at my disposal for the next three day , or for a long period if necessity require it . 

 I now have in my possession all the paper that I want the district registrar 's own copy of the certificate of death , and Sir Percival 's dated letter to the Count , be safe in my pocket - book . 

 with this write evidence about I , and with the coachman 's answer fresh in my memory , I next turn my step , for the first time since the beginning of all my inquiry , in the direction of Mr. Kyrle 's office . one of my object in pay he this second visit be , necessarily , to tell he what I have do . the other be to warn he of my resolution to take my wife to Limmeridge the next morning , and to have she publicly receive and recognise in her uncle 's house . I leave it to Mr. Kyrle to decide under these circumstance , and in Mr. Gilmore 's absence , whether he be or be not bind , as the family solicitor , to be present on that occasion in the family interest . 

 I will say nothing of Mr. Kyrle 's amazement , or of the term in which he express his opinion of my conduct from the first stage of the investigation to the last . it be only necessary to mention that he at once decide on accompany we to Cumberland . 

 we start the next morning by the early train . Laura , Marian , Mr. Kyrle , and myself in one carriage , and John Owen , with a clerk from Mr. Kyrle 's office , occupy place in another . on reach the Limmeridge station we go first to the farmhouse at Todd 's Corner . it be my firm determination that Laura should not enter her uncle 's house till she appear there publicly recognise as his niece . I leave Marian to settle the question of accommodation with Mrs. Todd , as soon as the good woman have recover from the bewilderment of hear what our errand be in Cumberland , and I arrange with her husband that John Owen be to be commit to the ready hospitality of the farm - servant . these preliminary complete , Mr. Kyrle and I set forth together for Limmeridge House . 

 I can not write at any length of our interview with Mr. Fairlie , for I can not recall it to mind without feeling of impatience and contempt , which make the scene , even in remembrance only , utterly repulsive to I . I prefer to record simply that I carry my point . Mr. Fairlie attempt to treat we on his customary plan . we pass without notice his polite insolence at the outset of the interview . we hear without sympathy the protestation with which he try next to persuade we that the disclosure of the conspiracy have overwhelm he . he absolutely whine and whimper at last like a fretful child . " how be he to know that his niece be alive when he be tell that she be dead ? he would welcome dear Laura with pleasure , if we would only allow he time to recover . do we think he look as if he want hurry into his grave ? no . then , why hurry he ? " he reiterate these remonstrance at every available opportunity , until I check they once for all , by place he firmly between two inevitable alternative . I give he his choice between do his niece justice on my term , or face the consequence of a public assertion of her existence in a court of law . Mr. Kyrle , to whom he turn for help , tell he plainly that he must decide the question then and there . characteristically choose the alternative which promise soonest to release he from all personal anxiety , he announce with a sudden outburst of energy , that he be not strong enough to bear any more bullying , and that we might do as we please . 

 Mr. Kyrle and I at once go downstairs , and agree upon a form of letter which be to be send round to the tenant who have attend the false funeral , summon they , in Mr. Fairlie 's name , to assemble in Limmeridge House on the next day but one . an order refer to the same date be also write , direct a statuary in Carlisle to send a man to Limmeridge churchyard for the purpose of erase an inscription Mr. Kyrle , who have arrange to sleep in the house , undertake that Mr. Fairlie should hear these letter read to he , and should sign they with his own hand . 

 I occupy the interval day at the farm in write a plain narrative of the conspiracy , and in add to it a statement of the practical contradiction which fact offer to the assertion of Laura 's death . this I submit to Mr. Kyrle before I read it the next day to the assemble tenant . we also arrange the form in which the evidence should be present at the close of the reading . after these matter be settle , Mr. Kyrle endeavour to turn the conversation next to Laura 's affair . know , and desire to know nothing of those affair , and doubt whether he would approve , as a man of business , of my conduct in relation to my wife 's life - interest in the legacy leave to Madame Fosco , I beg Mr. Kyrle to excuse I if I abstain from discuss the subject . it be connect , as I could truly tell he , with those sorrow and trouble of the past which we never refer to among ourselves , and which we instinctively shrink from discuss with other . 

 my last labour , as the evening approach , be to obtain " the Narrative of the Tombstone , " by take a copy of the false inscription on the grave before it be erase . 


 the day come the day when Laura once more enter the familiar breakfast - room at Limmeridge House . all the person assemble rise from their seat as Marian and I lead she in . a perceptible shock of surprise , an audible murmur of interest run through they , at the sight of her face . Mr. Fairlie be present   by my express stipulation , with Mr. Kyrle by his side . his valet stand behind he with a smell - bottle ready in one hand , and a white handkerchief , saturate with eau - de - Cologne , in the other . 

 I open the proceeding by publicly appeal to Mr. Fairlie to say whether I appear there with his authority and under his express sanction . he extend an arm , on either side , to Mr. Kyrle and to his valet be by they assist to stand on his leg , and then express himself in these term : " allow I to present Mr. Hartright . I be as great an invalid as ever , and he be so very obliging as to speak for I . the subject be dreadfully embarrassing . please hear he , and do not   make a noise ! " with those word he slowly sink back again into the chair , and take refuge in his scented pocket - handkerchief . 

 the disclosure of the conspiracy follow , after I have offer my preliminary explanation , first of all , in the few and the plain word . I be there present   I inform my hearer   to declare , first , that my wife , then sit by I , be the daughter of the late Mr. Philip Fairlie ; secondly , to prove by positive fact , that the funeral which they have attend in Limmeridge churchyard be the funeral of another woman ; thirdly , to give they a plain account of how it have all happen . without further preface , I at once read the narrative of the conspiracy , describe it in clear outline , and dwell only upon the pecuniary motive for it , in order to avoid complicate my statement by unnecessary reference to Sir Percival 's secret . this do , I remind my audience of the date on the inscription in the churchyard   the 25th , and confirm its correctness by produce the certificate of death . I then read they Sir Percival 's letter of the 25th , announce his wife 's intended journey from Hampshire to London on the 26th . I next show that she have take that journey , by the personal testimony of the driver of the fly , and I prove that she have perform it on the appoint day , by the order - book at the livery stable . Marian then add her own statement of the meeting between Laura and herself at the mad - house , and of her sister 's escape . after which I close the proceeding by inform the person present of Sir Percival 's death and of my marriage . 

 Mr. Kyrle rise when I resume my seat , and declare , as the legal adviser of the family , that my case be prove by the plainest evidence he have ever hear in his life . as he speak those word , I put my arm round Laura , and raise she so that she be plainly visible to every one in the room . " be you all of the same opinion ? " I ask , advance towards they a few step , and point to my wife . 

 the effect of the question be electrical . far down at the low end of the room one of the old tenant on the estate start to his foot , and lead the rest with he in an instant . I see the man now , with his honest brown face and his iron - grey hair , mount on the window - seat , wave his heavy riding - whip over his head , and lead the cheer . " there she be , alive and hearty God bless she ! Gi ' it tongue , lad ! Gi ' it tongue ! " the shout that answer he , reiterate again and again , be the sweetest music I ever hear . the labourer in the village and the boy from the school , assemble on the lawn , catch up the cheering and echo it back on we . the farmer ' wife cluster round Laura , and struggle which should be first to shake hand with she , and to implore she , with the tear pour over their own cheek , to bear up bravely and not to cry . she be so completely overwhelmed , that I be oblige to take she from they , and carry she to the door . there I give she into Marian 's care Marian , who have never fail we yet , whose courageous self - control do not fail we now . leave by myself at the door , I invite all the person present   after thank they in Laura 's name and mine   to follow I to the churchyard , and see the false inscription strike off the tombstone with their own eye . 

 they all leave the house , and all join the throng of villager collect round the grave , where the statuary 's man be wait for we . in a breathless silence , the first sharp stroke of the steel sound on the marble . not a voice be hear not a soul move , till those three word , " Laura , Lady Glyde , " have vanish from sight . then there be a great heave of relief among the crowd , as if they feel that the last fetter of the conspiracy have be strike off Laura herself , and the assembly slowly withdraw . it be late in the day before the whole inscription be erase . one line only be afterwards engrave in its place : " Anne Catherick , July 25th , 1850 . " 

 I return to Limmeridge House early enough in the evening to take leave of Mr. Kyrle . he and his clerk , and the driver of the fly , go back to London by the night train . on their departure an insolent message be deliver to I from Mr. Fairlie who have be carry from the room in a shattered condition , when the first outbreak of cheering answer my appeal to the tenantry . the message convey to we " Mr. Fairlie 's good congratulation , " and request to know whether " we contemplate stop in the house . " I send back word that the only object for which we have enter his door be accomplish that I contemplate stop in no man 's house but my own and that Mr. Fairlie need not entertain the slight apprehension of ever see we or hear from we again . we go back to our friend at the farm to rest that night , and the next morning escort to the station , with the heartiest enthusiasm and good will , by the whole village and by all the farmer in the neighbourhood we return to London . 

 as our view of the Cumberland hill fade in the distance , I think of the first disheartening circumstance under which the long struggle that be now past and over have be pursue . it be strange to look back and to see , now , that the poverty which have deny we all hope of assistance have be the indirect mean of our success , by force I to act for myself . if we have be rich enough to find legal help , what would have be the result ? the gain   on Mr. Kyrle 's own showing   would have be more than doubtful the loss , judge by the plain test of event as they have really happen , certain . the law would never have obtain I my interview with Mrs. Catherick . the law would never have make Pesca the mean of force a confession from the Count . 



 II 

 two more event remain to be add to the chain before it reach fairly from the outset of the story to the close . 

 while our new sense of freedom from the long oppression of the past be still strange to we , I be send for by the friend who have give I my first employment in wood engraving , to receive from he a fresh testimony of his regard for my welfare . he have be commission by his employer to go to Paris , and to examine for they a fresh discovery in the practical application of his Art , the merit of which they be anxious to ascertain . his own engagement have not allow he leisure time to undertake the errand , and he have most kindly suggest that it should be transfer to I . I could have no hesitation in thankfully accept the offer , for if I acquit myself of my commission as I hop I should , the result would be a permanent engagement on the illustrated newspaper , to which I be now only occasionally attach . 

 I receive my instruction and pack up for the journey the next day . on leave Laura once more   under what change circumstance !   in her sister 's care , a serious consideration recur to I , which have more than once cross my wife 's mind , as well as my own , already I mean the consideration of Marian 's future . have we any right to let our selfish affection accept the devotion of all that generous life ? be it not our duty , our good expression of gratitude , to forget ourselves , and to think only of she ? I try to say this when we be alone for a moment , before I go away . she take my hand , and silence I at the first word . 

 " after all that we three have suffer together , " she say " there can be no parting between we till the last parting of all . my heart and my happiness , Walter , be with Laura and you . wait a little till there be child 's voice at your fireside . I will teach they to speak for I in their language , and the first lesson they say to their father and mother shall be we can not   spare our aunt ! " 

 my journey to Paris be not undertake alone . at the eleventh hour Pesca decide that he would accompany I . he have not recover his customary cheerfulness since the night at the Opera , and he determine to try what a week 's holiday would do to raise his spirit . 

 I perform the errand entrust to I , and draw out the necessary report , on the fourth day from our arrival in Paris . the fifth day I arrange to devote to sight - seeing and amusement in Pesca 's company . 

 our hotel have be too full to accommodate we both on the same floor . my room be on the second story , and Pesca 's be above I , on the third . on the morning of the fifth day I go upstairs to see if the Professor be ready to go out . just before I reach the landing I see his door open from the inside a long , delicate , nervous hand   not my friend 's hand certainly   hold it ajar . at the same time I hear Pesca 's voice say eagerly , in low tone , and in his own language " I remember the name , but I do not   know the man . you see at the Opera he be so changed that I could not recognise he . I will forward the report I can do no more . " " no more need be do , " answer the second voice . the door open wide , and the light - haired man with the scar on his cheek the man I have see follow Count Fosco 's cab a week before come out . he bow as I draw aside to let he pass his face be fearfully pale and he hold fast by the banister as he descend the stair . 

 I push open the door and enter Pesca 's room . he be crouch up , in the strange manner , in a corner of the sofa . he seem to shrink from I when I approach he . 

 " be I disturb you ? " I ask . " I do not know you have a friend with you till I see he come out . " 

 " no friend , " say Pesca eagerly . " I see he to - day for the first time and the last . " 

 " I be afraid he have bring you bad news ? " 

 " horrible news , Walter ! let we go back to London I do not   want to stop here I be sorry I ever come . the misfortune of my youth be very hard upon I , " he say , turn his face to the wall , " very hard upon I in my later time . I try to forget they and they will not forget I ! " 

 " we can not   return , I be afraid , before the afternoon , " I reply . " would you like to come out with I in the meantime ? " 

 " no , my friend , I will wait here . but let we go back to - day pray let we go back . " 

 I leave he with the assurance that he should leave Paris that afternoon . we have arrange the evening before to ascend the Cathedral of Notre Dame , with Victor Hugo 's noble romance for our guide . there be nothing in the french capital that I be more anxious to see , and I depart by myself for the church . 

 Approaching Notre Dame by the river - side , I pass on my way the terrible dead - house of Paris the Morgue . a great crowd clamour and heave round the door . there be evidently something inside which excite the popular curiosity , and feed the popular appetite for horror . 

 I should have walk on to the church if the conversation of two man and a woman on the outskirt of the crowd have not catch my ear . they have just come out from see the sight in the Morgue , and the account they be give of the dead body to their neighbour describe it as the corpse of a man a man of immense size , with a strange mark on his left arm . 

 the moment those word reach I I stop and take my place with the crowd go in . some dim foreshadowing of the truth have cross my mind when I hear Pesca 's voice through the open door , and when I see the stranger 's face as he pass I on the stair of the hotel . now the truth itself be reveal to I reveal in the chance word that have just reach my ear . other vengeance than mine have follow that fated man from the theatre to his own door from his own door to his refuge in Paris . other vengeance than mine have call he to the day of reckoning , and have exact from he the penalty of his life . the moment when I have point he out to Pesca at the theatre in the hearing of that stranger by our side , who be look for he too be the moment that seal his doom . I remember the struggle in my own heart , when he and I stand face to face the struggle before I could let he escape I and shudder as I recall it . 

 slowly , inch by inch , I press in with the crowd , move nearer and near to the great glass screen that part the dead from the living at the Morgue nearer and nearer , till I be close behind the front row of spectator , and could look in . 

 there he lay , unowne , unknown , expose to the flippant curiosity of a french mob ! there be the dreadful end of that long life of degraded ability and heartless crime ! hush in the sublime repose of death , the broad , firm , massive face and head front we so grandly that the chatter Frenchwomen about I lift their hand in admiration , and cry in shrill chorus , " ah , what a handsome man ! " the wound that have kill he have be strike with a knife or dagger exactly over his heart . no other trace of violence appear about the body except on the left arm , and there , exactly in the place where I have see the brand on Pesca 's arm , be two deep cut in the shape of the letter T , which entirely obliterate the mark of the Brotherhood . his clothe , hang above he , show that he have be himself conscious of his danger they be clothe that have disguise he as a French artisan . for a few moment , but not for long , I force myself to see these thing through the glass screen . I can write of they at no great length , for I see no more . 

 the few fact in connection with his death which I subsequently ascertain   partly from Pesca and partly from other source , may be state here before the subject be dismiss from these page . 

 his body be take out of the Seine in the disguise which I have describe , nothing be find on he which reveal his name , his rank , or his place of abode . the hand that strike he be never trace , and the circumstance under which he be kill be never discover . I leave other to draw their own conclusion in reference to the secret of the assassination as I have draw mine . when I have intimate that the foreigner with the scar be a member of the Brotherhood   admit in Italy after Pesca 's departure from his native country , and when I have far add that the two cut , in the form of a t , on the left arm of the dead man , signify the italian word " Traditore , " and show that justice have be do by the Brotherhood on a traitor , I have contribute all that I know towards elucidate the mystery of Count Fosco 's death . 

 the body be identify the day after I have see it by mean of an anonymous letter address to his wife . he be bury by Madame Fosco in the cemetery of Pere la Chaise . fresh funeral wreath continue to this day to be hang on the ornamental bronze railing round the tomb by the Countess 's own hand . she live in the strict retirement at Versailles . not long since she publish a biography of her deceased husband . the work throw no light whatever on the name that be really his own or on the secret history of his life it be almost entirely devoted to the praise of his domestic virtue , the assertion of his rare ability , and the enumeration of the honour confer on he . the circumstance attend his death be very briefly notice , and be sum up on the last page in this sentence " his life be one long assertion of the right of the aristocracy and the sacred principle of order , and he die a martyr to his cause . " 



 III 

 the summer and autumn pass after my return from Paris , and bring no change with they which need be notice here . we live so simply and quietly that the income which I be now steadily earn suffice for all our want . 

 in the February of the new year our first child be bear a son . my mother and sister and Mrs. Vesey be our guest at the little christen party , and Mrs. Clements be present to assist my wife on the same occasion . Marian be our boy 's godmother , and Pesca and Mr. Gilmore   the latter act by proxy   be his godfather . I may add here that when Mr. Gilmore return to we a year later he assist the design of these page , at my request , by write the Narrative which appear early in the story under his name , and which , though first in order of precedence , be thus , in order of time , the last that I receive . 

 the only event in our life which now remain to be record , occur when our little Walter be six month old . 

 at that time I be send to Ireland to make sketch for certain forthcoming illustration in the newspaper to which I be attach . I be away for nearly a fortnight , correspond regularly with my wife and Marian , except during the last three day of my absence , when my movement be too uncertain to enable I to receive letter . I perform the latter part of my journey back at night , and when I reach home in the morning , to my utter astonishment there be no one to receive I . Laura and Marian and the child have leave the house on the day before my return . 

 a note from my wife , which be give to I by the servant , only increase my surprise , by inform I that they have go to Limmeridge House . Marian have prohibit any attempt at write explanation I be entreat to follow they the moment I come back complete enlightenment await I on my arrival in Cumberland and I be forbid to feel the slight anxiety in the meantime . there the note end . it be still early enough to catch the morning train . I reach Limmeridge House the same afternoon . 

 my wife and Marian be both upstairs . they have establish themselves   by way of complete my amazement   in the little room which have be once assign to I for a studio , when I be employ on Mr. Fairlie 's drawing . on the very chair which I use to occupy when I be at work Marian be sit now , with the child industriously suck his coral upon her lap while Laura be stand by the well - remember drawing - table which I have so often use , with the little album that I have fill for she in past time open under her hand . 

 " what in the name of heaven have bring you here ? " I ask . " do Mr. Fairlie know ? " 

 Marian suspend the question on my lip by tell I that Mr. Fairlie be dead . he have be strike by paralysis , and have never rally after the shock . Mr. Kyrle have inform they of his death , and have advise they to proceed immediately to Limmeridge House . 

 some dim perception of a great change dawn on my mind . Laura speak before I have quite realise it . she steal close to I to enjoy the surprise which be still express in my face . 

 " my darling Walter , " she say , " must we really account for our boldness in come here ? I be afraid , love , I can only explain it by break through our rule , and refer to the past . " 

 " there be not the least necessity for do anything of the kind , " say Marian . " we can be just as explicit , and much more interesting , by refer to the future . " she rise and hold up the child kicking and crow in her arm . " do you know who this be , Walter ? " she ask , with bright tear of happiness gathering in her eye . 

 " even MY bewilderment have its limit , " I reply . " I think I can still answer for know my own child . " 

 " child ! " she exclaim , with all her easy gaiety of old time . " do you talk in that familiar manner of one of the land gentry of England ? be you aware , when I present this illustrious baby to your notice , in whose presence you stand ? evidently not ! let I make two eminent personage know to one another : Mr. Walter Hartright the heir of LIMMERIDGE . " 


 so she speak . in write those last word , I have write all . the pen falter in my hand . the long , happy labour of many month be over . Marian be the good angel of our life let Marian end our Story .
